column121: I executed a flight in a Piper Arrow 28R-200 in the practice area; in which we came back doing a no gear landing. Today was [the student's] introductory flight in this aircraft. After taking off and leveling at 1;000 ft.; I noticed that the Gear in Transit Light turned on. I notice that the aircraft did not have an extra drag on it and the gear lever was still in the up position; breaker was in and that the emergency extension was not pressed; but the indicator was on. I radio for assistance in the practice area and two aircraft responded to my call; one of them was Aircraft Y. I let him know of my position and situation and he offered to help by flying next to Aircraft X and verify the position of the gears. I put the gear lever in the down position and flew along [a highway] waiting for verification from Aircraft Y. The flight instructor radios that the gears were down but not secure. At this point I had tried to text [ZZZ group chat] and advise my situation. [I] spoke to pilots over the radio for a solution on how to bring the plane safely back and made sure I did not miss anything on the manual or checklist. After cycling the gear and trying the emergency extension and realizing it was not working; I radioed ZZZ1 Tower and advise them of my situation. I did a low approach to verify if gears are down which I was told by tower that the nose gear did not appear to be down. After trying one more time in the pattern to cycle the gear and go over checklist and manual I decided to make the landing.

column121: [In] my preflight noticed that both engines needed a quart of oil. Filled both back to the proper indication. Closed both engines then continued with my preflight. Found nothing else that needed attention. I took my first load. I noticed a slightly low oil pressure on that flight and called another King Air pilot that trained me to ask about it. As I got out of the plane the owner and I noticed that oil was all over the left wing. Opened it up and I did not properly secure the oil stick/cap. A small amount of oil was still visible on the stick. I called the A and P and we were advised to put oil back in the engine; clean it up out of the bay and try a run up. The start went normally; oil pressure was back. I taxied down to the end of XX did another run up. Everything was in the normal range. Did a normal takeoff left hand pattern while watching the left engine. Proceeded to a normal approach rounded out; pulled power back then heard the gear unsafe warning. A split second later heard tick of a prop hitting the ground. My initial reaction was to push the power forward and take off but reason took over and I pulled power all the way back and bellied the plane in. Then I secured the plane and got out.I did several things wrong that day most importantly I did not follow the checklist and I did not do a GUMPS (Gas; Undercarriage; Mixture; Prop; Switches) check. I also had been driving 4 hours prior to the event. I am devastated. I should have followed the checklist.

column119: Environment - Non Weather Related; Human Factors
column121: I was on an IFR Flight Plan from ZZZ to ZZZ1.  I had poor speed control on the approach.  I was legally IFR current; but; as it turned out; I was clearly not proficient.  The COVID-19 prevented me from flying with a (COVID safe) safety pilot and getting recent practice.  I was scheduled for simulator training for the following week.I flew VFR out to ZZZ for two days.  The weather was predicted VMC for the next several days.  The day after we arrived; the wind shifted and blew in severe smoke from forest fires.  We considered staying for a few more days; but there was no available lodging and the smoke was getting worse.  I reviewed an IFR plan to go back to ZZZ1 and when the destination weather improved enough; I filed IFR; got my clearance; and departed.  The departure; on route; and vectors to final were smooth and uneventful. There was an aircraft ahead that was going slower and ATC requested that I slow to 90 knots; which is what I should have already been slowing to.  I agreed and reduced power.  The autopilot was holding the altitude coming up to the IAF and I inadvertently pulled off too much power.  I was coming up on the IAF and descent and I was verifying that everything (else) was ready to go; when I noticed that the speed was rapidly reducing.  The autopilot (AP) was trimming nose up to maintain altitude.  I hit control wheel steering; nosed down; and added power.  I had to do a lot of trimming to get the nose back down and I over shot the altitude and rapidly picked up speed.  I got the nose back up; on altitude; and thought that the AP would acquire the glide slope.  Nope; in the excitement; I missed arming the approach and flew right through it.  Now I dove for the GS and picked up more speed again.  Now I'm on the GS and check to see if the AP would acquire it and no it started another excursion and so I shut it off and hand flew the rest of the way.  It was stabilized at this point; so I continued the approach and landed.Mistake 1.  I should have already been planning and reducing speed approaching IAF.Mistake 2.  I should have paid more attention to my speed reduction technique.Mistake 3.  I should have requested ATC to bring me around again to have time to set up properly.Mistake 4.  I should have done what I normally do and that is to turn off the AP and hand fly anytime things get weird.My takeaway is that my IFR currency should be done more often; even if it is done in a simulator.

column118: Air Traffic Control Separated Traffic
column121: I was working two sectors during smoke and haze conditions requiring all aircraft to pick up IFR flight plans. A large TFR for firefighting was on the east side of my airspace and encroached on the finals for HWD and OAK departures which normally depart on 170 heading across the OAK [Runway] 30 final and towards SFO final which then have to be vectored back across the [Runway] 30 final to the northeast and across the HWD final and then the OAK [Runway] 28 final. Because of the TFR and traffic complexity; I issued a right turn of 040 and maintain 4000 ft. to HWD to issue to Aircraft Y. Per the NorCal TRACON and HWD LOA; released aircraft are to be airborne within 2 minutes. Aircraft X was approximately 11 miles from HWD and cleared for the ILS 30 approach to OAK when I released Aircraft Y. Aircraft Y took 3 mins and 7 seconds to tag up on radar; well outside the LOA requirements. I observed Aircraft Y going upwind and not turning which prompted me to call HWD Tower to confirm the heading to 040 which they affirmed. Aircraft Y was first observed in a turn at 1200 ft. and 1.5 miles upwind. This is well beyond expected pilot performance. Our Quality Control Department is stating that even if Aircraft Y had turned; it would not have been separated because divergence is not permitted with this aircraft. I have looked through JO 7110.65 and found nothing that should prevent me from applying the provisions of that paragraph. I contest that I had applicable divergence separation as Aircraft Y was assigned 040 (and not complying) and Aircraft X was assigned the ILS 30 which is a 296 heading.I am requesting the development of new departure headings and procedures to eliminate the congestion under the OAK final and eliminate the current procedures which are unsafe and nearly unusable given the noise abatement restrictions; which are in effect at all times; and wake re-categorization separation. I also propose new training for how management expects us to get HWD departure aircraft safely out of our airspace given such significant constraints. Also; I'm requesting a follow up to occur with the pilot since he failed to comply with his ATC assigned heading in a timely matter as this was clearly a pilot deviation. Furthermore; had HWD Tower complied with our LOA; I do not believe a conflict would have even been triggered through automation.

column121: Good rest; day VFR with clear skies throughout the flight. This was the second leg of the day; the first leg being a ferry flight. Uneventful flight. On arrival to the airport we both noticed and mentioned it to each other that ATC left us up a little high. Passing through 15;000 ft. or so and very close to what we thought was the airport. I mentioned 'rather than getting a lot of vectors let's just cancel and make a 360 to the left and go to ZZZZZ; which is the IAF on the RNAV GPS RWY XX); which we did. In no rush; I extended the outbound leg of the 360 turn. Coming out of the turn a little disoriented I asked; 'can you extend the centerline of the runway.' The SIC (Second In Command) which has little time in the plane and not very experienced with the FMS was having trouble putting in the centerline. I asked for a centerline from the RUNWAY; the SIC drew the path from ZZZZZ1; a fix on the approach. The approach is a 45 degree angle from the runway; so when he put the extended line it was from ZZZZZ1 not the runway. I asked again 'I don't think that's right; it doesn't line up.' It was still showing the 45-degree angle to the runway. I again queried him and said; 'What is the runway heading? Make the centerline off that; not any of the fixes.' By then; at around 1000 ft. AGL; we both saw RWY XX painted on the runway in front of us and thought; no worries; I see the numbers.On the landing roll out the chart did not pop up. I asked 'why are we not on the chart? The 350 shows the plane on all the charts'.Reaching the end of the runway the passenger came up and said 'guys; you are at the wrong field; ZZZ is 8 miles south of here; this is ZZZ1' He is a pilot also and was very sympathetic and said 'this happens so often; the operations people don't come out anymore.'I apologized and started planning the trip to ZZZ. I called dispatch and advised them of what happened and what we needed. We taxied back to the approach end of RWY XX and found a place to park and called the FODM (Flight Operations Duty Manager) while the SIC was getting the clearance from ATC. I was told 'he's not answering his phone'. I asked them to 'please find him; I need to talk to him'. A minute or so later [he] answered. I explained what happened. He was very calming and eager to work with me. We talked about the weather; the runway length and width; parachute activity in the area; VFR daytime only airport; he even gave me the frequencies to both airports. Our phones started to beep; at that time [he] said; 'I think they are trying to shut you down; I'll take care of it'. I mentioned that we both had plenty of duty time and felt fine to do the 8-mile flight. It is hard to recall every word but he mentioned something to the effect; 'ok; it looks good; let me check on something; I'll call you back'.After waiting a few minutes and thinking that we had covered everything we needed to for the flight; I decided to depart.The flight to ZZZ was uneventful. The passenger had no problem with the event and being a pilot himself in that area; he kept telling me 'don't worry it really does happen here a lot; ZZZ3 had a RWY XX also. They finally had to change the numbers to XY even though its heading is [different]'. We helped them load their car and they drove off.   Things we should/could have done...-Stayed with ATC and gotten better vectors to the airport.-Made sure the centerline was indeed off the runway NOT the FAF.

column121: During an ILS approach to Runway XXR; the PF failed to slow early enough on final approach. PF configured later than desired considering the situation (fast due to earlier ATC request) resulting in the aircraft becoming high on glide path while slowing to configure for landing. PF needed to use a sink rate higher than desired to return to glide slope. We met the published parameters at all of our approach gates; but the first portion of the approach was unstable. We landed safely on XXR and proceeded routinely to the gate.The main lesson learned was to slow earlier on final approach to allow a more stable approach. We accomplished all checklists and procedures and landed safely; but the crew would have been more able to look for potential threats if the PF had flown a more stable approach during the first portion of the approach. PNF was just back from several months off - take away for PNF was to address a rapidly evolving situation sooner and prompt the PF to configure sooner.
column123: [Narrative contained no additional information.]

column118: Flight Crew Returned To Clearance
column121: While on RNAV RWY 4 approach into UIN; between the initial fix (JISNA) and the FAF (WUSIS); we descended below the minimum altitude prescribed for that segment of the approach; which was 2100 ft. MSL . I did not recognize that we were below until about 1600 ft. MSL and by the time I had corrected our pitch and added power we between 1500 ft. and 1600 ft. MSL. We immediately climbed back up to 2100'; and at about 2 miles prior to the FAF; we re-evaluated our position; flight guidance panel and configuration and felt that we could resume the approach as normal; which was an autopilot coupled LPV approach. Part of the cause of this issue was my set up of the flight guidance panel. We had been descending from about 7000 ft. and navigating direct to the initial fix of JISNA. At some point in the descent; ATC cleared us for the approach and told us to maintain 2700 ft. until JISNA. We were given the frequency change from Center to CTAF at this point as well. At that point; I thought I was in VNAV mode and descending. I hit APPR (Approach); since we were cleared for the approach; and dialed my altitude selector to the bottom of the approach; which was 960 ft. MSL for the minimums. My assumption was that we would then VNAV to 2700 ft. at JISNA; or essentially just VNAV to 2100 ft. by WUSIS; grab the glideslope and continue the descent. I felt I had set it up so that all stepdown altitudes would be met. At that point; I may have become distracted with other radio calls and prepping for landing. During this time; the SIC (Second In Command) was running checklists; trying to reach the FBO  on unicom; and also conferring with ATC to ensure we had a good way to cancel IFR  once we were on the ground. Neither of us were totally focused on the task at hand; which was to monitor the plane as we were beginning this approach. I estimate we were doing about an 800 fpm descent rate. We may have been in VNAV mode; but I was not coupled to the glide path; so we were descending in such a way that would not allow the autopilot to level us off at each fix. 1. PIC/SIC should have been totally focused on the primary task at hand: entering; establishing; and flying the approach as published. This would have caught any errors in my programming of the descent and flight guidance panel before we descended below published altitude. 2. Both pilots should be looking at the flight guidance panel in these critical times to verify proper mode; and calling out altitudes around these fixes. 3. Once the altitude deviation was realized; I should have climbed up; executed a missed approach; and called ATC to vector us back around for the approach. 4. My decision not to go missed was influenced by my desire to keep my passenger's schedule. That is a hazardous attitude that I want others to be aware of and learn from.
column123: As our passengers were boarding the plane; they decided to change the destination to UIN due to the fact we were unlikely to be able to get into ZZZ due to low ceilings. The altitude deviation occurred on the RNAV (Area Navigation) approach to RW 4 at UIN. We had been cleared for the approach and we were to instructed to maintain 2700 ft. until established on the approach and had been approved to change to advisory frequency. I was the nonflying pilot and was busy verifying everything on the checklist had been completed and communicating with KC center and UNICOM at UIN to make position reports. I also verified with KC center that we would be able to contact them on the ground using that frequency or did we need to cancel our IFR through flight service. This took a little extra time to complete due to my lack of experience with the Proline Fusion avionics so I was not monitoring the PFD as closely as I should have. We were inside JISNA (IAF)and I was trying to determine our position from the airport in order to give an accurate position report when the pilot flying said something to the effect of wait why are we low. He immediately added power selected vertical speed up and set our altitude selector to 2100 ft. I was focused on other tasks in the cockpit so I cannot say with 100% certainty what autopilot modes were selected previously. We were somewhere between 1600 ft. and 1500 ft. MSL when he started the recovery. We reached 2100 ft. two miles prior to the WUSIS (FAF) intercepted the glide path and landed without further issue. During the debrief with my captain after the flight we discussed the need to treat such a critical phase of flight with the utmost respect and after getting established on the approach to keep all attention on the task at hand which is flying/monitoring the approach. We also discussed the need to make a verbal call out of all autopilot/ flight director mode changes to make sure both pilots are on the same page and are totally aware of what the automation is doing if it is engaged.

column121: We had just fully configured for landing; crossed the FAF and began our final descent on the RNAV RWY 17R approach into DWH. I was the pilot flying and had a customer pilot in the right seat for a demo flight. Tower called out traffic at our 1 o'clock and 2 miles unknown altitude that was apparently on final then began to turn north 'possibly NORDO'. We called visual and were instructed to continue the approach. Both of us in the cockpit saw Aircraft Y turning what looked to be base to final converging on our path. I broke off the final approach course to the left keeping Aircraft Y in sight and let tower know. Aircraft Y appeared to notice us as it made an abrupt climbing right turn away from us. Once I felt we had enough separation I elected to resume the approach; notified tower; received landing clearance and landed uneventfully. If I had to guess; we came within about 200-300 ft. of Aircraft Y. There is a lot of student traffic and flight schools at DWH. Aircraft Y was most likely a student training flight. Tower was transmitting in the blind to Aircraft Y repeatedly and told them not to circle on final and to look for light gun signals.Exercise heightened awareness when flying to/from airports where there is a lot of student training.

column121: At 10;000 ft.; sterile FD; descent into DFW; we began preparation procedures [in the] galley and cabin. Once finished; FA3 (Flight Attendant) asked who was in the forward lavatory and we determined the wheelchair passenger at 1F must have gone to the bathroom. FA3 knocked and told her we were landing now and to return to her seat now. She said; 'Ok; I'm coming.' FA1 & FA3 continued asking her to return to her seat and when the wheels dropped. She was still in the bathroom saying she was coming. FA3 and FA1 continued communicating with her and at less than 1 minute to landing the decision was made that she would not be able to safely make it to a seat and she was informed to 'stay in the lavatory.' The flight landed and taxied to toward Gate A19 at which time FA1 informed the Captain we had a passenger land in the lavatory. The Captain asked for basic information at this time. FA1 informed him that the passenger was still in the lavatory and if we were going to be stopped so she could return to her seat. The Captain stated; 'We're going to keep moving.' The passenger was told to remain in the lavatory until the seatbelt sign was turned off. Once we parked at the gate; the passenger came out on her own and apologized saying she was getting sick. FA1 confirmed her name as Passenger A. The Captain then brought FA1 into the cockpit and stated that he should have known we didn't have a passenger in their seat for landing and that he had to do reports now.
column123: After [the] Captain made [the] 'prepare for landing announcement' I; FA3 (Flight Attendant); walked up to [the] front and started clearing the cabin from there to [the] back. I finished preparing the cabin and headed up to my seat. When I was getting ready to sit; I saw that there was someone in front lavatory and asked FA1 if he saw anyone go in there. He explained that passenger 1F who came on [a] wheelchair had to use [the] lavatory last minute. I noticed how close we were getting to the ground so I started knocking on [the] door and told her; 'We are approaching landing and you need to come out;' to which she responded; 'I'm coming;' so I kept knocking on door and telling her she needed to come out already. Then; I noticed the landing gear is coming out. I then tell her; 'You need to come out NOW; landing gear is down we are about to land.' She said; 'Okay;' and seemed like she was going to come out but then didn't. That's when FA1 got up and told tell her to stay in the lavatory; which at that point; [was] so close to [the] ground and [since there was] such little time; [it] seemed like the best decision.We landed safely and checked on [the] passenger; she said she was okay. FA1 called [the] flight deck and explained to the Captain that we had someone in the lavatory [and] to let us know if we were going to make any stops so that we could have her go back to [her] seat safely since it takes her time to walk to her seat. The Captain told FA1; 'We are going to keep moving;' so [the] passenger remained in [the] lavatory until we came to a safe stop. The passenger came out and apologized for what she had done and said she understood she had done wrong but she really needed to go. Captain asked us why we didn't call him in the air we explained everything happened just so suddenly and we were so close to [the] ground with sterile flight deck and basically not much time.

column121: I was flying the RNAV (GPS) 34 straight in approach into FHR. The airport wall veiled in smoke/haze. The approach went well and I had visual contact with the airport 2 miles out. As I approached the runway; I was a little high and floated; so I decided to go-around. It was a mental slip. As I was flying downwind; I realized that I messed up; but I had visual contact with the airport and at that point thought it was safer to continue the pattern. I turned to base and then as I turned to final; I turned late. I decided then that I would just over fly the airport and fly the published missed. I contacted Whitney Approach for instructions to get back on the approach. Approach rightfully rebuked me for the go-around and then gave me instructions to return me to the approach. I flew the approach and landed without incident. Key learning from this event:1. If you cannot land for any reason; you must fly the published missed; regardless of prevailing conditions; unless you have cancelled IFR with ATC or until ATC has given other instructions.2. As time permits; scan the missed procedure on final to aid in preparation.3. Increase the time spent briefing the missed procedure when briefing the approach.

column121: On approach into ZZZ on ILS XR we had been asked to keep our speed up until the marker. We complied with the request. The weather was very gusty winds and both myself and the pilot flying communicated as to what the V-ref target speeds should be. Sometime before 1000 feet the pilot flying was trying to control his speed and configure at the last minute in order to comply with the Tower's request. We had traffic about 3 1/2 miles in front of us. With the gusty winds and both of us pilots being relatively new to the aircraft and because this aircraft was an X type aircraft I had relayed to the pilot flying that we didn't want to have a tail strike with such gusty winds; so I suggested that at any time either one of us felt that we weren't going to land in the touchdown zone or we didn't feel we weren't going to make a normal landing then we needed to 'go-around.' About 1200 feet we got another gusty wind report from the Tower that caused me to readjust the target speed with my head being down and losing situational awareness (SA). We never got too fast for our configuration but I called the'1000 feet' call at approximately 930 feet. I had already configured the aircraft for the landing checklist except for final flaps. I feel the pilot flying got distracted by me changing the target speeds that he lost his SA and forgot to call for flaps 30/landing checklist. I initiated the checklist and realized we were hadn't gone to flaps 30 and he hadn't called for the checklist. I selected final flaps and finished the checklist. I called 500 feet and he responded stable and we landed uneventfully.
column123: On arrival into ZZZ I was configured flaps 15; 180 knots until the marker. About the time I was going to call flaps 30 we received a new gusting  wind report from tower that required an adjustment to our target speed. We discussed the wind and adjusted for about a 15 knot add over flaps 30 ref of about 148 target speed. Upon completing landing checklist which at this point was also delayed and we discovered that the flaps were not positioned yet to 30. We immediately positioned the flaps to 30 and landed. The flaps 15 speed I was flying was very close to the new target speed and the aircraft was stable with respect to airspeed altitude and course. Contributing factors can include my new position as a requalification on the aircraft with only about 4 landings this year.

column121: I made a delayed turn from base to final approach while conducting the ILS XXR into ZZZ. The reason for the delayed turn was a momentary lapse in CRM skills. I was the flying pilot; my copilot was the monitoring pilot. We were on a base leg when the Controller gave us a turn towards the final approach. I remember dialing in the correct heading; but forgot to press heading select.  About ten seconds or so elapsed before the Controller asked if we were turning. I realized my error and immediately pressed heading select. The Controller gave us a new heading and an approach clearance. We turned onto final with a slight overshoot and above the glide slope. We configured for landing and used vertical speed mode to intercept the glide slope from above. We were on speed and altitude well before the 1;500 foot gate. Other than the late turn and the high glide slope intercept; there was no other incident or error.The fact is the I/we made an error by not following through with Verbalize Verify and Monitor. I know that we both verbalized the heading change; I recall we both Verified setting the heading; we both; however; forgot to monitor the FMAs for the change. I think that we realized the something wasn't right a split second before the controller queried us. There were many threats preceding this error. This is an all-night flight with an early morning low visibility arrival. The airspace is quite congested and there were many aircraft with similar sounding call signs. We had a runway change while on the arrival and the Controller kept us little higher and gave us a turn a little earlier than we had expected. I had a scheduling change in the middle of this pairing which reduced my rest time a day before this leg. Do to the current global pandemic and the reduced airline schedules; neither of us had flown very much in the past six months. I know I felt a bit rusty and tired.To mitigate this I gave what I thought was a good approach brief before top of descent; and we briefed the changed approach during the arrival. We still failed to monitor the FMA change at a crucial moment. It was an error I hope I/we don't repeat.

column121: While we were instructed to join the Runway XXC localizer ZZZ; Approach Controller handed us over to Tower Frequency. As soon as we joined the localizer; flying conditions started to deteriorate and became turbulent. Airspeed was fluctuating at 1;400 feet AGL. As a result of this; I had started to get focused on stable approach criteria; and work load gradually increased for both pilots. Over emphasis on stable approach criteria caused a distraction and both PF and PM forgot to obtain the landing clearance. Aircraft landed without any further incidents exited the runway and hold short of XXL. Since we did not hear anything from the Tower; both pilots became aware of the event and switched to Tower Frequency. Flight was completed without further incidents.Tunnel vision on stable approach parameters and Airspeed variations caused distraction. Since COVID-19 breakout; I was not flying as frequently as before. I believe this was factored into this incident.Give some extra time to double check all the required tasks are completed. As we are not flying as frequently as before; configure the aircraft early and passing 500 feet AGL; both pilots verbally verify one last time 'Cleared to Land.'

column121: I was the First Officer and PF ZZZ1-ZZZ. This was my first flight after not flying for over two months. The majority of the flight went fine; but as we approached ZZZ; ATC left us high (11;000 feet) very close to the airport. I was about to ask for lower when ATC gave us a descent. I thought we had just enough time to successfully descend and slow down; but when we were around 4;000 feet; ATC gave us a turn that made it clear they were directing us straight to the FAF. This was not what I was expecting; and left us high and fast and greatly increased our workload. I used the flight spoilers to slow down and then forgot to stow them after slowing enough to add flaps. Our workload was still quite high at this point; so we didn't realize the flight spoilers were still deployed until the spoiler caution message at 500 feet. At this point; the Captain immediately stowed them and we continued the landing.Because ATC left us high and then turned us directly to the FAF; our workload greatly increased. I deployed the spoilers in order to slow down; and then forgot to stow them because I was distracted by other things in the flight deck (arming the approach; making sure we intercepted the localizer and glideslope; making sure we were on speed and deployed our flaps and gear at the appropriate time; etc.). I was expecting something different from ATC and had a good plan in my head; but was thrown off when ATC gave us something I didn't expect. This was also my first leg after not flying for over 2 months; which I think could have contributed as well; because I was a bit rusty and a little bit slower than I normally am in the flight deck.We could have easily prevented this from happening by asking ATC for a longer downwind. I was planning a longer downwind in my head and when ATC gave us a turn directly to the FAF; we should have asked to stay on downwind instead. There wasn't anyone else out there to cause traffic issues; and it would have given us extra time to slow down and descend so that we weren't rushed. In the heat of the moment; I didn't think of this (though I used to remind my students of this option all the time when I was a flight instructor!); but I should have. When we stowed the spoilers; I decreased thrust and pitched down a little bit; which kept us on speed and caused only a slight deviation from the glideslope; which I was able to correct. Because of this; we made a completely normal landing. However; a go-around would also have been a good choice here; especially if we became unstable after retracting the spoilers.

column121: I was on LC1 with multiple aircraft in the south patter when Aircraft X checked in from the west requesting left traffic for runway 28L. LC2 was working runway 28R and the north pattern. I sequenced the pilot to follow Aircraft A on base turning final and also told the pilot about Aircraft Y on final for the parallel runway. Aircraft X reported the Cessna to follow in sight; so I cleared him to land. Aircraft X turned too early and I informed him that spacing between him and the Cessna was not going to work out. The pilot then says he's going to make a 360 and took action without realizing there is other traffic in his vicinity. I quickly informed him of traffic and he responded that he had traffic in sight. He made a right turn directly towards Aircraft Y who was on final for the parallel runway. Aircraft Y had to take evasive action to avoid Aircraft X. I told Aircraft X to go around and offset right of the runway and told him about traffic in the north pattern. He reported a Cessna in sight and began a turn to the right downwind quickly overtaking the Cessna on downwind. I instructed  Aircraft X to extend downwind to follow traffic on final. He reported the Cessna to follow in sight (Aircraft B). Again; he turned base too early and I had to send the Cessna around to get Aircraft X to land.The pilot of Aircraft X may have misjudged the spacing required to follow aircraft that are much slower than a BE20. The pilot took action on his own without being aware of his surroundings. He should have verified with ATC before making any turns.
column123: As Aircraft X approached the MYF D airspace; crew noted there were approx 10 TCAS contacts within the D airspace.  Crew discussed flying slow as possible to help see/avoid. Aircraft X was cleared into MYF class D by tower.  We were cleared to join left downwind for Runway 28 Left.  Just prior to downwind we lowered gear and flaps to fly as slow as possible (approx 120K IAS).  Approaching abeam landing point tower told us to extend downwind and to take interval on an aircraft already on final to 28 Left.  Flying Pilot saw aircraft on final and turned to take interval.  We were also told there was an aircraft on long final to 28 Right.  After turning to take interval on 28 Left traffic; Tower called and said something that made pilot flying think he was taking interval on the wrong aircraft...that there was another aircraft on final to 28 left.  Pilot rolled toward level to look for other traffic.  Doing so put us close to runway 28 Right final course.  Pilot called tower and began to execute a right 180 to clear the final for both parallel runways.  In the turn; pilot saw the aircraft that was making approach to Runway 28 Right and executed a level nose down maneuver to allow aircraft to fly over and laterally to the right.  Tower told us the 28 Right aircraft flew over us.  Aircraft X landed 28 Left and was told to contact tower for possible pilot deviation.Lesson learned.  Tower told Aircraft X that he was flying 'fast' making sequence difficult with GA aircraft and that 28R is preferred runway for Aircraft X size/speed aircraft.  Aircraft X reported that we requested 28L ONLY because we thought it would be easier for ATC not the crew.  Given the traffic saturation it seemed a direct entry to left downwind to 28L would be preferable than overflying field to entering right downwind for 28R.Aircraft X reported to tower that they were flying as slow as possible and that if tower had preference to which runway they could have communicated that to Aircraft X.  Also; tower informed Aircraft X that the two parallel runways use different control tower frequencies.  Aircraft X did not have as good of SA on runway 28R since they couldn't hear the transmissions to/from that aircraft. ForeFlight EFB is used by the Aircraft X company pilots.  ForeFlight lists both tower freqs but list one as 'secondary' not as for other runway.  MYF AFD also does not say that there are different tower frequencies with one being for 28L and the other for 28R.Tower is probably hesitant to 'counter' which of the parallel runways the pilot has requested to land.  However when a non-local aircraft if flying into an unfamiliar airport which is congested with many different types of aircraft. It would be helpful if Tower would make suggestions for appropriate runways.  Pilot would still have prerogatives to stay with what was requested.  AFD should have notes that indicate two different frequencies are used SIMULTANEOUSLY for the airport.See and avoid is still critical in VMC conditions.  Just because Tower or Approach gives an aircraft a clearance to land...or vector if IFR the pilot still need to keep a look out to avoid collisions.

column121: Descending into LGB on the DSNEE arrival; we were cleared to descend via the arrival. Captain was Pilot Flying. First Officer was Pilot Monitoring. ATC cleared us to descend via the arrival for Runway 30 but didn't specify which approach to expect. ATIS stated runway in use but not approach type. Captain loaded and briefed the RNAV (Z). While descending via; and inside the branch point for the arrival; Approach Control cleared us for the ILS via the arrival. At that time we were in VNAV/LNAV with the lowest altitude on the arrival set in the MCP. Our position was a few miles East of MCKYE and descending.Upon receiving the clearance the Captain quickly loaded the ILS and executed it without verifying there were no resultant discontinuities. Because we were inside the branch point of the arrival a discontinuity did appear; even though we were going to the same runway. Once the execute (EXEC) button was pushed the discontinuity appeared and VNAV disconnected. Because we were descending at the time; we continued to descend. The crossing restriction at MCKYE was at or above 7200'. The aircraft descended to roughly 6800' before the Captain corrected and returned us to the correct altitude. We were back up to 7200' prior to MCKYE. After correcting the flight path; the correct approach was loaded in the FMC and the remainder of the flight was uneventful.Proper verification prior to execution would help prevent this type of event from occurring again. Make sure both Crew Members have seen the changes and agree on them before pushing EXEC. Also; once the arrival has commenced do not execute any FMC changes unless you are on the LEGS page and have cleared any discontinuities resulting from the approach or runway change.
column123: We were on the DSNEE 4 Arrival for LGB and had previously entered the RNAV Y MIDDS transition (I did not close the MIDDS discontinuity per SOP). Weather was IMC due to fires in Southern California. We had been cleared to descend via and 4000' MSL was entered in the MCP window with VNAV PATH. Approximately 1 NM prior to DSNEE waypoint; we were given clearance for the ILS to Runway 30. I re-entered the arrival with the MIDDS transition for the ILS and immediately got a VNAV discontinuity from the FMC due to the re-entry. We were working on closing the discontinuity when I noticed the aircraft had descended 400' below the MCKYE restriction of 7200' MSL; approximately 3 NM prior to MCKYE. I immediately initiated a climb back to 7200' MSL to MCKYE and continued the descend via arrival. We did not see any traffic conflicts from TCAS and ATC did not mention anything.Expect the unexpected. I should have entered the next restriction altitude in the MCP before making changes to the arrival. Even though I did not expect a discontinuity; it would have been prudent because we were on the arrival and close to a fix with a restriction. Surprise at the discontinuity delayed action. I should have concentrated more on the restrictions and less on the FMC.

column121: During traffic pattern and landing practice in the first flight of a recently purchased aircraft; the PIC entered an incorrect CTAF frequency in the radio; resulting in a conflict with another landing aircraft. The type aircraft was a high-performance aerobatic biplane with very poor forward visibility; a high approach speed and poor glide performance; which necessitates using the turbine traffic pattern altitude (1500 AGL) and a steeper than typical; slipping descent on final approach. The PIC made numerous position reports and standard traffic pattern legs at the turbine aircraft TPA of 1500 AGL; but given the incorrect CTAF frequency these reports were unheard by another aircraft operating in the pattern (a Cirrus SR22). On a 1 mile final approach; the PIC of the Pitts S2B saw the Cirrus SR22 at approximately 100 feet in front and 40 feet below his glidepath making an evasive turn to the right. The S2B PIC made immediate deviation to the left; and circled away from the pattern; climbing to 3000 AGL to double check the radio frequency. The correct CTAF frequency was entered and communications with the SR22 pilot were established. The pilots discussed the near-miss over the radio; and no further conflicts occurred. The flight was terminated normally after 3 more traffic patterns and landings.

column121: I am a solo student currently working to get my Private Pilot Certificate. I was flying back to ZZZ1. I needed to Divert due to [weather] at ZZZ. I chose to divert to ZZZ which was quite busy at time of incident due to bad weather at ZZZ1. I approached ZZZ noting the high volume of traffic in the pattern. Traffic was flowing to Runway 12 making it the main landing runway at the time of the incident. As traffic was unusually high; I made the call to remain North of ZZZ until the pattern had cleared up. I did exactly that; while making all the necessary advisory calls on the frequency. As I exited my hold to join the left downwind for Runway 12; I noted the aircraft I will be following was Aircraft Y (I received this information using an in cockpit ADS-B receiver). Upon reviewing traffic info on ADS-B receiver I was looking out of the window to try to make a visual reference with the traffic I was following. Unfortunately I was not immediately able to make visual reference with Aircraft Y however I did verify adequate separation using ADS-B. As I joined the left downwind for Runway 12 I diverted my attention to any possible hazards coming from the within the traffic pattern (causing me to divert my attention to the left). As I did this and have determined the left side of me to be clear I made the call to enter the left downwind for Runway 12. At this point I realized there was a low wing twin engine aircraft about 150 ft. above me heading in the opposite direction (upwind). I immediately realized it was Aircraft Y I was following. He had made a 180 degree turn from his downwind to attempt to rejoin. Although while doing that he flew right into the flow of traffic in the pattern. I quickly called up on the radio to try to understand if he had seen me. He replied something along the lines of we had you visual - all good).

column121: Approach briefing: Briefed the TBARR 1 Arrival. Briefed both the ILS 35L and the RNAV Z Runway 35L including the runway transitions. ATIS reported that both were in use. During Arrival descent: was informed by Flight Attendant that a Passenger had difficulty breathing and was being attended to. Told Approach Control (second from the Final Controller) and we were given priority handling and he told us to expect Runway 35L. We confirmed the RNAV-Z 35L in the FMC.  Sent Dispatch a message. Flight Attendant called us numerous times during the arrival transition. The Final Controller told us 'after LDORA; cleared for the ILS 35L. We mistakenly did not execute the ILS 35L in the FMC; which now ended up going from LDORA to CRUPP instead of via the ILS 35L transition fixes. After passing LDORA Approach gave us a 060 followed by a 020 heading to join ILS 35L and cleared for us the approach.Our planning and preparation for the arrival and landing was performed thoroughly. During this high intensity period of time with many non-normal distractions we need to be cognizant when changes take place such as changes to arrivals and approaches and make sure those changes are loaded and executed. We could've also queried ATC of the approach that was being planned for us.  It would've been a great help if ATC; the second from the Final Controller told us which approach to expect instead of having the Final Controller just clear us for the ILS 35L via the transition not too far from LDORA fix.Navigated via wrong transition.

column121: During flight after obtaining the weather at [the] destination; [the] Captain and I decided that [the] most suitable runway for [the] given conditions would be the longest. Appropriate for landing would [then] be RNAV LP+V GPS Runway XX; available runway [at the longest length for the airport] - winds were calm; VIS 10; ceiling 1800; temp 27. Once we both agreed; I start programming the FMS for LP+V GPS Runway XX; selecting wet runway conditions and adding 1.15% factor for wet runway. Landing weight was well below max landing weight. Based on that; [the] distance required to land on Runway XX was [calculated]. There were no PIREP for braking action on Runway XX.ATC vectored us for descent and [we] were told to expect RNAV GPS Runway XX in ZZZ. We were given descent to 1800 and direct to ZZZZZ. [We] configured the aircraft prior to crossing ZZZZZ; [the] checklist was completed [and the] aircraft was configured for landing with autopilot engaged up to 300 ft. We did have visual contact with the runway at 1600 ft. I; as [Co-Pilot]; called all speeds; altitude and on glide slope and minimums which Captain was right on everything. At about 300 ft.; [the] Captain disengaged the autopilot and did a smooth landing on Runway XX. After touchdown; speed brakes were extended and brakes were applied. Halfway down rolling on Runway XX; [I] told [the] Captain that this thing wasn't stopping at all [and it] felt like we were sliding on ice; there was no friction between the main tires and runway surface. He responded I have max braking and [the] aircraft [was] hydroplaning. About 1500 prior to runway ending I said we are going for grass area. He responded yes; we are going straight for grass area. Aircraft came to rest 100 ft. to 150 on [the] grassy area. After full rest on [the] grass area; we assessed and ran the shutdown checklist. Then I exited the aircraft to do [the] post-incident check followed by [the] Captain. We both did not see damage to aircraft and we were both sure hydroplaning was the factor; otherwise we would stopped on the runway. We have been to this airport before.
column123: During our Part 91 flight to ZZZ; an incident occurred where the aircraft overran the available runway length. On descent into ZZZ; my SIC and I discussed plans for the arrival. Based on calm winds and approximately 1;700 ft. ceilings; we decided the RNAV GPS LP +V approach to Runway XX would be the most optimum approach given our direction of flight; since Runway XX was the longest runway. The [Co-Pilot] programmed the FMS based on the ASOS report and the XM weather loaded into the FMS. I confirmed the runway; the weather; and confirmed that 'wet' runway conditions were selected. Landing distance factor was set at its default value of 1.15 providing additional margin. After the incident; I returned to the aircraft and re-ran the same numbers to confirm that the FMS computed Actual Landing Distance Required was [the same as the calculation during the flight] and that our landing weight was computed at [the same weight as calculated during the flight] and was below the maximum landing weight for the aircraft.At the beginning of the approach; after being vectored for weather; we were given direct to ZZZZZ; an initial fix on the approach. We proceeded direct to ZZZZZ and were given a descent to 1800 ft. By this time; we had configured with approach flaps and had completed the approach checklist and briefing. Roughly a mile before ZZZZZ; I configured with landing gear down and full flaps. We completed the before landing checklist. Crossing ZZZZZ; we slowed to approach speed and the aircraft started down on the approach on autopilot. We broke out and had the runway in site well in advance of minimums. There was light rain on final. We approached the runway at Vapp and around 50 ft.; I pulled the power to idle in order to slow further to Vref and aimed the aircraft for the runway numbers so as to touch down before the thousand-foot markers. At this point; I deployed the speed brake and applied the brakes increasing to maximum braking. Braking action was very poor and we were slowing but even with maximum braking and the speed brake deployed - landing performance does not consider speed brake deployment - it was apparent the aircraft was not going to be stopped in the available runway length. Differential braking during the landing roll was ineffective so maximum dual braking was applied and a combination of nose wheel steering and rudder were used to maintain the centerline. Knowing we were likely to depart the end of the runway; I maintained the centerline so as to avoid the runway end lights and we entered the grass area beyond the end of the runway. We decelerated quickly; but smoothly at that point in soft dirt and grass; coming to a stop roughly 100-150 ft. beyond the end of the runway. We assessed and shut down; inspected the aircraft and helped coordinate moving the aircraft to a safe location. The aircraft had no externally visible damage and there were no injuries. Later inspection by [Company] technicians confirmed no damage to the aircraft and no repairs required. No airport property; other than the grass; was damaged. Based on what we observed and the standing water we found on the runway after the landing; the conclusion we came to was that the likely cause of the overrun was hydroplaning of the main wheels on a 'contaminated' rather than 'wet' runway resulting in extremely poor braking action and inability to stop in the available runway length.

column121: Before taking off this morning; I completed one final check of the weather in route to ZZZ. The forecast showed sky clear below 12;000 feet. About 1 hour and 45 minutes into my journey; I found clouds moving in below my flight path and in front of me. I checked the weather along the route and at my destination via ADS-B in the plane; and also tuned in the AWOS for ZZZ and other airports along my route. The ADS-B weather was still saying clear; but the AWOS reporting for airports were calling a low overcast forming. Realizing the weather was not getting better; I found a hole to get through the clouds; maintain contact with the ground; and landed at ZZZ1. After waiting for the weather to clear for 3 hours at ZZZ1; I took back off for ZZZ. The 38 miles flight over was slightly bumpy; but uneventful. I was cleared by Tower to land on Runway 32. I heard the Tower Controller give a wind check to the pilot who landed in front of me at 070 at 10; but winds were gusting. I descended on final with a right crab to counter the crosswind and added right rudder shortly before touchdown to straighten out the plane. The airplane touched down normally. After starting my roll out; the left wing suddenly dipped and the right wing rose. I was trying to counter with right rudder and aileron to straighten the plane out; but that caused it to nose over; causing a prop strike and a minor left wing tip scrape. I believe a strong wind gust hit the plane shortly after touchdown; causing the sudden raising/lower of the wings. I would like to review my control inputs with an instructor to find out anything I could have done differently.

column121: ZZZ Class D Airspace has been operating on reduced hours since COVID-19 started. The airspace in the traffic pattern reverts to Class E once the Tower is closed and pilots make announcements on the CTAF.The traffic pattern was very busy at dusk as I was arriving back from ZZZ1. I was entering the pattern by flying over the field at 2;200 and then executing a teardrop entry into a 45 for left traffic Runway XX. There was Aircraft Y on Left Downwind for Runway XX as Aircraft Z approached directly from the east (in opposite traffic); at pattern altitude in what it described as a 'wide entry' to join the 45 for left downwind. This entry was not wide as Aircraft Y reported that Aircraft Z had almost collided with it in the traffic pattern and reprimanded the pilot for his choice of pattern entry procedure. I was forced to abort my tear drop entry as Aircraft Z proceeded directly for my location. I executed a 360 degree holding turn and observed Aircraft Z pass approximately 800 feet below me. I sequenced in behind Aircraft Z and maintained visual contact. I extended my left downwind slightly for spacing when I observed Aircraft A transiting the airspace; NORDO; at 2;300 feet - this was the altitude I was previously at and the altitude which I had crossed midfield. While this aircraft didn't have to make any announcements; it is reckless to transit a busy airport; slightly above its traffic pattern; while NORDO. Immediately afterwards; while I was turning left base for Runway XX; an aircraft announced that it was entering 'Right traffic' for Runway XX. While we are used to flying right traffic when the Tower is open; this is not the prescribed procedure while on CTAF and can create a very dangerous situation. Aircraft B on the RNAV XX approach was forced to go missed given the situation in the traffic pattern.This temporary reduction in Class D services has created hazards for pilots flying in this busy airspace; as traffic has picked up again to levels that warrant full-time Class D service hours. This entire situation could have been avoided had ZZZ's Control Tower been in normal; pre-COVID; operational hours.

column121: The automated weather is available with 5 clicks on the Unicom Frequency at 3B1 Greenville; Maine. The automated weather reported wind calm. The RNAV 14 instrument approach was used and there was an 18 knot tailwind on short final. This was not acceptable for a straight in landing. The approach had to be a circling approach to runway 32 or a missed approach. The automated weather does not appears on ADS-B or on Sirius XM weather in the cockpit. It does appear on Foreflight which is not available in the air. This is a very serious safety issue. The weather should be available on airborne receivers such as XM or ADS-B and the information must be correct. I believe that this represents an accident looking for a place to happen. Can this be fixed?

column121: Approaching ZZZ VFR from the southwest we contacted Tower 9 miles out with the current ATIS at 2500 ft. beginning our descent. Tower gave instructions to make straight in Runway XX. On about a 3 mile final we were instructed to make a right 360 to allow for faster traffic 4 miles north to circle to Runway XX in front of us we read back the circling instructions and began a turn to the right. During this time Tower was attempting to establish communications with the aircraft on the circling approach. There was no response from that aircraft. About 270 degrees through our 360 we found ourselves nose to nose with a Lear less than 100 ft. above us on a left base to Runway XX. Tower attempted to issue control instructions but it was too late to do anything and ended up just making a sloppy traffic call; we acknowledge traffic in sight; cut power and initiated a dive while continuing our turn to the right. At the same time the Lear checked in with Tower and advised they were avoiding traffic and would make a 270 to final. Tower then instructed us to change to [Runway] XY and cleared us to land followed by a [Runway] XX landing clearance for the Lear.

column121: I was conducting a discovery flight with a student and we were entering the 45 to the downwind for Runway 5. It was a windy day with winds at 060 14 gusts around 20; but VMC conditions with no clouds. We were descending through about 1200 ft. AGL and I was letting the student fly the pattern while I made radio calls. I made the call we were on the 45 entry to the downwind and then the call that we were on downwind. Around abeam the numbers I did the before landing checklist and around that time took over control of the aircraft. Around 800 ft. AGL; I made my left base turn and called base on frequency. Descending through around 600-700 ft.; I heard a call from an aircraft stating they were turning crosswind; then correction for downwind Runway 5 and that they were a V-22. At that point I didn't consider them a factor and was on final approach at which point I called that I was on final for Runway 5. I was descending through 500 ft. when the ADS-B traffic annunciator came on the screen that an aircraft was 300 ft. above. Prior to this I had not visually seen any aircraft in the pattern; nor did I see anything on the GPS. I made another call that I was on a short final for Runway 5 and the other aircraft was shown at 200 ft. above me. I slipped down from 500 ft. to 200 ft. to lose more altitude but was not over the runway so didn't go too low. At one point; the ADS-B annunciation showed the aircraft 100 ft. above me during the slip or right before I began the maneuver. Supposedly from those who were watching and listening to the frequency; the V-22 had called stating they saw me and were going to maintain visual separation. I didn't know about this until I was on the ground. I do not know how far away horizontally the aircraft was from me during the time when this occurred. Around 1000 ft. from the runway I had the thought that this signal was a ghost signal from the receiver. I didn't realize it was an aircraft until I was on the ground past the hold short line doing the after-landing checklist when I heard the rotors from the aircraft approaching the runway. I think the transponder for the military aircraft may have been off until they were right behind me and that was when I noticed that they were there; and my fight or flight sense kicked in to avoid an NMAC. I didn't hear any radio calls from them once I saw the alert because I was focused on maintaining separation. If I had been able to hear and better understand what they were doing; I think I would have been able to give a better radio call about how high I was and how close to the runway I was. At no point in time did I see the V-22 until I was on the ramp parking. Personally; I believe the V-22 should have gone around. Doing a go around popped in my mind; but I dismissed it because the V-22 was above me and I didn't want to fly into them. I felt rushed to land and get off the runway because at that time I didn't know if they could see me. After talking to people on the ground that saw this and were listening to the radios; I realized I had a little more room that what I thought I had in the plane. An observer stated that the V-22 had called saying they were following me in and that they were well offset from me; behind and above me.

column121: After initial takeoff from ZZZ; departure notified the crew that ZZZ Tower found a tire and suspected Aircraft X had blown a tire either during taxi or takeoff. The crew had already raised the gear and flaps normally with no indication and decided to continue to ZZZ1. En route; the crew ran all appropriate checklists and decided to conduct a low approach for visual confirmation of landing gear status. The crew conducted a low pass at 500 ft. AGL and ZZZ1 Tower indicated that both tires on the right main landing gear appeared to be missing. The crew climbed to a safe altitude and made the decision to [advise ATC] and divert to ZZZ2 due to runway considerations. ZZZ2 offered a longer; wider runway. The crew briefed and flew the approach to Runway XX and elected to land on the left side of the runway to keep the drag in the center and reduce the chances of departing the runway. The crew landed on the left then maintained centerline due to the drag. The crew stopped the aircraft on the runway and egressed normally.The brakes on Aircraft X had been changed [five days prior]. The brakes appeared to be stiff throughout the trip with a noticeable pull to the right.
column123: After takeoff from ZZZ; while enroute To ZZZ1; Departure Control advised us that ZZZ Tower had told them we may have lost a tire during takeoff. We continued to ZZZ1 airport; assessed the situation; [advised ATC]; and advised the company.At ZZZ1 we flew a low pass; at 500 ft. AGL; with the plane fully configured; to allow Tower personnel to be able to see the status of our landing gear. They advised us that both wheels appeared to be missing. We inquired if he saw any wheels and he said only on one side. I had him confirm that the right side didn't appear to have wheels. We took delay vectors to reassess our situation. Decided to divert to ZZZ2 instead of ZZZ3 due to the volume of traffic at ZZZ3 vs ZZZ2. ZZZ2 has 12;000 ft. runways and good ARRF (Aircraft Rescue and Fire Fighting) support. We briefed the approach; the plan for a smooth touchdown; how to control the aircraft as the right side touched down; and we briefed the evacuation plan and reviewed that checklist in the QRH. Touchdown was smooth with good heading control. I was the pilot not flying and I elected to have [First Officer] execute the landing. He landed according to our briefed plan; left of the centerline (as briefed). [First Officer was able to maintain good heading control bringing the aircraft to a full stop on the centerline. Once stopped; ARRF approached the airplane; established communications with us; did a visual inspection for any possible fires or other visible hazards. As ARRF was inspecting the airplane [First Officer] secured the engines; we completed a normal shutdown and exited the airplane.

column121: Our LOA / COA with the UAS operators states they are required to establish 2 way communication and ATC approval prior to exiting R-XXXX. They are restricted to the recovery route called the Green Route; specifically designed to remain clear of 2 military airport finals in case of a lost link emergency scenario. Aircraft X exited R-XXXX on the green route; with Aircraft Y in trail; without establishing communications and approval with FAI approach. The controller attempted different frequencies; MAINS; STBYs; RTR's; and even guard calls with no success. Finally; the controller had to call FBK ATCT to request the call sign of the chase aircraft. The controller was successful in reaching the chase aircraft and had the chase aircraft relay to Aircraft X to contact approach. By the time communication was established; Aircraft X was 8.59 miles outside of R-XXXX; inbound to FBK on the green route. Follow the COA / LOA; or update it with more current operations since it is over 2 years old.

column121: In descent from 9000 to 7000 with autopilot engaged hit by moderate jolt of wake turbulence. Continued descent and aircraft began to roll past 30 degrees angle of bank. Autopilot disengaged and aircraft rolled upright. Autopilot reengaged. Checked on flight attendants; all were ok. No passengers were up at the time. Enquired ATC about who we were following. Appeared to be a B777 ahead going to a different runway.
column123: While descending from 9;000 to 7;000 we were given a turn to 170 for vectors to intercept the ILS 9R. In the turn; we experienced wake turbulence - with a bank angle approximately 30 to the right. The Captain disengaged the autopilot and hand flew the airplane back to level flight. I checked on the flight attendants to make sure they were all ok because it was so abrupt and sudden. All 4 of the flight attendants reported to me they were fine. Within a couple of minutes at 7;000'; we encountered wake turbulence again. This time it was a 40 - 45 degree bank angle to the right. My Captain was still hand flying and recovered the airplane. I asked ATC what type of aircraft we were following; the controller responded we weren't following anyone but there was a 777 ahead of us but 2;000' below us. The 777 was being vectored ahead of us for [RWY] 10C. Perhaps more spacing was required even though we were being vectored for different but parallel runways.

column121: ILS Z XX was briefed by FO (First Officer). Discussed early configuration by ZZZZZ per 10-X page. Approach informed us GS (glideslope) was OTS (Out of Service). Changed to RNAV (GPS) X XX. Started getting vectors and approaching ETARE we received a GPWS warning. I had the terrain in sight and instructed FO to continue approach. Aircraft was in config 3; gear up...I believe;The [manual] pages do discuss the FOQA (Flight Operations Quality Assurance) issue at ZZZ. However; the note lists two intersections. ZZZZZ (ILS Z XX) and ZZZZZ1 (RNAV (GPS) X XY. At the time I thought maybe the courses were off enough that GPWS warnings only occurred on those approaches. Obviously; it applies to ALL approaches to XY&R.I think the two intersections on the 10-Y page FOQA Alert should be removed and should read something to the effect that the aircraft should be in final approach config at least 10 miles from TD (touchdown) and that it applies to ALL approaches.
column123: Inbound to ZZZ I was the pilot flying. About one hour out we set up for the arrival and reviewed the applicable company charts. I believe that the airport was VFR but visibility was limited due to FU. We were expecting XX and so read the FOQA Alert and discussed the need to be configured By ZZZZZ1. As we got a closer we were given the RNAV X with vectors. We reconfigured the box and ran through the RNAV requirements. We were vectored east of Course cleared to 8400 ft. than once on the approach cleared to 7400 ft. (between ZZZZZ2 and ZZZZZ3). At 7400 ft. the path came alive about 2 miles from ZZZZZ3. ATC gave us a current altimeter that was .01 off of our display. I Believe we got the GPWS over ZZZZZ3. I immediately disconnected the auto pilot and began a climb. The captain said 'its because of our configuration'; he could see terrain and we should finish configuring and the approach. Landed successfully at ZZZ.On the FOQA alert page we discussed the need to be configured by ZZZZZ1 which is on XY (I later figured out). When I reviewed the approach for XX my eyes fell to ZZZZZ4 which is for XX. So I highlighted ZZZZZ4 on the approach so I would be configured for (what made sense) a 7000 ft. peak about 10 miles out. When we got the RNAV in my mind the restriction was lifted because we weren't flying over that peak and configured as usual. Ok so I knew I was going to get a FOQA call but didn't expect [report] so I'll write what I was planning on saying to FOQA. The 10-XY-X Page is confusing and limited. Confusing that XY and XX have similar sounding fixes. When you take someone like me with a short memory and say 'OK we need to be configured by ZZZZZ1. Exit out of 10-Y pages pull up the approach look for the fix......'Ah ZZZZZ1 there it is' I thought I was being compliant. Limited in the fact that those two fixes are the only ones mentioned and didn't cover the RNAV to XX. I believe we would have avoided the GPWS if the Emphasis items said a generic 'inbound be configured no later than 10 miles' I would have caught that. I realize that if I would have; upon reviewing the 10-Y page transposed the ZZZZZ fix to the RVAV its roughly ETAR. But we have to defer to the lowest common denominator (which is me). Thank you for your review.

column121: Aircraft X was landing LGD.  I checked the NOTAMs and advised the pilot of a runway closure (16/34).  He said there was only one NOTAM published and [that] was not it.  Yesterday I informed another pilot of the closure and he said he was unaware of the NOTAM as well; but didn't tell me it wasn't published.  I know it is the Controller's responsibility to verify NOTAMs; but in many cases the workload is so high and the NOTAMs are written in such a cryptic manner that [they] are difficult to decipher that it is easy to miss important closures.  The runway closure; which in my opinion is the most important NOTAMs; was sandwiched 4 NOTAMs down between information about a taxiway and a change to minimum climb rates for the ODP.  The system is clearly broken and has been for some time.  My impression is that the FAA really doesn't care and is waiting for a serious accident and subsequent lawsuit before they will take any meaningful action.NOTAMs need to be in plain language and ordered in a manner of most important to least important.  There also needs to be a way that pilots can obtain NOTAMs while airborne.I informed management what the pilot told me about the NOTAM not being disseminated.  They verified the NOTAM about the runway closure was in effect but I'm not sure they took any other action to make sure it was being properly distributed.

column121: The event occurred on a flight between ZZZ and ZZZ1. We had been delayed multiple times at our base of operations due to mechanical issues and eventually were switched to a different aircraft that became available late in the day. Throughout the rescheduling and refiling/updating flight plans we had taken consideration of our duty time and had set a latest takeoff time from base that would allow us to complete our flights and return to base before the end of our duty time. However in all that I failed to consider night landing currency for carrying passengers. It was not until after we had picked up our passenger and were enroute to ZZZ1 and the sun had set that the FO (pilot flying) looked at me and said that he couldn't make the landing as he was not night current. Then I realized I was not night current either. At this point we realized it was too late and continued to our destination. I had the FO do the landing which in retrospect as PIC I should have shown more initiative in taking full responsibility by taking the landing instead of putting the FO in the position of making a landing out of currency.A contributing factor is that in the past I have seldom carried passengers for my employer. Most often I am doing other type of flying with only required crew on board due to the aircraft being in the restricted category. But I have more recently been doing an occasional passenger carrying flight where I have to think about passenger carrying currency more. Also due to economic slow down there has been less opportunity to keep up currency especially night currency with our normal operations. For corrective actions I have made a checklist for myself that goes in my knee board specifically for passenger carrying flights; because they are so infrequent; that lists the pertinent things that should be considered during pre-flight and more specifically the more pertinent things like passenger carrying currency. This will be a checklist that I see when I open my knee board prior to flight and work through at the latest; just before engine start.

column121: On practice ZZZ LOC XX; approximately 4 miles from the end of the runway; flight in VFR conditions above an undercast we received a low altitude alert from ZZZ Tower. We advised Tower were VFR and leveling at the MDA (900 MSL) and; at the same time; while cross checking our position on the LOC both pilots noted that the GPS had not updated from the Final Approach Fix (ZZZZZ) to the end of the runway. After cross checking position on the EFB display we elected to execute a missed approach at the end of the runway.The reason for this report is both systems and human factors related. Both pilots were concentrating on the descent and level off from FAF altitude to MDA using a 'dive and drive procedure' which is no longer commonly used in heavy jet aircraft. Concentrating on the altitude an equipment error to update the next waypoint automatically was missed and the low altitude alert from Tower compounded the 'startle factor'. The startle factor was amplified by the fact we were VFR above an overcast with no terrain in sight.  Based on training; we executed a missed approach to create time and regain situational awareness; from a personal perspective when an unexpected event occurs; even in seasoned pilots; it takes a few minutes to understand what happened; not second guess yourself and reset situational awareness.As a follow-up to the event; I contacted ZZZ Tower who related that they have issued numerous low altitude alerts on this approach; for aircraft at the correct altitude; usually involving a high descent rate approach to MDA. I reviewed the approach with the PF (Pilot Flying)and confirmed that he had set an excessive rate (1500 feet/min) when our company procedure calls for less than 1;000 feet/min within 1;000 feet of the terrain.A better briefing on use of automation during the crew brief may have mitigated the high descent rate; and as Pilot Not Flying validating the waypoint switch over at FAF should have been accomplished. Although we were given missed approach instructions ahead of time from approach control (practice approach); if we were relying on GPS for the published missed; it would have been difficult to reprogram the GPS (during the missed approach) to fly the published missed approach (our aircraft does not have TACAN (Tactical Air Navigation system) (raw data) capability and in this situation would require GPS to fly the published missed). As a final observation; we did not receive any onboard GPWS (Ground Proximity Warning System) or TAWS (Terrain Avoidance and Warning System) warning with respect to terrain and have asked company maintenance to verify proper operation of both systems.

column121: Very clear; minimal winds. AWOS (Automated Weather Observing System) - Calm winds. Monitoring [radio] all runways were being utilized. Calm wind preferred Runway is XX/XY. Cirrus eastbound called straight in landing Runway XX. Another aircraft (Aircraft Y) stated XY was being used. Cirrus changed to Runway XY. I entered pattern on 45 to left downwind Runway XY as #2 (Behind Cirrus). Cirrus was in sight. Aircraft Y was on the ground and stated he was at [Runway] XY but would wait for the landing Cirrus. Cirrus made all calls in pattern (Downwind; Base; Final and Short Final). I made my call around the pattern and Aircraft Y stated he would wait for me. On my short final; I think I heard '[Aircraft] taking off XZ'. I called out short final XY and landed just past the numbers with full flaps. Within 200 ft. of rolling; I saw what I believe was a C-172 accelerating in the South to North (Left to Right) direction. I depressed my brakes as hard as possible; maintained directional control; while deciding whether I should deviate to the left or right. The C-172 decelerated and turned into the grass between taxiway C and runway XY soon coming to a complete stop. Aircraft Y called out warning of event on radio. There was another plane awaiting takeoff behind Aircraft Y. Runway XY and Runway XA can't see each other on the ground. The 2 planes departed Runway XY at the request of the plane in the grass. He then taxied out of the grass. I reported the event to the Fixed Base Operator but they were unaware. The cirrus pilot was also unaware. When I hangered my plane I [noticed damage] on left main tire. After mechanic inspection; I had to replace both main tires.

column121: I was plugged in with a trainee in local control when we received inbound information from Flight Data that they had just received it from Center on Aircraft X on a Visual Approach. The trainee and I looked at the RADAR display and saw him turning onto a 5 mile final moving pretty quick with several VFR aircraft in the rectangular pattern on the intersecting runway. We had no time to build a hole for Aircraft X and the spacing was going to be too tight so we sent Aircraft X around. This was an issue since our LOA (Letter of Agreement) with the Center says they shall pass inbound information 10 miles or more which they did not do after they had just had a similar situation minutes before with Aircraft Y they had inbounded 2 miles south east of the airport on the Visual Approach with a busy pattern.I plugged a handset in the CIC (Controller in Charge) position and called Center and told them they need to inbound these IFR arrivals a little sooner. I did this at the CIC position as Local Control was busy and I was trying to not distract the trainee as he was working the traffic. The response I got from Center was he was waiting for a down time for Aircraft Y. I told him that he should review our LOA. I found this unacceptable as there is a reason why we have a LOA and it allows us time to make plans to adjust traffic to accommodate IFR arrivals and lets us know what Center is going to do with the inbounds. Its difficult to separate aircraft that we don't know about that may be descending or on an approach. After we sent Aircraft X around we coordinated for the missed approach with Flight Data. Flight Data informed us that Center wanted runway heading to 3;000 feet. We issued the missed approach instructions to Aircraft X and switched the aircraft to Center.A minute or so later Flight data passes us the next inbound information on Aircraft Z south east of the field then follows it up that Center is going to turn Aircraft X north bound and he is doing the Visual Approach. Very shortly after Aircraft X checks on roughly 3 miles northwest of the field. This is a problem because again we thought Aircraft Z was going to be number 1 who was well over 10 miles from the airport; followed by Aircraft X. We had let the VFR traffic on the rectangular pattern on resume and started to plan for Aircraft Z arrival. As soon as Aircraft X checked on; we were shocked and began moving aircraft out of his way that were roughly a mile in front of Aircraft X. I asked another controller to relieve us as this now was 3 situations back to back that the trainee had handled very well but was very dangerous. I wanted to call over to Center and try to figure out what was going on. Follow the LOA between ZMP and TVC.
column123: After Aircraft X was sent around Center inbounded Aircraft Z about 20 miles southwest for a Visual Approach. A minute or two later Center called back and asked what we wanted to do with Aircraft X and if it was ok if they turned him north. I said affirmative but we have a lot of small aircraft using Runway 18 and Center said that they saw that. Center then said ok I will turn Aircraft X to the north and clear him for the Visual Approach. A minute or two later Aircraft X calls local inbound on the Visual Approach about 3 miles northwest and about 1 mile in trail of a Cessna doing more than double their speed. The Cessna on approach to the intersecting runway was broken off to bring Aircraft X in. Local Control and I were surprised that Aircraft X turned in like that and was switched over. We thought that the aircarrier was number 1 to the field since we never got an official inbound on Aircraft X. We thought that Aircraft X was being sent north to get setup on the visual approach after the air carrier.Follow the LOA.
