column121: 3rd day of flying. 3rd flight of day. We had set up for the ILS Approach in accordance with ATIS. No mention was made of Visual Approaches being in use. Despite that fact; we briefed the restrictions over a satellite airport as a precaution.We were taken off the RNAV Arrival and were given a series of step down clearances as we approached the field. Simultaneously; we were told to expect the RNAV Visual Approach. The Pilot Monitoring loaded the new approach as we were below 10;000 ft. which I as the Pilot Flying rapidly briefed. Shortly thereafter; we were cleared to a point not on the arrival nor the RNAV Visual. As the Pilot Monitoring began searching for the fix; I maintained a 3:1 descent rate via VSpeed/Open Des. I was also approaching the point where I was going to need to configure if I wished to have a stabilized approach. The Pilot Monitoring found the fix on the RNAV (GPS) Approach and raced to reconfigure the FMS. Then; having given no prior warning; the Approach Controller cleared us for the ILS Approach which would have been impossible at this point. Having the runway in sight and fearing that any further 'assistance' from the controller would do little to aid my rapidly deteriorating situational awareness; I elected to disconnect the autopilot; and fly the approach visually. I pulled my Pilot Monitoring back into the loop by asking for him to clear my flight directors; configure the aircraft; and ask approach for the Visual Approach. The controller cleared us for the Visual Approach; 'as long as we fly the glideslope down'; which I'd submit is not a legal clearance; but was in any case irrelevant as following the glideslope had always been our intention. After doing some fishing to configure the aircraft and get down onto glideslope; the Pilot Monitoring reselected the approach and flight directors and we flew a stabilized approach to landing. In debriefing this event; we discovered that the fix was also present on the ILS Approach; which was the approach that ATIS had specified; the approach that we had initially briefed; and presumably the approach that the controller wished us to fly all along. I can't say what the Approach controller was thinking during this event. However; on an approach as infamous as any to I can only suggest that last minute changes are inappropriate and unsafe. I also think that the controller has an inaccurate impression of what is required of aircrews when changing approaches. I would suggest that the reality of flying this approach be made clear to her as controlling that is this cavalier will only lead to further unstabilized approaches in the future; or worse. The last minute change to the RNAV Visual followed by clearing us to a point not on the approach; followed by a second clearance for the ILS risked completely overwhelming the aircrew. That this was on a runway that is notorious for unstabilized approaches only exacerbated matters. Frankly; any acceptance of other than ILS approaches to this runway should be disallowed. Flying Visual Approaches to this runway; with it's unique peculiarities is a procedure designed by ATC; for the benefit of ATC. By definition and data; flying this Visual Approach is a compromise of safety for the sake of expediency; and sets aircrew up to be 'the last line of defense'; with generally predictable results.

column121: While executing a visual approach to Runway XX ZZZ airport; I was on a proper visual glide path until approximately 1/2 - 1/4 mile final.  A breakdown in my scan resulted in descending below a normal glide path; as a result we were unstable below 500 feet AGL.  Neither pilot called for a go-around; though; after debrief it was agreed that a go-around would have been appropriate. I was able to correct back on profile; approximately at 1/4 mile short final. The landing was safe; on speed and uneventful.Contributing factors included light turbulence requiring constant power adjustments. Also; lack of recent flight time due to taking leave--this was my first approach/landing in a number of weeks on top of very limited flight time in the past 6 months. Additionally; as experienced from the previous ILS approach to the same runway conducted earlier the same day; it was noticed that while on or above glide path; the PAPI's indicated well below; despite glide slope and VGSI not being coincident per the procedure.  The note on the procedure indicated that the VGSI would bring the aircraft across the threshold at approximately 50 feet; however; coming across the threshold at 80 feet; the PAPI's still indicated low (one white over three red).  This issue was briefed to be mitigated by referencing altitude/distance check points on descent; however; it still became an issue.  The lack of proper visual guidance may have contributed to a departure from a stable glide path; however; did not contribute to the failure to call a go-around.  The PAPI guidance issue was brought to the attention of the airport manager.The potential lack of flight time/currency due to COVID is a potential universal issue.  I have a luxury of flying with the X Group to maintain a scan but for those that don't; a severely reduced schedule will likely result in some loss of proficiency.  A solution may be to promote those with reduced flying to take more legs/landings than normal within a given pairing.  Additionally; aside from notifying the airport or ATC of malfunctioning equipment; e.g.; PAPI's; there should be a means by which to relay this information to the company for immediate communication to air crew; as there is no guarantee of a swift response or fix.  (aside from NOTAMs).
column123: Was pilot monitoring. We were coming in to land on the visual to Runway XX in ZZZ. During the last 1/4 to 1/2 mile on the approach. The pilot flying misjudged the distance and descent rate during the approach and started to get low on the approach. Pilot flying corrected but we were unstabilized on the approach and did not execute a go-around. We landed from the approach without incident within the touchdown zone area on the runway. The pilot flying had not been flying very much recently so lack of recent experience was a factor. A failure of the pilot monitoring to call for a go-around from an unstable approach. A failure of the pilot flying to call for a go-around from an unstabilized approach. Complacency on approach and not wanting to inconvenience passengers. Poor decision making by pilots.As pilots we have to always remember safety is top priority. Even if it's an inconvenience to both [of] you and everyone on board. If an approach becomes unstabalized a go-around or missed approach must be executed. Even if the landing happens safely and the landing is 'salvaged' it's important to remember that one day it might not be.  As a pilot monitoring if you see an unstabilized approach you should speak up. Don't worry about hurt feelings. Missed connections. We can't get complacent and think that we can just continue from an unstabilized approach.

column121: We were flying to ZZZ; assigned the XX LDA starting at ZZZ1 VOR. We were instructed to cross ZZZ1 VOR at or above 15;000 and cleared for the approach. We had programmed the avionics to fly the approach and descend on the approach to the glide slope intercept altitude. During the setup we neglected to arm the VNAV. After passing ZZZ1; descending; the aircraft did not level off at 14;000. We noticed the missed level off just before the controller alerted us to a probable altitude deviation. The Captain immediately disconnected the autopilot and returned to the appropriate altitude. The flight concluded uneventfully.I believe there were several factors that contributed to the event. Primarily; the flight crew was legally current but a little out of practice.  A full procedure instrument approach in real-time in mountainous terrain is different than currency and maintenance flights in non mountainous terrain. We have a very strong safety culture; attend recurrent training more frequently than required; and have extensive backgrounds including airline and corporate jet experience but are still susceptible to being 'rusty'.

column121: I was on Visual Approach backed up with the RNAV Approach. On Visual Approach had a couple big down drafts around 1500 ft. with some precipitation when I went near a big dark cloud. The rest of approach was normal. Fully configured by 1;000 ft and on speed and glide path stable approach. The winds reported were variable at 4 kts. Over the threshold began to flare and pull engine to idle; at about 15-20 ft; at 115 kts; all lift went away; felt bottom drop out of us; and aircraft dropped approximately 15 ft to runway. Control inputs did very little to correct situation to arrest the increased rate of descent. I got a big tail wind gust or got caught in a microburst of some sort. The aircraft slammed onto the runway right main first; breaking and collapsing the right main gear. I slid down runway 2;000 ft. or so before leaving the pavement to the right. No injuries. Pilot and passenger unhurt.

column121: As I finish my descent checklist and approached to ZZZ from the East 20 NM out I got my ATIS; called ZZZ tower and reported my position. I was instructed to enter the left downwind for runway XX. As I entered the left downwind and completed my before landing checklist I called the tower to let them know my position and I was clear to land. Abeam the numbers I began configuring my flaps after I slow down to my white arc. On base I selected my second notch of flaps and slow to 80 kts; on final I configured to flaps landing and I maintained my final approach speed. On final I stay on my center line; followed the PAPI and crossing the threshold I kept my airspeed about 70 kts began my flare and landed. It wasn't until my mains touched down and the nosewheel was on the ground and on the centerline that I felt a pulled to the left. I corrected to the right to no avail. The nosewheel did not steer to the right. As the airplane slowed down it continue to pull me to the left while I attempted to correct but there was no response on the airplane's nosewheel. The aircraft's left main gear and nosewheel went off the runway. I proceeded to do my shutdown checklist followed by a call to ZZZ Tower to informed them of the event. The tower sent a vehicle to pick us up. I called the tower on the telephone number as requested to provide my information.

column121: Was being vectored for the GPS XX approach at ZZZ.  Was a plane ahead with a similar call sign.  Controller gave me a approach clearance for the ILS XX; I was expecting the GPS XX and the position sounded off.  I turned to join the approach course as I was positioned for this; but then questioned the Controller telling him I thought he meant to give the clearance to the plane ahead.  He said he had but the plane ahead also stated they were given no clearance.The Controller had given me; Aircraft X; the clearance in error.  I then turned back to my assigned heading and the Controller then cleared the other plane.  I never left my assigned altitude.  The Controller seemed over loaded throughout the flight.  The current staffing at ATC seems to be straining their capacity with controllers covering many more frequencies which means pilots tend to step on each other as we can't hear the other frequencies.

column121: The Pilot Flying (PF) was flying the LINKK1 arrival to ILS 27 at IAH. The PF initiated a VNAV descent on the LINKK1 and was below path during the arrival. Inside of RDFSH while in VNAV we were cleared for the ILS 27. 1500' was called for and set in MCP. Since we were in VNAV and the step downs were in the legs page; I agreed with this altitude in the MCP. Just inside TRANN the PF asked for approach mode; this disengaged VNAV and since we were below the VNAV path we continued to 1500 no longer protected to 2000' at DENTO or FESTA. We were VMC the entire time and had the airport ad runway insight. ATC issued a low altitude warning. We climbed to 2000' and completed the approach normally.

column121: In the logbook and on our flight plan we had MEL 27-88-02 which was suppose to deactivate the Kruger flap system. The logbook was reviewed and the MEL was discussed between the First Officer and myself. All the placards were in place and signed off by maintenance that the DMP was accomplished. We started the aircraft and taxied out to the runway. I did notice a Kruger light on the SFPI; which was in the MEL that it might be displayed. We took off and climbed out. As we retracted the slats we received an ECAM warning and a single chime. The First Office was flying so I handled the ECAM. The ECAM message showed a Kruger not retracted and a notation of MAX SPD 300KIAS/.65. We did our after takeoff flows and checklist and I told the First Officer to fly the airplane and to not exceed the speed shown in the notation on ECAM. I did remember that besides getting the Kruger light that you could also get an ECAM message saying Kruger Fault. This was not the message we received after bringing up the flaps. I decided to get Maintenance Control on the line with Dispatch. I queried the maintenance people about our situation. I explained what we were showing and that it did not correspond with the MEL we had in the log book. We both concluded that if we could we would continue .65/300 knots. Maintenance was sure that the MEL was complied with and that it was probably an erroneous message; but to be safe we would comply with the speeds on the ECAM. After a discussion with dispatch about extra fuel burn and a longer duration flight we decided we were safe even if the Kruger Flap was extended. The dispatcher did run the numbers for fuel burn with Kruger Flap extended. The flight was uneventful and we landed with no further problems. I did make a comment in the logbook about what had transpired during the flight so if this was an anomaly maintenance could add this message to the MEL. We exited the aircraft where I noticed the Kruger Flap on the left side of the aircraft was partially extended. I immediately got the mechanics involved and told them of the situation. We headed to hotel for a 3 hour layover where we were to return to fly later. After returning to the gateway; and heading to the same aircraft we noticed the gear doors down and a panel on the wing root open. I met up with the maintenance people and asked if there was a problem. He told me that the aircraft was out of service. I asked him what was going on and we were told that the DMP for the KRUGER Flap MEL had not been complied with even though it had been stated so in the log book. Our flight was eventually cancelled as they were having some issues with the log book and could not do the DMP for this MEL.
column123: The aircraft had MEL 27-88-02. During initial climb we received an ECAM for kruger flap not retracted. The MEL did not mention this warning so we complied with the restrictions and called dispatch and maintenance to see if they had any additional information for us and if they preferred us to come back or continue to our destination. Maintenance and the crew felt safe continuing to destination using the restrictions for the ECAM message and dispatch agreed.

column121: I did two flights both returning from the north. IFR route to ZZZ vectors to ILS Runway XXR with the heading toward ZZZZZ about 150 degrees at 5500 ft. As I recall on both approaches at about 3 miles from ZZZZ; coming from the north; on a heading of 150 I was assigned a 180 heading and 4000 ft. Inside of 3 miles I was assigned 250 heading and descent to 3800 ft.; maintain 3800 ft. until established; then cleared for the approach.Because the turn is originating so close to ZZZZ; the aircraft has to turn more than 100 degrees to intercept the final approach course. This puts the aircraft in a roll AOB (Angle of Bank) of at least 30 degrees. This is a steep AOB for a Medevac flight; the crews are typically working on the patient. The turn also puts us inside ZZZZ; before ZZZZ3.This vector also puts us above the glide slope; from 4000 ft. to the glide path the aircraft needs to descend 400 ft. in less than a minute and continue that descent at 500 fpm to be on glide path. This puts the aircraft at almost a 1000 fpm descent just to get to the glide slope and then adjust to 500 fpm to continue on course and on glide slope all occurring within about 2 minutes. The distance from ZZZZ to the final approach fix is 4.3 miles and the aircraft would need to descend from 4000 ft. to 2500 ft. at 90 kts. in about 3 minutes. My point; it is aggressive and in my opinion not necessary to put us in that position. As a general rule glide slope should not be intercepted from above. This type of profile forces us to slow down and have a rapid rate of descent; this can cause an unstable approach. This vector profile is a recurring trend for TRACON controllers. I have addressed this many times with the supervisors and directly with the controllers as its occurring. Controllers have cited there is a MVA (Minimum Vectoring Altitude) in that area and they can't get us lower or there is 'a lot of traffic'. I am well aware of the MVA and TERPS (Terminal Instrument Approach Procedures). What I don't understand is why can't the controllers vector us at 4000 ft. and closer to ZZZZ2; or between ZZZZ and ZZZZ2; before turning us onto the localizer and glide slope? There is a feeder route on the initial approach from ZZZZ1 (Initial Approach Fix) to ZZZZ2 at 4200 ft. This is in the sector that we are arriving from the north; but the controllers will not use the higher altitude and vector closer to ZZZZ2 before turning us onto the localizer. Also the initial approach segment between ZZZZ2 and ZZZZ goes from 4200 ft. to 3600 ft.; yet the controllers never put us at 3600 ft. prior to ZZZZ within the TERP of the approach. Filing a flight plan to ZZZZ1 or ZZZZ2 IAP has no effect; controllers just disregard and vector as stated.

column121: Throughout the arrival ATC requested best forward speed; comply at ZZZZZ: 230kts; 10;000 ft. After ZZZZZ; we were vectored for the approach to XXL. We called the airport in sight and ATC cleared us for the approach. We were also requested to slow to 160kts for spacing with an aircraft in front of us for the same runway. At this point I had flaps 8; and requested flaps 20. [Another] flight was also being vectored for XXL; coming from the west. ATC then asked us to slow to final approach speed and if we had the aircraft ahead insight. We did not have it insight; so were quickly told to turn left to a heading of 270. I believe the [other] flight was then cleared for the approach ahead of us; behind the first aircraft we were following. They then gave us a right turn back to the East and told us to intercept the localizer for XXL; but we were 90 degrees to the course and wouldn't have been able to make it and we weren't configured. I think I remember the controller stating that 'this isn't going to work' and asked if we could accept runway XXR. We said yes; and ATC began vectoring us for XXR. At this point I was very task saturated and should've done a go around. I was very concerned that the aircraft was set up for XXR and verbalized this. He said it was. In hindsight; I should have just disconnected the autopilot. We were configured by the FAF and a stable approach was completed. A go around should have been executed before this situation got so far. We did not have time to find the original aircraft in front of us; and then the [other] flight was thrown into the situation. When ATC switched us to XXR; a localizer frequency should have been offered. The Captain called the tower after we landed to discuss what happened with the supervisor. He said they were happy he called; and the supervisor was watching the whole situation. He also agreed a report should be completed.
column123: After being vectored on to final for runway XXL from the west at on approach control frequency; I called the airport in sight after we descended below the clouds; approximately 3;000 ft. AGL; ATC cleared us for the visual approach and instructed a speed reduction to 160 KIAS. There was another aircraft on final about 4 miles ahead of us. I offered to slow to approach speed and the controller immediately requested for us to do so while asking if we could also see the aircraft ahead of us. I indicated that I was unable to see an aircraft ahead. First Officer already had flaps 8 and called for flaps 20 to start slowing when I replied that I was unable to see the other aircraft. Suddenly ATC issued instructions for us to turn left to a heading of 270. At the same time; [another] flight was cleared to join the localizer ahead from the opposite direction; from the east; and midway between us and the previous aircraft. As we rolled out on the 270 heading; we were instructed to turn back to the right to a heading of 090 to intercept the localizer. As we rolled through 360 I could clearly see the [other] aircraft; rolling onto final about 2 miles ahead of us. By then it appeared as if the controller was going to want us to join the LOC on almost a 90 degree angle; not fully configured on a 3 mile final. I was about to ask for re-sequence when the controller asked if I could take runway XXR. I responded that we could; without taking the time to consult the flying pilot. As we crossed the approach path for XXL I was quickly pulling up the LOC frequency for XXR and trying to get the frequencies retuned. [Pilot Flying] asked if I was sure it was XXR and not XXC. I followed procedure and got verification from ATC prior to tuning it on the RTU (Radio Tuning Unit). This seemed to take longer than [Pilot Flying] had expected and I was not yet fully aware of his level of stress over the sudden unnecessary changes. We were then vectored through the XXR LOC and given right turns back towards the airport and cleared for a visual to XXR. The approach and landing was stabilized and without further incident. In retrospect; I think not giving [Pilot Flying] time to give his input about accepting a different runway; understandably added to his stress. While I may not have been uncomfortable with what I saw outside the window; I could have made my fellow pilot less comfortable by not asking him what he was thinking. Other contributory factors were compounded when the controller did not offer a LOC frequency whilst changing runways after getting us to less than optimal airspeed and giving us an impromptu tour of his airspace. Our company procedures require us to use an ILS as a backup whenever available; even in visual conditions. This meant that our crew had to simultaneously deal with clarification of communications; following ATC instructions; practice slow flight; and setting up navigation for a late runway change. To add to the equation; there were 80 souls in the main cabin who were certainly wondering why we were zig-zagging around the airport. I think most pilots don't like being loaded up that much in the simulator. Even less do we enjoy such practice in a real airplane at low altitude; and in the most critical phase of flight. Once at the gate; I asked the tower for a phone number to Approach and was given the number that connected me immediately to the supervisor who had been standing behind the controller during the entire ordeal. ATC stated first that he was glad I called. He then explained to me that he had told the controller to re-sequence us and not try to save the situation after his first mistake had put us in a position for an unstable approach. He apologized for the inconvenience and indicated that the [other] aircraft should not have been squeezed into the line. He should have sent the [other] aircraft to runway XXR to begin with. But by the time the situation had developed; it was better for us to break off of the approach than toattempt to turn the other aircraft around. [The supervisor] was professional and wanted to reassure me that such traffic management is not acceptable and that the controller at the helm during the situation was made well aware of that fact. He and I agreed that a report should be filed if it has the potential prevent at least some similar situations from occurring. I can't speak for the ATC practices and procedures; but from my perspective; there are a few variables at play here that should receive a little more focus in the near future. This applies several large class B airports facilities as well warrants adequate and candid discussion. I can't speak for the ATC practices and procedures; but from my perspective; there are a few variables at play here that should receive a little more focus in the near future. This applies several large class B airports facilities as well warrants adequate and candid discussion. RNAV arrivals have been created to help reduce controller and pilot work load as well as simplify clearance delivery. Issue them; let us all fly them as published; line us up; and watch us keep the line moving as you switch us to the next sector. Trust us to do our part as pilots. TCAS tells us how far behind the next aircraft we are. You want separation; ask us to keep 4 to 5 miles and we will do it. We will be far less confused and take up less time on frequency getting clarification of what you actually need us to do. Keeping the frequency more clear will also allow for us confused pilots to actually ask for the help we need when we don't understand something rather than have to wait for a lengthy exchange while a potentially adverse situation gets worse. It would also be nice if there were some regularly scheduled meetings between professional pilot groups and ATC groups. Before 9/11; it was as easy as asking on the radio if we could come visit the tower in between flights. But after all the bureaucrats got involved; common sense seemed to get tossed right out the window and now we have a generation of pilots who have never been to an ATC facility; seen a radar screen; or had the chance to ask questions directly to a controller and vice versa! I would love to see that decision get more attention.

column121: There are way too many airfields in Central/South Florida that are in somewhat close proximity on the same CTAF (Common Traffic Advisory Frequency) frequency 123.05.   These airfields include GIF Winter Haven; 2IS Airglades; X04 Apopka; PCM Plant City; X26 Sebastian; and X39 Tampa North. On busy days usually towards the end of the week including Thur; Fri; Sat and Sun when the weather is good and many people are out flying; this frequency is completely jammed up much of the day with radio calls from all of the different airfields all at once. It is difficult sometimes to even get a quick call in. Some of these airfields to include GIF; PCM; X04 and X26 are very busy with flight school traffic; local GA (General Aviation) traffic and skydiving (at X26). This creates an unsafe situation when pilots cannot even hardly get a word in on CTAF to give a standard traffic call. Often times pilots will step on each other making calls which creates a jamming type sound which further makes comm more difficult. There shouldn't be this many airfields this close on the same frequency.
column125: Flight Instructor reported that the close proximity of several airports using the same CTAF causes frequency jamming.

column121: Went out for practice approaches. Wind was calm on departure and enroute; cold front came in on the way back and encountered severe turbulence; severe downdrafts and heavy precipitation in IMC after I was cleared for the GPS RNAV 1L approach. Lost about 2300 ft. causing me to go below IFR minimum altitude in the approach. Could not control the aircraft because of downdrafts and turbulence. Ended up breaking the clouds at about 1200 ft.; no downdrafts at this point but still encountered severe turbulence because of the gusty winds at low level. I was cleared for the visual. Landed safely and no injuries for both pilots on board. Aircraft got inspected by maintenance and there is no damage.

column118: Flight Crew Became Reoriented
column121: I was conducting a visual approach to [Runway] XXR backed up by the RNAV Y XXR. I was the Pilot Flying and the Captain was the Pilot Monitoring. We had briefed all the threats and the approach per the AOM prior to the TOD and were planning flaps full for the landing configuration. We were being vectored onto a base leg to join the final visually. I was faster than I should have been on the base for the distance we were out (180 selected speed I think) and so I had to delay calling for the flaps. I think the last altitude we were given was 8;000 feet on the base before we reported the field in sight and were cleared for the visual. The approach had been activated and I went to managed speed. We then set a lower altitude just above the altitude for the next fix after the one just ahead of us on the approach so I could use vertical speed to start down and stay on the path since the approach was not quite captured. I had armed the approach and it annunciated FINAL APP as we were joining the final and the aircraft continued descending on the path. I was using the auto pilot for lateral and vertical NAV until established on final. We extended the gear prior to joining the final to help with slowing the aircraft to flap deployment speeds. The vector had put us in closer than I had anticipated and I had also started slowing later than I should have. The speed did not come back very quickly; possibly because of a tailwind shifting to a headwind as were turning final; so I had to disconnect the autopilot and arrest the descent to get the aircraft slowed for flap deployment and also to prevent an overspeed. Both the Captain and I noticed the RA was close to 1;000 [feet] as we deployed final flaps; and our speed was still well above approach speed; but we were VMC and our assessment was that we had been in final configuration prior to 1;000 AGL. We continued the approach and were stable at 500 [feet] so we landed out of the approach within the required criteria. We were at the gate when the ACARS printed a Self-Assessment Flight Report. We weren't surprised to see Y for speed and descent; but we were surprised to see a Y for flap change. We discussed it; and although we were unstable at 1;000 [feet]; we felt that we had been configured and thought the report generated a flap change Y at 0974 RA because the terrain rises to the north and the spot elevations are around 300-600 feet higher than the airport elevation near where we selected final configuration. Regardless; the final configuration selection was closer to 1;000 AGL than it should have been and we decided we would file an report just in case. Normally; I call for gear down and managed speed at 2;000 AGL so as to have plenty of time to reach final configuration prior to 1;000 AGL; but I missed that cue because I was in the yellow due to being tighter and faster than I had anticipated; and I didn't realize it because I was busy trying to get back in the green slowing and turning final and descending to stay on the path. This is also a case where my experience exceeded my performance due to being new to being back in the right seat and not flying much in September. I also didn't say anything about being in the yellow; and so I didn't get as much help as I could have used. I also failed to brief that we may need to be configured on base depending on where the base leg is and to say something if I don't ask for the gear down by 2;000 AGL. The other factor was conducting a visual approach to a high-altitude airport where AGL altitudes happen at much higher MSL altitudes than were are used to and neither I nor the Captain had been into ZZZ in a long time. I will more thoroughly brief visual approaches regarding the gates I want for configuration and speed changes that provide plenty of margin for a stabilized approach.
column123: As we turned to intercept final and configure for landing with a near 0 VSI; a hill on final gave us an unexpected 1;000 ft. radio alt call. We where visual and comfortable with continuing the approach due to distance. I recall being fully configuration when the second 1;000 foot reading on final and on speed at 500 ft. We failed to slow down in time on base leg not taking into effect high alt difference and ground speed. We did not brief the ground speed difference and the delayed slow down or terrain around the base turn. Haven't been to ZZZ in years.

column121: On touchdown at ZZZ; spoilers came out and caused the nose to rise. As a result; we had a tail strike. Reported this to MX (Maintenance) Control. Green band on tail strike cartridge was still showing but paint was scraped off. FAs (Flight Attendants) in rear of aircraft also heard the strike. Also spoke with [Company Safety] and [Flight Operations].I realize this is mentioned in the FH (Flight Handbook). I will add this to the brief for now on. I have personally only experienced it once. We may also want to add this to the initial or recurrent sim training.

column121: I have flown into MYF several times on an Instrument Approach; ILS 28R. Every time; I am given headings and vectors to final. I have asked for the full approach sometimes; but usually given vectors. The issue is that the altitudes and heading make it very difficult to fly a controlled and standard approach. This last approach I was given a heading to the final approach course at a higher altitude; then turned right and left and then back to final and then a steep descent to be able to get back on the right altitudes for the approach. This has happened about 5 times and usually the same sequence. I am kept high; then a step descent; various headings and then cleared for the approach at the last few minutes. It makes it very difficult to manage speed and altitude. Also; when in instrument conditions it provides an opportunity to become spatially disoriented with the turns and the assigned descent profile. I believe it would be MUCH safer to be given the full approach and follow the listed altitudes. I can tell there is a lot of traffic and a lot of training going on in the area; 2 parallel runways at MYF also present a challenge for controllers; but we should find a way to make the instrument approach be according to the plate. I believe this will make it safer for us all. I respect the Approach controllers and they are dealing with all the traffic the best they can. It just makes the Approach more difficult to fly correctly. If the approach is not workable; perhaps it should be revised.

column121: I was working Local Control which manages the class D during north flow operations. Aircraft X had been doing air work at an adjacent airport which is about 8 to 9 miles west of this airport. Aircraft Y was inbound on the ILS. Aircraft Z and Aircraft A were inbound from the southwest. Approach called for a point out to Flight Data/Clearance delivery on Aircraft X and when asked what he was going to do we were told he was going back towards the adjacent airport. Aircraft X entered from the southwest and made maneuvers about 2 to 3 southwest at 2;300 feet and then turned southeast directly overflying Aircraft Y on the ILS by 400 feet. Aircraft Y was issued traffic alerts and broken off the approach to a 090 heading. This put them in conflict with Aircraft B; inbound from the southeast. Another traffic alert was given to Aircraft Y for Aircraft B. Just before Aircraft Y was broken off the ILS approach; Aircraft Z a VFR inbound from the southwest was issued a traffic alert since Aircraft X initially turned opposite direction to them. Aircraft A VFR from the southwest also had to be diverted to the east prior to Aircraft Y being broken off the approach because Aircraft X's intentions were unpredictable and not as described. When asked why flight check was turning east (right instead of left) toward our traffic we were told by approach that 'it is what it is.'Flight Check operations are a high risk to anyone else operating in the area. When they arrive their intentions are incredibly vague other than what they are checking. They often seem to just do whatever they want and let you know as they are doing it. From a local standpoint I think that flight check needs to be a lot more clear of their exact intentions) while operating in the vicinity. VFR arrivals need to be rerouted or placed in holding by approach to a different quadrant other than the one that Flight Check is operating in. This is not the first close call Flight Check has caused over the past few years.

column121: I was the PF (Pilot Flying) doing CA (Captain) upgrade line check. Coming on the left downwind for runway 19L in ZZZ at 3000 ft. IMC we got a TA/RA and the TCAS was showing a traffic about 3 miles 12 o'clock same altitude; I disconnected the A/P (Auto-Pilot) and A/T (Auto-Throttles) and initiated the TCAS maneuver following the guidance which directed us to climb; PM (Pilot Monitoring) advised ATC and ATC informed us that there were no traffic in the vicinity of the airport and advised us to get back to 3000 ft. and vectored us for the ILS approach 19L and while we're descending back to 3000 ft. the FO (First Officer) side inbound DU (Display Unit) went blank; few seconds later the same display unit on my side went blank as well at this time we were already turning final and I saw the runway and decided to continue to land meanwhile the PM called the ATC and advised them that we had multiple display failures; 2 minutes later we got the displays back and we continued the visual approach to landing.False TCAS TA/RA and multiple display failures.

column121: We were on a 6 mile final when tower cleared a Cirrus to land in front of us. When this happened separation from the traffic began to decrease; tower then cleared the Cirrus for a low approach. This seemed to cause confusion in the Cirrus cockpit. We continued until the spacing was unsafe; the Cirrus appeared to be on the runway. At around 400 ft. we executed a go around. As we began to climb at go around thrust flaps 8 the Cirrus began to climb as well giving us a TA and tower asked us to side step to the right. I began a turn to the right as the pilot monitoring watched the traffic below us carefully. While our focus was on avoiding the traffic we neglected to call 'positive rate' and raise the gear. This led to a delay is raising the flaps form 8 to 0 to clean up the airplane. Since we were already at pattern altitude we made the decision to leave the gear down since we were moments away from needing to lower it again. Towers poor spacing was the initial cause of the event. However our focus on keeping visual separation from the traffic led us to neglect some of our duties. Although traffic avoidance was and should be our top priority in that situation. We could have called a go around earlier when we saw that there could be a potential conflict. This would have given us more time and led to less task saturation.
column123: While on about a six mile final tower cleared a Cirrus to perform a short approach to a touch and go in front of us. As we continued the rate of closure was greater than tower anticipated and tower changed the Cirrus clearance to a low approach only. We continued our approach until about 400 ft. AGL. At this time it appeared the Cirrus did a touch and go and was on the runway for a long time. I felt that the separation margin was too small and called for a go around. We completed the first portion of the go around but then the Cirrus had started his upwind and was directly in our path. We prioritized maintaining visual separation with the Cirrus and complying with ATC separation instructions and failed to bring gear up and do a climb sequence until we were on a downwind and ran the after takeoff and then another decent check. After this we completed all check lists and had a safe landing. Poor aircraft separation by ATC. It sounded like there were two controllers in the tower; one being trained and the other a supervisor. Waiting too long to call for the go around. We waited until a go around caused too much going on and made us miss checklists and call outs due to high work load. Misinterpreted instructions by the Cirrus. When ATC changed the landing clearance for the Cirrus it was unclear if they understood what ATC actually wanted. I think the biggest thing that could be done to avoid similar situations is to call for a go around earlier when we saw there might be a conflict with spacing. There were cues earlier in the approach indicating a potential for loss of separation; but it was also possible for us to land had the Cirrus followed instructions in a timely manner. We should have erred on the conservative side to not put us in an overloaded task situation.

column121: While being vectored by Approach Control on approach to Runway XXC at ZZZ; we were held at 4;000 ft. on the base leg with instructions to maintain 210 KIAS. We were subsequently given clearance for a Visual Approach to Runway XXC. The pilot flying from the left seat was the PIC (Pilot in Command). I was the SIC (Second in Command)/pilot monitoring from the right seat and acknowledged the visual approach clearance. The pilot flying made appropriate use of power levers; speed breaks; flaps; and landing gear to set up for a stabilized approach as he attempted to descend and intercept a normal glide path on final approach to the runway; while maintaining the requested 210 knot speed restriction. Once established on final; but still above a normal glide path; the PIC asked me to query ATC as to whether we could resume normal approach speed. The controller replied; 'When I clear you for a visual approach; the speed restriction is removed.' Noting that we were still above the PAPI and glidepath on final approach as a result of being kept at high altitude on the base leg and while descending through approximately 2;000 ft.; both crewmembers agreed that the aircraft could likely be configured to salvage a stabilized approach. However; as we descended through approximately 1;000 ft.; we realized that we had not been handed off to the control tower. As the approach control frequency was busy; I attempted to switch to the control tower frequency automatically; and was trying to reference the approach chart on the pilot's side display for the appropriate frequency; as there were three listed for the various runways. As a break in communications presented itself; I quickly queried approach control; 'Aircraft X to tower?' The controller responded that we should already be on the tower frequency; which he had not yet instructed us to do; and which was not the published frequency for Runway XXC. As the aircraft was now on short final; slightly above glide path; notwithstanding the long length of the runway; and the fact that we had not received landing clearance from the tower; the crew immediately agreed to go around; and smoothly initiated a go around procedure. I checked in with ZZZ Tower to advise we were going around. We were vectored by tower in a left hand traffic pattern at an appropriate altitude; without speed restriction; and were able to properly configure; execute a visual approach and land without incident.The crew could have solicited a lower altitude from Approach on the base leg and/or stated that we were unable to maintain the 210 KIAS speed restriction in order to establish ourselves on the final approach course at an appropriate altitude and speed for a stabilized approach. We could also have solicited a handoff from approach to tower frequency sooner. The decision to go around was prudently made and smoothly executed. However; we should have made the decision to do so at 1;000 feet AGL due to our position above the normal glide path (unstable approach) and not having been handed off to tower.
column123: While being vectored for a visual approach to XXC at ZZZ; approach had us turning a close in base at 4;000 ft. and 210 knots as assigned. Once we were on the final approach course; we were cleared for the visual approach. I knew that we were both too high and too fast; however I thought with the use of drag devices we could become stable prior to 1000 ft. per our FOM. I noticed that we were on a 2-3 mile final and had not been switched to the tower controller yet and asked the PNF (Pilot Not Flying) to query the approach controller. The approach controller stated that he had handed us over to the tower controller; neither of us in the cockpit heard that or acknowledged that instruction. The PNF was searching on the approach chart for a tower frequency and there was some confusion as he was trying the read the chart and which frequency he should be on. By the time I could have the PNF query the controller again; I realized that we were not going to be stable within the parameters of the FOM. I made the decision to go-around and informed the PNF to tell the approach controller we were executing a missed approach. The approach controller gave us instructions to fly runway heading to 3000 ft. and vectored us around for another visual approach which ended up in a stable; uneventful landing.I should have realized earlier on in the approach that we were vectored onto the final approach course too fast and too high. The PNF should have preloaded the tower frequency into the Radio Tune page. The decision to execute a go-around was correct but could have possibly been made sooner.

column121: Pilot and a singe passenger were returning for a landing in VMC (winds light and variable); and cleared For landing by Tower. After what appeared to be a normal landing; on-centerline and during roll-out;(the plane was rolling below 30 kts); the aircraft unexpectedly began to pull to the right. The pilot attempted to correct using left rudder pressure and toe brake. The combination of left rudder pressure and left toe brake did not appear to exert sufficient braking authority or correct the turn. The aircraft seemed very slow to correct direction. The pilot continued with left rudder pressure and applied the handbrake. The plane immediately slowed; but rolled off the right side of the marked runway (and onto the apron) as the pilot was able to regain directional control. This was just past the first taxiway; where the underside of the right wing scraped the top edge of the taxiway sign. There did not appear to be any damage to the sign; but the bottom skin of the right wing was later found to be torn; in a line perpendicular to the leading edge; on the outboard side of the wing; about half-way to the from the front of the wing; and the skin tear exposed the bottom edge of the wing spar; which appeared to be nicked. Using the handbrake only from that point on; the pilot was able to return to the runway centerline and taxi the plane on the runway to the next taxiway and then navigate to parking with no further directional control problems. The incident was promptly reported and the plane was grounded for inspection and repair at visual inspection by a qualified A&P.It had been noted that on one prior flight that the plane had unexpectedly pulled to the right; but that the problem had been evaluated and resolved. On pre-flight taxi and takeoff during the flight on which the incident occurred; the plane did not appear to be pulling excessively to the right; so the pilot believed that the earlier squawk had been resolved; and the pilot decided to conduct the flight as planned. The pilot was well-rested; in good health; and alert during the flight. A potential distractor was that the oil access door on the cowling popped open on climb-out after takeoff (this resulted in the decision to return to land and correct the issue before continuing the flight). It is possible that this issue may have distracted the pilot. Also; the pilot may not have reacted quickly enough when the plane began pulling to the right on roll-out; and may not have transitioned quickly enough from applying the toe brake (which did not appear to be effective)to using the handbrake (which applied effective braking on both mains). Finally; as a final possible preventive action; the pilot might have elected to touch-down on the left side of the wide runway; anticipating the possibility of the plane pulling to the right.

column121: First aircraft inbound for ZZZ; we're on a north operation.  Aircraft X comes from the SW; checks on with correct ATIS; I assign him Runway XXL tell him to expect radar vectors for a visual approach; second aircraft; Aircraft Y checks on with correct ATIS from NE I assign him radar vectors VA XX L; third aircraft checks on from SW Aircraft Z; checks on with correct ATIS tell him to expect visual approach XXL and I give him a caution wake turbulence because he'll be following Aircraft Y.  Since Aircraft X was my first aircraft it's customary for me to get flight conditions; winds bases etc.I wasn't very busy; some departures from ZZZ and nearby satellites; got one VFR for flight following.  I know something had gone wrong at some point with our radar; something with ASR and I think some shout lines also OTS (Out of Service) which I believe Supervisor was working on due to phone calls in background.  CIC was watching the satellite sector he seemed to be getting busy.  I vector Aircraft X to the back of the box; for a straight in visual approach approximately 15 miles; I get the last of the PIREP; I clear him and switch.  I slowed Aircraft Y on downwind to follow; and I had vectored Aircraft Z towards final.  Aircraft Y calls field in sight; I withhold clearance to give Aircraft X room to get off runway.  Finally give a turn and clearance to Aircraft Y and switch to Tower.  Then I hook Aircraft Z to join the final approach course and advise field when I notice go-around; and Aircraft Y going to XXL; I assumed FOD on runway; I immediately change the runway on Aircraft Z; tell him to plan on a VA app; and told him to stand by while I find the localizer frequency; he says he has field in sight so I clear him to XXL; at some point I switch him. Aircraft X comes off; I start my point out with my satellite; climb him to 4;000; and turn him.  I asked him if he went around due to FOD; I don't think he understood my questions; I asked him if he went around due to FOD; what was reason for go-around and he said runway was closed; he went around when he saw the flashing 'X' on runway then he asked the tower controller if runway closed. That's when I realized I had been vectoring to wrong runway and forgotten west complex [was] closed.  I had been previously distracted prior to taking position from union forcing us to no longer social distance and having to train; so that had been something I was discussing with my peers and the possibility of changing schedules for a sixth time this year that had definitely been a major distraction when I came to work today.  In the end it's on me; I'd like to know if ATIS was correct because I had three pilots all check in with current ATIS and no one balked at XXL.  Also straight in; over 15 miles; tagged for the wrong runway; and tower didn't look at the scope or out the window and cleared for the wrong runway.  Just a lot of Swiss cheese [stuff] went down. A lot of things went wrong; involving a lot of people; maybe I should have taken sick leave today.

column121: Cleared for visual approach and cleared to land on RWYXXL. [Commercial flight] canceled their take off clearance so [ZZZ] Tower told us to continue the visual approach with a canceled landing clearance. We were 7.5 miles out direct to the Final Approach Fix in LNAV (Lateral Navigation) and the armed glideslope had captured at about 3;000 MSL providing a constant descent. We were Flaps 5 at 180 kts. To create spacing I called for gear down and flaps 20 and rolled the speed back above 20 maneuvering speed. The plane began to slow and pitch down with flap extension. I called for flaps 25 when the gear showed 3 green and the EICAS (Engine Indication And Crew Alerting System) showed 'Autopilot' indicating it was still working but in a degraded mode. I then saw GS FMA (Glideslope Flight Mode Annunciator) now had a line thru it. At this point I disconnected the autopilot and leveled off visually. The altitude was about 1400 MSL; 1000 AGL. I told the Pilot Monitoring I was going to maintain 1000 AGL until established. Right after I leveled off the [ZZZ] Tower announced low altitude alert. During the debrief the Pilot Monitoring said he saw the glideslope go full scale below us and then shoot up to full scale above us near the autopilot disconnect. I continued to maneuver visually to the runway centerline at 1;000 ft. Once established we cycled the Flight Directors and the glideslope centered back to where we were on the approach. Continued visually and landed. Wrote up the discrepancy in the logbook.  Fatigue didn't cause it; but it may have slowed my processing of everything that was happening kind of at the same time.  Don't allow the glideslope to capture early. Choose another vertical mode until on or very close to the LOC (Localizer).
column123: We were cleared for the visual to [Runway] XXL and subsequently cleared to land approximately 10SW of the airport. We were level at 3000 when approach was armed and glideslope captured. Aircraft began descending on glideslope. ATC canceled our landing clearance and cleared a [Commercial flight] for takeoff on [Runway] XXL. In order to slow and create space the Pilot Flying called for gear and flaps 20. After we received an AUTOPILOT EICAS and the GS FMA (Glide Slope Flight Mode Annunciator) had an amber line through it. The Captain disconnected the autopilot and autothrottles and leveled the aircraft. We were approximately 1400 MSL (1000AFE). About this time [ZZZ] Tower called a low altitude alert. The glideslope indicated full scale deflection below us and shortly after indicated full scale deflection above us. The Captain continued visually onto centerline; the Flight directors were turned off and turned back on and approach rearmed. LOC and GS FMAs activated and appeared to act normal. Autopilot/Glideslope malfunction. ATC canceling our landing clearance and clearing an aircraft for takeoff on our planned runway caused a distraction about the same time as the malfunction.

column121: During the flight from ZZZ1 to ZZZ; while attempting to acquire the landing data for the arrival into ZZZ; the ACARS indicated NO COMM. The PM received the ATIS information through the radios and determined the landing data numbers via the company manuals in the EFB. The ILS XX for ZZZ was subsequently placed in the FMS prior to the brief; and Runway XX was briefed as the landing runway as per ATIS. While established on the ZZZZZ 3 arrival and checking in with final approach; ATC stated to expect Runway XYL. The PM verbally stated the fixes for the XYL ZZZZZ 3 arrival. Both the PM and PF then switched to the ILS XYL approach plate on the EFB and briefed that approach. While flying the ZZZZZ 3; both the PF and the PM discussed the two different split-off points. The first split-off is for landing East or West. The second Split-off is for landing XX or XYL. I was the Pilot Flying and was established on the ZZZZZ 3 Arrival when I noticed the indicated waypoints were still for Runway XX. As I stated that we were navigating for Runway XX; Approach Control asked us to make a right turn towards the proper waypoint for the XYL arrival. This event was caused by not verifying the correct runway was placed into the FMS after a runway change was given by ATC. Contributing factors for this event would be the initial distraction resulting from the failure of ACARS. Another contributing factor would be the lack of currency/proficiency for both the PF and the PM. This lack of currency/proficiency was briefed as the threat in the brief for both the takeoff as well as the arrival. A final contributing factor would be the lack of familiarity with ZZZ for the PF as this was the first time flying into that airport.First and foremost; flight path management is paramount to a successful outcome to any flight. When given any changes in altitudes; airspeeds; headings; runways etc. by ATC; each pilot is responsible to confirm and verify those changes. If task saturation begins to set in; notify the other pilot and let them assist you with reducing your saturation level. Always clearly understand where you are during any aspect of a flight. Within the extremely dynamic environment we find ourselves in during any flight operation; a lack of currency or proficiency can and does have a negative effect. Under the circumstances of COVID-19; it is still incumbent on each pilot to maintain the highest level of professionalism possible.
column123: I was pilot monitoring; prior to descent the ACARS went NO COMM; so I was gathering weather and doing landing data manually- we had set up for and briefed for XX as that was the previous active runway on our ACARS there is a reasonably complex STAR with altitudes and speeds.  On checking in with Final Approach we were given [Runway] XYL - I inadvertently verified the verbiage of the arrival on the plate and somehow didn't realize I hadn't executed the FMS. We had verbalized the fixes and were anticipating the XYL transition when we were at 4;000 feet with the final approach before Tower- the aircraft began a turn to the left on the XX transition and the PF asked if that was correct- simultaneously the controller had us turn- ultimately descending and intercepting the final approach course for XYLAs PM I was becoming task saturated in the descent and ultimately didn't execute the proper inputs for the runway change-- of note; I am returning from extended time off as was my PF -- we had briefed currency in our brief and were communicating well about the arrival and landing.Aside from not noticing my mistake of not executing the runway change we were communicating well and except for my being task saturated with getting the ATIS and monitoring a complex arrival I believe we could have attempted a full instrument briefing- however it was VMC for a visual and we had briefed the runway including taxi etc.  The change of runway at a critical phase along with our lack of recent experience at ZZZ led to my not preparing for the appropriate runway initially.

column121: A friend who has a multi engine and instrument rating was interested in possibly buying my Cessna 310 so we flew to breakfast in IMC with me in the left seat acting as PIC. I suggested that on our return; he sit in the left seat and we fly to ZZZ where they had longer runways than our home field and he could practice some landings. While he was rated; I was still PIC from the right seat as he was not multi engine current and I was a multi engine CFI.We flew down and executed an RNAV/GPS (Area Navigation) approach to Runway XX in VMC conditions. I said that our first landing exercise would be to take the plane almost down to the runway and just fly a foot or so off of the deck to help visualize the sight picture; before cutting the throttles and landing. We'd then taxi back for more pattern work.The approach was very successful and everything went well until about 50 feet when he started to flare. I said we needed to keep coming down; we were nowhere near the altitude we needed to be at to begin the flare; but he leveled off; seeming to feel for the runway from that point. We quickly reached the midpoint of the runway and I began thinking that a go around would be necessary but I let him continue a bit further. Shortly thereafter I called for a go around but he cut the power; raised the nose; and landed firmly; about 2/3 of the way down the runway. I did not think we had room to stop on the remaining runway and at 80 knots we were close to flying speed. He told me to take over and I applied full power. At the end of the runway we still were slower than I would want to be but I raised the nose and we were airborne in ground effect. The plane was very slow to accelerate or climb and I felt my friend pulling back on the yoke. I pushed forward not wanting to stall or leave ground effect. At this point I realized that our gear was still down and that we had landing flaps. I knew that if I retracted landing flaps we had a very strong potential to sink into the ground so faced with that option I left the flaps down. I was not sure of the drag effect from gear retraction so I left it down as well. Again I felt him pulling back on the wheel while I yelled 'stop pulling' and pushed forward.Soon there was a row of trees in the windshield and I gathered we had gotten all of the speed we could for the time being and I pulled back on the yoke. The tower controller had seen what was going on and asked us if we were ok; did we need assistance; and did we see the trees. He also asked if we would be avoiding the trees. These calls were a distraction and I did not answer as my first priority was to fly the aircraft.We climbed out; retracting the gear and flaps. The controller asked if we wanted him to cancel IFR (Instrument Flight Rules) and for us to resume our pattern work. Shaken; I was not interested in pattern work and I asked him to cancel IFR and we would leave the area. Since he had questioned if we had hit the trees I decided to fly about 5 miles to an airport where I have maintenance done to have the plane inspected. Apparently we had made contact with the very top of the tree line as evidenced by some leaves on the nose gear. The mechanic came out to inspect the plane and could find no damage at all after inspecting the gear; flaps; wings; fuselage; and general condition.In retrospect; I had put my friend in a bad position. Even though he had watched me land many times; and had landed himself in the past; I should have demonstrated the first landing. I know better than to land outside the touchdown zone and yet we did. At the midpoint of the runway with limited runway ahead of us; I should have had an aggressive transfer of control and taken the plane around myself. If I had done that I have no doubt I would have raised the gear and flaps allowing a successful climb out; but in the uncertain transfer of control with the pilot monitoring suddenly and unexpectedly becoming the pilot flying; the sequence of a successful go around were lost.I can't help but think that I put the pilot flying into a bad position by not doing the go around sooner and not having the plane in proper climb configuration was a bad error. I have replayed this many times and reminded myself that in this situation; even though you are not the flying pilot; you have to stay on top of things mentally and be prepared to take over at any point.The better decision after the touch down would have been to get out of the mental go around mind set and just kept it on the ground. A couple of blown tires and a plane in the overrun would have been nothing compared to the threats of staggering into the air in a bad configuration with trees ahead.

column121: While conducting a Flight Review; my client pilot was practicing a power-off 180-degree approach to landing (simulated emergency) when we encountered wind shear. The winds were approximately 13 gusting 18 knots from 210 and the aircraft was on a modified left base while angling toward Runway XX at ZZZ. The descent path was normal for a glide to the beginning of the runway and airspeed was approximately 85 KT. At about 1;000 feet from the runway and 300 feet AGL; the pilot lowered the landing gear while passing into the lee of a dense woodlot that stands about 600 feet south and west of the extended runway centerline; and there we experienced a sudden and dramatic loss of airspeed; down to approximately 65 knots. (Of course; some loss of airspeed was expected as the gear deployed.) I called for power as the aircraft began to pitch down and descend. (The Pilot Flying was guarding the throttle.) I continued to shout for more power over the next 2-3 seconds as the aircraft descended to perhaps 15 feet AGL; still 500 feet short of the runway.I took control of the airplane as the pilot brought in full power. We were now grazing the tops of the corn in the field below us and I could see a 15' fence coming up quickly. I felt we had very little angle of attack available - perhaps just enough to climb 10' to clear the fence. I increased elevator as much as I dared; then lowered the nose again once past the fence. Because of our low airspeed and high angle of attack; though the propeller cleared the fence; the top of a fence post contacted and tore the skin of the airplane's belly just aft of the gear legs. As airspeed slowly recovered; I climbed to perhaps 20 feet AGL; then reduced power as we reached the runway and made a normal landing. I have been flying for more than XX years and I have never experienced such a dramatic loss of airspeed. My corrective action to prevent a similar crisis will include the following.1. I will not use the actual beginning of runways to practice power-off landings; but instead have clients treat the touch down zone (1;000 feet down the runway) as their landing target.2. I will be more prepared to push; move; or overpower client pilots if they do not immediately respond to verbal instructions. 3. I will be more mindful of the potential for wind shear; especially when operating downwind of significant obstacles (the wood lot; in this case) on windy days.

column121: Our Lincoln transmitters are intermittent and randomly go unusable.  Sometimes at unacceptable and critical times. During this time I had about a dozen on my frequency; 124.0; when it just randomly became unusable.  I had 3 heavies in my IFR pattern for practice approaches.  I had an IFR jet inbound.  There were 2 VFRs inbound.  There was a VFR on a practice approach.  There was a VFR doing photo work over the lake area just west of the finals.  Plus additional satellite operations. It just suddenly wouldn't transmit.  I checked my headset.  Then I switched the plug into the second set of holes.  I tried using the foot pedal instead of my button.  I tried switching to the backup transmitters.  I tried guard frequency.  The Supervisor tried to use the PET [radio].  I switched to the override orange box and could reach 2 or 3 of them; but then only about 1 of the 5 tries would go through and the responses were broken; unreadable or very static-y.  We tried to put everyone on our backup 133.575; but it had the same issues.  We could hear pilots; but were unable to transmit anything back.  I told Tower to keep everyone on their frequency they had; and everyone was point out approved.  We frantically kept trying to reach the aircraft sitting on my frequency as they began to go through final and some began to get dangerously close to MVAs.  Finally; the combination of everything and the frequency seemed to snap back to working.In the next hour; we had probably 25% of the transmissions to aircraft not be received.  At many altitudes in areas that would typically work.  I used aircraft I could reach to relay to the ones I couldn't.  It was a nightmare. I may have had separation losses.  I think I kept altitude separation; but it was too busy and chaotic for myself or the Supervisor to work it and see. The VFR photo guy just decided on his own to head east; directly across the two finals in front of aircraft on [an] approach; without me telling him to; or without me being able to issue any traffic to anyone.  One of the heavies I had on a right base to RWY 18 at 040 took it upon himself just to turn in to the airport.  He ended up overflying at 040 before finally switching himself to tower frequency to enter their frequency.  This was the 2nd scariest thing I've ever had while working.  Not okay. The frequency transmitter issues need to be identified and fixed.  Just because they start working again; is not good enough.  We also need a real backup that we can use that transmits from a different source.  When our backup seems to go out at the exact same time; it is useless.  Our Omaha and Offutt frequency options are not in range for low Lincoln sector aircraft.For the last 2-3 weeks; something is wrong with the transmitting capabilities of our Lincoln sector frequencies.  Our primary 124.0 frequency will all of a sudden just not go out to the pilots.  We try switching to main/stand-by and it makes no difference.  We switch and do all the coordination with other facilities to use our backup frequency; 133.575; but then it has the same issue.  Sometimes we can use the Orange Voice Backup Override boxes; but it is also intermittent and when it goes out is maybe 2 by 5 at best with a lot of static and crackling.  If we try to work them in by leaving them on Omaha and/or Offutt frequencies; 120.1 or 124.95; the range isn't good enough to reach low aircraft on the south/west side of Lincoln and we lose contact that way completely. This is an urgent; serious issue.  When we called it into MOCC (Mid-States Operations Control Center) a week or two ago; the tech responded to us that 'it was probably due to the storm passing through' even though there was only light (level 1 and 2 precipitation).  When it came back online they just called it back in service without fixing anything or identifying the problem.  Yesterday it occurred while I had around a dozen aircraft on frequency.  The sudden loss of communications nearly caused major incidents and could have been catastrophic.  Luckily I had most of them altitude separated as an additional precaution at the time; but we still had a near MVA (Minimum Vectoring Altitude) bust as a result and may have lost separation in the chaos.  The Supervisor tried to use the PET; but our range doesn't reach Lincoln on them; and when it did intermittently go through; only stepped on my transmissions I was making through the override box and made it worse.

column121: Upon reaching downwind for RWY XX; cleared for the visual approach; at 4;000 ft.; 200kias; I called for flaps 8. The slats did not extend; triggering a SLATS FAIL caution and the flaps came down to 8. I elected to call for the QRH ; took the radios from my First Officer; and stated that we were [advising ATC] and needed to hold over ZZZZZ to run our checklist. We ran through the QRH by the time we reached ZZZZZ and had a quick discussion about fuel; time; landing distance; and the situation in general. I ACARS'ed our Dispatcher to inform him of our situation and status; asking if he wanted us to proceed to a different airport as we had a surplus of fuel. I let the flight attendants know of our situation first; but did not brief the TEST items at this time. I informed the passengers that we had a minor mechanical issue and that we would need a few minutes before landing in C to take care of it. We further computed the increase in landing distance due to the 10kias Vref additive and found ZZZ was a viable destination for our situation as well as receiving a reply from the Dispatcher stating that he was satisfied with ZZZ; but the final decision was ours. We elected to change runways to XY; as it has an ILS and approx. 2;000 ft more length to stop on. We set up; re-briefed the approach; I then briefed the flight attendants the TEST items; and then informed the passengers that we would be landing in 6 minutes. We landed without issue at flaps 45 per the QRH. Upon exiting the runway; I elected to cancel the [advisory] with Tower as there was no way a SLATS issue would preclude a safe taxi. We also elected to not retract the flaps upon clearing the runway and deferred that decision to MX. We proceeded to park and shut down without issue. After shut down; I became involved with talking to MX (approving retraction of the flaps); the Chief Pilot; local fire safety and crash rescue; and Oeps personnel about the status; events; and the aircraft state. I ran the Securing Checklist without having retracted the flaps and left them at 45 deg; matching the handle position. I realized this after having left the secure area of the airport and could not return to correct the aircraft state.  Slats were jammed at 0 degrees per MX BLIN code and fault description.

column121: Upon final approach to XXL on [an] ASR approach into ZZZZ the Pilot Flying elected and performed a go around approximately 2-3 miles from runway. The decision to go around was due to being high on glide path and in bad position to land at the final descent point. After the go around; Approach Control gave vectors to 7-mile final for another approach to XXL. The Pilot Flying was able to acquire a better sight picture and glide path; then landed without further issue. The crew had to do a radar approach as no approaches for ZZZZ are available in the FMC database for the open runway; XXL/XR. The ASR final descent point did not put the aircraft in a position for pilot flying to see PAPIs to refine glide path. In addition; the sloping terrain just off the end of the runway gives illusion of lower position on glide slope that actual position. Runway XXR/XL is closed for an extended duration due to construction. All approaches in FMC database are for the closed runway. Our company version of Flight Deck Pro has approaches available to XXL/XR so consideration should be given to adding these approaches to the FMC database since ZZZZ is [an] airport with regular flights by company aircraft.

column121: We were coming into ZZZ on the ZZZZZ Arrival. We were a bit high and fast so we had to slow down a bit and loose altitude. On the descent we picked up some ice so I turned the REF speeds [switch] to INCR (increase). As we descended we checked for ice and there was still some on the right wing so I left the REF speeds to INCR. As we were approaching 3;000 ft we were fully configured and about 5 to 10 kts above open bug; which was about 115 to 120. The red line approached but we never exceeded it. When we got the stick shaker the FO (First Officer); who was pilot flying; just lowered the nose a bit to correct. It then stopped and we continued on with the approach and safe landing. At that point I turned the REF speeds to off. I should have done another ice check on descent so we would have turned the REF speeds off.
column123: Operating Aircraft X from ZZZ1 to ZZZ as we began our approach and passed through 10;000 ft. We completed the approach checklist and checked wings for icing. I identified my wing still had icing present so we continued our decent with the ref speeds increased. As we continued our decent we went through our configuration checks and began slowing down. As we continued our decent a few moments later I checked my wing again and identified the icing was no longer present. Before we went through 1000 ft. my speed was set between solid bug and open bug in preparation for the approach; I noticed that the Vso speed was indicating near the open bug speed. I was maintaining approximately 5 knots above the open bug speed when I got a momentary stick shaker; I immediately increased pitch and announced 'stick shaker'. A quick scan to the ED (engine display) and I identified that the ref speeds were still increased and told the Captain; at that time he turned it off. We continued the remainder of the approach without incident. I am filing this report after being notified by Manager of Flight Safety that he recommended an ASAP should be filed. Lesson learned for me as a FO (First Officer) is that I should have verified that the ref speed switch was turned off after verifying that the icing was no longer present. And then again when I noticed the Vso speed was higher than I anticipated; I should have cued in that something wasn't set properly.

column121: After passing waypoint JILNA on the RNP Z RWY 30L a scratch pad message showed; check altitude constraint. Cross checking the altitude the airplane was at; it was descending through 6800-6900 MSL and the altitude restriction is at 7000. JILNA was a melded waypoint on our flight plan off of the BRIXX 2 RNAV Arrival; that had been melded once we were cleared for the approach. Breakdown in cross check during higher demand phase of flight. While also forgetting about pilot bulletin XXXXX-X. Which pertains to this situation. Section XX of the bulletin states: VNAV not complying with altitude constraint during approach. When the last waypoint of a STAR procedure is common to the first waypoint of the instrument approach procedure; the FMS may automatically meld the waypoints. The altitude constraints contained in both procedures for that particular waypoint is expected to be melded as well. In other scenarios; the last STAR waypoint contains a 'fly vectors to intercept' leg. This waypoint can also be the first one for the approach procedure and the pilot may manually delete the 'fly vectors' or copy the approach waypoint of the same waypoint on the STAR; which will also meld both waypoints.Embraer has received reports of the airplane not complying with the published altitude constraints during transition from STAR to instrument approach procedure. Investigations have revealed that an altitude violation may occur when two common waypoints are melded; either automatically by the FMS or manually by the pilot; where the last waypoint of the STAR and the first waypoint of the approach procedure has the same name; but different altitude constraints. As a consequence; the FMS constructed vertical flight path may not respect the altitude constraint; despite the altitude constraint indications of the FMS FLT PLAN page; VSD (Vertical Situation Display) and PFD (Primary Flight Display) are correctly displayed. The VNAV guidance may result in a constructed flight path above or below the published constraint. To prevent the violation of an altitude constraint; after a common waypoint is melded automatically by the FMS or manually by the pilot; perform as follows; Delete the altitude constraint at the melded waypoint; manually re-insert the altitude constraint in the FMS.
