column121: This is now the 4th Practice Approach (PA) issue we have documented in the past 30 days. ZZZ1 (TRACON) continues to not coordinate PA according to our LOA. They are introducing risk to the NAS by leading pilots to believe they are cleared for a PA and creating uncertainty. LC (Local Control) would have time to anticipate and better accommodate this pilot if ZZZ1 would have simply coordinated this procedure. Due to ZZZ1 not following their requirement in the LOA they are jeopardizing safety and not providing a good service to the flying public. We must get the answers to the following questions before we can move forward. Why did ZZZ1 issue a Practice Approach Letter to Airmen (PALTA) about 3 years ago? What are the reasons for it not reinstating the PALTA? Why was the workforce not briefed or trained on this change? What are the pros and cons of not having a PALTA?On (date); our General Manager signed the now expired PALTA in question. There were many requests from HQ for PA clarification and interpretations on this very topic throughout the past decade.Since the LTA expired on date; we have gradually started to see a thread of confusion among pilots and controllers. The very purpose of having a PALTA was to prevent this from happening. This has led to misinterpretations of many rules within the 7110.65 and the 7110.3. ZZZ ATCT has compiled over 10 falcon replays over the past 10 months that references this very trend and ZZZ2 ATCT has been experiencing similar issues as well. I would like those who review those replays to listen to the disappointment and frustration from the pilots. We are wasting their valuable time and not providing them with the best possible service. It must be clear there are only 3 possible separation rules that can be applied when handling a PA request. These rules are not to be applied by CPC choice at any given moment. 2 out of the 3 rules are defined on whether or not there is an effective LTA that lists your facility. With that being said; CPC and pilots must be trained and aware of these differences. Separation Rules (7110.65) a.1 [states that] IFR aircraft practicing instrument approaches must be afforded approved separation in accordance with Chapter 3; Chapter 4; Chapter 5; Chapter 6; and Chapter 7 minima until the aircraft lands; and the flight is terminated; or the pilot cancels the flight plan. For this argument we have not discovered any issues when complying.[Section] a.2 [states that] where procedures require application of IFR separation to VFR aircraft practicing instrument approaches; IFR separation in accordance with Chapter 3; Chapter 4; Chapter 5; Chapter 6; and Chapter 7 must be provided. Controller responsibility for separation begins at the point where the approach clearance becomes effective. Except for super or heavy aircraft; 500 ft. vertical separation may be applied between VFR aircraft and between a VFR and an IFR aircraft.The issue we have is some CPC are still following a.2 and some a.3. We must train our workforce on which they are to follow; which is dictated on whether or not we have an LTA. We can't allow them to apply a mixture of both these rules. For 2 years our effective LTA has dictated and conditioned pilots; instructors; students; examiners; and CPC that we must follow a.2. This basically implied to all users that they were going to be treated as if they were IFR doing a VFR PA. This was implemented to give VFR pilots and instructors the full experience so they can perfect the instrument approach procedures they are trying to practice. Various air traffic incidents have indicated the necessity for adoption of measures to achieve more organized and controlled operations where practice instrument approaches are conducted. Practice instrument approaches are considered to be instrument approaches made by either a VFR aircraft not on an IFR flight plan or an aircraft on an IFR flight plan. To achieve this and thereby enhance air safety; it is company policy to provide for separation of such operations at locations where approach control facilities are located and; as resources permit; at certain other locations served by ARTCCs or parent approach control facilities.We need to understand that a.2 is simple for all users involved and reduces risk to the NAS. Not to mention it provides a great and safe service to the flying public. The experience we afford the users when utilizing a.2 enhances safety; achieves a more organized and controlled operations where practice instrument approaches are conducted.[Section] a.3 [states] where separation services are not provided to VFR aircraft practicing instrument approaches; the controller must instruct the pilot to maintain VFR; advise the pilot that separation services are not provided; and provide traffic information or advise the pilot to contact the appropriate facility.The implementation of a.2 and the LTA requirement was to detour facilities from using this rule if they have existing resources. This rule creates confusing and is why we are now seeing unsafe trends since our LTA expired about a year ago. Some CPCs are following this rule and are lead to believe that no coordination is needed because they are not clearly them for the approach as they would when utilizing a.2. Some CPCs are still following a.2 and some are doing a mixture of both. We must train our workforce on which procedures must be followed when handling PA. We also need to brief the flying public as well. If ZZZ1 is not going to reinstate the LTA; all the LOAs with the towered airports listed in the LTA must be amended with detailed procedures on dealing with PA approaches; which include coordination requirements. I'm certain Mission Support would support my argument that we should follow a.2 and reinstate our LTA to achieve more organized and controlled operations where practice instrument approaches are conducted. The volume of VFR traffic in the ZZZ1 is on an upward trend and the last thing we need is uncertainty. We also must ask ourselves; why do most all other major approach control facilities in the NAS choose to issue a PALTA to enhance safety? Having the ability to issue a PALTA was given to us to migrate the very issue we are now having. By not choosing to issue a PALTA; ZZZ1 is jeopardizing safety by not creating a more organized environment where PA are being conducted. We understand that the approach control ATM has the discretion on whether or not they need to issue a PALTA however it is important that the workforce understands why they are making this decision. Especially when the data shows that we are now having mass confusion among pilots and CPC. A PALTA will mitigate this confusion immediately. Choosing to not reinstate an LTA on the sole purpose of pilot and CPC complaints is ludicrous and negligent. We did not see this chaos when the LTA was effective.   Over the past 2 years ZZZ ATCT has noticed and documented a trend with PA and other facility are starting to notice as well. Our workforce and all the users involved deserve the best possible service and training. Why are we choosing not to provide pilots with a.2 service and waste their valuable time with a subpar service (a.3) instead? I'm asking that the national recurrent training team design a course on this very argument.

column121: On Arrival; ATC left us high. We were setting up for the approach and dropped in at the same time. I used flight spoilers on the descent due to being kept high by Approach Control. Pilot Monitoring was aware that we were dropped in and was monitoring my usage of spoilers the whole time. There were no checklist errors or airspeed errors and because this was at night into surrounding high terrain I made sure to observe altitude constraints and intercept the ILS approach outside of FAF. I briefed the FO and there were no questions. The approach went very well. Airspeeds were normal and so I believed were aircraft configurations. Though we had been rushed by being kept high. At no time did I or my FO ever notice anything out of the ordinary nor did my FO announce that we were not configured. On short final we received a spoiler deployed caution message. This occurs at 300 ft. AGL. The spoilers were only partially deployed still. They should have been stowed by 1000 ft. AGL per company policy. I stowed the partially deployed spoilers immediately and the message went away. The remainder of the approach was uneventful and I touched down in the touchdown zone normally and it was a nice landing. At no time was the aircraft unstable on the approach. Afterwards I was congratulated for the nice landing by the FO. At no time did the Pilot Monitoring alert me that the spoilers were still partially deployed. I noticed the caution message at the same time. I took the corrective action and stowed them as the aircraft completed the approach and landing without incident. At no time before; during; or after the event was I alerted by my FO that there was a problem. At the debrief after the flight; he made no mention of his part in also not noticing the flight spoilers were partially deployed. My error was to leave the flaps partially deployed due to a high workload. I stowed them once alerted and at no time was the aircraft unstable or in a dangerous position regarding airspeed or control on the approach. It has been brought to my attention that this was reported by my FO without sharing his concerns or any concerns with me regarding this event even though he also was at fault in his Pilot Monitoring duties. I feel as though while this was a configuration error per company SOP's it is not a safety report and should not have been filed as such by my FO. I had concerns over my FO's performance over the course of our two trips together. He had programmed an earlier approach incorrectly which I had to correct [while] turning final. He had read back incorrect headings to ATC on one occasion and had failed to stow the thrust reversers after landing at the end of one flight. With all that being said; I worked those things through with him and talked about them with him. Perhaps he felt a little insecure about those issues and this was his way of 'getting back' at me. I certainly wish he would have discussed this occurrence with me as I had with him out of a sense of mutual and constructive support and respect. Communication is always the best policy. I wish my FO had brought his concern to my attention; as I did with his previous errors; constructively.
column123: Configuration of Flight Spoiler Caution message was received at 300 ft. on short final at ZZZ. Captain was flying and forgot to stow flight spoilers after ATC left us high on arrival. At 11;000 ft. and 20 miles west of the airport; we had to hustle down. Captain forgot to stow flight spoilers after turning final for Runway 24. Caution was noted by both crewmembers. Flight spoilers were stowed and an uneventful landing was made. I believe a good possibility for this error was because of not ensuring the 'IM SAFE' checklist was satisfactory. At the end of our trip last week; the Captain received sudden notice that a fellow co-worker passed away that he was close to. I observed the impact to the Captain's emotional state; confronted the Captain and asked if he was safe to complete the rest of the trip. He insisted that he was. Finishing our last few legs; I could tell the Captain wasn't into the game and I was pointing out minor errors and missed tasks which he thanked me for and acknowledged. The Captain concluded our trip but stated he would be spending the next several days traveling to the coworker's funeral and would be back in time for our next trip; which commenced today. After the first trip I contacted professional services to report my concern for the Captain's safety condition for our next trip that was quickly approaching. Today; the Captain and I greeted at the gate and he indicated he had gone to the funeral; as well as traveled home; and also traveled back to work within a span of 3 days. I was hopeful the Captain might take some time off. However; he did not and showed up for work today; still contending with the death of our coworker. Our flight to ZZZ was uneventful until short final; which is when we received a Caution Flight Spoiler Configuration message at 300 ft. AGL. The Captain stowed the spoilers and we landed uneventful. I am filling out this safety report because I feel I did my due diligence to show my concern for my Captain in a respectful and professional manner; hoping he might excuse himself from a trip to ensure his safety condition was back to 100%. That may have been a contributing factor to forgetting the flight spoilers on a VFR approach and landing tonight.
column125: CRJ flight crew reported stowing the spoilers late on short final as well as communication issues between both pilots.

column121: Day 1; leg 1 of a 4 day trip from ZZZ to ZZZ1. I was the pilot monitoring on this leg. Had not flown much in the last few weeks. Captain was PF. We flew to ZZZ1 at a pretty low altitude; I believe it was 14;000 feet cruise altitude. After starting the descent; we did not realize we had passed 10;000 feet so quickly; and noticed our speed was beyond 250 knots. The Captain immediately slowed the descent rate and airspeed; in order to return to 250 knots.We were distracted. We were on the first leg of the trip; had woken up pretty early; and the fact that we cruised at such a low altitude were possibly all factors. We did not pay enough attention in the descent.I should have kept better monitoring skills during the descent and avoided distractions.

column121: While doing pattern work; I was number two to land following a Citabria; I believe; which was doing a touch and go; and the spacing was close; but I was certain there was enough spacing for the Citabria to take off prior to my landing; but the tower issued a go around to me. When I reached down to retract the flaps I lost sight of the Citabria momentarily and when I reacquired it I was closing on it very fast; just left of my course. I immediately made a right climbing turn to avoid the Citabria.The tower gave me traffic that was in the right pattern; I had originally been using the left pattern; and I acquired that traffic and was instructed to follow it; and completed a touch and go. After the event; my passenger said he thought he had heard the controller instruct me to turn left on the go around. It's possible; but my passenger had just said something to me as the controller was issuing instructions and I missed that.It appeared the primary tower controller was a trainee; we could hear someone in the background instructing him to correct instructions to aircraft in the pattern and approaches. This whole event could have been avoided had the controller not instructed me to go around when there was enough spacing to avoid a conflict with the departing aircraft.

column121: I'm not sure if; or how many errors I had; but I'm submitting this report for consideration. Everything described below happened within about 3 minutes and it wasn't until the dust settled about a half hour later I had time to reflect on things and wonder what just happened. I took [position name] Radar at or around XA:57z. I was working all TRACON airspace combined; no hand-off; no radar split and no CIC to help. I was all things TRACON. Sector was busy with; I don't know; 12; maybe 15 flights of aircraft talking to me. Everything fell to pieces 5 minutes after taking the position.Aircraft X [flight of 2 VFR] were returning from the Range Complex from the south; set up for about a 10 nm left base; coming fast; at 310 kts to beat Aircraft Y straight in for RWY XXR. I vectored Aircraft Y on a 270 Hdg down to 7000 (6500 mva) for sequencing to follow the Aircraft X [flight].  At or around XB:02Z; Aircraft Z in the southwest practice area; about 12 - 15 nm southwest of ZZZ called me but was blocked. He called again and stated he is 'Priority inbound; my engine just let go'. I told him to enter left traffic RWYXXR; I called Ground Control on the override; told them to roll the equipment for Aircraft Z emergency inbound had lost his engine. I tagged Aircraft Z as an emergency and then started issuing traffic to Aircraft Z to follow the Aircraft X flight; about 8nm straight in. I knew I needed to slow the problem down; and began by slowing my Aircraft A on north downwind to 180kts. I slowed Aircraft Y and all the other traffic in the queue to 180 kts and began vectoring people wide for sequencing. I looked about 15 nm east and realized Aircraft Y was at 6100 in my 6500 MVA; and descending! I told him to maintain 6500 (forgot to say low altitude alert). He acknowledged and descended to 5700. I told him to climb immediately to 9000 no delay through 8000. I needed to break him out to the south because it was going to be a three-way tie with him; Aircraft X and Aircraft Z. I had to get him above my MVA's to break him out to the south for re sequencing.Without benefit of reviewing the tapes to hear the audio; I'm very sure Aircraft Y was never cleared for a visual approach; but maybe I did and that was why he was so low.  Again; I don't have tapes to listen to and there's no management in the building to help me answer that question. Did I miss him read-back a wrong altitude? At some point in this fiasco; I realized the Aircraft Z way too fast (280kts) and catching the Aircraft X flight and told tower to move the Aircraft X flight to the south runway; 'Aircraft Z does not have them in sight'.By now; Aircraft Y was at 6000 slowly climbing but still in the face of Aircraft Z; 5 nm opposite direction at 6000; talking to tower with the Aircraft X flight are on short final. Next thing I know Aircraft Z is basing at 5000 and the Aircraft X flight are in the flare.  Aircraft Z had his hands full and mis judged his approach so he made S-Turns or a right 360 on final to get down and land.  I got Aircraft Y out of 7000 and turned him Heading 230 for sequencing following another flight of Aircraft X flight on a dogleg to follow the Aircraft A on the north downwind and another commercial aircraft straight in. All the fighters and air carriers parked north side and wanted RWY XXR; but I knew it would close behind the Aircraft Y so I changed them all to the south runway. I got everyone in line; speed controlled; cleared and visual separation applied.I switched Aircraft Y to the tower earlier than I would normally when I saw the Aircraft Z starting to base to about a 3 mile final and put him on the tower for landing clearance. I don't think I ever got a response from him that he had Aircraft X flight in sight. I was very rattled; in damage control mode and trying to fix the air carrier too low on final where I didn't expect him to be.Now that I had time to think about what just happened. Here's what I don't know:1.) Did Aircraft Z have Aircraft X in sight and pilot applied visual separation? He flew around a bit on  final and maybe even 360'd; was above the Aircraft X flight altitude at all times; was never behind them on final but did overfly the south final to maneuver north of it for XXR. 2.) Did I clear Aircraft Y for Visual approach?  I don't think so because I was intending to have him follow Aircraft X flight and I didn't even have time to try and tell him about Aircraft X flight yet. Did he read-back an incorrect altitude when I told him 7000? If he did; I didn't catch it.  Did he bust his altitude assignment? I believe he did because I wouldn't have issued such a low altitude in that 6500/7100/8000/9000 area on our final extending out from 20 nm. What was he doing so low in there? Well; for starters; I should have said 'LOW ALTITUDE ALERT' to Aircraft Y when he was at 6100' in my 6500 MVA. It was just unreal; I couldn't believe what I was seeing. What was he doing below 6500 there? I didn't clear him yet! I told him to climb; he acknowledged yet he descended further to 5700'!  I was incredulous and told him to immediately climb through 8000 to maintain 9000. He finally understood that and did what I instructed. Once Aircraft Z dropped his situation in my lap; I immediately and with out delay coordinated with the tower; did an 'EM' Slew/Enter so they could see he was the emergency and modified the SPAD for the north runway. I had data tag over-lapage (multifunction O/E doesn't work at TRACON so our auto-offset feature didn't do anything to keep data blocks from overlapping) and I didn't realize Aircraft Z was SO fast on a 2nm downwind. With the Aircraft X flight on a 5 nm straight in that math wasn't gonna work. He was catching them FAST and I pointed out traffic; he said 'looking'. I know he was busy and in a bind and I needed him to see those Aircraft X flight. I tried to fix it; saw that Aircraft Z was in position; already basing; needing a landing clearance; not going to physically catch the Aircraft X flight so I dumped him to the tower so I could try and fix the Aircraft Y who was not doing anything I wanted; needed or expected him to do. Plus; I had 12 other pilots going places and calling me all this time too. Everything transpired in 3 minutes; from the time I understood Aircraft Z was in trouble to the time where he was on final and I could deal with the other traffic.There's nothing that can be done 'to prevent a re-occurrence of this event'. You can't anticipate emergencies. That said; I should have issued a low altitude alert; and I should have had the Aircraft Z call the Aircraft X in sight or break the Aircraft X flight off final; sterilize everything and let the Aircraft Z do his thing. Also; this very same Aircraft Z has pulled this stunt on me in the past. He called with an engine failure; bombing into the left downwind at 280-300 kts. Prior to this incident; I didn't have other traffic to conflict with him. This was a rapidly devolving situation I did my best to manage and in retrospect realize a couple lapses in my control actions. I was just working the problem in the moment; trying to make planes miss and not overly concerned over 'words'.

column121: On short final into ZZZ Runway XXL; we received a GPWS 'Too Low Terrain' warning. We were on glideslope and on PAPI (VMC conditions). We executed a go-around and as we leveled off we received several error messages in the scratch pad [including] 'GPS L incorrect' and a 'Runway/Approach CRS ERROR'. The Captain was flying and could not get NAV data to display on his PFD (Primary Flight Display). He also was unable to tune the ILS frequency for the subsequent approach. We consulted the checklist; then transferred the controls to the First Officer whose displays and NAV data appeared to be reading correctly and hand flew an approach in VFR conditions to a landing without incident. There was a lot of confusion with multiple errors occurring in the cockpit. The GPWS that sounded while we were on the glideslope during short final seemed to be erroneous; but then again with all the errors with the NAV data on the Pilot Flying side; maybe his glideslope was incorrect. In the end; going around and troubleshooting put time on our side and allowed us to determine the best course of action.
column125: A B737 pilot reported several erroneous warnings and display errors on the Captain's side; who was Pilot Flying; during short final. A successful go-around and landing was performed by the First Officer.

column121: We were just outside the MNARK waypoint descending via the TBARR1 into Denver when I requested an unrestricted descent in order to expedite down through some turbulence. ATC granted my request and cleared us to descend and maintain 14;000 FT. I took the aircraft out of the managed descent mode and used open descent to descend to 14;000 FT. ATC subsequently amended our clearance as follows; cross BDIVN at or above 14;000 FT then descend via the TBARR1. The next waypoint after BDIVN is KUSHH which has an altitude restriction of 14;000 FT. The Pilot Monitoring (PM) put 13;000 in the FCU as the bottom of the TBARR1 is 13;000 FT at LDORA which is the next waypoint after KUSHH. I acknowledged the change to the altitude in the FCU but failed to put the aircraft back into a managed descent. We were between BDIVN and KUSHH when Denver Center switched us over to approach. As the PM checked in with approach I was expecting the aircraft to level off at 14;000 FT in order to meet the restriction by KUSHH. Just as the aircraft approached 14;000 FT two things happened simultaneously. One; I realized the aircraft wasn't leveling off as I expected; and two; approach cleared us to descend to 10;000 FT and turn left to a heading of 040. I don't believe there was an altitude deviation but I was certainly very fortunate that approach gave us a lower altitude when they did.
column123: On TBARR1 arrival asked for expedited decent to 14;000 feet to get out of moderate turbulence. I put 14;000 feet in altitude window; captain started open descent. Next ATC transmission was a decent via following BDIVN intersection. I put 13;000 in altitude window. Approaching KUSHH intersection captain realized plane was not going to capture altitude to cross KUSHH at 14;000 feet. Managed descent was not selected. At same time got ATC frequency change and new instructions heading 020 descent to 10;000. I don't think we went below 14;000 feet; but would have if not given new ATC instructions while in an open descent Mode.

column121: Upon being cleared for the visual approach to XXR ZZZ; Pilot Flying set FAF altitude and continued descent from 4000 ft. to 2400 ft. PM (Pilot Monitoring) contacted tower to inform them of their flight path: turning base [runway] XXR. The crew turned base inside of the FAF; and therefore continued descent to an intermediary altitude appropriate for the glide path intercept inside FAF. In the base turn; the aircraft aurally alerted to a group of antennas in the vicinity of our flight path. As we were already in the base; we continued in the base without interruption and continued the approach to land XXR. The cause was an EGPWS aural alert to man-made group of obstacles; antennas; in the vicinity of flight path. Crew suggest [increased] distance in the turn from proximity of antennas to prevent future aural alerts from EGPWS.
column123: Flying the visual backed up by the ILS XXR; we were cleared for the approach; turning right base for the XXR runway during VMC; crew was aware of the set of antennas in the vicinity; however the GWPS was trigger issuing a 'Caution Obstacle' alert. Pilot Flying increased rate of turn as a preventive course of action to stay south of the obstacle. The crew safely remained out of the path of the manmade obstacle and landed without any interruption. EGPWS ALERT of the antennas in the proximity of the field Increase lateral distance from obstacles to prevent alerts from anything around or in the EGPWS to avoid situations like this.

column121: I read the company e-mail concerning Runway 16 at AVL being closed. At the time I didn't realize I had a trip coming up to go there. During the flight over to AVL; my FO (First Officer) and I looked over the special airport qualification pages for AVL. Pages indicated that the closed Runway 16-34 was completely torn up; and just dirt. I forgot the e-mail I had read that stated that the runway was actually almost finished. It was hazy in the AVL area. I had not landed there in several years; so we decided to do the RNAV approach to Runway 17. I expected to see just one runway as shown on Jeppesen 19-3 chart. We got the airport in sight and the Tower cleared us to land. When fully configured and below 1;000 AGL; Runway 16 was much more prominent than Runway 17. We both thought 16 was 17. Both of [us] saw a small light flash on the runway that looked like an aircraft taxing onto the runway. I saw the flash again; and it looked like a very small 'X'. I reported that to the Tower; and they instructed us to go around; which we did. After talking to the Tower and being vectored back around we saw our error; and successfully landed on Runway 17. I blame myself for not putting the information together and noticing that there was a discrepancy between what the e-mail stated and the Jeppesen charts. It would be nice if Jeppesen could keep their airport qualification charts updated.
column123: We were going to Asheville North Carolina which was my first time going there. I asked the Captain to request the RNAV approach going into Asheville since there were mountains and I'm not familiar with the airport; we have reviewed the special airport on the Jepp chart which was showing one runway and the runway [that] was shown was much torn up and under construction. When we were on final; I noticed there was an X on the runway which I thought was an airplane. I did not see another runway next to it even though there was. But the runway that was next to it; was very small; narrow; short and nothing like the runway [that] was showing on the [Jepp] chart. I realize that's not an airplane on the runway but it was an X flashing. I asked the Captain to let Tower know what's going on. Tower said go around; and after completing the go around procedure Tower has informed us that the main runway is closed and we are supposed to land on the adjacent runway. The adjacent runway is nothing like what was showing on the special airport chart. The picture that was showing in the special report chart is very different than what is in real life. People need to be aware that the predominant runway is not the runway that is used.
column125: Air carrier Flight Crew mistook a runway at AVL Airport under construction as an active runway.

column121: I was conducting a flight training event with my student pilot. We just returned to the airport to practice performance takeoff and landings. We had just made a full stop - taxi back. We called holding short that we were ready for touch and goes. We were told to hold short for landing traffic. Several planes land and depart. Both my student and I hear what we believe to be our abbreviated tail number being cleared for takeoff. We read back cleared for takeoff instructions; finish final items on the checklist; and take the runway. On our takeoff roll we hear Tower ask who is on the runway and I respond with our tail number. Tower then advises to either exit the runway or begin our roll. We were already on the roll; so we continued the takeoff. After our lap in the pattern we terminate the training flight and contact the ATC number. I believe I heard our tail number and believed it was a takeoff clearance. However; due to a high volume of traffic the morning of the event due to a lengthy TFR that lasted most of the day; there was a lot of training and jet traffic. I could have heard a similar call sign and my student could have stepped on the intended recipient of the ATC instruction. I know when there is a high volume of traffic waiting to arrive and depart it could be best to use my full tail number to lessen confusion; and take extra measures to ensure all radio communications are properly received and copied.
column123: Aircraft X called up requesting pattern work during a high traffic volume session and was given hold short instructions; due to the fact that there was an IFR arrival jet on about a 3 or 4 mile final. Aircraft X subsequently read back his hold short instructions. I talk to 3 different aircraft before I cleared Aircraft Y for a touch and go. Apparently Aircraft X read back 'Aircraft X cleared touch and go' and then subsequently got onto the runway without me catching it. Aircraft Y clarifies their touch and go clearance and I canceled the touch and go clearance and gave them a left 360. During this time inside the cab my CIC/GC/FD was trying to clarify information about a TFR that was supposed to be going into effect shortly and we had received no prior information on. This resulted in several phones ringing during this already busy time. After scanning the runway I saw an aircraft who appeared to be holding on the runway with my jet traffic now within 2 miles of the runway; since I had multiple aircraft holding short of the runway I tried to quickly identify the aircraft; so I could taxi them off the runway in a timely manner. However Aircraft X didn't identify himself by name until about the 3rd time I reached out to them. With minimum time to react I told the aircraft to taxi clear of the runway or depart immediately. Aircraft X started their departure roll and as soon as airborne I told them to offset the runway so I had sufficient spacing with my IFR aircraft on final. Immediately following my Jet traffic landing the OM entered the cab and started trying to have the CIC help him find our local weather form; so our new trainee could fill it out. We started the day short staffed with no prior knowledge that someone would be coming in to cover for a couple hours; so the rotation wasn't going to happen as expected. When there are less people in the cab we have enough space to work without our masks and due to the fact that there were extra nonessential people in the cab I had to replace my mask; while I firmly believe mask should be required; they do interfere with the side tone which affected my ability to catch Aircraft X's incorrect readback. No one in the facility had received any information on the TFR that was supposed to be going into effect. This resulted in several miscommunications and confusion.
column125: Flight Instructor and Local Controller reported the instructor's flight departed without clearance with traffic on very short final after mistakenly taking another aircraft's clearance for takeoff.

column121: Aircraft X was on landing roll for Runway XXR. I called LC2 (training) to give permission to cross after Aircraft X lands. LC2 had crossed Aircraft Y at taxiway [letter] prior to me calling. Inquiring after LC2 had meant to cross another aircraft for his runway. He called to the wrong aircraft. LC2 Controllers have been training all day and have not had good breaks. I was working all day heavy traffic as well with minimal breaks. We were pushing 100-150 movement hours. Staffing included 2 CPC's; 1 OS and 1 trainee for the morning; a second OS came at XA:00.   We all pushed hours and took minimal or no breaks due to staffing and traffic would not stop.  I told Aircraft X to turn left at Runway Y when I meant [taxiway name and number]; but my mind felt like mush.  I believe the trainee made an error due to fatigue as I was also very fatigued from numerous complex requests today. Training has been pushed on the trainee to 'help the facility.' I believe this has put undue stress on the trainee to save the facility since we only have X working CPC's on schedule currently. The pressure to work long hours maximize time on position and 6 days a week for months on end has taken its toll on us.I recommend adequate staffing for the facility.  I understand we need to push training but pressure should not be on a trainee to 'save the facility;' when this is an agency issue. I recommend management push for good breaks for proper cool downs so we can take on traffic with more of a sound and ready mind. Some controllers take on everything without regard for their selves due to trying to perform for the agency. This is a big safety issue.

column121: I was the FLM on duty in the Tower cab providing OJTI to a CPC at the time of the incident. I was explaining MORs (Mandatory Occurrence Report) and significant events notifications and showing the CPC where to find the contact numbers. Traffic was light and there were only two positions open as per the COVID staffing; LC1/LC2/LA were combined as CD/FD/GC were also combined. I had one other person on a break.While giving instruction and demonstrating required activity for significant MORs to the CPC; I heard the ASDE-X alarm 'Runway XR go-around' I immediately looked up and scanned Runway XR to assess the situation and determine what caused the alarm. I observed Aircraft Y; 1/2 mile final landing on Runway XR and a small GA aircraft on Taxiway [5] turning South on Taxiway [3]. I didn't observe any other targets or obstructions on the runway surface area. At that time Aircraft Y was already flaring out on the runway. I then asked the LC Controller why wasn't Aircraft Y sent around. The Controller responded; 'The other aircraft cleared the runway.' I then informed the Controller if the safety logic says to send someone around on a runway which the aircraft was landing it was mandatory to comply. The Controller advised me that they thought it was safer to land than send the aircraft around. I then informed the Controller that we are obligated to send the aircraft around per the JO7110.65 and if the pilot decided to land anyway then I would have a conversation with the pilot.Remind controllers that the safety logic overrides same runway separation and that it's mandatory to comply with the safety logic instructions. I did have this conversation with my crew after the incident and was shocked to know they weren't aware of this and thought it was a discretionary decision.

column121: Aircraft Y was cleared for an approach and was cleared to land. Aircraft X called Ground to taxi and was given taxi instructions. This instruction should have resulted in Aircraft X coming out of the Ramp and making a right turn on to the taxiway. Aircraft X instead crossed the taxiway and made a right turn onto the runway. When Aircraft X entered the runway; Aircraft Y immediately stated; 'There's something on the runway;' and I initiated a go around. Aircraft Y went around and conducted another ILS approach to successfully land. Aircraft X exited the runway and continued down the taxiway to Runway 07; where they then departed.Aircraft Y was at 300 ft. at a ½-mile final when the go around was initiated. I asked the pilots of both aircraft to contact the Tower and notified them that I would file a report on the event. I did not say the words 'possible pilot deviation' to Aircraft X; as required to issue a proper brasher warning. I also did not get the pilots' names; phone numbers; and certificate numbers; as I did not know that these items were required until I began filling out the report. I did note on the report that the aircraft were both air carriers; and the information was able to be obtained by referencing the callsigns.I believe the weather was a contributing factor to Aircraft X making an incorrect turn; as it was raining moderately with periodic gusting wind. A ground based radar system or a taller Control Tower would have been incredibly helpful in this circumstance; as the taxiway and runway are pretty much indistinguishable from each other at night. The lights of the two blend together and you cannot tell which is which.

column121: We are concluding our last week of COVID scheduling. Normal operations during this reduced staffing period involve two open sectors: Combined Sector Name X (aka; The West Side with Sector 1 and Sector 2) and [other combined Sector Y] (aka; The East Side with Sector 3 and Sector 4). The West Side is generally a little busier because it handles most airport approaches save ZZZ1. The East Side is mostly departures. Splitting up the West Side in order to diffuse traffic would require opening both [Sector 1 and Sector 2] because the '[Combined Sector name] only' traffic isn't very busy. This would require a minimum of 4 CPCs and I only have five to cover the shift. Complexity picked up when 'Controller A' was working; so I went to an unused scope to help assist where needed (make/take hand offs; update data blocks; highlight untracked VFR targets; etc.) During this time of day; the airport was reporting haze and between 4 and 5 miles of visibility. The ILS is out at ZZZ2 and we use the VOR-B which under ideal circumstances works similar to the ILS but aircraft can hang the altitude up higher all the way to the VOR. This is unusual but not unheard of in VFR situations. In fact; to my recollection; all aircraft had executed the VOR-B in a 'straight in' manner which produces a sharper rate of descent. The traffic started to subside and Controller A was being relieved by Controller B. I returned to the Supervisor Desk to attend to some things there. When the controller on [Sector Y] got relieved; he alerted me to the fact that there may have been a separation error over ZZZ2 involving Aircraft X; ZZZ2 arrival on the VOR B and Aircraft Y on the ILS to ZZZ3. When I reviewed the replay; there indeed was a LoSS [Lack of Standard Separation]. Aircraft X remained at 026 until the VOR and then circled to right traffic to land; conflicting with Aircraft Y before divergence. Controller A's expectation had led him not to employ positive separation nor highlight the potential conflict to Controller B in the relief briefing. Further; Controller B never caught the development of the conflict nor issued a Traffic Alert after notified (late) by ZZZ2 Tower as to Aircraft X's intentions. I discussed this with both controller. Also; Controller A has been dealing with personal family issues. He had been given the opportunity to take as much time away from work as he needed; but indicated he wanted to work. He had not shown any outward signs of fatigue or stress in his work; but it should probably be noted.There are no set procedures or training in how to account for this because of the dynamics of the traffic load and who has responsibility to 'stagger' approaches in a manner to ensure separation. For example; a general rule of thumb might be that the [Sector 2] controller will stagger ZZZ2 arrivals for the Sector 1 controller; because the controller has more airspace to work with. But what if the majority of traffic is ZZZ2 arrivals? What if both airports have heavy inbounds? The problem is alleviated greatly when the ILS is put back in service; but when the area if experiencing IFR conditions; a 'go-around' produces the same problem. Because of the airport s' proximity to one another; the mountains to the east and the ZZZ4 BRAVO to the south; there aren't other approach options available.  Adding an emphasis for these scenarios in classroom training should probably be incorporated as a 'trap' to watch for; and mitigation strategies to account for it. This is a known issue; but a long term workable solution has been problematic and always seems to fall back on the controller fighting expectation bias and ensuring standard separation in a complex scenario. Perhaps a long range proximity alert system that helped a controller highlight these potential conflicts specific to this dynamic would help remind controllers that this needs careful monitoring at a minimum; advanced planning and positive separation at a maximum.

column121: I was working Local Control and Tower CIC.  Aircraft Y was on final to Runway X and Aircraft X was ready to depart Runway Y.  I cleared Aircraft X for takeoff and issued a right turn heading 210.  The proper heading for the departure was 120; per LOA.  I transposed the numbers and missed my error on the readback.  After I observed Aircraft X start his turn; he began to enter a cloud layer and I switched him to departure.  A few moments later; I realized my error upon watching the radar.  I issued a traffic call to Aircraft Y on approach and he called Aircraft X in sight.  No TCAS resolution or CA alert were issued for aircraft proximity.I believe the only recommendations I could make to stop this sort of incident from occurring would be to develop Standard Instrument Departures at ZZZ; which I am not sure is feasible.  Also; I believe the COVID-19 staffing needs to be changed; and more personnel brought back to mitigate the lack of for control positions and lack of oversight.  (ZZZ is reverting to a more normal staffing schedule during Pay Period XX).

column121: When cleared for the visual approach for Runway 12L coming in to Saint Louis Lambert airport; I aimed for the numbers starting from the vector I was on. When I got to short final at a slight dog-legged angle I was instructed by Tower to go-around and join a left pattern for Runway 12L. After landing via the left pattern Tower informed me that a new system Saint Louis control was using had flagged my landing and the controller had no choice but to comply and give a go-around instruction. [Caused by] sensitivity of the new system being used by ATC. After the event I was instructed to submit this report in response. The system should be fixed as to not give errant flags. As for what I can do about it; I now recognize that the system is new and is prone to such errors so in the future I will make sure to square off a pseudo base leg in an attempt to avoid being flagged errantly.

column121: I reported on CTAF my final approach to land at 1-mile and 1/2-mile. While on very short final less than 1/8-mile and 200 feet AGL; a Aircraft X announced he was departing and entered the runway from the run-up area (A6). Other aircraft holding in the run-up area called on the CTAF for him to stop. However; the pilot did not acknowledge the CTAF calls and continued to pull onto the runway; apply power; and begin takeoff. I commenced a go-around at about 200 ft. AGL; sidestepped to the right; and announced my actions on CTAF. The Aircraft X continued takeoff roll undisturbed. My sidestep to the right put my go-around quite low over several aircraft operating and holding on Taxiway A. As I climbed out over Taxiway A; I noticed the departing Aircraft X drifting to the right of the runway centerline; and I was closing on the aircraft. To avoid further conflict; after checking for traffic; I turned a sharp right crosswind at mid-field; overflying the transient parking and several airport buildings at about 600 ft. AGL; and reentered the right downwind pattern (standard at BDN). I safely landed without further issue. The departing Aircraft X never made another radio call.This conflict has occurred several times at BDN; but this is the first time I have filed a report.

column121: During the transition to landing in a C-172 at approximately five feet above the runway surface; two helicopters flying in loose formation; were also preparing to land in the grass field just south of the runway at West Point/Middle Peninsula Regional Airport. As the helicopters were making a descending right turn; the rotor vortices apparently were directed into the flight path of the landing runway (Runway 10). The wake vortex caused the C-172 to immediately bank left and climb without any input from the student pilot. At this moment; the instructor took control and executed go-around procedures to avoid going off the runway and impacting the terrain at a high rate of descent just north of the runway. Pilots in all three aircraft were in visual contact with one another. However; it does not appear any of the pilots in any of the aircraft involved were aware of the effects the rotor wash would have on the runway of intended landing. The effects of rotor wash from two helicopters flying in loose formation may have also contributed to the incident.It is my objective that pilots in all aircraft conducting approaches to landing understand the importance of maintaining proper separation from rotorcraft. There is a high volume of rotorcraft traffic in Virginia's Tidewater region. They frequently practice traffic pattern operations at uncontrolled airfields where many students are learning to fly. This incident could have easily resulted in an accident causing serious injury or death under slightly different circumstances. If a student pilot was flying solo; I don't think the outcome would have been the same.

column121: I listened to the AWOS and noted wind at 5mph from 90 degrees with gusting to 10mph. On final approach for landing; I experienced light to moderate wind gusts that bounced the aircraft around a bit. Under those conditions; I decided to limit flaps to 20 degrees and maintain a slightly higher airspeed since the runway length was over 6;000 ft. and gusting was intermittent. Upon crossing over the runway threshold at 65kts; I reduced power to idle and flared 10 feet over the runway. The aircraft floated in the ground effect for a long distance and ultimately when the wheels made contact it bounced up into the air again and floated further in ground effect. At that moment; a wind gust pushed the tail to the left. I tried to correct with opposite rudder but the deflection did not correct the alignment before the wheels made contact with the runway. The aircraft was by then oriented at a 25-30 degree angle to the right of runway centerline. The aircraft then bolted violently to the right and rolled quickly off the runway into the grass; heading toward a rock lined; 4-ft deep drainage ditch just beyond a taxiway. As I attempted to maneuver the rolling aircraft back to the left; it began to skid and tip over to the right. I righted the aircraft to avoid driving the right wing and nose into the ground and potentially flipping the aircraft; but by then my only choice was to either roll toward the ditch or in the direction of hangars to the right. I braked but wasn't able to stop before the aircraft rolled into the ditch.The left wingtip; propeller and landing gear received damage. No injuries to myself or damage to airport property resulted. I immediately contacted the aircraft owner and airport authorities; who arrived to the scene quickly to assess the situation. The airport manager confirmed no damage to airport property. The aircraft owner and airport manager coordinated to make arrangements to remove the aircraft from the ditch.

column121: Aircraft X checked on inbound at 13;000 feet descending to 9;000 feet. I told him to expect vectors for the ILS as that was what was being advertised and issued a heading of 130 and descended him to 3000 ft. He stated he did not want to go southeast bound because of weather south of the field. I was only showing small areas of light precipitation building 15 miles south of the field. After his request I told him an approach to the opposite runway circle to land will probably be okay but I will need time to get it approved as I was busy and a few other aircraft were deviating and one was having some radio issues. He then says he'll take a vector to the southeast for the ILS Approach. Once he is about 10 miles southwest of the field he reports the airport in sight. I clear him for the Visual Approach and call the tower to tell them of the change. I switched him to tower. I noticed him turning and figured he was either turning directly towards the field or deviating for weather since he was complaining about it before. He suddenly made a sharp left turn heading due north and descended to 1;000 feet. The low altitude alert goes off and I called the tower to issue it. They informed me he had lined up for the wrong airport. Tower handed him back off to me and I climbed him the 3;000 feet; re-vectored him for the ILS Approach and he landed safely. The pilot seemed rushed or impatient. I should have verified the azimuth and distance to the field even though he called the field in sight himself. The private airport should be displayed on the main map instead of a random roadway emergency map.

column121: During descent to ZZZ for visual approach; FO PF (First Officer Pilot Flying); CA PM (Captain Pilot Monitoring). Crew called the airfield in sight and was frequency changed to tower. Once on tower frequency; we heard Tower call a Seneca directing them to clear the approach path of runway XX. The next call was to a departing aircraft calling out our aircraft for separation which they did not see us. TWR then called our aircraft saying traffic 12 o'clock 3600 ft. north northeast bound. We responded with leveling and we have them on TCAS. Noting on TCAS the aircraft climbing toward us and it's very close proximity and rapid closure rate; we leveled our aircraft. The approaching TCAS target continued climbing from less than 1;000 ft. below us at our 12 o'clock into our flight path rapidly. TCAS quickly displayed a climb for the RA OF 3;000 fpm. The PF added thrust to TO (Takeoff) power and climbed with the RA at 3;000 fpm. The PM called tower notifying them we were responding to an RA. The TCAS target continued climbing passing 400 ft. directly under the aircraft from the 12 o'clock position to the 6 o'clock position. After the RA was complete; the crew began the descent again coordinating with tower for a right 360 degree turn in order to reestablish the approach. The crew landed on runway XX without further incident. No heavy aircraft loading during climb and no passenger complaints on landing. No PA's were made to passengers [in] reference [to] this event.Frequency changed from approach to tower at 10 miles for the visual without any traffic notifications. Contacting tower we were given a traffic advisory with less than a 1;000 ft. separation descending while traffic climbed with a rate of closure of at least 400 knots.Before being given a frequency change by approach control crews should be given traffic advisories in the area in order to build situational awareness.
column123: While descending into ZZZ out of approximately 3500 ft. MSL; and on about a 10 mile left base for XX; we received an advisory and then an RA to climb. We had been talking to approach and had just reported the field in sight. We were cleared for the visual and advised to switch to tower frequency. The FO (First Officer) was the PF (Pilot Flying). I switched to tower and heard the tower advising another aircraft to stay clear of the approach area. I checked in and was cleared to land. The tower then advised a Seneca that he needed to clear the approach area due to our approach and was advised to turn left. We then received a traffic advisory from the tower that was simultaneous with TCAS traffic alert; followed shortly by a RA to climb. I was looking outside and did not see the conflicting aircraft. The FO advised it passed underneath approximately 400 ft. below us. The entire event took less than 15 seconds as best I can recall. At that point we advised the tower we had complied with an RA and that we were high and needed a right 360 to re-establish on the approach. The tower responded that where we were was class E airspace and that the opposing Aircraft was not under radar control; and words to the affect that we were not in his airspace. He cleared us for the maneuver and again cleared us to land. Once on the ground we advised the tower via ground frequency that we would be filing a report for a near miss.Changed from approach control to tower after reporting field in sight without being given any traffic advisories before the frequency change. Also departing conflict aircraft departing along approach corridor.Approach controller should coordinate with tower to ensure approach path is clear prior to allowing frequency change.

column121: ILS approach into ZZZ. I briefed the approach with course and frequency and confirmed the points in the FMS. Vectors to final were activated and I missed the course was not set on the inbound and I think when I briefed the frequency I was reading the frequency from NAV 2. We turned inbound and not seeing the errors I told the PNF that he had control; but this time we were well above the GS. We notified ATC that we were going to go missed and were given new instructions. After the missed; I reset the inbound course and reset the frequency. I then told the PNF that I had control and the next approach was done to a safe landing.I haven't flown in several months and the other pilot was his second flight in this type aircraft. I believe was a contributing factor on this approach.
column123: While being vectored to the ILS for Runway XX; the crew noticed that the localizer failed to auto-tune when the approach was armed. When it was determined that the course was not going to be captured the pilot transferred the auto pilot to the pilot monitoring side where the localizer did tune correctly. However; by this time the aircraft had flown through the course. The autopilot attempted to capture the course; but completed one or two 'S' turns. The crew agreed that the approach was not stabilized and we indicated to ATC that we intended to go-around and try the approach again. We were given vectors and an altitude and were cleared for the approach again. The following approach to landing was completed without issue.  Crew should have been more vigilant to ensure that the correct NAV frequency was tuned and the decision to transfer the coupled side autopilot could have been made earlier.

column121: I was working Local 2 and Ground Control combined during slow traffic and the airport was in a North flow deice configuration.  The aircraft were landing XXL with med-good braking action reports from pilots.  Aircraft X was given a clearance to land with a med-good braking action report.  Aircraft X rolled quickly pass to high speeds so I figured he was trying to roll to the end quickly.  I instructed Aircraft X to turn left at the end.  At that point he was approaching the end at a good rate of speed.  I sent the next aircraft around as I watched Aircraft X go slightly passed the end of the runway; make a hard left turn back onto taxiway M from M10 taxiway.  Aircraft X verified that he was off of runway XXL so L let the next aircraft continue and land without any issues.  Aircraft X asked to have airport vehicle to check his aircraft in case he had hit any taxiway lights.  The airport closed runway XXL.  I later observed Aircraft X taxi back to the gate on his own power.Other aircraft seemed to be able to make the high speed taxiways without any issue.  Maybe if the pilot came in with a slower approach speed it could have helped him not to roll long and slide.
column123: Completed all briefings prior to approach. We were expecting a VFR approach but briefed like full IFR approach and were prepared for the weather to change. We were preparing for a normal approach and landing to Runway XXL. ATIS Bravo showed three miles and overcast 4100 feet. ATIS BRAVO also advised RCC CODE 5-5-5 for Runways XXR; XXL; XYL. Planned a flap 15 landing. We used conservative number of CODE 4 RCC giving us Autobrakes 2; 1530 stopping margin. In essence 3000 feet; touchdown zone being the most restrictive of the 12;000 foot runway. On final approach;  Tower stated Braking Action Good to RJ in front of us.We landing uneventfully. I believe we landed within 3;000 TDZE; however; I do remember floating a little with 10 knots ice additive. We did not have any markings visible outside center line do to white snow. Decelerated to 60 knots; F/O called 60 knots. I stowed the thrust reversers and pressed brakes to disconnect autobrakes. Nothing seemed unusual at that point. Deceleration rate seemed normal up to that point. Tower stated: 'Roll to end; aircraft behind you two-mile final.'I depressed brakes to further decelerate and realized there was zero braking available. I stated 'No brakes' to F/O a couple of times. F/O immediately told Tower and aircraft behind us went around. I extended full reverse thrust again to try and get aircraft stopped. We drifted about 45 degrees toward intended exit. We never left hard surface of runway/taxiway. I briefed F/O to contact Ground immediately and notify 'NILL BRAKING LAST THIRD OF RUNWAY XXL.'I also had F/O ask for inspection of runway/taxiway lights to make sure aircraft had no damage before we taxied any further. He also notified Company Ops what had just occurred. I briefed what had happened to Passengers on PA. ZZZ Ground Ops notified there were no runway/taxiway lights damaged. We taxied aircraft to gate without any further issues. Maintenance was waiting for us at gate and we described what had occurred. They inspected aircraft and found an actual blue taxiway light laying on top of wheel carriage on right main. There was no aircraft damage noted by the Maintenance inspection. I put a note in logbook and we deadheaded to ZZZ1.I take briefings very seriously. This was not the type of flight that either Pilot was out of loop or just not having a bad day. We were configured at the marker and on speed the whole time on approach. I may have floated a little do to 10 knot increase of VREF. The ATIS reports from ZZZ were RCC 5-5-5. The two previous aircraft reports BRAG. The only difference was; the two previous aircraft exited 2nd high speed. We rolled past the second high speed for passenger comfort. We never expected NIL braking under light snow covered runway in front of us. The airport needs to actually test the runway; if they're going to advertise 5-GOOD. It was one of the worst feelings a Pilot can have realizing he/she has lost effective control of an aircraft. Had I not redeployed the thrust reversers; I believe we would have encountered aircraft damage and maybe even injuries even though aircraft would have been exiting hard surface at a slow speed. I am actually shocked the runway had not been prepared for arrivals with so much equipment and men available at that airport. Very disappointed.

column121: Flying into ZZZ. Because of the weather we setup for a CAT 2 to Runway XXL. I briefed the CAT 2 off the pages. We were cleared for the approach. I armed the approach. CAT 3; dual. I was looking outside to pick up the runway. We got the Land green. I picked up the lead in lights then the runway. I said landing. At approximately 50 feet the aircraft said retard. I retarded the thrust levers. At approximately 30 feet and descending quickly I realize the aircraft was not going to flare. I pulled back on the stick and we landed firmly. We taxied to the gate. I called the maintenance control person and explained what went on. He look at the data. He said there was a 'momentary glitch' in the FMC. I don't know if we received a flare display and neither does the FO. I think when I said landing the FO also started looking outside. We debriefed the approach and landing sequence. I think also so many of our FOs are very distracted because of the virus situation; the furloughs; etc. If we were in the military it would be a good time for a Safety Stand-down. I understand we can't do that; but it is very tough out here for the line pilots; FA; Gate Agents etc. [I suggest] ensuring everyone does their job all the way to engine shutdown.
column123: On descent into ZZZ we were prepared for an ILS approach to [Runway] XXR when the weather was reporting below minimums for the approach. 3;000 RVR was reported and 4;000 RVR was needed. We requested an approach to XXL which has a CAT 2 ILS approach and were vectored around for the approach. The approach was loaded and CAT 2 autoland procedures briefed. The approach was normal breaking out at about 200 feet with blowing snow but fairly good visibility. As we approached the 40 foot flare point I was distracted and not looking at the PFD. The Captain noticed the aircraft was not flaring and turned off the autopilot and landed the airplane firmly.We briefed the autoland approach and I quickly reviewed the required callouts. I was distracted when we entered visual conditions and my sight was drawn outside the aircraft. I missed the flare annunciation and not sure if it was presented. Captain noticed the lack of flare and took the appropriate action landing the aircraft. If I had called out no flare at 40 feet a go-around probably would have been more appropriate. When conducting autoland approaches the First Officer needs to remain focused on the flight mode annunciator during the whole approach all the way through rollout. Emphasis needs to be placed on this importance during the briefing. We rarely conduct autoland approaches and thus can be overlooked along with the natural tendency to want to look up and out the window when going visual on an approach.

column121: Cleared for closed pattern work Runway 21R; PDK XA:50 local time; attempting to modify IFR flight plan which had been annotated from original filing by ATC; advised that communication with Atlanta Departure could only be done when airborne.  Requested closed pattern work to allow communication with Controller.  A request to the Tower granted permission to contact Controller and leave Tower frequency momentarily.  While speaking to [the] Departure Controller; he advised Tower was calling; switched back to Tower frequency while approaching 'R base 21R' and the Tower directed us to immediately turn 90 degrees left; which was done; to avoid close proximity to landing aircraft descending to 21L. Intentions were requested by Tower; responded that we needed to straighten out the anomalies in the ATC amended IFR flight plan; Tower cleared us to land and that they were working with the Controller to deal with modifying IFR flight plan.  Note: The aircraft had a 'new engine with only 5 hours accumulated time (in process break-in).'  The proposed modified IFR flight plan routed the flight across the mountain range to ZZZ at night and was considered a safety risk. It would seem that communications could have been accomplished while on the ground.  Ultimately; the flight plan was corrected and we were cleared to a waypoint of Chattanooga before proceeding to ZZZ (as originally filed).Secondary to communications and closed pattern traffic distraction; PDK airport runway lighting was dim and difficult to identify even in the traffic pattern; until the aircraft is on final and lined up with the appropriate runway/PAPI lighting is ok but - end of runway lighting seems to be less than adequate for pilots not familiar with the airport. This aircraft had a crew of two private pilots searching in a 'sea of lights' for runway identification lighting.

column121: We were on a left downwind for [Runway] XXC at ZZZ at 4;000 feet.  The Captain; who has only flown 1 trip since February 2020 (and done 1 [training] event); was the pilot flying.  It was at the end of a ZZZ1 - ZZZ all-nighter.  Approach Control assigned us to turn left to heading 360.  I acknowledged the heading assignment while the Captain entered the heading in the MCP and pushed HDG SEL which I watched him do.  A few seconds later I realized that we had not started turning; so I pointed that out to the Captain who re-pushed HDG SEL.  We both verified the FMA and the airplane started its turn.  A few seconds later Approach called; said something about being behind the turn and gave us farther left to heading 310 which I acknowledged and the Captain dialed in.  Then a few seconds later Approach called again and asked if there was something wrong with the initial heading assignment of 360 and he mentioned that we went 3 - 4 miles before actually starting our turn for 360.  I answered: 'No; we dialed it in; pushed for heading but it didn't take and it just took us a few seconds to realize it.'  The Controller responded with 'OK' and the rest of the flight went uneventfully.
column123: Final Approach Controller gave us a left turn to 350. I put 350 in the window and pushed heading select. I looked to the left to see if the airport was in sight. I did not verify the FMA. After 10-15 seconds my First Officer said; you're not turning. Immediately reselected heading select; VERIFIED the FMA and verified turn.

column121: While on final to RWY 1R we were following an A350 for 1R.  We elected to remain 1/2 dot - 1 dot above glideslope as the winds aloft and at surface were relatively calm with slightly quartering tailwind.  As we descended below 100 feet AGL we experienced the tell tale signs of wake turbulence.  Luckily the aircraft did not experience any violent pitch or roll oscillations.  A missed approach was executed and we immediately cleared the wake vortices.  We were given vectors for another visual to RWY 1R.  On the second approach we had momentary wake turbulence at 2000 feet MSL following an A320.  As stated earlier the wind conditions being calm at all altitudes were prime for wake turbulence. The main purpose of this report is to draw attention to hazards of wake turbulence.  While we all appreciate efficiency of the national airspace system; lots of separation I feel is the key to preventing these occurrences.  We had noticed the aircraft ahead of us was left and right of centerline on approach and feel there may have been glideslope deviations as well.  This very well may have caused staying high on the glideslope on our part to [be of] no benefit if they were high as well.
column123: While receiving vectors for the visual approach to RWY 1R we were told to follow a heavy A350. The controller notified us we were 3 1/2 miles in trail and to caution for wake turbulence. The Captain (pilot flying) told me he would stay above the glideslope to avoid the wake turbulence. We remained 1/2 dot to 1 dot above the glideslope for the entire approach with no signs of turbulence. As we descended below 100 feet AGL we experienced two rolling moments to the left; both of which were corrected by the Captain. These oscillations were not extreme; but were clearly due to the wake turbulence. As these occurred the Captain called and executed a go around; immediately exiting the heavy aircraft's vortices. We were then vectored around for a second visual approach to RWY 1R; this time following an A320. During the approach a light quartering tailwind was reported. We once again remained slightly above glideslope and only experienced minor turbulence on the approach. This situation would have been avoided with increased separation from a heavy aircraft. The efficiency gained from using minimal separation on landing aircraft does not outweigh the risks of wake turbulence. Even by remaining high on the glideslope we experienced an uncommanded roll at a low altitude.

column121: I was about to begin the flight portion of a lesson with my student. We were holding short of runway; allowing multiple planes to safely land before we had a good opportunity to depart. While holding short; another plane had landed and was about to pull off of the runway onto A3; but there was an Aircraft Y on the other side of the hold short line. A radio call was made asking what the Aircraft Y was doing; and then he taxied out of A3 and Aircraft Z was able to taxi back to midfield parking. After Aircraft Z was clear of the runway; another aircraft landed on Runway 18; did a touch and go; and took off for a south departure. After waiting for about 5 aircraft to land on 18; the traffic pattern was now not busy; there was one aircraft on the downwind. I was positioned to where I could see the upwind; left downwind; left base and final of the traffic pattern; as well as the entire runway and taxiway and determined it safe to now make my radio call announcing my departure. After the radio call was made; it was not blocked; again I visually inspected that the runway was clear and slowly rolled onto 18. It was my student's second flight lesson; and his mother accompanied us in the backseat. While talking him through how to taxi onto the runway; how to takeoff and where to be visually scanning; I performed a normal takeoff; lifting off at rotation speed; the runway was still clear. After about five to ten seconds after rotation; I looked down to see my ground track over the runway and saw Aircraft Y directly beneath me. While maintaining positive control of the aircraft; talking through what I was doing with my student I flew at the speed that safely allowed us to gain as altitude. I made a radio call asking what the pilot in the Aircraft Y was doing and if he had not heard or seen us take off and if he had us in sight at that moment. He did not respond. Then I said 'What the hell dude?' on the radio. Due to how fast everything happened; and trying to remain calm and fly the airplane safely; it was hard to determine exactly how close the aircraft actually was to me. At this point I determined the safest action was to get us as far away from this plane as I could. I continued on the upwind; to where I could not see the Aircraft Y anymore. I was hoping that he had aborted his takeoff. Continuing out and scanning; maintaining a calm demeanor for myself as well as my passengers; I then heard an early crosswind call and knew it was the pilot in the Aircraft Y. Another instructor from FBO said '[name]; what is going on with you today?' A few other aircraft responded 'Agreed;' 'Agreed.' After this I did not want to return to the pattern knowing the Aircraft Y was still flying. I decided to take my student to the practice area to where I could continue my lesson and also monitor the traffic pattern to know when it would be safe to return; as I always do. After about 30 minutes I determined it was safe to return to the airport and land; because I didn't hear or see anyone in the traffic pattern. We landed safely. My passengers did not even notice what was happening until we were almost clear of the traffic pattern; I had told them someone was trying to takeoff underneath us. Still; they do not know the severity of how close it was because of being distracted by it being one of their first flights and not paying attention to the radio calls or doing the constant visual scan. I am extremely fortunate that my passengers and I are alive. This pilot; name; is the definition of unsafe. He chooses to make extremely unsafe actions. I will never fly if I see this man at the airport and will tell every student I have and pilot I see to also not fly if this man is out. If he is not held accountable for his actions; I fear that someone could be tragically hurt. My coworker; another CFI was a witness standing at midfield parking near A3 taxiway; and said it was a miss of 5 feet.

column121: IOE (initial operating) student was attempting a flaps 22 landing. During the approach; gusty winds from outer bands of an approaching hurricane contributed to an unstable approach and generating two glide slope EGPWS annunciations. We initiated a missed approach and were put in sequence to reattempt the approach. The vectors from the second approach brought us in above the glideslope. We engaged the vs (vertical speed) mode initially at 1;200 feet per minute (per GOM) and then 1;000 feet per minute as we closed in on the glide slope and stabilized approach criteria. The student had NAV armed; not APP; which as PM (pilot monitoring) I should have caught. My attention was briefly diverted to reassessing our fuel; which was 200 pounds over final divert at the final approach fix. At 1;000; instruments cross checked; the GS (glideslope) was exact (though not captured). At this point; we had been in visual conditions for several minutes. The runway did not have visual guidance (PAPI nor VASI); I looked out the window to verify the runway was clear; of which I annunciated. At approximately 700 feet AGL; we fell below the glide slope; generating additional glide slope EGPWS annunciations. Due to the winds; fuel nature; incoming weather; and diversion airport expecting similar conditions; I used my command authority to take control of the airplane; reestablish ourselves on the glide path; and land without further incident.

column121: ATC had given us a series of headings to avoid an active MOA. After the headings; they gave us direct CULAB then DIESL for the DRLLR5 arrival into IAH. I was writing the clearance while the captain put the change in the FMS. I copied the correct clearance (direct CULAB; DEISL then the DRILR5) and the captain input direct CULAB then DIESL for the MSCOT4; our originally assigned arrival. A proper cross check was not applied and the change was CONFIRMED. The MSCOT4 and DRLLR5 are very similar coming from that direction and both start at DIESL so upon looking at the first page of the FMS all seemed correct to me. It was not until later in the arrival were we/nor ATC aware we were on the wrong arrival into IAH until around the SUUNR intersection on the MSCOT4. Proper cross checking was not implemented on the arrival change. We did confirm the route change to CULAB then DIESL. A more thorough cross check of the arrival change would have caught the issue. Neither of us caught it and continued the flight to IAH on the MSCOT4; even fully briefing it. ATC said 'no worries' and left us on the MSCOT4 all the way into IAH.

column121: I was acting as an instructor to a CPL who was new to flying the Aircraft X Type. He had spent all of his time up to this point flying the Aircraft Y Type; so I was helping him familiarize himself with the different aircraft. We were doing pattern work while several other aircraft were in the pattern.  Upon getting abeam the numbers and beginning our descent; there was an aircraft on long final (roughly 2-2.5 miles) that we did not hear on the radio.  We continued with our base; and final. The CPL whom I was instructing did not speak very strong english; and was having some trouble with the radio calls in the pattern; and occasionally neglecting to make them. In this instance; he neglected to call his base; and also neglected to call final.  I noticed this only after him turning final without saying anything; and immediately announced our one mile final. This set up a situation where a near miss occurred between us and the other aircraft who was on long final. The aircraft that was on a long final did not call an extended downwind; or a base.  They called final and neglected to give any mileage to indicate they had flown a much larger pattern than usual for a light general aviation plane. The other aircraft noticed us only because I called our final; and immediately broke off his final leg to avoid collision.  I spoke with their pilot after this incident and he said we were slightly above them and cut them off. I believe this incident was caused by a number of factors mentioned above; and could have been avoided with better radio work on the part of my aircraft and the other one on final.  I had a high workload at the moment; which caused my attention to lapse when the CPL turned base without making the call.  However; the pilot on the other aircraft also did not specify that he was purposely flying a large pattern with an extended downwind/base/final.  His failure and mine to accurately give position reports caused the incident.   I also believe that often pilots become complacent and fly a pattern which is far too large at this airport.  There are several times in a week where I have to extend my pattern to a point where I would not be able to make the runway in the event of an engine failure.  Routinely; general aviation aircraft at the airport fly a 2+ mile final; which should be considered a 'long' final.  Like in the incident mentioned; this can cause confusion when someone joins flying a smaller; more appropriate pattern.
