column121: After a diversion to our planned alternate we had a routine enroute and descent phase. We contacted Tower on about a 10 mile final. We were approximately 5 miles in trail of a B737; and we heard them check in as well. I overheard tower tell them about a Medevac helicopter who was operating to the east of the airport and transiting to the west. Once we were on about a 5 mile final; tower provided us with a traffic call for the same helicopter. The first call we received was traffic at our 10 o'clock and about three miles; and that the traffic had us in sight and was maintaining visual separation. Due to the lights of the city; I did not see the traffic; and it was not yet showing up on our TCAS.Inside of a 4 mile final; at approximately 1200 ft. we received a Traffic Alert for the traffic. On TCAS it showed him at 200 ft. above us and inside a mile. I immediately saw the traffic visually and told the first officer that I had the traffic in sight. Based on the traffic's track and altitude; I very quickly suspected that we might receive an RA. I instructed the first officer to go slightly below the glide slope to increase our separation. Very shortly after that; at 1050 ft. we received a 'DESCEND DESCEND' TCAS Resolution Alert. The First Officer followed the TCAS guidance and I informed ATC. Due to the switch at 1000 ft. to Traffic Alert only we never received a clear of conflict; but we were able to visually identify that we were clear of conflict at about 800 ft. We were one dot low on the glide slope but correcting and all other approach criteria were stable so I quickly asked the First Officer if he felt stable. He responded that he did; so we agreed that we were okay to continue the approach. At our closest point; the helicopter was directly above us and approximately 200 ft. above; leading to a significantly reduced margin of separation.This event was caused by several factors. The helicopter pilot had us in sight and was maintaining visual separation; but in our opinion; the separation provided was not adequate. I was not able to visually identify the traffic until the Traffic Alert occurred; and at that point there was very little we could do to avoid a loss of separation. I do not believe we should have been sequenced by air traffic control to pass directly underneath the medevac's flight path. After landing I communicated my concerns to tower and he said that once the helicopter reported maintaining Visual Separation; he had no further responsibility to separate us. If that is correct; I believe it is a fundamentally unsafe procedure. In this event it led to us receiving a descending RA at 1000 ft. leaving us with very little maneuvering room. I should have made more of an effort to find the traffic prior to receiving a Traffic Alert; but with the city lights it was difficult. This was at the end of a very high workload; 3 hour blocked flight with significant weather; holding; a diversion; and fuel considerations. I believe that this type of event could have been prevented with better planning from air traffic control. There was clearly a difference in expectations between the helicopter pilot and us; and the helicopter pilot may have been comfortable with 200 ft. of separation; but we were not.
column123: We diverted due to weather. On final approach we received a TCAS Resolution Alert due to a medical helicopter crossing the final approach. I was Pilot Flying for the approach. When we received the Traffic Alert; I initially assumed it was an aircraft on a parallel approach. The Pilot Monitoring reported the aircraft in sight. The helicopter had also reported us in sight. Only moments before the Resolution Alert did I realize the aircraft was going to cross directly in front. We received a descending Resolution Alert. I complied with the Resolution Alert. The helicopter passed approximately 200 ft. above. I don't recall ever hearing a clear of conflict call. We descended below 1000 ft. and the TCAS went into Traffic Alert Only mode.A medical helicopter was crossing approximately 3 mile final at approximately 1200 ft. while we were on approach. I was manually flying a challenging approach due to high winds and did not recognize the threat until a few seconds before the Resolution Alert.
column125: CRJ900 flight crew reported 200 ft. of separation with a helicopter on approach.

column121: Let me say to begin I am not familiar with Flight Check. By this I mean I never came across it in any of my FAA materials or non-FAA reading; online; or video materials. It was not on any of my private; instrument; or commercial FAA exams. It was never brought up to me on any flight checks or check rides. I have never seen anything written about it in any online materials. No CFI or CFII ever mentioned it to me. I have never seen Flight Check aircraft at my home airport or any other airport I've ever flown into. Yesterday I experienced a conflict with a Flight Check aircraft. Just after I made my routine call for my pattern entry '...6 miles to the northeast inbound for a left downwind...'; a Flight Check aircraft made the call they were on an approach to the same runway as me and that they would need to drive inbound on the final until just short of the approach end for on an obstacle check. We're looking for inbound traffic. Other calls by various aircraft as well as Flight Check and my aircraft were made. One of the flight check calls was stepped on however and was therefore not audible. What is important to note too; is that there was an aircraft 2 to 3 miles behind me following me in. Because Flight Check and I were on an entry for the same runway in opposite directions I felt the situation was beginning to become dangerous; I considered doing a 360 turn at 6 miles out just to give spacing to the Flight Check aircraft coming in from the wrong direction. However; I did not do this because there was an aircraft directly behind me and I felt a 360 might put me into him. Therefore; for safety reasons I decided against it. I felt the safest course of action was to proceed on because I felt a King Air at 6 miles out was going to arrive much sooner than I was at 6 miles out because of his faster speed and because he was doing a straight in and I was doing a standard approach. Additionally; he said he was not going to land but instead 'break off just short of the approach end...'. Lastly; he said he was 'looking for inbound aircraft' which I felt meant he was aware of me and would divert if needed. I also briefly considered the option of extending my downwind but felt that to be unsafe because other aircraft were in the area and I had lost track of exactly where everybody was due to the constant traffic chatter both for ZZZ and other nearby airports. It was becoming stressful and my goal was to fly the aircraft; look out the window; and either land or see and avoid this other aircraft. As our approaches proceeded and I turned final I could see an oncoming aircraft above me ascending and turning south as I was descending to land. As I was about 50 feet off the runway I received a tongue lashing from the Flight Check aircraft and my landing was hard and it took several moments for me to calm myself after parking. Only after going into the Fixed Base Operator and asking about what a Flight Check aircraft was did I understand what had just happened. I now understand; after I did my own investigation; what Flight Check is and what I should do if I encounter a Flight Check aircraft in the future. I will now avoid Flight Check if I ever encounter one. I will advise other pilots of what Flight Check is and to avoid them. What caused the problem and what can be done to prevent a recurrence or correct the problem.The problem was caused by me not knowing what Flight Check is and that they have the authority to fly wherever they want and however they want. I will stay away from all Flight Check aircraft in the future. Flight Checks should be included in NOTAMS. I checked NOTAMS prior to my departure and there existed no such NOTAM regarding Flight Check aircraft that day. ATC; such as Approach did not advise me of any Flight Check aircraft. Flight Check pilots should include such wording as; 'This is the Federal Aviation Administration advising all aircraft to avoid (airport name) until (time).' This should be repeated over the appropriate frequency every 1 to 2 minutes until Flight Check is clear of the area. Include Flight Check information in FAA teaching materials. Include Flight Check information in FAA testing materials. Flight Check pilots should avoid berating pilots on frequency especially just prior to them landing as this can cause an unsafe distraction.
column125: A General Aviation pilot landing at a non towered airport reported a Flight Check aircraft which the pilot was aware of had to take evasive action to avoid them on short final.

column121: I am a flight instructor I was on the ground observing the traffic pattern. I had just sent my student on his first solo flight. There were three Citabrias in the pattern; all same company. My solo student was Aircraft X. Aircraft Z was on ½ mile final. Aircraft Y was on an extended downwind. My student; Aircraft X; was abeam midfield in the downwind. All traffic was in a left pattern. Tower called and told my student 'traffic to follow ahead in the downwind'. My student replied with 'traffic in sight'. My student had mistaken Aircraft Z on final as the traffic he was following; instead and of seeing and following Aircraft Y in the downwind. Tower proceeded to clear my student 'follow that traffic; clear to land Runway XX'. Because my student was following the wrong aircraft; he turned base (about a half mile) in front of Aircraft Y and cut him off. Aircraft Y was on a 1 mile final at the time. Aircraft Y saw the error and made appropriate maneuvers. Tower noticed when Aircraft X had turned final in front of Aircraft Y and proceeded to tell both aircraft to go around. No further events took place. The pilot in Aircraft X did not see or follow the correct traffic and turned base accordingly. He unintentionally deviated from the ATC clearance. The plot of Aircraft Y noticed and took evasive actions as necessary. I was on the ground and all distances are highly estimated.
column123: I was given traffic information by ATC while on downwind leg of pattern; a Aircraft X in the pattern ahead and to my left. I saw an aircraft matching ATC description on short final approach and responded that I had the traffic in sight. ATC gave landing clearance with instructions to follow that aircraft; so when I had sufficient distance behind I began my turn to the base leg of the pattern. Upon turning I spotted another aircraft ahead and to my right at about 500 feet clearance on final approach. I took a hard left turn to evade and was told by ATC to go around and that the aircraft I flew in front of was the traffic I was originally advised of and that I had cut them off. I continued in the traffic pattern and landed without further incident.
column125: A Flight Instructor observing from the ground and their student reported the student turned base leg and followed the wrong aircraft causing a subsequent aircraft to take evasive action.

column121: My student and I were cleared for the RNAV 18 from Tower we were told to report the FAF. At this point we were roughly on a 13 or so mile final. I hear Tower talking to another aircraft that was VFR wanting to practice approaches and also shoot the RNAV 18. Tower then told this aircraft to also report the FAF and advised them there is a Warrior on a 9 or 10 mile final and to look for us. The pilot responded that he does not have us in sight yet. I advised Tower that we are still in fact farther away than 9 miles. We had a strong headwind so our ground speed was pretty slow. Tower said we were still cleared for the option and to still report the FAF at this point we were at the fix half way between the FAF and the IAF. Tower again advised the other traffic that we were an 8 or less mile final and to report and he has us in sight. The Cessna still responded he didn't have us in sight so Tower told them to do a right 360. I then reported that we are .5 away from the FAF. At this point I got a traffic notification and I looked up [to see] the Cessna is at the same altitude off my right nose and passed right in front of me at the same altitude. I then took the controls; banked right and pushed the nose forward. The other pilot [said] 'holy crap I see them now' and I let Tower know we were going off course and low. Tower didn't seem like they were paying attention. Or seem to ask about it. We then continued on with the approach and went missed and Tower didn't ask us about it. When I reported going missed we didn't hear back from Tower till after several calls.

column118: Aircraft Aircraft Damaged
column121: When I started the flight I had a normal taxi and run-up nothing seemed to be out of place. I experienced a normal takeoff with no abnormalities. Once at altitude my student pointed out the fact that [the] flap had become dented during some phase of flight. At this point I decided to stop the flight and head back to ZZZ. Because the flap was dented I decided that I would take the controls and do a flapless landing. When I started to land I put the weight of the aircraft on the main wheels and noticed that the left main wheel was now missing as the left wing started to keep getting lower and lower. I then decided to cut the mixture and come to a complete stop on the runway and wait for assistance. What I think happened is the one of the bolts that hold in the landing gear came loose and on takeoff came undone and took the rest of the landing gear with it. On preflight I did not see anything out of place as well as on taxi and run-up. When we got out the aircraft like I thought there was no landing gear and I made the right call to cut the mixture on the runway. All things considered I think I did the best I could have for the situation; which is minimum damage to the aircraft and no one was hurt.

column121: My instructor and I were on downwind approach for landing. Meanwhile a C-172 had just performed a go-around. We were told they came around at a faster velocity than us; but at nearly our same altitude/path. We later were told (I believe the C-172 instructor told my instructor) that the C-172 was only 100 feet away from us; I believe from above. We had no concrete idea about any of this at the time. We proceeded to land. Afterward; my instructor asked me if I was aware that we were almost in a mid air collision. He had just spoken with the other pilot near the fuel pump. We then looked at radar tracking website to see the activity but we only saw our airplane and not theirs. Pilot from C-172 came into the office to get water (Instructor I believe) and saw us looking at the radar map but didn't seem too concerned about the incident. That's all the information I have.

column121: On an early evening flight to IAH we were descending via the DRLLR 5 RNAV arrival being set up and briefed for the RNAV Y 26R with our altitude set to 6000'. When checking on with the approach controller we were told to expect the runway 26L transition. We selected the new runway in the FMS but failed to reset our altitude selector to 7;000' at that time. As we neared ZOEEE Drag Required appeared on the FMS scratchpad and the Captain said; 'Careful not to be too hot at ZOEEE'. I deployed the speed brakes and slowed us to 210kts and checked the FMA and we were still in VNAV PATH. Shortly after passing ZOEEE; I noticed the FMA showed VNAV SPEED as we were leveling at 6;000' and I no longer showed SKLER as a waypoint on the screen. I then looked more closely at the DRLLR 5 chart and noticed the point we'd briefed as the lowest altitude on the arrival had (Runway 26R) beside it and thought we should've stopped at 7;000'. I brought it to the attention of the captain who then queried ATC. The ATC response was that we were ok and to now descend to 5;000'. We started the descent and shortly after were told by what sounded like a different voice on the radio to maintain 6;000'. We reversed the approximately 200' descent and returned to 6000'.The rest of the approach and landing were uneventful and the flight ended safely at the destination. I believe we should've stopped at 7;000' but am unsure since the controller didn't have a problem with us being at 6;000' so I listed the event as a possible altitude deviation.

column121: ATC cleared us for a visual approach to Runway 16R into Denver after turning us on to a right base approximately 5 miles from MERYN intersection on the ILS 16R. After being cleared for the visual approach the First Officer (FO) selected the approach mode and a selected a slower speed. The FO was slow to turn towards MERYN and over shot the final approach course. I instructed to FO to turn immediately toward the runway and then back to the final approach course. The approach controller asked if we need a vector and I declined. The FO had previously briefed the ILS to 35R. During the approach briefing we discussed threats that included high elevation; an MEL that restricted RNAV approaches; and that we were tired. The flight was cleared to descend via the TBARR 1 RNAV Arrival with the Runway 16R transition. The transition ends at CLFFF intersection at 11;000' with a speed of 210 knots. ATC was informed that we could not accept an RNAV approach and we were told to expect a visual approach; which we backed up with the ILS approach. The flight was turned to a right base heading at 210 knots. While turning the FO requested a slower speed which ATC approved. ATC asked if we had the airport insight. When I replied that we had the runway insight we were cleared for the visual approach. The FO selected the approach mode. As the FO was selecting a slower speed I mentioned that she should turn toward MERYN intersection. She slowly selected a heading toward MERYN and over shot the final approach course. I think this was due to the angle in which the course was intercepted. I would suggest adding a discussion about accepting visual approaches to the approach briefing; as a possible threat.

column121: I had just descended through IMC and was worried about the potential icing and high winds. I hand flew the approach and due in part to lack of recent experience (rustiness due to 2020/COVID); I inadvertently descended to ~2;100 feet MSL between ZZZZZ (IF) and ZZZZZ1 (FAF) on the RNAV XX approach into ZZZ; about 200 feet lower than the 2;300 feet minimums. I recognized my error and climbed back to 2;300 feet by ZZZZZ1 then continued to an uneventful landing. I should not have hand flown the approach given my rustiness--despite having done well in an IPC (Instrument Proficiency Check) a week or so ago--as I was preoccupied with studying wings for evidence of icing in the fast approaching twilight/dusk; and I was fighting strong winds that had me watching airspeed more than altitude.  All in all; it was 100% my fault and reemphasized to me that currency does not equal proficiency.

column121: I had been off from flying since [Month] due to the Covid19 slowdown and my leave over the summer. This was my OE after completing training to get me refreshed and up to speed with line flying. I was definitely a currency and recency of experience threat that was discussed in the cockpit and prior to the top of descent going into ZZZ. After being cleared for the ILS approach to XXR; we were using VNAV and either LOC or LNAV to track the approach course to the runway. There was a lot of haze and smoke from the fires in the area that was obscuring our ability to see the runway.  We flew the approach and eventually selected APPR mode on the MCP with approx 5 miles until touch down. Approach told us to contact tower around the Final Approach Fix. I was almost exclusively focused on flying and failed to cross-check the Captain to make sure he contacted Tower for our landing clearance. Even at the 1;000 foot call; I thought we were most likely cleared to land; and I was so focused flying that I didn't suggest the Captain query ATC. Poor workload management on my behalf. I felt very compressed as the work load increased. I flew a stabilized SOP ILS approach and landed uneventfully on XXR. Upon contacting Ground Control we realized that Tower was never contacted and clearance to land was never received. Ground Control made no mention of this failure and cleared us to our gate.

column121: There was a line of storms crossing ZZZ.  We were told to expect holding at ZZZZZ.  We ended up holding.  We did one turn and then we got sent back on the arrival.  We ultimately got vectors from ZZZZZ1 and were following one Company aircraft.  The plan was to fly northeast about 30 miles at 5;000 ft to go around the weather and then come back south to land.  We couldn't get in front of it or through it.  Company turned around to try to get around the weather from the other direction.  We did the same thing.  We got a 270 heading and a descent to 4;000 ft and an assigned speed of 170 knots.  On that vector; I noticed airspeed decreasing and added some power.  Then the airspeed dropped to approximately 140 knots and the autopilot and autothrottles were disconnected.  I announced to the PM (Pilot Monitoring) that we were in windshear and I added power and started a climbing turn to the left.  The aircraft started accelerating and climbing; and we retracted the flaps.  The highest speed I saw during the recovery was 220 knots.  During this period; airspeed; pitch attitude and altitude was very difficult to control and we encountered what we believed to be severe turbulence.  The PM told ATC that we had severe turbulence and windshear and that we were turning to the left and climbing.  We tried to level at 5;000 ft and flew southwest.  ATC told us to climb to 6;000 ft for traffic.  I added power and the aircraft responded poorly.  Eventually; we got a climb going; but we were unable to stop the climb at 6;000 ft.  The aircraft continued to about 6;450 ft and ATC asked us what altitude we were going to.  PM told them that we were trying to get to 6;000 ft but were having a difficult time maintaining altitude with the turbulence.  On our way back down to 6;000 ft we got a TCAS TA for traffic at our 1-2 o'clock at 700-800 ft above us.  I continued down to about 5;800 ft to make sure we avoided that traffic.  We continued southwest and diverted to ZZZ1. If we had asked to stay in holding until we saw that the line had passed even further northeast of the field; it would have prevented this.  We had about 13.0 of fuel when we entered holding; so we had the time. [We were] unable to maintain assigned altitude; heading; and airspeed due to windshear and severe turbulence.

column121: I was operating flight to ZZZ. The weather was good and after detecting inadequate fuel planning and requesting additional fuel we departed on time and flew the filed flight plan. We were clear direct to ZZZZZ. Our ATIS was showing that we were expecting to land on Runway XX left.Knowing it was early in the morning we did anticipate a possible runway opening of either XX right or XY. When we were about three minutes from ZZZZZ; We were given the instructions to depart ZZZZZ on a heading to intercept the XY localizer. Although we had planned for the possibility of XY; this was a fairly abrupt and last-minute instruction from ATC. As briefed; this falls under the 'slam dunk' which was is always a threat; as I briefed for departure.We departed ZZZZZ on the assigned heading and began to set up for the intercept of the XY localizer. We were given a descent to 9000; then very quickly a decent to 6000; then a speed reduction to 170 and then briefly after that another speed reduction to 160 (until 5 out). All of this seem to happen in [a] very quick sequence. I was set up to intercept; set up to descend and began to slow down According to our instructions.We established our self on the ILS [Runway] XY without incident but when I realized that we needed to slow down; I called for flaps 22 'out of sequence.' (Typically flaps 22 is called with 'Gear Down'). I had already called for flaps nine; and just naturally called for flaps 22 knowing I needed to slow down.My First Officer (FO) followed the command and put in flaps 22. I naturally look down and scanned my instruments as I always do after a change command and I immediately realized that we were above the 200 knot threshold. I immediately took action - I pressed in the TCS (Control Steering) button; pitched up and began to slow down. As quickly and as safely as I could I brought the aircraft under the 200 knot limitation and completed the rest of the flight without incident.Upon landing I called my dispatcher; explained what happened; maintenance came out; they completed a full inspection and downloaded the information from the airplanes computer. We swapped airplanes so that the inspection could be completed and by the time we landed in ZZZ1; I had heard back from the maintenance supervisor. He told me that the inspection was completed no defects were found and the airplane was returned to service.I detected the problem. I always do a brief scan during critical phases of flight to confirm that the command and input was followed. So I did my scan after calling for Flaps 22 and immediately realized the error. I did not alert FO; as it was not necessary and I simply made the adjustments to the pitch and speed by using the TCS button. I feel there were 2 factors contributing cause to this event. The first was the additional time we had to spend on considering our planned fuel situation. This was definitely an added concern that both FO and I collaborated on before we even left the gate. Then; as Runway XY was opened and we received the 'Slam Dunk' vectors onto the ILS XY; was a second major contributor to this situation. My suggestion as to how to avoid this situation is to simply avoid the 'slam dunk' vectors. Had I asked for better vectors; I'm certain that this situation could and would have been avoided.

column121: The flight from ZZZ1 to ZZZ was dispatched under our new fuel management program. The 1 hour and 25 minute flight was dispatched with planned fuel of 5830 lbs. The FO and I both noticed and mentioned that the landing fuel numbers were very low for our standard operation. The NAV Log section of the release showed 1;829 lbs. which is planned fuel minus taxi and fuel burn. But the fuel calculation section showed 1;660 lbs. This concerned both the FO and I; as such a long flight with very little fuel to be landing with. We both discussed that the airplane was [under] fueled by at least 200 lbs. Therefore we both felt landing with 2;000 lbs. or worst case scenario 1;800 lbs. was low but a safer and acceptable number. The performance manual shows around 1;700 lbs. at 5;000 ft. is around 45 minutes of fuel. Knowing our fuel was tight; we did what we could to help conserve fuel by delaying the second engine start as long as possible; and we attempted a Flight Level Change climb by several and normal intermediate level off altitudes with only 7 miles in the opposite direction. We continued to monitor our fuel burn. Upon our initial descent; we noticed we had burned more than planned; but we had received a shortcut and still calculated landing with around 1;800 lbs. On a few turns before final; the right fuel gauge began to flash amber at us then continued to flash in and out as fuel moved around throughout the final approach. Upon landing; we had around 1;800 lbs. of fuel. I feel this margin of safety is extremely low for a safety conscious airline and program. The fuel management program should be providing us with more fuel not less fuel. We were [under] fueled and received no undue delays and were proactive in managing and conserving fuel. Our end result was landing with 1;800 lbs. of fuel. In a medium case scenario; if the airport shuts down for an aircraft emergency or anything else; a go around diversion to the closest company destination (ZZZ2 is 78 miles away); say 25 to 30 minutes at 8;000 ft. (1;100 lbs.); would have me landing with 400 lbs. of fuel and I would have had to make that decision immediately. ZZZ3 is the next closest at 117 miles. That is also if those stations both have personnel staffed to service the aircraft. While legal; and probably acceptable; this practice seems to create an unreasonable reduced margin of safety standards with the trade-off being cost savings. In addition; as a Check Airmen and experienced pilot; I have more wherewithal to be neurotically monitoring developing situations; and weather. I can see an average pilot easily being forced into a no-win situation or very unsafe situation with dire outcome.[We] detected and attempted to manage [the issue] upon review of the release and even with proactive measures; the aircraft still landed with an uncomfortable amount of fuel. [We] took proactive fuel burn reductions with no changes in landing fuel. The airline's new fuel management program and a lack of safety margins created by the program [were contributing factors].I suggest changing the standard practice of providing 45 minutes of cruise fuel from flight level altitudes and provide the crew with an accurate and manageable 45 minutes of fuel near 10;000 ft. which is where most people will need that extra fuel in the case of holding; delays; reroutes and diversions which more likely occur below the flight levels. Add fuel and increase the amount of minimum fuel required to be on the airplane for dispatch.
column125: An air carrier Captain reported receiving a low fuel indication during final approach and suggested that the company's fuel management program did not allow enough fuel for adequate safety margins.

column121: On approach into ZZZ Runway XXR; the Captain called for Flaps 1. The slats extended as expected. Shortly thereafter Captain called for Flaps 2. An ECAM message appeared and flaps had not moved at all. We followed ECAM procedures to no avail. Several miles out; we went around to diagnose the problem with the aircraft and requested priority handling. We ran the ECAM [procedure] and landing data as required then flew a zero-flap approach to an uneventful landing and roll out. The Captain notified Operations and filled out the logbook as required. Two weeks later I received a call from the gatekeeper looking for information on the event. I turns out Maintenance said they could not find a problem and signed off the logbook. The next day the following crew had the same issue flying into ZZZ1 according to the gatekeeper.

column121: I was planning to fly from the ZZZ airport to my home on the lake a distance of approximately 5 NM. I was solo; and did a weight and balance and determined I needed 45 pounds of ballast in the nose anchor compartment; and confirmed it was there. The winds were very light out of the south and I took off on Runway XX and turned towards my home. I went to do a 'splash and dash' on the lake before going to the house so after climbing to about 500 ft. I reduced power and did my pre-water landing checklist. On touchdown I immediately did a water loop with no injury to me or any other property; and minor damage to the plane. I must have let the nose get too low; and possibly was hit by an unnoticed boat wake. Since I had lake specific recurrency training on [Date] and [Date1]; have done [Number] water landings; [Number1] in the last year and [Number2] in the last 90 days I do not know what else I should have done.I did notify the tower that I had a problem and was heading back on the water to the seaplane ramp. They called the fire department and asked them to launch a boat to assist if needed. When I got to the seaplane ramp their launch vehicle; trailer; and boat were on the left side of the ramp. I tried to call the tower to ask them to have the equipment moved; but could not raise them. I then tried to get up the right side of the ramp; and the right main gear went off the ramp and was damaged. I feel personnel should not block runways; taxiways; or seaplane ramps when trying to help.

column121: Upon arriving at MSO airport on [Date] at approximately XA:00 the winds were not favorable to execute the ILS to RWY (Runway) 12. The weather conditions to perform the RNAV GPS D were alternating between ceiling and visibility going below minimum conditions. We were communicating with Dispatch along the way and were discussing diverting to our first alternate; which [was] Billings (BIL). The ceiling and visibility at MSO was above minimums at time of arrival. We started the RNAV approach with a circle to land on RWY 30. After crossing the final approach fix at 6;800 ft.; and descending to our MDA of 4;700 ft.; we started to have some ground contact. The FO (First Officer) said that he saw the PAPI lights to the right of us. The approach did not allow for us to circle to land northeast of RWY 12/30. The only way to commence the circle was to fly over the airport and circle around to the west. We briefly discussed circling to the west and quickly decided that it would be unsafe to circle around the airport at night in low visibility with LLWS (Low Level Wind Shear) conditions in mountainous terrain. During the decision making phase I had inadvertently disconnected the auto throttle. We continued to the missed approach point. As we went around I had realized that the MAP fix was not in the box. As we looked at the approach plate and quickly made a climbing left turn on a heading of about 340. We asked ATC for a heading while we headed in the proper direction. Once WENKE was back in the box we realized we were right on course.At approximately 8;000 ft. we were back in radar contact and was giving a vector off the final approach course. ATC asked us if we wanted to commence another approach or divert to a different airport. After discussing the conditions that we saw and weather forecast to be deteriorating; we unanimously decided that it would be unsafe to conduct a circle to land night approach based on the current weather and forecasted conditions.We then told ATC that we were going to divert to BIL. We sent ACARS message to Dispatch telling them we were diverting as well. After landing at BIL; Dispatch told us that we were to refuel and proceed back to MSO. Dispatch then said the weather was not good enough to go back and was delaying our departure. After waiting about an hour Dispatch said the winds were improving and we should be able to do the ILS RWY 12 back into MSO. We departed BIL for MSO with BIL as our alternate if we could not get in. We executed the ILS to RWY 12. While in Billings we contacted ATC and they told us at that time that they did not have radar until about 8;000 ft. The controller said that he was not working at that time but discussed with the previous controller that we were right where we were supposed to be and we said we just were at a high work load and wanted to verify with them that we were on course.High workload with low ceilings; LLWS; at night in mountainous terrain. Having to circle with visibility below what was projected and inadvertently disconnected auto throttles to land.I feel like this approach is not a safe approach to do at night. I spoke with several carriers that land at this airport and this approach is not authorized.
column125: Air carrier Captain reported difficulties while conducting a circle-to-land approach at night in mountainous terrain in low visibility conditions followed by an improperly executed missed approach.

column121: Crew was descending into ZZZ Airport at about [night] time. Weather revealed winds 350 with good visibility and MVFR conditions. With the higher winds out of the north; the crew elected to fly the RNP Approach which requires the PAPI to be operational to fly the approach. A review of the NOTAMs revealed this would not be an issue; though the REIL was out of service to this runway. Icing conditions on the approach prompted to crew to conduct a flap full landing with ice numbers. Additionally; Tower mentioned that they would be closing just prior to our arrival. The Captain; myself; briefed the autothrottles would be disconnected on the arrival; with the autopilot off once established inbound. I disconnected the autothrottles and left the autopilot on as briefed. We continued to configure through the landing checklist and saw the runway on the approach. Once we rounded out on final; I disconnected the autopilot and began slowing to our approach speed when the lights at the airport clicked off. I briefly shallowed the descent anticipating the possibility of a go-around and had my First Officer turn the lights to high which failed to bring the PAPI back online. Being VMC and within required company parameters for a safe approach; I inquired to my First Officer if he was comfortable with continuing under the conditions which he was. I corrected descent angle and the flight landed without any further incident.This was the flight crew's first time flying this approach; my first RNP approach in a while; and my fourth night landing in about 2.5 months. This RNP Approach requires the PAPI to be operational to use. With there being no PAPI NOTAM; it was determined that it would be appropriate to fly it. After we had landed we found that the Tower; attempting to enable the pilot controlled lighting; had inadvertently switched all lights off. This was corrected with my First Officer turning the lights back on but the action failed to turn the PAPI on. The ATIS had called for gusty winds out of the north but; once we were established on final; I noticed we had a quartering tailwind at 15 kt. which caused me some confusion. The light issue and shifting winds prompted me to shallow out the approach due to the possibility of a go-around. However; with the airport lights on minus the PAPI and in VMC conditions; I figured the safest course of action was to continue given the fact that we were within parameters required to continue the approach and land. My thinking was a go-around late at night; in mountainous terrain and icing conditions with a possible changing weather pattern; may not be the best idea given we were still within tailwind parameters and touchdown requirements for landing. To me; this incident illustrates how important it is to appropriately manage [non-forecasted] distractions; especially during high workload phases. Additionally; it's imperative that we strive to remain current even during prolonged periods of stagnation.When weather is in vicinity; ensure [that] the most accurate weather is obtained from Tower if necessary. Relay concerns of airport issues to ATC or go-around. Be wary of Tower closures near arrivals to airports. A reminder that higher elevation airports present unique challenges which must be reviewed and briefed prior to flying into them. A reminder to chair fly approaches to destination airports; especially if the approaches you intend to follow are unfamiliar. The importance of maintaining appropriate approach currency. Speed management and adequate monitoring during all flight phases. A reminder to fly the airplane first and; if mounting distractions cause confusion; go-around.
column123: I briefed the descent and approach with Captain; Captain as pilot flying. Reviewed FMS approach briefing card. Verified all NOTAMs and approach requirements per the chart and current ATIS. Both First Officer and Captain acknowledged Runway End Identifier Lights were inoperative; [but was not] not required for approach clearance. Received approach clearance from Approach. Tower closed while just outside the FAF with acknowledgment from Tower on Tower frequency of no traffic in the area on frequency and no issues to report from the last reported ATIS. Broke out from IMC to VMC conditions before the FAF. Acknowledged 'runway' by First Officer and 'landing' by Captain. Clicked the CTAF frequency 5 times by [the] First Officer to assure lights would remain illuminated while on final; [as] requested by [the] Captain. Runway lights removed from view while just past the FAF.Then; [the] First Officer clicked 5 times on CTAF frequency again; after [the] Captain asked about the runway lights turning off; both runway lights illuminated again. Moments later we both acknowledged that we didn't see the PAPI to the left. CA (Captain) asked if I found the circumstances to land safe versus a missed approach late at night and snow in the forecast. [We] agreed it would be safe to land given the real time circumstances. Upon landing with no issue; Tower Controller got on CTAF frequency to apologize that they configured the runway lighting incorrectly; which is why it turned off momentarily. The First Officer reported the PAPI was also not working when lights returned. [The] ATC Controller reported on CTAF; 'That is weird;' and would be reporting it to airport operations. Great example in how reality and goal for safety require pilot judgment.

column121: Our aircraft had just landed at ZZZ when a single-seat aerobatic biplane began to do low-level aerobatics (hammerheads and rolls) opposite normal traffic in the pattern. A Cessna 150 entered left downwind Runway XX; well spaced from the biplane; which was in a modified right downwind/base; when the biplane crossed the field and entered a midfield left crosswind-to-downwind behind the 150. The 150 continued downwind; turned base then final; when the biplane banked hard from downwind directly to final abeam the 150 and approximately 25 feet above. The 150 initiated a go-around and the biplane did the same; nearly colliding which each other. The 150 eventually landed on a second go-around while the biplane continued to execute high speed low passes over the runway at about 50 feet AGL. The problem likely arose because the biplane; which was NORDO; was engaged in a low level acrobatics display for a group of onlookers in the tiedown area and was not scanning for conflicting traffic. The 150 likely saw the biplane traffic near the airport; but the biplane's erratic behavior and rapid altitude and direction changes would have complicated efforts to maintain separation. The conflict could have been resolved if the biplane pilot refrained from low-level aerobatics in the vicinity of the traffic pattern; was visually scanning for traffic; and vacated the traffic pattern area when other traffic was inbound to land.

column121: During a Medevac flight to ZZZ1 from ZZZ I initiated an approach with less than the required RVR to do so. Our return flight had been delayed by 4 hours because the patient was very difficult to stabilize. I had checked the weather before departing ZZZ and it was above the minimums that were required for the approach. While enroute to ZZZ1; I acquired the ATIS and found that visibility had decreased to 1/8 mile. The situation at the two nearest options that would allow me to get the hospital was the same. I chose to go to ZZZ2 because it was near enough that we could get the patient to ZZZ1 and the automated weather was well above minimums for the approach. After two attempts at landing in ZZZ2; I realized that the weather was worse than reported there. While I had been maneuvering I was monitoring the conditions at ZZZ1 and they seemed to vary minute by minute. I decided to divert to ZZZ3 since the medic told me we had no time to waste with another approach as he was running low on medication for the patient. When I requested the change to ZZZ3; the controller asked me if I wanted to attempt an approach to ZZZ1. After two go around and working on my fifth backup plan; I was desperate to get the plane on the ground and the patient to the hospital. I accepted the clearance and started the approach. I saw the runway lights at 400 ft AGL and the runway at 200 ft AGL. I decided to land. As I was rolling out I noticed that ground visibility was very low and I realized I had landed with less than the required 2400 RVR that I was supposed to have. Contributing factors: I had the weather and forecast from when I left ZZZ1 6 hours prior printed out. I should have reviewed the forecast more thoroughly rather than just checking present conditions before returning to ZZZ1. I did not realize that night that I was getting sick. During the climb out I was queasy and I attributed this to a lack of horizon at night. The next day I had full flu like symptoms that precluded me from working. What I would do differently: I should have asked the medical team where they wanted to go if ZZZ1 did not work out. If I had that we could have all been prepared to go to a different hospital if the ZZZ1 airport did not work.

column121: VFR instrument currency flight at ZZZ on RNAV GPS Runway XX with safety pilot.  When outbound to ZZZZZ IAF at 3;300 MSL and entering the course reversal saw airspace indicated on GNS 530.  I realized the floor of ZZZ C airspace was 2;X00 MSL and penetrated airspace.  I Immediately descended to 2;Y00 MSL and continued the course reversal at that elevation and completed the practice approach.  Traffic display from ADS-B did not show any traffic conflict while at 3;300 in ZZZ C airspace. Lack of flying in past 6 months due to COVID limits contributed to reduced situational awareness and unfamiliar with this area and airspace.  Flying more and better use of EFB charts would have corrected this situation.

column121: ZZZ Tower was closed due to mandatory cleaning when a Controller tested positive. The airport was operating under CTAF frequency. We got the ATIS after pattern work at ZZZ1; then decided to shoot the RNAV XX; with the winds reporting 300 @ 12 knots. We contacted approach and got vectors for the RNAV XX. After being switched over to the CTAF frequency from Approach; we heard multiple aircraft utilizing Runway XYR. With the traffic using Runway XYR we decided it would be best to circle to Runway XYR; since a circle to Runway XYL is NA per the approach plate. When reaching circling minimums and within the circling radius we began to circle east of the field on a left base to Runway XYR. This required us [to] cross over the extended centerline of [Runway] XYL. We flew the rest of the pattern and landed with no incident or traffic conflict. Additionally; no traffic was in the pattern for [Runway] XYL at the time and no traffic was affected by this decision.I believe that the other traffic using Runway XYR instead of Runway XX (based on what the wind was calling) was a huge factor. My student and I were concerned on entering right traffic for Runway XYR and overflying the field roughly 1;480 MSL/600 AGL feet above and potentially creating a traffic conflict for those operating on [Runway] XYR. This is what drove us to make left traffic for Runway XYR.
column123: I was flying the RNAV XX into ZZZ with CFI (Certified Flight Instructor). ZZZ Tower was closed early due to mandatory cleaning after Controller tested positive for COVID-19. Airport was operating under CTAF frequency. After checking ZZZ ATIS after pattern work at ZZZ1; the decision was made to shoot the RNAV XX into ZZZ as winds were 300@12. Contacted Approach; who granted us the approach clearance. Upon switching to ZZZ CTAF; it became clear that traffic was using [Runway] XYR/L despite the winds favoring Runway XX. We made the decision to break off the approach at CAT B circling minimums and circle to [Runway] XYR as a circle to [Runway] XYL from RNAV XX is NA per the approach plate. This required us to cross over the extended centerline of [Runway] XYL. We circled east of the field and entered a left base for Runway XYR and proceeded to fly a normal pattern with no incident or traffic conflict.If we were to fly right traffic instead for Runway XYR that would have required us to cross midfield at 1;480 MSL (approximately 600 FT AGL); which could create a potential conflict for traffic on go-around/missed. Rather; we elected to essentially perform a circle [Runway] XYL with a sidestep to [Runway] XYR (as the circle to land [Runway] XYL from [Runway] XX is NA). Additionally; no traffic was affected by this decision.

column121: I was conducting right base to new private airport level at approximately 500' AGL in order to evaluate appropriate traffic flow for the landing site. ZZZ is awaiting final activation in the ADIP (Airport Data and Information Portal) portal system. While in my right turn; a local property owner was conducting legal drone operations. There was not a collision hazard; but the potential for such an event was identified by the drone operator. After engagement with the operator; I agreed to adjust pattern corridor to avoid overflight of the area of concern at low altitude. Prior to further low altitude flight operation in the immediate vicinity of ZZZ I will await final FAA activation of the landing site. I will also engage the immediate community to inform them of flight operations and solicite any additional safety concerns. It was a good learning experience on community engagement and to be more proactive in risk identification/mitigation.

column121: On short final to land Runway XXR we heard a 'too low; flaps' audible warning. As the PM; I saw we were at flaps 25 with a planned flaps 30. I called this out to the First Officer who was the flying pilot as I selected flaps 25 and performed the landing check which we had apparently missed on approach. We made a normal landing. This was our second day in a row of very early flights with wake up calls and 3 time zone changes. While I didn't feel fatigued; I suspect I was more tired than I realized. We had just flown a nearly 5 hour cross country flight with a breakfast of a couple spoonfuls of granola; a couple spoonfuls of yogurt; and a few grapes. This was our company provided meal for the flight. While I normally take responsibility for providing my own food; this is not always possible in the current COVID environment. I believe this was a significant contributing factor. In hindsight; the proper response would have been to perform a go around and set up for another approach. While I don't feel my handling of the situation was unsafe; it was not consistent with company procedures. With reasonable sleep and nutrition I'd like to think I would have made a different decision.

column121: Intended flight was from my home base at ZZZ; to ZZZ1. Flight time was calculated to be about 20 minutes. Approximately 4 miles from ZZZ1 the engine power suddenly rolled back. I immediately looked for a landing field while at the same time trying to adjust the mixture and the throttle to regain power.As I was setting up to land on a field; the engine power came back up again. I deducted that the partial power failure was due to either water on the fuel tank or a clog in the fuel line. I decided to abandon the forced landing and try to land at the airport; which was only about 3 miles away. I lowered the landing gear; execute my Before Landing Checklist and set to land. The engine quit shortly after that. I had to quickly pick another forced landing spot because there were trees all around. The aircraft impacted hard; 1.2 SM from the airport. I sustained injuries.

column121: I'm writing to report the challenging nature/difficulty in making visual approaches in a jet to Runways 35L/R at Henderson Exec Airport (HND); where terrain is a prominent factor as close as a 3-mile final approach.  Compliance with ATC spacing instructions can leave the pilot and airplane in a position where neither of the options for trying to land straight-in are particularly good options:  Maneuver in proximity to high terrain; knowing in advance that you will be facing the prospect of repeated GPWS warnings as you try to visually get into a position to make the final approach as stable as possible; or; remain high until well clear of the foothills; but face the prospect of a 'chop and drop' close-in final approach; which is neither stable nor desirable.Our afternoon arrival was in excellent VMC conditions.  HND was landing 35L and 35R. I have been into HND a few times; and from several years ago I remembered the terrain issues to the south; so I made it a special point of emphasis prior to top-of-descent to brief the SIC that I intended to maintain visual separation with the terrain and expected we would be dealing with the GPWS while maneuvering to get the airplane in a position to fly a stable final segment.We did not fully fly our filed STAR; but instead we were vectored SW of LAS and eventually turned inbound on an E-NE vector on what looked like a 10-15 mile left base leg.  This initial vector looked promising; as I thought it might allow for a close-in base to final; which I considered preferable to having to navigate the mountains to the south of the airport.  However; as we got inside of 10 miles; approach control instructed us that Tower wanted us to 'widen out more to the east; and square the base turn to final...cleared visual approach 35L.'  This instruction basically put us in the position of having to navigate the mountainous terrain on our left base; with a turn to final at approximately 5-6 miles.  I set the autopilot to descend for 5;000 MSL (approximately 2;500 AGL above touchdown elevation); but I knew that I would have to get the airplane down to a lower altitude before rolling out on final; or else be faced with either a 'chop and drop' situation or needing to ask for a circling approach over the airport in VMC.I opted to hand fly the airplane and visually maneuvered between the saddles of some higher peaks as I transitioned from left base to final.  I was able to gradually descend another few hundred feet as I was flying this portion of the approach; but the GPWS had become active with constant terrain cautions; as expected.  I was able to keep the terrain in sight at all times while maneuvering; maintaining a 500-700 foot margin on the radar altimeter over the path I was flying.  Beyond dealing with the GPWS audio until we cleared the highest points; I did get the airplane into position to fly a stable final segment and land.  Everything transpired pretty much how I thought it might; starting from the descent briefing all the way to touchdown.  But I walked away from the airplane not feeling satisfied; thinking there has to be some better way to set up and coordinate visual approaches to 35L/R.  Of course; the pilot always has full responsibility to maintain terrain avoidance on visual approaches; but where difficult topography exists; such as south of HND; some extra assistance from ATC in the form of additional vectoring and/or coordination with the local tower could go a long way in helping to better manage visual approaches to 35L/R at this airport.When we were instructed to contact the tower; the approach controller was appreciative of our cooperation in squaring the base as instructed; but I am left wondering if he realized that making me deal with the high terrain on extended final made flying the visual approach that much tougher.I've already decided that if I am put into a similar situation at this airport in the future; I am not going to consider either of the two aforementioned solutions to try and make it straight-in on a visual approach.  Going forward I will opt to negotiate a circling approach in the traffic pattern with the controller(s) instead of battling issues with terrain or excessively steep final approach considerations.  It may make things a bit more complicated for the controllers and traffic flow; but I think this course of action is really the safest alternative of the lot.

column121: We were on arrival into ZZZ and on vectors with Approach Control. Prior to switching over to Approach Control ATC advised us that we could expect moderate turbulence; I believe from 9;000 to 12;000 feet; on descent. We were higher up on our descent when they notified us that we could expect the moderate turbulence in the lower altitude structure. We were switched over to approach control.  I remarked to approach that we were picking up moderate turbulence; again I believe we were at or above 6;000 feet to give them a heads up. We were given a left northerly turn to 010 degrees and a descent to 5;000 feet.  The Autopilot was engaged. It was very turbulent. We received an Alts Cap which is a normal indication on the FMA (Flight Mode Annunciator) that the autopilot is now armed to capture the selected altitude. We were getting bumped around pretty good by now; full continuous moderate turbulence. It was at that moment when the autopilot was capturing the altitude that the upset occurred. The aircraft was rocked to a 45 to 50 degree left bank and a sinking drop occurred similar to what you would receive in an airliner if you ever have hit an air pocket. This exceeded the limits of the autopilot and it kicked off. I took over manually to right the airplane and that is when we received what I would call a severe bump which led to the second upset that did the same thing; left bank to 45 to 50 degrees and a pitch downward.  I was already in the process of advancing power from the first upset but this time I went to full thrust; TOGA power if you will; to initiate the unusual attitude recovery.  I was aggressive in the recovery; put some G on the aircraft; airspeed bled down from 250 to about 180 or 190 at that moment which is still a safe flying airspeed in our type aircraft; well above stall.  I traded airspeed for altitude to expedite the recovery.  We recovered to altitude shortly after.This all happened so quickly.  Severe turbulence upset coupled with automation exceedance were contributing factors.  Lessons learned are significant. Turbulence is always a threat.Constance vigilance is always required.  Secondly we stress at our company to never fully rely on automation and that it will not always work in all situations.  We also stress that situations can happen to anyone regardless of experience. An Incident/Hazard Report has been filled out as part of our Safety Management System (SMS) program and submitted to the Director of Safety at our company. We train for upset situations every X months as part of our X month continuation qualification and training. I've flown many types of aircraft as well as fighter type aircraft and have never experienced a situation such as the one described above.

column121: My first trip 'solo' as CO [Commanding Officer] was going well. I had a good crew and we performed well as a crew. On arrival after receiving vectors for approach to Runway XXL and established; the PF was looking around - sighting...flaps were at 15...I was focused on the required speed and frequency change at ZZZZZ; and the Relief Pilot was daydreaming as well.  At 1;000 feet the PF called 'stable'...and I saw his speed and ILS tracking were spot-on...and then we both realized at the same time that the gear was still UP and the flaps were at 15!!  He called gear down and flaps 25; which I did out of habit as response to the fallout.  I realized then; at the same time he did; that the approach was NOT going to work; at which point we both called 'go-around'.  The go-around was at 800 feet AFL and was UGLY!!  Go-arounds from this altitude are often UGLY!!  The subsequent vectors and second approach went as it should and we landed safely...the desired outcome.  The debrief at the gate was thorough and each of us contributed; as well as took appropriate blame for our individual contribution to such an embarrassing and actually humiliating event.We each had recently been through a simulator check.  The Relief Pilot and I (CO) each having less than 40 hours in type; and the FO having just returned from a 7 month period of not flying.  Combined total flight time of this 3-man crew exceeded X0;000 hours.  However on this day we were each victims of a few different causal factors:  fatigue...we had just flown XA+45 hours from ZZZZ; neither of us slept well the previous night; and we were each the victims of relative inactivity due to COVID-related restrictions; each of these led us to be distracted; lackadaisical; and only partially effective as a crew.Causal factors contributing to this outrageously ridiculous display of airman-ship are deemed to be at least the following: longer layovers; [lack of] more well established activity schedules during our layovers-even during times of social restrictions; [lack of] better food onboard flights for crews; [lack of] better procedural discipline is possibly another even considering each of us had been through simulator training very recently.

column121: On Aircraft X to ZZZ; we were given a turn to base for a visual approach to XR inside the final approach fix 600 feet above the FAF.  We realized we weren't going to be able to complete the approach under the stable approach criteria so I called the go-around at 1;000 feet AGL. Once we got the aircraft climbing I called ATC saying that we are going around. ATC said climb and maintain 3000 feet; we were already climbing and had a high rate of climb because we only had about XX people on board.  While we were cleaning up the aircraft and setting up the FCP; I realized our Climb rate was high and we were approaching 3;000 feet quickly. I said 'watch your altitude' and asked ATC if we could get 3;500 feet. ATC denied it and said to maintain 3;000 feet and contact departure. Unfortunately; the correction of bringing the nose down and power back happened too late and we climbed through our altitude by about 200 feet. The correction came and we corrected our altitude back to 3;000 feet. On the second attempt approach tried to do the same thing to us again however we denied it and asked to be vectored for the ILS.  ATC turned us to intercept the final approach course too close and I told them we won't be able to do it due to us having a tailwind; and that we are going to need to intercept it from the other side. I told ATC the intercept we would need. On that attempt we completed the approach successfully and landed with no further complications.Due to having a poor vector for the approach; and no warning of a close approach; we ended up high on a visual approach to Runway XR. For that reason we decided to go-around. The altitude assigned to us was only about 1;200 feet above the altitude we decided to go-around at. By the time we got the aircraft configured for the go around and contacted ATC we were even closer to that altitude. Due to having a high workload in that environment; a light weight; and being already close to the altitude assigned to us the climb rate was too high when I made the call out to the Captain. I also tried to rectify the situation before it happened by asking ATC for a higher altitude but was denied.Deny the visual approach and ask ATC to just fly the ILS to give us more time. Say unable instead of asking for a higher altitude.
column123: Today on Aircraft X to ZZZ we were given a turn inside of the FAF for Runway XR into ZZZ. ZZZ Approach had us at 3;000 feet. The FAF is 2;400 feet. We were unable to widen the approach due to a snowstorm to the west and had been cleared for the visual. We were too high; so we completed a missed approach. On the missed we went above our assigned altitude of 3;000 feet by 200 feet. We requested higher but were rejected; so we descended the 150-200 feet. On the second attempt; approach tried to give us the same turn at the same altitude. We asked for the ILS. We were again given a bad vector; however; farther out. We were given a 060 heading to intercept. Due to a 40 knot tailwind we flew through and requested a 120 heading to join. It was accepted. We landed successfully.We got a bad vector inside the final approach fix while 600 feet above the FAF altitude. We were given no indication or expectation of a short approach. We were too high and too fast for our approach. We conducted a go-around and subsequently climbed 150-200 feet high.

column121: Lost SA partially on arrival. Approach gave us a 240 heading and asked us to call the field. I had selected managed speed out of 10;000 and was configuring. Extended gear descending through 9;200; so we could continue to slow. Aircraft wasn't slowing. Captured LOC; but overshot a bit. Glide slope didn't capture. Once I saw the PAPI I saw I was low I leveled; continued to configure and slow. Leveled at 1;000 feet AFL momentarily to rejoin glideslope and ensure configured for stabilized approach. Had runway in sight the whole time after turning final; met gates at 1;000 feet AFL and 500 feet AFL; but felt behind and should have gone around. Maintenance delay; unfamiliar airport; late runway change; long day (nearly 9 hours of flying); no approach control (ZZZ Center handling approach due to COVID outage).I should have asked for vectors onto the approach rather than accepting visual. ZZZ is surrounded by high terrain; I was unfamiliar even though I felt I had prepared carefully; vectors would have provided a less dynamic environment. Also; if I were in a similar situation; I would go-around and ensure I don't press when I feel at all like I have lost SA. Also; the high altitude made slowing the aircraft down more difficult. I didn't include that in my briefing. I know better; but after the long day I failed to consider that as a factor.
column123: ATC gave us a 90 degree plus vector for Runway XXR and asked us to call the field. We had it in sight and were cleared for the visual. We were above the glideslope; with flaps 1; put the gear down to slow down; and used managed speed. The approach was armed; caught; but overshot the localizer because the aircraft wasn't slowing down. The glideslope didn't capture and we were then low on the glide slope as we passed through it from above. We leveled off; slowed down and finished configuring. We were within stable criteria at 1;000 and 500 feet; but it was a messy approach.Cause - Long duty day; extended due to maintenance. Bad vector from ATC and pressure to go visual. Approach was closed and Denver center was handling approach.Don't be pressured into accepting a visual at night with mountainous terrain. Go-around if an approach is feeling uncomfortable. Slow down earlier when at a higher elevation airport.

column121: What happened: Aircraft Y was given a take off clearance for a runway that was occupied by a helicopter that had landed on the opposite end and was awaiting taxi instructions.What led to this:  The helicopter was coming in from the east and requested to land on the western side of the airport.  The active runways at the time were south to north (XXL and XXR).  The Tower cleared the helicopter to land on the numbers of an inactive runway XY; which would keep the helicopter on the eastern side of the airport; away from the flow of fixed wing traffic. About 5 minutes elapsed between the time the helicopter landed and Aircraft Y flew over the helicopter.During that time I believe the Controller may have been distracted by pattern traffic; Aircraft Z arrival (this was a factor prior to the helicopter landing which resulted in the controller deciding to keep the helicopter on the eastern side of the airport); a change in Aircraft Y IFR clearance; and by a pilot of an airplane who mistakenly made a final approach to the wrong parallel runway.Starting from the point at which the helicopter lands; the following exchanges were heard.Aircraft A was cleared for touch and go #2 behind Aircraft Z for runway XXL.Aircraft B was cleared to Land runway XXR.A few seconds after Aircraft B acknowledged the landing clearance the tower issue a taxi instruction to the airborne Aircraft B 'hold short of runway XXR I am going to have a departure off runway XZ.'Aircraft B responded 'I am in the air.'The tower replied 'Disregard; Land and hold short of runway XZ; land and hold short of runway XZ.'Aircraft B responded that this was their intention and would be parking in an area that would not put them at risk of crossing into runway XZ.The tower approved a request to taxi back to parking after Aircraft B completed a 180 degree turn on the runway after landing.30 seconds later the tower controller said to Aircraft B; 'Disregard the land and hold short clearance. I won't have that departure.  He will be holding in place; though.'The tower instructed Aircraft Y to line up and wait on runway XZ.The 737 had already landed on XXL and the tower cleared a Cessna for takeoff runway XXL.20 seconds later Aircraft B realized he was about to land on top of Aircraft A who had just been cleared for takeoff and asked the tower if he should go around.  There was no response.  Aircraft B hailed the tower again.  I observed Aircraft B descend below 100 ft AGL before deciding to go around.  40 seconds elapsed between the 1st and 3rd time Aircraft B called the tower.The tower gave the departing Aircraft A; a mixed up call starting with 'line up and- cancel take off- cleared for takeoff runway XXL'The tower then gave Aircraft B instructions to remain in the upwind; he would give him a crosswind instruction to follow.Aircraft B apologized for lining up to land on the wrong runway.As soon as the frequency congestion allowed; knowing there was a jet on the opposite side of my runway; I made a call 'Tower; Helicopter (call-sign) holding position on the numbers of [Runway] XY ready to taxi to (name of ramp)'.  The call was not acknowledged and 55 seconds later an amended departure clearance; combined with a takeoff clearance; was given to the waiting Aircraft Y.   I did not hear the departure clearance and Aircraft Y began racing towards us.  Ironically; I may have turned to my student and said to keep an eye on Aircraft Y.  As it rushed overhead I made an aggressive maneuver off of the numbers and onto the closest available vacant spot beyond the runway safety area.Previous experiences at this airport have made it clear when a helicopter accepts a landing clearance to a spot; taxiway; intersection; or helipad; they are expected to remain there until given a clearance to taxi.  Quite often the controllers will vector an arriving helicopter far around the approach or departure center lines of the active runways and restrict them to altitudes below 600 MSL instead of accommodating a flight path crossing the active runways at a higher safe altitude. Less often are we allowed to land as in the case above in a place safely away from the departing/arriving aircraft; and in a position where we can easily air taxi at a moment's notice.  A helicopter is highly maneuverable and this aspect should be used to the utmost advantage when minimizing delays to arriving or departing airplanes.  As mentioned earlier the helicopter's arrival was prior to Aircraft Z checking in with the tower.  There was Aircraft A on a half-mile final to runway XXL ahead of Aircraft Z.  With several miles of spacing between Aircraft Z and Aircraft A; the tower began to ask the helicopter to pass behind the landing Aircraft A.  We accepted.  The tower immediately reversed their decision due to Aircraft Z scheduled arrival and cleared us to land on the numbers of [Runway] XY with the restriction 'remain north of XX'. We accepted the clearance to land.  It is very likely that a helicopter crossing the approach end of XXL shortly after the landing Aircraft A would have had little to no effect on any of the other present arriving or departing aircraft.

column121: On date I was conducting training in an R22 Helicopter departing out of ZZZ at XA:45 local time to ZZZ1. Winds were predominantly out of the west at around 10 kts and we joined left closed traffic for RWY 26 at ZZZ1. There was high training volume and I estimate 7 aircraft were on the ground waiting to depart or actively in the Right Closed Traffic for RWY XX plus our Helicopter. At around XB:10 local time a Jet taxied to the active with no Radio (or no radio communication was made) as we were on a left downwind for [Runway] XX. Local pilots made calls on the radio so all would be aware of his departure and be vigilant/use caution. The jet took off without incident and departed to the North. As pilots worked together to sequence traffic we made our base and final leg before again departing XX to rejoin left closed traffic. At approximately XB:15 local time on the upwind of XX roughly 300ft AGL without warning the jet made a low pass on runway XY at very high speed. I would estimate the pilot was 300ft off our starboard side and about 100ft below our current altitude. I was able to track his flight after the incident to find his Ground Speed was in excess of 330mph. The Jet than proceeded back out to the North again and ended up circling to land on RWY XY this time to a full stop. (The following is my personal opinion) I believe this pilot does not have any remorse for his action. He has an attitude that the FAA can't/won't get him in trouble for this reckless behavior. He wasn't breaking any REG by operating without a radio in an ECHO airspace but his speed certainly was in violation of FAR 91.117(a) and given the high training volume being conducted on this day the pilot showed a clear disregard for other pilots or even his/her own safety.

column121: Flight was from ZZZ to ZZZ2 with a stop to pick up a passenger at ZZZ1. ZZZ Approach provided flight following all the way to ZZZ1 as I was unfamiliar with the airport and had difficulty identifying it from the air. After overflying the field at 2;000 ft (Approach assigned flight following altitude) a frequency change to the CTAF was approved and made. I descended to pattern altitude and crossed midfield before entering downwind leg for runway XX. Crossing midfield; turning to downwind; base and final; position reports were made on the CTAF. No other aircraft operating at ZZZ1 were heard on frequency. Landing XX; proceeded to back taxi to the end of the runway. At the XY threshold; my passenger saw me and began approaching the aircraft. So as to not create an undue hazard to my passenger; I exited the runway onto the available taxiway and turned parallel to XY. At this point; my left wingtip was impinging on the runway.I continued to hear no other aircraft on frequency as I performed a safety briefing for my passenger and ensured he was ready to fly. Shortly thereafter; another aircraft landed on runway 19. This aircraft proceeded to back taxi on XY and then made contact on CTAF. The other aircraft pilot reported to have been making calls on CTAF and attempting to reach me prior to landing; but I did not hear him. The other aircraft pilot reported landing left of center due to the runway incursion. On resuming flight towards ZZZ2 another aerodrome on the same CTAF frequency performed a radio check with me and assured that they could receive me loud and clear.

column121: While on arrival into Nashville; we were being vectored by Approach.  We were just assigned vectors outside of the arrival.  We were notified by the controlling agency that we could expect moderate turbulence between 9;000 and 12;000 feet in the descent; which we did encounter.  Upon reaching 5;000 feet; we were still encountering moderate turbulence.  As we accepted our vector to heading 010; we encountered a pocket of severe turbulence which sent the airplane into an upsetting unusual attitude.  My Captain immediately recognized the issue; as the autopilot kicked itself off; he initiated the proper recovery procedure.  As that happened; we encountered a second pocket of severe turbulence which further upset the aircraft attitude and resulted in additional altitude loss.  My Captain went full thrust and maximum nose up attitude to recover.  We experienced moderate turbulence all the way to the ground that day; but were able to land safely.

column121: On a flight from ZZZ to MPR; I flew a practice approach into MPR (RNAV 18) visually in VFR conditions with no view limiting device. As I was touching down on Runway 18 at MPR; I observed another aircraft taking off from the grass Runway 8. The other aircraft crossed in front of me about 400 feet ahead of me and was about 100 feet above me and climbing. I had made a number of radio calls including a 10 mile straight-in final; 5 mile final; 2 mile final and short final. I never heard the other aircraft make any calls. Upon landing; I realized that although I had plugged the MPR CTAF frequency into my radio; I never flipped that frequency to active. Therefore; I had been transmitting on and listening to the incorrect frequency (departure Tower frequency). I take full responsibility for not having the correct frequency active in my radio and will use this as a learning opportunity and a reminder to be more vigilant about confirming proper frequencies. I am surprised that the ZZZ Tower did not hear my 10 mile; 5 mile; 2 mile and short final calls and inform me that I was on the incorrect frequency.

column121: Early morning at roughly XA:30; Aircraft X flew from the west into ZZZ for pattern work. At the time Aircraft X was the only aircraft that was currently in ZZZ's airspace at the time of the event. Initially Aircraft X made a call to ZZZ Tower asking for pattern work. Since the aircraft was positioned over the mountains the response for ZZZ Tower came in weak. Clarification was requested by pilot flying and stated that the Air Traffic Control Tower was closed. The pilot flying treated the airport as a non-towered airport and switched frequency to XYY.Z. From that point the pilot flying used non-towered airport call outs to announce position. After completing three laps in the pattern an airport operations vehicle flagged down the pilot in command. The pilot in command then preceded to reject the takeoff and continue to the run up area. Airport operations advised the pilot to contact Tower at XXZ.A where the pilot was advised that he could continue pattern work yet had to take a phone number down. Pilot took down phone number and contacted Tower staff after the flight.Due to COVID-19 many ATC facilities are having to temporary close down due to cleaning. ZZZ has also been changing hours of ATC operation in the last ninety days. Pilots from ZZZ1 have also run into the problem of not properly reaching Air Traffic Control due to altered non-posted hours.The pilot in command flying was a student pilot that had recently soloed. When contacted by an airport operations vehicle; [they] only asked for intentions which the pilot responded that he was doing pattern work. At the time it could not be depicted if the airport operations vehicle told on XYY.Z to contact Tower on XXZ.A. In the future [if] airport operations could do this it would help. At the time the pilot thought the airport operations vehicle was attempting to do a FOD patrol of the runway since it was the early morning. The aircraft has been in the past known to have radio issues at random times.The student pilot will go over procedures with Class Delta airports with a flight instructor to mitigate problems in the future.
