column121: High speed abort.Captain at controls.Cleared for takeoff and Tower advised aircraft on 5 mile final as we approached the RWY (Runway). Approximately 1/4 way down runway into our takeoff roll; Challenger announced going around left side. At approximately 1/2 way down runway into our takeoff roll; Tower directed us to abort our takeoff. We were in the high-speed regime at approximately 115 kts. I could see the jet going around on our left abeam; in my peripheral vision as we were aborting.Maximum reverse application w/ maximum brakes. Uneventful abort; cleared runway onto Taxiway F and brought jet to a stop. Manually held brakes while running all applicable checklists. 2 MLG (Main Landing Gear) trucks reached 610 C; others approximately 590 C. Brake temp cooling chart referenced 35 mins used; brakes took approximately 50 min to cool to 270 C. In the meantime; ZZZ Fire-Rescue examined MLG. No evidence of overheat. Conferred with [Maintenance Control] for any further guidance. Later; Maintenance came to the jet; examined MLG per [Maintenance Control] guidance and gave us a thumbs up.In the meantime; we had a conference call with Flight Ops Management and ZZZ Station Ops. Both gave us the ok to continue'.Maintenance Control sent us a new maintenance release; Dispatch sent Release 3. Brake temp left main was the highest temp; at approximately 240 C when we began taxi. No noticeable increases.We departed ZZZ; kept gear down for approximately 5 minutes til all MLG brakes were below 200 C and decreasing. Retracted gear and continued to ZZZ1 via the flight plan.As a side-note; we received an ACARS message at approximately 7;000 ft.; on climbout; directing us to return to gate for another check. We leveled at 9;000 ft.; called Dispatch for clarification. He talked to Maintenance Control and surmised the return to gate message was an old message and may have been caught up in the ACARS system somehow and decided to transmit to us after takeoff roll. Anyhow; message was deemed as received in error and we got a thumbs up to continue OPS (Operations) normal to ZZZ1. I'm not sure if this is something systemic to Company 2 ACARS interface or not; but this issue might need to be examined."
column123: Cleared takeoff at ZZZ with aircraft on 5 nm final. As we began our roll I heard the aircraft on final say they were initiating a go-around. Around 115 KIAS the Tower directed us to reject which we accomplished. We told the passengers to remain seated and we taxied clear to find a place to cool our brakes; get Maintenance to inspect our brakes/tires; and coordinate for a new maintenance release and Dispatch release while re-initializing our FMC. We accomplished all pre-flight; before push and before takeoff checklists again and accomplished an uneventful takeoff over an hour later and landing uneventfully in ZZZ1.

column121: On [Date]; we operated Aircraft X from ZZZ-ROA and I was the CA (Captain) onboard. It was our Day 3 and Leg 4 of the day. I was the Pilot Flying; while my FO (First Officer) was Pilot Monitoring. Both of us were new to this airport; so we reviewed the 10-7 pages and airport diagram to make sure we selected an adequate runway for landing; because they both were relatively short runways in mountainous terrain surrounding area. Once we were in the air and leveled off at our cruising altitude; we started setting up for the approach and briefed it all. As we got closer to the airport and were with Approach; he descended us to 6;000 ft. We both saw the runway and reported it. The ATC asked us to give him at least 6 mile final and we concurred. We looked at the runway and descended down to 4;500 ft. approaching from the southwest to intercept [Runway] 34 localizer. As we got closer to the intercept point for the localizer; the aircraft annunciated 'Caution terrain' 'Pull up' simultaneously; there was no break in between at all. There was also no terrain data that displayed on the MFD on both of our screens. I disconnected the AP (Autopilot) and added power needed to climb immediately. The 'Pull up' annunciation was only said once and went away. We only climbed 500 ft. to 5;000 ft. and continued to intercept the course to land. We also reported the event to the ATC and they didn't say anything. The 'Master Warning' switch light also didn't go on. This event lasted about 2 seconds and we continued to land at the airport.Both of us were new the airport and should've stayed with ATC until intercepted and then call for visual.This event could've easily been avoided by simply just staying with the Approach Control until established on approach; because it is a very mountainous area. Being an unfamiliar crew flying to this area and going visual to land this runway can put you in this situation; even though being in VMC conditions and having runway in sight. I would also like to suggest a guidance in 10-7 to be added regarding landing in this airport and staying with ATC until cleared of mountains and only going visual once cleared and over the valley.
column123: On [Date]; my Captain and I operated Aircraft X from ZZZ to ROA. It was Day 3; Leg 4 of our trip. On this leg; I was the Pilot Monitoring; while my CA (Captain) was flying the aircraft. This was the first time either of us operated to Roanoke; so we reviewed the 10-7 pages; and airport diagram to make sure we selected an adequate runway for landing; since they both were relatively short runways in a mountainous area. Once in cruise flight at our final altitude; we started setting up for the approach and briefed it as necessary. The plan was to set up for the visual backed up by the ILS for Runway 34. As we got closer to the airport and were with Approach; the controller descended us to 6;000 ft. We both saw the runway and reported it. ATC then cleared us for the visual; but asked us to give him at least 6 mile final and we concurred. We looked at the runway; surrounding terrain; and approach plate; and decided to descend down to 4;500 ft. approaching from the southwest to intercept the Runway 34 localizer. We figured this was a correct altitude; as the highest obstacle on the plate was 3;508 ft. As we got closer to the intercept point for the localizer; the aircraft annunciated 'Caution terrain' 'Pull up' simultaneously; there was no break in between at all. There was also no terrain data that displayed on the MFD on both of our screens. My Captain immediately disconnected the AP (Autopilot) and added power as necessary. The 'Pull up' annunciation and all other GPWS alerts were only said once and went away as soon as we started the climb. We only climbed 500 ft. to 5;000 ft. and continued to intercept the course to land. The event was reported to ATC as well; and they acknowledged. This event lasted about 2 seconds and we continued to land at the airport.This was the first time either of us have operated to this particular airport; and should have either stayed above the approach's MSA of 5;700 ft.; or even stayed with the Approach Controller; making him give us headings and altitudes rather than calling the airport in sight and getting cleared for the visual.This event could have easily been avoided by staying with the Approach Controller until established on the approach; rather than calling the airport in sight and getting cleared for the visual. A crew flying to this area that is unfamiliar with the surrounding terrain and conducting a visual approach to this runway can end up in this situation; even with VMC conditions and having the runway in sight. I would also like to suggest some guidance in the 10-7 pages to be added regarding landing at this airport and staying with ATC until cleared of the mountains and only going visual once cleared and over the valley. Something similar to the 10-7 pages for ZZZ regarding the GPWS events would be helpful to other crews in avoiding a similar situation.

column121: Being vectored onto the final approach course 26R; we had a brief encounter with a wake turbulence. And then again during the landing flare to the point where it was becoming a problem to maintain a stabilized approach path above the runway in ground effect. Did not have time to notify air traffic control that this happened. I don't know if we were behind a heavy previous arrival. But we were getting really close to having to go around from the landing flare. The roll motion was becoming such a problem with keeping the aircraft stabilized; and aligned with the runway. I was actually shocked that we touchdown as smooth as we did. I asked the pilot flying if he thought that was a bit unusual his statement was 'I was just one more jolt away from going around'. Wake turbulence encounters in Atlanta are becoming an every week problem; more like almost every inbound leg into Atlanta problem. Also it seems there is little consideration given to the fact that we may be departing behind something that might be a threat as well. I'm beginning to think that there's nothing much going to be done about it until something serious happens. Often the wake encounter threats are not much of a problem if there's sufficient crosswind push the wake away from trailing aircraft; from my experience it does not take much to all. However I've learned that this is still a problem at some point on the arrival or departure procedure. For example a crosswind on the departure runways will clear you of the wake threat for takeoff; but might present a very real problem in trail of a heavy navigating to a point; to the same on that departure or arrival. Especially if it's a direct head; or tailwind now.
column122: Reporter stated wake encounters in ATL are becoming far too frequent and he recommended a change to arrival and departure procedures to increase spacing.

column121: I file this report for two reasons: to note a particular pilot's decision-making; and to talk about a larger potentially dangerous airport-environment situation.  Here is the relevant data:- The airport in question is Montgomery County Airpark; in Gaithersburg; Maryland; GAI. It is a very busy *uncontrolled* airport. It is within the DC SFRA; so pilots must obtain a discrete transponder code from Potomac Approach before taking off; and before re-entering the SFRA from outside. - The airport's only runway is 32/14; about 4000 feet long. The airport ATIS specifies that Runway 14 is preferred in calm-wind conditions. Also; the only approach to GAI is an RNAV/LPV approach to Runway 14. - There is a very; very active primary-training type airport. Nearly all of its students are [from] overseas; and the frequency of their flight means that the traffic-pattern at the airport is usually 'full.' It is typical to have one plane doing a runup; another taking off; another on downwind turning to base; and two or three others approaching from the north; all at the same time.- On the morning of this incident; winds had been in the 5 kt range--but from the north. So traffic through the morning had been all on Runway 32. - Now; the time in question. It was about XA:00 local EST. I was approaching from the north; and I received a code for entering the SFRA from Potomac Approach. As is usual practice; when I was about 12 miles north of GAI; I told Potomac; 'Gaithersburg in sight;' and they gave the formulaic response: 'Stay on discrete transponder code until landing; remain outside Class Bravo; switch to local frequency approved.' So I switched to the local UNICOM; 123.075.-I heard on the frequency what I also saw on my ADS-B display: That the pattern was; as usual; 'busy;' with three or four planes at various points in the circuit. And all of them were using Runway 32. (The winds at that moment were reporting calm on ATIS; but the pattern had been in steady use all day; on 32.)-Over the next eight minutes or so; between first radio contact and landing; I noticed two recurrent phenomena. One was many planes reporting their progress and position in a pattern for Runway 32.  I made calls; beginning at 8 miles out; with intentions to follow the established local practice. ('Forty-five to join right downwind for Runway 32.') The other notable entry was one airplane (Airplane X) that reported it was to the west; and 'straight in landing; Runway 14.'-A plane reports 'downwind 32.' Aircraft X says; 'straight in; Runway 14.' Another plane says; 'Aircraft X; we are using 32; please circle to land.' Aircraft pilot says; 'Straight in Runway 14.' At least twice; and I think three times; flight instructors say 'Aircraft X; PLEASE circle to land 32. We are all using 32.' Aircraft X replies; 'Straight in; 14.'-I kept reporting my own position; and said at one point 'Aircraft X; I am turning base to final for landing 32.' At that point I could see him descending to 14 -- i.e.; head on. The distances were such that it was clear to me that I could land safely and normally; and get off the runway; before he got too close.   And I proceeded to land. I turned off the runway halfway down; while he was still probably a mile out on opposite-direction final. All the other planes in the Runway 32 pattern extended their downwind etc.; to land. -The two planes landed at XA:07 pm EST; but in opposite directions.-Later; I saw someone who I assumed to be the Aircraft X pilot. He was; and I asked him: 'Why did you do that?' His answer was that he was fine-tuning the coupled-autopilot and wanted to fly an approach all the way down. -Note: I have been in this situation myself too; wanting to check out autopilots. But I choose airports where I can do so in keeping with the local traffic flow. -The main problem in this scenario was the Aircraft X pilot; who insisted on plowing ahead despite repeated requests on the frequency to switch to join the busy local traffic flow. I realize this is not an 'enforcement' forum; and I mention it just FYI.-The larger point is that the Gaithersburg airport has become unsustainably busy; for its uncontrolled status.  I have landed there probably X times over the past XX years. I *always* view the most challenging part of any trip to be the final 10 minutes of trying to fit into the uncontrolled but increasingly busy traffic pattern there.  With many primary students in training; they're often doing unexpected things in the pattern; or are uncertain in radio calls. Suggestion / request: Sooner or later there is going to be some safety incident at this airport; coming from too much traffic with too little control. A tower controller would *never* have allowed the situation I'm reporting to emerge. This is a problem waiting to happen at this airport.
column125: GA pilot reported that while landing at an uncontrolled airport another aircraft landed on the same runway; opposite direction. Reporter stated the airport was always busy and should become a tower-controlled airport.

column121: While flying [my aircraft]; I experienced an engine failure approximately 15 NM prior to the destination runway while flying at approximately 2;300 feet.  I had been experiencing extreme headwinds since a refueling stop and the leg had taken me longer than I'd planned. Because of the added flight time; I was thinking about fuel; but I thought I had enough fuel to make the intended destination with minimum reserves.  The engine failure was accompanied by a lack of fuel pressure that was not remedied by turning on an electric fuel pump.  I firmly believe it was due to fuel exhaustion.  With no airports within gliding distance; I angled the plane towards the interstate; and eventually lined up on a dark area parallel to the highway and attempted a landing onto an area about 100 yards from the highway. As I approached the field; I flared; and initially landed the main wheels successfully and rolled for a couple of seconds; probably 20 yards; but as soon as the nose wheel touched the recently plowed soft dirt; it dug in.  I did not suffer any serious injuries that I'm aware of and the plane had surprisingly minor damage.  Upon checking the fuel tanks after exiting the plane; they were indeed empty.

column121: I was returning to BED from the West. It was dark. I tuned in the ATIS. The current ATIS was broadcasting that BED was 'landing and departing Runway 23.' After copying the ATIS; I called BED Tower and reported my position as near Minuteman Airport (6B6) and informed the Tower that I was inbound landing with ATIS. The Tower Controller initially cleared me to enter a right downwind for Runway 29.  This confused me because the ATIS had stated that BED was 'landing and departing Runway 23.' I asked the Controller for confirmation of the runway assignment and he acknowledged that he had 'made a mistake' and told me to enter a right downwind for Runway 23. After I entered the control space; and near the airport; I noticed the high intensity runway lights and landing traffic was on Runway 29; while runway 23 had their lights on 'dim'.  Since the ATIS had not mentioned simultaneous operations to intersecting runways; I became disoriented. I mistook Runway 29 for Runway 23. I was so concerned that I even called the Tower and asked the Controller: 'Is the Aircraft Y lined up with the right runway?'The Tower Controller realized that something was not right and called me and asked me to confirm that I was setting up for right downwind 23 and offered me a clearance to enter a right downwind for Runway 29. I told him that I thought I could still correct; but I ended up flying through the final for Runway 23 and ended up on a left downwind for 23. At about this time; another aircraft; which I later learned was Aircraft Z arriving from the west had also turned inbound to Runway 23 from the north. The Tower Controller told me to turn 20 degrees further right which I did to widen out my downwind leg. Thanks to the Controller; the Aircraft Z was able to land safely and so was I.I now realize that several other factors contributed to my confusion.  I was more tired than I realized. I had a very long work week; yet this night was an opportunity to start flying more night flights; after being absent from this experience for over 16 months.  The pandemic means hazard [but not] if I bring an instructor to help me re-acquaint to night flying.   I allowed myself to be fooled by the warm weather; the light winds and the great visibility into thinking that it would be a good night to re-acquaint myself to cross-country night flying.  Tonight; with the lights dimmed in the cockpit; and the high intensity lights on Runway 29; a plane landing on 29; the lights dimmed on Runway 23; and my lack of night flying practice did this happen.I now realize that I had vastly underestimated the combined effects of fatigue; distraction and 'rust.' Added to that; my confusion resulting from the initial mistaken clearance to enter a right downwind for 29; the combination of bright runway lighting on a conflicting runway without any warning that such operations were being conducted; and traffic operating in unexpected directions all combined to make for a much more confusing situation than I ever would have expected.The controllers explained to me that even when Runway 23 is the operating runway; they still allow traffic to land and depart Runway 29 because Runway 23 is too short for some aircraft that use the field. Nevertheless; I believe that safety would be greatly improved if; during nighttime operations; the ATIS included the warning concerning operations being conducted simultaneously to conflicting runways. This is especially true at BED where runway alignment issues are already an issue. I believe that such a warning would have eliminated my confusion at seeing traffic landing and departing on Runway 29 and then mistaking it for Runway 23.

column121: I was doing pattern work at ZZZ between XA:00 and XB:00. The airfield had a large amount of arriving traffic during the hours I was flying. After a touch & go on RWY XX; I was asked to extend the upwind before flying a right pattern. About mid-field on the downwind; tower requested a left-360. After my first 360; I was instructed to extend my downwind. A second plane [Aircraft Y] was approaching the airport from the northwest. Aircraft Y was asked to 'make a right 270; keep it tight and get ready to line up behind a [jet]'ATC then asked me to widen out my downwind and look for Aircraft Y making the right 270. At this point it was at my 11 o clock; 3 miles. About 5 seconds later I was able to identify the aircraft; let tower know that I had them in sight and was preparing to line up behind them. About 15 seconds later; tower called me up again; asking 'Aircraft X - extend it out a little bit; actually just follow behind and I'll call your base you will have to follow a jet behind on final'. I read back and confirmed.About 30 seconds later; tower called me up again; 'Aircraft X just so I don't get you too far from the field; make a left 360'. I read back the instruction and began my left 360. At this point; I was already thinking about Aircraft Y as I was planning on how I would get back on the downwind and line up behind them. I believe Aircraft Y was just about to finish its right 270 as I was about 90 degrees into my 360. I was able to spot and track the plane on Foreflight with Sentry; but since I had to make a 360 I lost visual when I initiated my turn. Tower; likely receiving a traffic alert called the other plane to make them aware of me: 'Aircraft X about a mile south making a left 360 - he has you in sight'. I do not think he asked them to confirm that they had me in sight and I can't tell from the live-ATC recording whether they read it back. The added statement 'he has you in sight' was extremely dangerous and could have been fatal. Yes; I had the traffic in sight when I was heading toward it and planning to get behind them; but once I started my left 360 there was no way of maintaining a visual with the other plane since I was now heading the opposite direction. I immediately recognized the potential conflict and started looking both outside the plane and on Foreflight trying to spot and track the other plane's relative position. The other plane did not make a 270 turn; but instead kept turning toward the airport/runway i.e. cutting into my path.As I was about 270 degrees into my 360 turn tower called 'Aircraft X - as you are coming out of that 360 turn do you still have Aircraft Y in sight?'At this point; I was already banking hard left in a steep turn trying to pull away from the converging traffic that I saw coming closer toward me. Turning right was not an option as it would have put me in the middle of final with jets arriving. I do not think the other plane confirmed whether or not they had me in sight. If they did; they flew way too close to me for my comfort and should have initiated an avoidance maneuver. In response to tower's question I responded 'Aircraft X unable' because I had to focus on flying the airplane away from the other traffic. We then passed by each other with much less separation than I feel comfortable. The other plane heading toward the runway; and me pulling hard left to the North-West; initiating a climb up to gain altitude. It is hard to tell exactly but horizontal distance could have been less than 500 feet Vertical distance was virtually the same. It happened very quickly. If I had not realized the potential conflict and mitigated by tracking on foreflight and pulling hard left this could have been a really really bad situation. The biggest mistake here; in my opinion; was ATC communicating to the other plane that I had them in sight AFTER I started my 360. There is no way one can maintain visual during such a maneuver while also flying safely and ATC shouldunder no circumstances make that statement. I also think that having two planes completing maneuvers in a way where their exit paths can conflict (right 270 and left 360) in close proximity should be prohibited.There were multiple safer options here. Aircraft Y could have flown a proper base rather than cutting it short onto final. I could have been asked to fly west; or I could have been asked to fly another 360 right after the first one. The fact that ATC changed its instructions relative to how I should fly behind/around the other traffic 3 times in a minute indicates to me that they were not planning ahead but rather responding to/running behind.In the future; I will call out if ATC tells other people that I have them in sight when I don't (i.e. after starting my left 360). I am also surprised nobody (tower/the other plane) complained about how close this was. I am glad to be back on the ground.  I hope this helps keep other people safe in the future!
column125: GA pilot reported experiencing a NMAC that resulted from ATC sequencing 3 aircraft for landing by using 270- and 360-turns.

This is a hazardous situation.

column121: At the beginning of my rotation I let the company know that my tire was looking bad and needed to be replaced.  As PIC I agreed to continue to fly with a worn tire for a few more legs; it was an educated risk as there was still some tread showing and no cord visible from what I'd seen.We departed ZZZ headed to ZZZ1 for maintenance on a variety of items; on departure the ZZZ Tower reported a loud noise and was concerned it was our tire. There was no indication on taxi or takeoff of a bad tire and Tower didn't observe a flat tire.Landing at ZZZ1; the tire popped on landing and the aircraft had a shake to it on roll out.  Upon parking; we looked at the tire and it had exploded and thrown pieces at the lower wing surface; flap; and engine cowl; causing some damage.In the future; when a tire is looking like it needs to be replaced soon; I'll have it replaced instead of giving it a few more landings.
column125: Medium Transport aircraft Captain reported flying several legs with a known worn tire until it possibly failed during the last takeoff. The flight continued but the tire shredded to pieces during the landing roll causing the aircraft to shake.

column121: Approached ZZZ Runway XX for landing at approximately XA:50 from the south. After monitoring radio communications and no one in the pattern I made the decision to approach for landing straight in. I made a radio call stating my intentions and reporting 3 mile final for runway XX. I reduced throttle and speed to within flap range. I input flaps adjusted trim and further reduced speed. I turned on the runway lighting and adjusted course to the left then made a radio call for a mile final approach to runway XX. I verified my indicated airspeed was 62kts as I approached the runway; aimed for the numbers; and reminded myself the runway is closer than it appears in the dark. I then touched down hard on the runway and the nose of the aircraft bounced high sounding the stall warning. When the nose gear came back down in contact with the runway again it did not support the aircraft and the propeller came in contact with the runway stopping the aircraft on runway XX. My first concern was that I was obstructing the runway and tried to make a radio call. However; the radio was not working; at which point I powered down the aircraft and was attempting to open the canopy when I was approached by bystanders at the airport. They asked if I was okay and moved the aircraft off the runway via [Taxiway] 1A; by holding on to and lowering the tail pulling the aircraft on its main landing gear.

column121: The First Officer was flying a visual approach to ZZZ Runway XXR with right traffic from ZZZZZ3 arrival. Radar vector to final; cleared to 3;000 feet from 4;000 feet and cleared the visual. First Officer was in vertical speed and I set 3;000 feet in the MCP window. He armed approach mode and captured the localizer. The aircraft leveled at 3;000 feet and did not capture the glide slope as we were too high. Gear was down; flaps 20 and speed was 163. First Officer disconnected the autopilot and dove down to capture the glide slope. I suggested speed brakes; which he used to get down. This rapid descent caused the Tower to issue an altitude warning. I then became very busy stowing speed brakes; landing flaps; checklists; and verifying stable approach parameters. We never did the 1;000 feet call for set M/A altitude and cleared to land check (which we were). The event; although satisfying the stable approach parameters; was highly irregular and borderline go-around scenario. The debrief included mostly what went wrong and why. We discussed and I noted the main errors. The First Officer said not flying for 7 months was the issue.

column121: Descending into LUK on the LNAV 3R we first received a report from tower of a low altitude alert in between the FAF and the intermediate step down fix (ZUGMI at or above 1480). I had the aircraft in LNAV/VS with a commanded vertical speed mode of -700FPM. I glanced at the altimeter and saw 1550. This is above the step down altitude and above the MDA. The copilot and I both quickly agreed we were above MDA. At this point also I looked out and could see the runway. We briefly leveled off and planned to continue visually. At this point we received a too low PULL UP. Aural warning. Immediately we went around. After getting back level and configuring we talked about the approach and decided to do it again with less vertical speed and just stay a bit higher on the glide path. We got down to out MDA without issue and landed.I never saw any altitude any lower than the step down altitude on the approach. LUK airport is sunk in the valleys between some hills and I think I should've maybe thought to have a gentler vertical speed.Not accept a fairly close to minimums LNAV only approach with a lot of turbulence into Lunken when ZZZ is right around the corner. Fly the instrument profile at lowest possible vertical speed and visual only after VDP. Although we never busted any altitudes I could've made it a much gentler descent to MDA at the VDP since the VDP is 2.4 from the runway and there are lots of hills on the approach prior to the runway.IMC until ragged bases around 800 AGL to 1;000 AGL. Some Visual conditions at time of the event. Runway/PAPI were in sight.
column125: Light Transport aircraft flight crew reported getting a terrain warning while descending to LUK airport through uneven terrain.

column121: I was the Captain and Pilot Flying (PF) for Aircraft X from ZZZ to Richmond (RIC) on November XX; 2020; and observed the following. During cruise flight my First Officer and Pilot Monitoring (PM) alerted me that the last time (back in July) that she flew into RIC the airplane caught a false localizer signal and turned no where near the centerline of the respective runway. Having reviewed the Weather Briefing Package; I determined that RIC would be landing to the north; and elected to load the Runway 34 ILS approach. I confirmed all the waypoints with my PM and set up accordingly. I hard tuned the localizer frequency of 110.7; set the approach course of 337 degrees; and set the minimums of 370 feet on the Primary Flight Display (PFD). We received a clearance to cross the NEAVL intersection at 11;000 with a speed of 250 knots. We met that restriction; and then got a clearance to proceed to the KAFKA intersection and pilot's discretion to maintain 2;000 feet. That waypoint was loaded and confirmed. We began a descent. About 12 miles from the airport I slowed the aircraft to 210 knots and had selected Flaps 1. We were about 3;000 feet; and I slowed further to 180 knots; with a setting of Flaps 2. We were about 7 miles from the KAFKA waypoint; and confirmed the airport was insight. I began slowing the plane to 160 knots. About 3 miles from KAFKA; and the approach mode in the armed position; the airplane selected the LOC mode on the PFD and began to turn towards Runway 2. I turned the autopilot off; and manually continued to fly towards the KAFKA waypoint to lose more altitude. The airplane was about 2;300 feet; with 1;700 feet selected in the altitude selector. I continued the approach; and we landed safely. My First Officer questioned the Tower as to why this would happen. The Tower stated that both the Runway 34; and Runway 2 localizers were operational. He said that he would inform the staff at the airport.

column121: I issued an approach clearance to cross a fix at or above 11;000 ft. until established on the localizer; cleared ILS approach. The aircraft went through the localizer and was correcting back to the localizer and began a descent into a 10;900 ft. MVA. I was giving a relief briefing and looking at the checklist. When I looked back at the screen the aircraft was at approximately 10;700 ft. and already correcting back to the localizer and exiting the 10;900 ft. MVA into a 9;000 ft. MVA. No low altitude alert went off and I didn't issue a low altitude alert as it seemed unnecessary leaving the higher MVA and by the time I would have issued it the aircraft would have been in the lower MVA. My recommendation would be for the pilot to have complied with instruction to maintain 11;000 ft. until established on the localizer prior to beginning descent.

column121: I was the PM; CA was flying. At about 7;100 MSL while being vectored on the NE side of CLT airport for Runway 36R (cross side runway) we noticed a drone off our right side about 1-2 O'clock position; passing by off our right side.  It was close enough to distinguish it was a 4 propeller drone. It was hard to determine exactly how far away the drone was but my guess would be within a few hundred feet. As we passed by in our descent we appeared to be slightly below it as we passed. We notified ATC and continued the approach to landing uneventfully.At first I thought it was a bird until I quickly realized it was a drone. I kept my eyes on it as long as I could as we passed by. There is no doubt that it was a drone. Cause was drone flying in Class Bravo airspace near CLT at an altitude high enough to be in the way of commercial airline traffic. We were being vectored from the NW to the NE side of Charlotte airport for Runway 36R. I notified the CA when I first saw something; and he too saw the drone. We did not make any altitude or course changes as it happened too quickly and the drone was not in our immediate flight path.

column121: On approach to ZZZ; the ATIS was obtained with reported winds of 340 @ 15 G 28. The airport was configured to land east; utilizing Runways XL and XXC. The First Officer (FO) and I discussed how this was odd and not desirable as the tailwind component with gusts was at our 10 knot limitation. Additionally; the ATIS stated there were wind shear advisories in the area. On final approach inside the FAF; maintaining target airspeed within Vref +10 was extremely difficult due to the high wind gusts; shifting winds; and shear. As pilot flying; my airspeed was hovering at +10 with occasional jumps up to +20. The wind vector on the MFD was showing winds at 030 between 20-35 knots until 100 feet or so; where the winds sheared to 340 at an unknown speed. I touched down within the touchdown zone but off centerline by approximately 10 feet. Taxi to the hanger was uneventful and no damage was noted. During the debrief; the FO and I agreed a go-around with a request to land on a Westerly direction should have been made. The cause of this event were gusty winds; poor traffic flow decision by ATC; and undesirable speed control by the Pilot Flying due to windshear.  ATC decision to land to the east with reported winds of 340 @ 15 G 28. This decision by ATC caused an unsafe tailwind condition when coupled with very strong and gusty crosswinds. Additionally; myself or the FO should have requested a landing to the west. However; the First Officer and I determined that any request would be denied as ZZZ has a reputation for not accommodating these types of request in order to maintain efficient traffic flows. There is pressure to land with the prevailing flow of traffic at a major airport like ZZZ when the crew sees other aircraft landing in the existing conditions; even if the crew would prefer an alternate runway. I strongly believe ATC created an unsafe situation that may have inadvertently pressured pilots into landing with a tailwind in gusty conditions. When I made the decision to conduct the approach; a go around would have been the correct action when the wind sheared at 100 feet.
column125: Air Carrier Captain flying CRJ-200 aircraft reports unsafe tailwind condition during approach to landing.

column121: The Captain was flying. We got switched from the ZZZZZ to the ZZZZZ1 arrival and told to keep 300 knots. ZZZ was busier than normal since COVID and we got task saturated. They cleared us for the ILS XX approach and direct to ZZZZZ2 to join the final. We were then cleared for the approach and proceeded to descend to 2;300 feet via VNAV before hitting approach mode. Since ZZZ had wanted us at 300 knots we were in manual speeds. We descended below 10;000 feet at 300 knots and noticed it at about 9;300 feet. We leveled off to slow to 250 knots by 9;100 feet and continued as normal. It was a simple and common error caused by task saturation and typical ZZZ switching approaches 3 times and arrivals twice. ATC did not say anything.This is a common issue with ZZZ but also something we should have seen. We were more careful to monitor throughout the rest of the trip. This is also an issue of COVID and not flying much. No pilot is flying the amount that we used to. Especially Captains and First Officers on reserve. That is not an excuse; but an observation. ZZZ was busy like it used to be and none of us have been used to that since before the pandemic.We will be more careful; and I will more closely monitor events as well as prevent task saturation so this doesn't happen again.

column121: During final approach to ZZZ; We were instructed by Approach to descend and maintain 7000 ft. and expect vectors for visual approach Runway XX. When we were crossing approximately 9000 ft. and 235 KIAS PF (Pilot Flying) requested flaps 9. PM (Pilot Monitoring) double checked the airspeed and proceeded to select flaps 9 position. Right after moving the handle to the 9 position; we got the EICAS message FLAP FAIL. PF took the radios and requested ATC to stop the descent at 8000 ft. because we have to run a checklist and ATC cleared us to stay at 8000 ft. PF leveled off and PM took the QRH and started looking for the associated procedure. PF and PM agreed to follow procedure XXX-XX-X FLAP FAILURE and PM started to read every step in the checklist. In that moment PF requested a Hold while we solved the issue but after receiving confusing ATC holding instructions; PF requested a square box flying vectors and [advised ATC] due to Flaps Failure. We followed every step in the checklist; verified the new approach speed; the new required landing distance; Pulled the J7 CB; sent an ACARS msg to company and advised FA (Flight Attendant) of the issue. After double checking that conditions were safe to land and the available runway landing distance was suitable for the situation; we decided to continue with the approach to Runway XX and advised ATC. ATC gave us vectors for the visual approach and a normal landing was performed. Brake temperatures were ok after landing and no further issues were found during the rolling after the touchdown; then we requested a normal taxi to the ramp and a normal deplane process occurred. No injuries were reported. At the gate; captain pulled out the information from maintenance messages and FLAP JAMMING and FLAP LH MOTOR BRAKE FAIL were found.
column123: On descent to 7000; we were at speed 220 and I asked for flaps 9. We received EICAS message flaps fail. I asked FO (First Officer) to recycle back to zero. We asked ATC to hold and they had us stop the descent maintain 8000. We leveled off and requested box turns. We ran flap fail checklist. Communicated with Dispatch and FA (Flight Attendant). [Advised ATC]; landed with no issue. I [Advised ATC] because at zero flaps; possible long landing with higher approach speed is expected as well as potential for hot brakes. So we wanted to err on the caution side. Great CRM by crew.

column121: ZZZ Approach was closed for staffing reasons due to COVID illness / quarantine on the midnight shift.  Sector X (my sector) was in control of the airspace.  Sector Y had Aircraft X arriving to ZZZ from the southeast.  The pilot dialed up the ATIS at ZZZ which told the pilot to come up on my frequency for a weather update. I read the weather to the pilot then sent them back to sector Y freq. I kept the datablock up on my scope assuming that I would get a hand off soon and clear the Aircraft X for an approach. Since ZZZ is really close to the boundary with [sectors] X / Y; the Controller decided to work the aircraft all the way into ZZZ.  I treated it like a point-out as did he although we made no official coordination.At the same time; I had another aircraft (Aircraft Y) about 60 miles north of ZZZ inbound to the field and was asking about runway conditions at ZZZ.  I had a number to call the field operations for updates because the TRACON /Tower was closed.   When calling from the line; I got a recording staying the 'This line is no longer in service and to hang up and try again.'   I asked the second Controller in my area if he knew of an issue with the phone number.   He got on the Supervisor's phone and called for an update.  The field ops told him about the snow on the runways as well as Runway X Left being closed for snow removal. This was not in our NOTAMs.  That Controller then ran down to the other area who was working the Aircraft X and told them what was going on.  Luckily the Aircraft X wasn't too low for radio coverage; the Controller issued them present heading and a climb back up to 6;000.  The Aircraft X was then put on my frequency where I let them fly out to the west a bit farther and set up for a turn back to the IAF for Runway X Right.  The plane then landed safely at ZZZ.There should have been formal coordination between Sector Y and myself on who was working the Aircraft X into ZZZ.  It doesn't change the fact that a runway was closed at ZZZ without a NOTAM in our system.  The only reason we found out was because of the Aircraft Y inbound from the north asking about updates on the field conditions and the other Controller making the phone call for me.  I'm not sure how long it takes for NOTAMs to populate in our system but it's not fast enough.  I guess they hit enter on the keyboard and immediately jumped in snowplows and took to the runway. The NOTAM did eventually populate in our system; but it was after this event took place.
column123: I was notified of a poorly handled situation on the mid shift.  There were several major breakdowns in communication causing a 'swiss cheese' situation that could have ended devastatingly. Likely some minor changes to ZZZZ/ZZZ (ZZZ ATC 0) Contingency Plan. At roughly XA:00 in the morning Sector Y was working Aircraft X; during the enroute/initial descent portion of the flight arriving ZZZ. Keeping in mind ZZZ Approach is ATC 0 and Sector X assumes ALL of their airspace with procedures in place. At some point Aircraft X calls Sector X; likely on a second radio while in communication with Sector Y; to obtain the weather since the ATIS is not updated hourly and instructs pilots to do exactly that for the latest weather and field conditions. Presumably the pilot was then instructed to return to Sector Y frequency since the flight was a little ways out (and no radar contact) where Sector Y proceeded to work the aircraft all the way into the airport with no coordination to Sector X. Sector X Controller proceeded to watch this unfold without attempting to initiate the coordination to Sector Y to say 'radar contact' or even 'point-out approved' since there was no traffic for this aircraft. This was wrong in and of itself for several reasons including questioning the reach of Sector Y frequencies.Sector Y has this aircraft set up for an instrument approach; runway unknown to Sector X. Simultaneously; Sector X is in communication with Aircraft Y; inbound to ZZZ. The pilot of this flight asked us for runway conditions. The Controller working the sector was unable to get a hold of ZZZ airport operations with the line for an unknown reason. The Sector X; A-side called on another phone and after a brief conversation with operations learned that operations had tried calling Sector X on frequency to inform us that Runway XL is closed while they clear the runway of snow. It was also about this time that the NOTAM populated in ERIDS (En-route Information Display System) which is definitely not enough time between NOTAM being received and giving [the] Controller time to see [the] NOTAM relative to the location and phase of flight to Aircraft X arrival; especially if NOTAMs had already been checked and issued at the initial descent per 7110.65. While this phone call was taking place; Aircraft X was on final at approximately 3;500 feet or less. A-side ran to Sector Y and asked what runway the flight is set up for and was told Runway XL and was immediately told to cancel approach clearance and climb aircraft to 6;000 runway heading and place the aircraft on Sector X's frequency. It's amazing that Sector Y's frequency was able to even reach the aircraft. With poor visibility and snow plows kicking up snow; the plows would never have been seen even if the runway had been.Recommendations:1. Airport operations not getting a hold of an actual person to communicate runway closure and in a timely manner.2. Sector Y working an aircraft into airspace that wasn't their own. Breakdown in understanding ownership of ZZZ1 or negligence?3. Sector X Controller not initiating 'radar contact' of this flight.4. Retrain OM's.  Many overnight OM's are not properly executing text files at time of ATC 0 placing ZZZ approach airspace at a sector that's adjacent to the actual sector being worked. Sector X is one scope on the mid at Sector Z. ZZZ approach is being opened at Sector X and more often than not; is NOT followed up with a CS message to combine with Sector Z. Until a few years ago; Sector X was the mid scope and text file needs updated. This second step was clear in an email prior to this period of ZZZ; ATC 0 but is being overlooked. Controllers in Sector X assume it's being done correctly by having the airspace next to them and don't speak up about it. So if an aircraft didn't call Sector X for weather; Sector X could potentially be completely unaware of Sector Y working aircraft into ZZZ as 'auto-pop' would happen on the adjacent scope. During this incident; ZZZ Approach was opened at the adjacent scope as I was told. That did not cause this though or factor into it; however.5. Airport operations told to contact ZZZ Center on frequency which was never going to work. Needs to be a phone call to center and nothing done until this takes place.It was discussed this morning after this incident that regardless of the weather; ZZZ (Sector X) shall reach out to airport operations for ALL arrivals to make sure they are clear of the runway. Supposedly one of the operators on the runway had a radio with a dead battery and obviously would not have heard a pilot calling short final or any other calls. Why wouldn't a radio be tested with another handheld and how could it have been unknown that it was dead?Changes to the ATC-0 plan may need to happen to stop this from happening again.

column121: Getting radar vectors to intercept the final approach course to Runway 34L RNO;  ATC instructed us to fly direct to SPOON at 11;000 ft and intercept the localizer. The direct routing to SPOON took us on a westerly heading approximately 90 degrees to the final approach course. SPOON is defined as a radar fix on the approach chart and is D24.6 from the field. As such; we were reluctant to use the localizer to guide our turn to final and instead used GPS to navigate to SPOON and turn onto final. Upon overflying SPOON; the GPS commanded an aggressive; yet smooth; right turn to final. Once on final; we intercepted the localizer and descended on the glide path while observing the altitude restrictions at APIME; WAGGE; and EPOSE. The FO (First Officer) and I were fully 'dialed in' for this approach as we had briefed the procedure; identified the surrounding terrain; and associated threats. Landed uneventfully. Three days later I was advised ATC identified our airplane as one that lost acceptable separation with terrain during our approach. The FO was making his first trip to RNO and I had only been to RNO a few times in at least a few years. We were initially planning to fly an approach to the south until ATC told us to expect one to Runway 34L. The routing to SPOON put us at (approx.) 90 degrees to the final approach course with (from memory) an unreliable localizer signal at D24.6. That large angle and our reluctance to use the localizer so far from the field forced us into using the GPS which caused an overfly situation; and thus; and overshoot of the final approach course. I think getting a heading to intercept the final approach course inside SPOON and at a lesser angle would alleviate the overshoot possibility and the resulting loss of separation.
column123: We were cleared to and were proceeding direct to SPOON at 11;000 ft on a track of approximately 070 when we were cleared to intercept the localizer for the ILS 34L.  The Pilot Flying (Captain) and I discussed intercepting the localizer so far from the field (SPOON is 24.6D from the field) and the PF elected to stay in Nav [mode] until we had turned on to the final.  We saw the line the nav was going to take and that it was going to fly over SPOON and then turn us onto final with a slight overshoot and the PF verbalized this.  We had done an extremely thorough review of the RNO brief and of all of the pages in FD Pro and based on the clear weather and our awareness of the terrain hazards we concluded that this would be an appropriate course of action.  We overflew SPOON; 'nav'd' to the final approach course and then selected the approach mode and flew the rest of the approach and landing uneventfully. The extremely steep intercept angle and relatively close proximity to SPOON when we were cleared to intercept the localizer along with the far distance from the field.  Emphasize to both ATC and aircrew the ramifications of a steep intercept angle to final.

column121: Upon being vectored for the ILS approach to Runway XX at ZZZ; ATC cleared us to descend from 3;000 MSL to 2000 MSL. The PNF was distracted while trying to control the cabin pressure that had been wildly uncontrollable since the beginning of descent from FL410. As the PIC and PF; I set the Altitude Alerter to 2;000 and vertical speed to 700 FPM descent; with autopilot engaged. During the descent to 2;000 MSL; I briefly looked up at the Pressurization Control Panel to monitor the PNF progress in controlling the cabin pressure; I noted the cabin pressure was finally close to the aircraft's actual altitude. I directed the PNF to select 'RAM AIR' ON.  That action stabilized the cabin for landing. ATC called to confirm our 2;000 feet clearance limit and I realized that the aircraft did not level off at 2;000 feet and the autopilot was not engaged. I acknowledged our clearance to ATC; manually flew the plane from 1;700 feet to 2;000 feet and reengaged to Autopilot. Since the cabin pressure was finally under control; an uneventful approach and landing were completed. Due to the distraction of the cabin pressure control issues; as PF I briefly failed to monitor the aircraft flight profile. Since I had tasked the PNF to attempt to stabilize the cabin pressure; I take full responsibility for failing to continuously monitor the aircraft flight profile.No matter what cockpit distractions occur; it is incumbent upon the PF to continuously monitor the aircraft flight profile. And if using the Autopilot; PF should continuously monitor that it functions as programed and does not inadvertently disconnect.
column123: The flight went well up to the time of descent when the cabin pressure started to malfunction. All attempts were made to control the pressurization using ADC 1 & 2 and Manual control. As preparations were made for landing; air traffic control issued further descent to 2;000 feet and the flying pilot was in control of the aircraft with the auto pilot engaged. The aircraft did not capture the 2;000 foot altitude because the autopilot was inadvertently disconnected and the aircraft continued descent to 1;600 feet at which time the deviation was noted and immediately corrected.  The aircraft was subsequently vectored onto the final course and the rest of the approach and landing was uneventful.  The pilot monitoring should have noticed the altitude deviation as a result of the auto pilot not being engaged while manually controlling the pressurization and the pilot flying should have been aware that the auto pilot was not engaged. It is imperative that the pilot flying concentrates on flying the aircraft while the other attends to the malfunction and refers to the checklists.
column125: GIV flight crew reported an altitude deviation occurred when they became distracted by a cabin pressure issue and did not notice that the autopilot had disconnected.

column121: Day VMC. Conducted initial approach [Runway] XXR. PF (Pilot Flying) called missed approach into ZZZ [Runway] XXR due to windshear with airspeed loss below 500 ft. AFE (Above Field Elevation) with advancement in override thrust. Subsequently received windshear caution. After selecting TOGA and pitch up flew flight director. No windshear escape guidance provided as the windshear was not sustained. Thrust was advanced to GA (Go-Around) RSV as a precaution. Stable in the missed approach maneuver with no caution windshear; PF called Flaps 4 due to flaps full configured. Gear retracted at a safe altitude with positive climb rate. Flew ATC assigned runway heading with missed approach climb profile. Subsequent approach flown to the ILS profile with Flaps 5. Flew the glide slope to RWY [Runway] XXR. No windshear event occurred. Received wind report with gust factor 28 kts. Selected manual speeds to increase approach sped until 4-5 kts. until after passing 500 ft. AFE 'stable' call the target speed was reduced. Maintained visual descent rate with visual cues and instrumentation of the ILS. During round out to flare automation set the aural 'sink rate' message. FO (First Officer) announced correcting due to the aural call and confirmed with a cross check the sink rate was induced by maintaining descent rate with the descent maneuver to coupled round out pitch rate simultaneously for the landing maneuver; not due to windshear as the speed was stable. FO and CA (Captain) on debrief both acknowledged the aural alert at post flight and both agreed with given conditions the approach to landing was stable given the conditions. Touchdown was within LTP (Landing Threshold Point) distance.Second approach with Flaps 5 configuration allowed for a higher approach speed and acknowledging the wind factor with increase in target speed for gusty conditions allowed for less susceptibility for the same condition. Automation with sensor inputs from other automated systems is subject to its own set design limitations and may not reflect positive confirmation of the event with pilot judgment of the same situation monitoring the same event. Such as automation latencies detecting the windshear event prior to the windshear caution; or sink rate alert while the pilot is initiating real time assertive maneuvers in turbulent conditions. Inertial vertical speed descent rates measure acceleration as it filters out noise of turbulence conditions causing a lag as opposed to real time judgment of the flight crew.
column123: Approach into ZZZ on our 1st attempt we conducted a missed approach due to wind shear. On the 2nd approach in the flare we briefly went to a sink rate of 1;100 [ft. per minute] the FO (First Officer) was already correcting and we received a 'sink rate' aural tone just prior to touchdown.

column121: Working a slow but steady local session with Supervisor working GC/CD combined. Very low staffing due to COVID. Steady traffic all day; I wouldn't call it too busy though. Very last transmission of my shift was to Aircraft X.Aircraft X called on about a 7 mile final (new pilot). I cleared it to land on Runway XX right and then started my position relief briefing within seconds of my transmission.Finished the brief and my 2 minute overlap; said bye to my fellow coworkers and left. Aircraft X was probably on a 2 mile final by then.I was paged at the bottom of elevator and Supervisor asked 'Did you give Aircraft X Runway XX right or Runway XX left'? I told him I didn't remember.Turned out aircraft landed on Runway XX left instead of Runway XX right. No separation issues luckily.All they said was 'cleared to land' I didn't even realize it at the time.I was distracted; ready for the briefing to be done after a long; busy day with a skeleton crew.Expectation bias all the way; I just assumed they said XX right in their transmission.All controllers tend to get complacent in this job when everything goes right for some time; I need to stay vigilant and listen to every transmission. If I would've just listened to that pilot's readback instead of thinking about getting off position and being done for the day; I would've noticed it and I would've got verification that she was cleared to land on runway XX right and all would've been good.

column121: Upon arrival into the terminal environment at MGM; flight crew was informed of a portion of runway lighting reported as inoperative. Information was not published in NOTAMs and Dispatch was not aware. During the time spent in researching suitability of the given conditions; the flight crew observed Rwy 28 with VMC conditions. The crew determined that the runway lighting that was visible was suitable to execute a safe landing. Some factors to take into account: filed alternate was ZZZ. Crew knew that the weather to get back to ZZZ was not ideal. Having experience occasional moderate turbulence; and 30 knots more headwind than planned. Extended taxi time leaving ZZZ; and stair-stepped altitude climb out had us approximately 300 pounds behind on fuel. Having had a same day previous min fuel declaration; we determined ZZZ was not best action. In our judgement at the time; landing at Montgomery was the safest course of action. Unreported facility malfunctions.Safest decision was madeBuy more time. Request vectors; or holding to further coordinate airfield suitability with all available resources.

column121: While we were on base leg on the RNP Y Runway 2 approach to MGGT [airport]; someone on the ground pointed a green laser and it flashed in the cockpit a few times; twice hitting me on the right eye. We were in between GT504 and GT503 with the aircraft fully configured; I was PM and since it was my first time flying into MGGT; I was looking outside to get a mental picture for future reference and situational awareness; trying to identify the runway;  when I saw a green laser beam being pointed at the aircraft; I called it out to the Captain and he kept his head down but I was hit twice on the right eye before I could go head down. I reported it to the Tower (in Spanish). The Captain asked me if I was OK; and I just mentioned that my eye felt drier than normal. (I've had the misfortune of being hit by a laser while flying before; so I knew the sensation). We continued to approach and landing without any further incident.Unfortunately; these types of events are almost impossible to stop; unless we wear protective eye gear; there is nothing we can do but lower our heads if a beam is being pointed to the aircraft.
column123: While on approach; circling left arc to final; RNV Y 02; MGGT; about 145 knots and 2;000 feet AGL; FO (Non-Flying Pilot) observed a green laser coming from the low 10 o'clock position.  We continued approach to landing. FO reported it to ATC; giving them location; and Controller said it's been happening more often now and that they'll report it to authorities. I asked FO if he was OK and he said his eyes were drier than normal.Suggestions - Patrol the terminal area in vicinity of airport more often. Make area more sterile. Prosecute those who attempt to lase airplanes as they land at airports.

column121: Tuned radio to frequency while approaching ZZZ airport from the west.  I listened for traffic at ZZZ and announced intentions to land on Runway XY after crossing midfield Runway XY-YX.  These announcements were 10 miles and again 4 miles west of the airport.  Crossed midfield from the west; made right turn to the south to enter Runway XY from 45 degrees. Announced again on frequency entering left downwind Runway XY; announced base; then final for Runway XY.  Saw that I was high and went around.  At midfield; while departing and at about 100-200 feet AGL; I saw another aircraft approaching from straight ahead and about 75 feet above me and to the left.  The opposing aircraft banked right; as did I. The other aircraft must have been landing on Runway YX and was also going around; because I did not see an aircraft on the runway or taxiway as I approached.  I checked the airport traffic CTAF against my entered radio frequency and saw the correct frequency was frequency.  I had been announcing on frequency and did not realize I was on the wrong frequency. I had made an error in tuning radio and did not hear the airport traffic; nor did they hear me and my announcements.

column121: Randall Airport; 06N; in Middletown New York has often been a decent place to train. Runway 8 is noted as having a substantial displaced threshold and the winds on that day favored it. At 150 feet AGL my student elected to go-around and when I questioned why they said; 'I cannot read any of the runway markings nor see the displaced threshold.' They were right to do what they did. The runway is in a sad state of repairs with large cracks and areas of heaving. But worse; the paint is so faded that an unsafe landing could be made without even knowing it on the displaced threshold(s) especially on Runway 8. I implore the appropriate people be contacted so the owner/operator of this field can do some maintenance on this field to keep it open; safe; and operational.

column121: I decided to do some practice approaches with my safety pilot. I got gas at ZZZ1 and it was a direct line from there for the approach into ZZZ so I did that one first. I got the weather; which was favoring the opposite direction; and changed to the advisory frequency. Another airplane announced that it was on the the approach for Runway XX but was farther out than we were. I announced that I was on the approach for Runway XY and would break off early. There was a lot of chatter on the radio both at ZZZ and at other airports that shared that frequency. We heard someone else mention Runway XX but couldn't make out what they were doing. I broke off the approach at about 800 ft. and 2 miles from the end of the runway as I knew I'd be far from the aircraft on the opposite approach. That's when my safety pilot saw the near miss plane that had departed Runway XX at some point. We never heard them announce taking off or on upwind and they did not show up on ADS-B (Automatic Dependent Surveillance-Broadcast). We immediately turned left for the missed but were past them by that point. After we passed them and announced the missed; the pilot of the other aircraft said something on the radio that I couldn't make out.Contributing factors were me continuing an approach in the wrong direction longer than I should have given the heavy chatter on the radio. The other aircraft was also not announcing enough or not announcing correctly or getting stepped on by other chatter or otherwise unintelligible. Another contributing factor was the other aircraft not having ADS-B Out installed. It wasn't required there but it would have greatly helped avoid the near miss. Given that we did hear another mention of Runway XX; I should have asked for their position since I didn't hear it the first time. In the future; I will break off opposite direction approaches much sooner when there is other traffic in the vicinity.

column121: On the Arrival into ONT SoCal Approach assigned vectors to sequence behind a 747. We were vectored directly behind and above the preceding 747 for 26L when we were cleared for the visual approach to 26R. ATC advised to maintain visual separation. At that point; due to concerns of descending behind the heavy aircraft; I offset right of course and attempted to parallel the 747. My intention was to remain clear of the 747's wake vortices. As we continued the approach and began to converge with the other aircraft; ONT Tower issued clearance to S-turn to the right if necessary. At that point we were too close to the runway to make any effective maneuvers and still maintain a stabilized approach. We then received a 'Descend' RA and continued descending on the visual approach to 26R while the adjacent aircraft executed a climb and go-around from 26L. In retrospect I believe a better course of action would have been to discontinue the visual approach or ask for delay vectors to increase separation from the heavy aircraft. ATC should have issued more realistic vectors to sequence us further behind the 747.
column123: Descending on the Arrival into ONT; SoCal Approach Control gave a maintain current HDG [instruction]. We visually acquired a 747 at our 12 o'clock position approximately 5 NMs. SoCal then gave us a right turn to a 140 HDG; then subsequently a 170 HDG that placed us at the 6 o'clock position of the 747 approximately 5 NMs behind. Once established on the 170 HDG; SoCal cleared us and the 747 visual approaches; 747 to Runway 26L and us to 26R and told us 'Caution wake turbulence and maintain visual separation with the 747'. The Captain did not want to be in a position to be at a 45 degree angle behind the 747; which would've increased the chances of getting into the wake of the 747; so as we were descending for the approach we received a descend RA and Aircraft Y executed a go-around for a climb RA. When we noticed that it would be challenging to do the approach and simultaneously staying out if the heavy aircraft's wake turbulence; should've asked the Controller for a vector or turn before starting the final portion of the approach.

column121: While on the RNAVM XX in ZZZ1 we encountered a GPWS event. At the beginning of the approach we had a tailwind and the descent angle of the approach caused us to be a little fast starting the approach. As the pilot monitoring I should have caught this issue sooner and brought it to the attention of the PF (Pilot Flying). Because of the speed the PF disconnected the autopilot/auto throttles and slowed the plane to the proper approach speed. During the turn onto final the PF was visual with the runway and let the nose drop which caused a momentary don't sink. He corrected and the warning went away. Still with the airport visual he tried to correct on the VASI and received another momentary don't sink call at around 700 ft. We continued the approach and the landing was normal. I don't have anything to add for [this report].I have learned many lessons from this very short flight. I haven't had much ZZZ experience and my FO (First Officer) who was PF has had very little. We tried to mitigate the possible issues by having a slow cruise speed of 210 kts. and trying to get ready for the approach early. We briefed the 3.4 GP and felt we were in a good position for the approach. With the unfamiliarity of the situation and the weather in ZZZ this day I missed the strong tailwind that was making our situation worse. This put us behind and we had to play catch up. It was turbulent the whole flight and when he disconnected the AP/AT my [situational awareness] went down because I was worried about his flight path at that time. As I look back at the event and if I could do this again I would have us discontinue the approach early and come around to try again. Once we continued and got out of the [weather] and had the runway visually climbing back into it in that terrain did not seem the safest option. But if we had bailed early it would have made the situation easier the second time.

column121: While flying into Burbank at night PF (Pilot Flying) / PIC (Pilot-in-Command) elected to do visual to [Runway] 15. Clearance was granted around JANNY [and] PF took a course that meant we would intercept the final approach course from the left of centerline. PM (Pilot Monitoring) noted that we were left of centerline and that there was terrain in the area. We crossed over the ridge line at the standard 5;500 ft. but thereafter while still left of centerline the PF started a descent. PM had terrain display up on MFD and was watching; there was never a yellow or red terrain ahead of the aircraft at anytime. Yet PM noticed that the radio altimeter was showing a reading while still left of centerline. The radio altimeter reading went below 1;000 ft. and got to around 800 ft. The PM called out the altitudes as soon as they were decreasing below 1;000 ft. and the PF took evasive action by disconnecting the auto pilot and stopping the descent. Almost immediately after the radio altimeter reading went away and we continued a normal descent to landing on [Runway] 15. Taking a visual to [Runway] 15 at night is unnecessarily risky. With margins very tight on the left side of the approach course; situational awareness is reduced at night. The benefits certainly do not outweigh the risks. We never received a GPWS yet we came within below 1;000 ft. of terrain on around an 8 mile final. It is easy to become complacent with your surroundings when you can not see them; so don't bother putting yourself in this situation... The company needs to put into writing that visuals to [Runway] 15 should not be allowed at night. As PM I should have called an immediate go-around the second I saw the radio altimeter go below 1;000 ft.
column123: High terrain in the form of a mountain ridge exists just north of BUR. The BUR airport is located at the east end of the San Fernando Valley. Additional mountains are present just to the east of BUR.Discontinue night visual approaches to BUR Runway 15.
column125: Air Carrier flight crew reported flying uncomfortably close to terrain on a night visual approach to Runway 15 at BUR; and suggested that the procedure be restricted to daytime operations.

column121: I was the PNF and the First Officer on this flight to ZZZ. On our approach into ZZZ we were slightly high on vectors and then cleared for the visual approach. The Captain PF was at a speed of 230 knots indicated airspeed (KIAS) and was trying to descend and slow to land. He was unable to slow to 200 KIAS as we were entering the Class C airspace and we determined the approach was unstable and executed a go-around. We entered the traffic pattern and landed successfully. I believe the cause is a decrease in proficiency due to reduced flying during the pandemic. We were doing 220-230 KIAS while entering the Class C airspace.

column121: While on upwind leg on a go-around from Runway XX at ZZZ we were alerted by our ADS-B of an aircraft 400 feet below us. This traffic appeared to be on the upwind from the parallel runway also in use at the time. I was not able to visualize the aircraft below us and now ADS-B was indicating the aircraft 300 feet below and directly underneath our aircraft. I took the control of the aircraft from my student; who was maintaining runway centerline; and initiated a left turn to avoid a possible collision while continuing the climb. Before I could contact ZZZ Tower they inquired about our abrupt course change. I informed them of the traffic advisory; mistakenly stating it was a TCAS advisory instead of an ADS-B traffic alert. Though having had a near midair collision over a year ago; I know the ADS-B in the aircraft I was flying is very accurate. This was at least a near midair collision and my best estimation the second aircraft was drifting into our flight path. ZZZ Tower requested a phone call once the flight was complete and I complied with the request.

column121: On visual approach; turning base to final inside 5 miles to touchdown on Runway XXL at ZZZ; the #1 Engine lost thrust and rolled back. It indicated 20 to 21% N1 for the remainder of the flight irrespective of the thrust lever position. I manually flew the airplane to the runway and landed. I requested my FO/PNF (First Officer/Pilot Not Flying) notify the Tower we had an engine failure just after passing below 500 ft. AGL. The Master Caution illuminated in flight; with ENG illuminated on the Master Caution annunciator. No other warning and no other caution lights illuminated. The engine ran at idle thrust and within acceptable parameters; but the thrust lever would not command additional thrust.Unknown why engine rolled back to idle in flight.Manually flew airplane single engine to a full stop landing on intended runway. Verified condition of engine my self and later commanding PNF to call out what he saw as failure. Notified ATC/Tower of engine failure.

column121: Practicing landings and flight pattern. As practicing the landing I was told to pull quickly for the flare process of the plane and caused a miscommunication as to pull on the yolk quicker. With the miscommunication caused the airplane to balloon and land on the nose wheel hard and causing internal damage to the nose wheel internals; ripping almost all the screws off the nose wheel screws keeping it attached to the plane. After the incident the instructor took control of the plane and went on a go-around and landed the plane with the nose being as high off ground as possible.
column123: I was instructing my student on landing; focusing on the timing of flaring. He flared too much; which made him balloon. And to correct for it; he pushed down on the control to bring the nose down and landed nose first. The process was too quick for me to intervene. I heard a cracking sound as the nose wheel touched down on the runway. I executed a go around and made a soft field landing; wheelbarrowing the plane all the way down to the taxiway connected to our flight school's entrance. When I exited the plane; I visibly verified that the nose strut was sitting abnormally. I alerted our maintenance tech; who has verified that the nose strut was damaged.

column121: Briefed the ILS 26L IAH prior to top of descent (DRLLR 5). Center delayed our descent due to traffic. When descent clearance was given; told to maintain 310 kts. or greater. We were already over 6;000 ft. above descent profile. We were able to get back on profile just prior to approach handoff. When switched to Approach Control; they told us to expect RNAV Y 26R. After positive aircraft transfer control; I loaded the RNAV 26R and briefed the approach. We were expecting vectors to final RNAV Y 26R. I became PF (Pilot Flying) again. We were still descending on the arrival passing through approximately 8;000 ft. and 6 miles from SKLER when approach cleared us the RNAV Y 26R approach. We then realized the RNAV approach started at SKLER and included an arc segment to final. The Captain reloaded the approach with the SKLER transition. As we approached SKLER; everything looked good as we accomplished approach procedures. Autopilot was still engaged. Approaching DRKAR; we were expecting the aircraft to start a right hand turn on the arc. It didn't turn as we continued straight ahead. Captain said we need to turn as I selected HDG SEL (Heading Select) to start a right hand turn on the arc. We were a couple seconds late to start the turn. At this time ATC said we were late on the turn and gave us vectors to intercept visual [Runway] 26R. Visual approach and landing were uneventful. We debriefed after block in and recreated load the FMC. Although the arc portion showed on the route; there was also a vectors line after DRKAR. We think this is why the aircraft didn't turn on the arc and maybe we needed to sequence the FMC.

The RV9 was asked to report us in sight. After several moments; they reported us in sight and Tower cleared them number two to land. We then turned base and the RV9 turned base inside of us. The RV9 was then cleared to land number one; however; he had already entered an evasive 180 degree turn to his right and when I gained visual contact again; he was directly above us with less than 200 feet separation. column121: Aircraft X entered a terrain area at 7;200 ft. with a Minimum IFR Altitude (MIA) of 7;400 ft. Aircraft X was cleared for the RNAV [Runway] XX approach. Aircraft X executed the missed approach. Aircraft X requested vectors for the RNAV [Runway] XX Approach. I issued a turn 30 degrees left when able direct to the Initial Approach Fix (IAF) and notify me when he was direct. Aircraft X reported they were ready for the approach clearance. I issued '[Aircraft X] maintain seven thousand until established. Cleared RNAV approach Runway XX at ZZZ.' Aircraft X read back the correct clearance.Aircraft X continued on their heading past the IAF without beginning a procedure turn for the approach. I was busy with multiple requests from other aircraft and fighters departing a military base for active Warning Areas. And didn't notice Aircraft X missed the fix. I was giving a relief briefing and noticed Aircraft X was not on his cleared route. I asked him where he was heading. Aircraft X stated he was waiting for vectors to the approach. I pulled up the MIA's and noticed he was about to enter a 7;400 ft. MIA block. I issued a safety alert for the terrain and climbed Aircraft X to 8;000 ft.In my scan I knew that Aircraft X was out of position but assumed he was unfamiliar with how to execute the procedure turn at the IAF and was going to get back on a correct course soon. Every time I checked back in my scan he was a bit further off course. The workload was high. Frequency congestion was causing a lot of missed and stepped on clearances further increasing workload. I had requested help from the Controller-in-Charge twice prior to this happening. I needed assistance with a flight break up flight plan and to notify the surrounding facilities the Special Use Airspace went cold. A dedicated D-side (Radar Assist) would have helped notice the aircraft off course.
column125: Center Controller reported frequency congestion and a high workload caused them to not detect an erroneous altitude read back and an aircraft flew below the Minimum IFR Altitude.
