column121: There was some confusion going into work as I was notified at XA:00 pm for my XA:30 shift  and told not to report to work until XD:45 due to Level 3 cleaning that was taking place. When we arrived at work the control floor was empty and ZAU ARTCC resumed operations at XD:55am with no briefings at all. I was working the MID configuration which includes the following sectors (sector numbers removed) along with the following approach controls (ZZZ1/ZZZ2/ZZZ3/ZZZ4/ZZZ5). During my stint which started at XF:00 am and ending at XI:00 am around XX:50 ZULU I spoke with Aircraft X looking for the ILS to Runway X at ZZZ2. I asked the pilot if he had the current weather at the field along with the NOTAMs and the pilot responded with a yes to the weather and that he had read the NOTAMs yesterday. I then read what I thought was all the pertinent NOTAMs to the aircraft. I vectored Aircraft X for the ILS at ZZZ2 and cleared the aircraft in for the approach. I switched the aircraft to advisories and requested a timely cancellation due to a second aircraft being vectored for the same approach. Aircraft X soon cancelled his IFR and reported a down time along with the condition of the runway and that the ramp area was in a much worse condition. I inadvertently missed the snow removal NOTAM and was later on informed by management that Aircraft X had landed on a runway with snow removal equipment on it. Any usage of the active runway should require call to either the facility or on frequency to prevent any confusion on what is going on at the airport. Any and all NOTAMs that directly effect the runways should be better identified. ZZZ2 had over 15 NOTAMs listed at the airfield which all look identical; if the ones like snow removal were BOLD or even a different color it may help the next person avoid the same mistake I made.
column123: I was notified today upon arrival to work that Aircraft X had landed on a runway occupied by snow removal equipment at the time. I was unaware that this had happened. I was working the assist position when the aircraft was cleared for the visual approach. The Radar Controller had issued the weather and read the NOTAMs to the aircraft and neither of us were aware that snow removal was taking place at the airport. The aircraft canceled IFR prior to landing. I was not made aware of the hazardous situation until the following work shift. This is the third report that I have written on this type of event at this airport! Several years ago; I myself had an aircraft execute a missed approach due to snow removal equipment on the same runway. I of course submitted a report detailing the situation and offered recommendations to prevent the situation from occurring again. I received no feedback. I told my Operations Manager; who was aware of the incident; and was dismissed. I then took it to the local safety council who actually looked into it. However; after some time I was informed that the current procedures would remain in effect. No definite reason was given. If a runway is possibly occupied by snow removal equipment; an aircraft should not be cleared for an approach. Having an aircraft switch frequencies to contact the snow removal crew themselves to land obviously has many drawbacks. The snow removal crew should simply notify the control facility prior to entering the runway. ATC could then close the runway until notified that the snow removal crew has exited the runway. This could be done by radio or phone; thus preventing the situations that have previously occurred.
column125: Center Controllers reported an aircraft landed on a closed runway due to snow removal equipment and not being able to identify the correct NOTAM for the runway closure..

column121: ATC assigned a vector '[Heading] 140 and maintain 3;000 ft. until established on localizer....' As Pilot Monitoring I heard the vector and maintain 2;000 ft. until established. Captain heard 2;000 ft. as well and entered 2;000 ft. I read back 2;000 ft. to the controller. The Captain briefed and all checklist items were briefed prior to this event. The Captain flew through the localizer due to his failure to move the localizer frequency from Standby to Primary. He quickly recognized it and visually maneuvered aircraft back toward course. We received a Terrain Alert and we discussed to shallow our intercept angle. The course was intercepted safely. The controller transmitted that the altitude was 3;000 ft. until established. We were at approximately 2;000 ft. at that time. I transmitted that we had heard '2;000 ft. until established' and read that back to him earlier. We maintained 2;000 ft. until glideslope intercept. We landed uneventfully.
column123: Beautiful day; 30 mile visibility; clear skies; airport in sight 30 miles away. Routine departure from ZZZ. At altitude; we were given direct to a fix and descend to 3;000 ft. Approaching the fix estimating 5;000 ft. altitude ATC gave us a clearance: 'Left turn of (EST) about 30 degrees and intercept localizer' which was inside of the fix 'and descend to 2;000 ft.' The First Officer clearly repeated to ATC '2;000 ft.' and no ATC correction was said. I set 2;000 ft. in the altitude alerter and pointed at it until the First Officer repeated '2;000 ft. A slight north wind caused my track just slightly south of center line of final; well within reason; estimate one dot right. As I was correcting and back on centerline the GPWS (Ground Proximity Warning) sounded; when back on center line the GPWS stopped. The hill was always in sight and we were never left of center line; never was there any danger. At that moment ATC asked if we had the airport in sight and we said yes. ATC cleared us for a visual approach to the runway and said our clearance was only to 3;000 ft. Uneventful landing. I clearly heard and after discussion; the First Officer also clearly heard '2;000 ft.""
column125: Flight Crew reported they read back to ATC descend to 2000 feet when ATC meant 3000 feet and then flew through the localizer and received a Ground Proximity Warning.

column121: The timing of the situation was ripe for a mistake; as a similar-sounding call sign received the exact type of clearance we would have expected as we approached the end of the runway while I was task saturated with completing the required checklist. Ideally; I would not have misheard the call sign; but ultimately I should have realized my attention had been entirely focused on the checklist; not the radio; and asked Tower for a short delay; clarification; or repeated clearance. We were operating the second leg of the day as Aircraft X from ZZZ to ZZZ1. We were given a short taxi from the terminal to Runway XXR. Approaching the departure end of XXR I switched to Tower frequency while still completing the Before Takeoff Checklist. As we made the turn from B onto B5 and while still finishing the last few steps of the checklist; Tower cleared another aircraft with a similar-sounding call sign; to line up and wait on XXC. I mistakenly thought the line up and wait clearance was for us and read back a clearance to line up and wait on Runway] XXR. Tower did not acknowledge my call; and while the Captain and I both saw an aircraft on final for [Runway] XXR; it appeared there was adequate spacing for the line up and wait clearance to be reasonable. As our aircraft passed the hold short line; Tower cleared the similar call sign for takeoff on XXC. The Captain and I simultaneously realized there had been a possible mix up of call signs. We advised tower we had read back the line up and wait clearance and asked for confirmation that it had been for us. Tower advised that it had not been for us; but cleared us for an immediate takeoff to accommodate the aircraft on final for [Runway] XXR. We immediately transitioned to a rolling takeoff and were able to get airborne before any go-around or other action was required by the aircraft on final.
column123: It did not sound right to me when the Tower said to line up and wait; but after the First Officer read back the instructions and the Tower did not correct us we pressed on. Next time I am in a situation that does not feel right; I will stop what I am doing and verify the instructions before taking the runway.We were taxiing to Runway XXR in ZZZ. As we were approaching the hold short line we heard the instruction to line up and wait. The First Officer read back 'Aircraft X line up and wait XXR.' Both of us looked at final approach and saw an  aircraft on approximately a 5 mile final; I verified this with my MFD. As we turned on to the runway the Tower cleared an aircraft for takeoff that had a very similar call sign as ours. At this point; I verified our instructions to lineup and wait. Tower said that he did not clear us to line up and wait. The Controller immediately cleared us for takeoff and so we took off.

column121: Prior to block-out of Aircraft X; ZZZ airport Tower and Approach Control closed due to a COVID cleaning. Dispatch informed the flight crew to remain in the gate and not block until a new flight release could be sent with an alternate. Additionally; a non-tower operation worksheet was needed which includes approval from the manager and flight operations duty officer. Once the crew signed the new release and had a copy of the approved non-tower ops worksheet; the aircraft blocked out and operated to ZZZ.  Later that night; the captain called the Flight Operations Duty Officer around XA:15pm to inform the Duty Officer that when he landed the aircraft in ZZZ; the airport lighting was not operational. However; the flight crew was able to safely land the aircraft in ZZZ because the moonlight was bright. There were no NOTAMs indicating the airport lighting was INOP. Additionally; Dispatch was never notified by the crew that the lighting was INOP. Dispatch was only notified by the Duty Officer four hours after the aircraft blocked in at ZZZ. I called the airport Tower after it reopened; and the controller stated he wasn't aware of any lighting issues and the lighting was operating normal.Even though this was a short flight; communication from the flight crew to dispatch was poor. They're several things Dispatch could have helped with prior to the outcome; including calling airport operations to inquire about the lighting issues and suggesting diverting to the alternate airport.
column123: Just before we were ready to pushback ground ops messaged us to let us know that ZZZ was going to non-towered ops for COVID cleaning. After we reviewed the FOM to ensure that we collected all the required documents and procedures.  We had all the required items per the FOM. With that we departed 1hour and 8 minutes late. We knew that Tower and Approach would be closed upon arrival. We were being vectored in by ZZZ Center. We requested to come in between ZZZZZ and ZZZZZ1. We requested the visual into Runway XX at ZZZ the weather was VMC.   On the base leg I has a visual on the PAPI and the captain intercepted final around 8 miles out and was flying the glideslope towards the runway. We were cleared the visual and switched to CTAF frequency.  We made multiple calls on final reporting our position. We also tried to activate the lights on the field 3 times with no success (there was nothing on the approach plate indicating it would be pilot controlled. But we hoped that it being listed as the CTAF would give us control). Around ZZZZZ the runway was clearly coming into view. By 1;000 feet we could clearly see the runway and all the markings. The Captain then proceeded to land and we taxied into parking. The landing occurred at XA15z which was in the twilight hours. At no point did we feel that we were in danger or unsafe. We mistakenly used the takeoff lighting requirements. After landing we looked everything up in the FOM to double check ourselves. We do feel it would have been dangerous to go around in a CFIT moderate environment with minimal coverage from Center. This was my second flight in ZZZ with the Captain this week. We were comfortable with the approach leading into Runway XX.The event was caused by ZZZ Tower not leaving the lights on when they closed and us as a crew using the takeoff lighting requirements vs landing requirements for CATI table.We could have delayed longer out of ZZZ1 to arrive when ZZZ was definitely going to be open. We could also call the Tower before departure to ensure that they leave the lights on. We could also plan to shoot the ILS all the way from the initial approach fix.

column121: During arrival to HAN; I was a jumpseat observer. We were at 2;000 ft. on the localizer; cleared for the ILS [Runway] 11 Left approach. Just outside the Final Approach Fix; I heard the Captain (Pilot Flying); say glideslope captured; and he set the missed approach altitude of 5;000 ft. in the altitude alerter. I was confused; because looking at the FO's (First Officer's) PFD (Primary Flight Display); I could see that we were below the glideslope; and it was no captured. I could not see the Captain's PFD to verify whether or not the glideslope was captured. The aircraft started descent from 2;000 ft.; even though we were not on the glideslope. I called out to check the glideslope and/or altitude. I did not have a hot mike; and maybe they weren't hearing me. The aircraft passed the Final Approach Fix approximately 500 ft. low. I called out the glideslope deviation again. The other jumpseat observer was also telling them to climb; and I yelled 'Go Around'. Then we got a GS caution with a line strike through the FMA on the PFD. At approximately 1;200 ft.; the go around was finally initiated with full scale deflection indicated on FO's PFD.During the surprise go around; we were instructed to maintain heading and climb to 5;000 ft. At some point the autopilot was disconnected; and situational awareness was deteriorating. The aircraft was veering to the right of track; 20 degrees nose up; and not following the flight director commands of 10 degrees nose up. I called out something like 'Who's flying the airplane; fly the airplane!' The flying pilot then steered back toward the flight director; regained control and the autopilot was re-engaged. After reaching 5;000 ft. we were vectored back for another approach to Runway 11 Left.Also of note; there was runway/taxiway construction at HAN airport that could have possibly created a glideslope anomaly. Though I could not see the Captain's flight instruments; it is possible there may have been a loss of glideslope indication at the start of the event. On the FO's PFD; I could see that the ILS was identified; and localizer/glideslope were displayed at all times.The Pilot Monitoring should have noticed on his instruments that the aircraft was not on glideslope; and should have called out the deviation immediately. When I saw the deviation; I was trying to alert the crew to fix the situation. I should have called out go around immediately.
column123: While inbound on the localizer for RNWY (Runway) 11L; the glideslope was captured and the 'G/S' FMA was active. Shortly after capturing the glideslope; the 'G/S' FMA blinked and had a yellow strikethrough. The glideslope then started to fall beneath us; while the autopilot tried to increase the rate of descent. The Pilot Flying switched to V/S [Vertical Speed]; and rolled in more V/S down to stay on glideslope with a more than normal descent rate. I instructed to 'level off; level off' followed by 'climb back to 2;000 ft. It was then I noticed the glideslope dot had blinked and reappeared at two dots high; indicating we were low. The Captain in the observer seat then said 'Go Around; Go Around'. The Pilot Flying initiated the go around with the 'TOGA' [Takeoff Go-Around] switches and sometime thereafter turned off the autopilot. He then hand flew the go around to 5;000 ft. and present heading. After notifying Tower of our missed approach; we were told to contact Approach frequency. During this event; with my seat position; I was only able to observe the Captain's PFD [Primary Flight Display]. After landing safely and gathering all facts observed between crewmembers; it was apparent that the autopilot tried to capture a false glideslope and failed; thus giving us the yellow strikethrough the 'G/S' FMA."

column121: I as Pilot Flying; was initially on a stable approach descending to 3;500 ft. on base for [landing]. I had briefed a Flaps 22 landing. As I was turning base to final (a left turn) and starting further descent; the winds shifted and resulted in a loss of tailwind; and my airspeed increased in the turn. At that same time Tower told us to turn 10 degrees right I believe due to traffic. Through all of this; my approach became unstable with too steep a descent; and got [we] a GPWS sink rate alert. I immediately executed a go around. ATC issued a new clearance and we came back and landed without incident. Suggest some more insight; and different scenarios taught on Flaps 22 landings possibly during training. Because of the higher airspeed vs. Flaps 45; it seems there is an increased chance of unstable approaches; especially if the winds shift as in my case. I carried too much power on this approach and also got distracted with the instruction from Tower to turn opposite of my base to final turn.

column121: During a tailwheel transition training flight; the aircraft departed the runway surface on landing. The winds upon time of landing favored Runway XX and a right-crosswind component of about 10 knots was present at the time of the occurrence. No other traffic was in the pattern and the approach was stable throughout. The touchdown was in a three point attitude and did not bounce. On the rollout; the student improperly managed rudder control and the aircraft rapidly developed a significant turn to the left. As Instructor; I took control of the aircraft and stopped the ground loop from further developing. The aircraft departed the hard surface of the runway at roughly a 10 degree angle at a fairly slow speed and came to rest in the grass about 20 feet from the edge of the runway. Upon shutting the airplane down; both the student and I exited the airplane to assess the situation. No signage or lighting was hit nor was there any damage present on the airplane. The grass was smooth and no holes were present.The situation developed from a normal crosswind landing to a runway excursion quickly. The cause was an improper control input by the student that was not caught in time by me; the instructor. Despite not reacting in time to prevent the excursion; the aircraft did not ground loop and no damage was sustained by the airplane. Any corrective action would be on my part as the Instructor; to take control sooner before such a significant turn could develop.

column121: I was entering the traffic pattern at 45 degrees for a downwind entry for RWY14 at FIT. I had previously announced my intentions and location on the 122.7 CTAF. I did not see or hear the conflicting traffic during its go around and climb out from RWY14.  I was alerted to the traffic by an ADS B traffic alert on my iPad. At this point I could not see the traffic in my field of view. I turned 45 to downwind and added power to get away from the traffic.  Earlier I had heard the conflicting aircraft on the CTAF advising it was on the RNAV20 approach and would be breaking off the approach for a straight in to RWY14. Calls to the conflicting traffic before and after ADS B alert we're not answered.It all happened very quickly and I only glanced at the ADS B display; figuring my attention should be outside the airplane looking for the traffic. But the combination of repeated ADS B traffic warnings and not being able to visually see and avoid the traffic was quite stressful.  Today; I was able to get the conflicting traffic's flight path from flight aware and compare it to my own from Garmin Pilot. It appears the conflicting traffic did a low approach to RWY14 and a go around; making a left climbing turn close to my own flight path. From the flight paths; the time stamps; and the fact that I never made visual contact with the conflicting aircraft; I think it passed behind me; and estimate it may have gotten as close as 500 ft.Being more vigilant to visually identify and track what I thought was landing traffic; would likely have avoided this dangerous conflict. The fact that the conflicting traffic never responded to my radio calls should also have raised a red flag.

column121: ATIS was advising ILS approaches in use. We requested the RNAV Y for pilot proficiency reasons. The request was approved by the final approach controller. At the end of the RNAV arrival; we were put on the published heading and told to expect vectors onto final for the RNAV Y approach by the final approach controller. We requested but were denied direct both initial approach fixes to start the approach. We were given 2 vectors; the last being [Heading] 290 and the clearance to fly heading 290 maintain 4;300 ft. until established on the final approach course cleared for the RNAV Y approach as we were vectored toward the course between the initial approach fixes. The approach became challenging at that point because the final approach course and glidepath anticipation cues were not visible and therefore we were unable to arm the approach. Additionally; the 4;300 ft. restriction left us high rather than leave us in a position to descend to 2;900 ft. in the space between the initial approach fixes because the width of the lateral course at that point is much narrower than a localizer signal. The IMC and terrain rich environment added to the concerns at this point. I feel that part of this challenge was created by the technique US air traffic controllers are using to get aircraft onto RNAV approaches. Rather that clear an aircraft to a fix on the approach; they are trying to vector them onto final as they would for an aircraft flying an ILS approach. My first safety concern is that there is not final approach course to intercept when flying an RNAV approach such as the localizer transmitting out bound from the runway for many miles. Rather there seems to be courses from one fix to the next all the way down the published approach. When ATC says 'fly heading xxx until established on the final approach' there isn't a final approach to intercept. I question whether a clearance of that wording is procedurally correct on RNAV approach. There are no examples I can find in company manuals or the Airman Information Manual the depicts starting an RNAV approach at anywhere other than a fix on the approach. I have had this type of vector onto final clearance for an RNAV approach for RNAV approaches in other U.S. destinations also so I feel the safety threat posed by this type of RNAV clearance is widespread in the USA. I have never received a clearance like that outside of the USA. Outside the USA; I have always been cleared to fix to start the RNAV approach. My second safety concern is that if that type of RNAV approach clearance is correct; we do not train that at any point during initial or recurrent training. All of our simulator training clears the aircraft to a fix on the approach when cleared for an RNAV approach. If the type of ATC clearance we got is procedurally correct we never see it in training.
column125: Air Carrier pilot reported ATC issued a vector to intercept the final approach course for an RNAV approach which does not have a final approach course. The reporter stated this type of clearance is issued routinely by U.S. ATC but overseas they are always issued direct to a fix to begin the approach.

column121: After doing 5 touch and goes on runway 28R; I received clearance to land. I did a wheel landing; let the tail come down; and when the aircraft was traveling slowly at about 15 kts or less; I started turning left to aim for the taxiway exit; and the aircraft kept turning to the left and did a complete 360 ground loop on the runway. The aircraft did not suffer any damage or touch any part of the aircraft to the runway; except the wheels of course; and I did not leave the runway. Due to this unexpected delay the aircraft landing behind me had to go around. I attribute this ground loop to unexpected strong crosswind; and the fact that we just put air in the tires and the aircraft was a lot more responsive to rudder control inputs that on my previous flight. She just got away from me for a moment.

column121: This was my first flight at the controls of a tail wheel aircraft. I was in the left seat receiving flight instruction from the CFI in the right seat. A thorough pre-takeoff briefing including experience levels and positive transfer of controls was accomplished before departure.As we approached ZZZ; we briefed the fact there was a right crosswind with gusts and the appropriate actions to take regarding this. The approach was stabilized. A normal wheel landing was accomplished and the tail was successfully lowered to the ground. The left main tire failed; at which point the instructor stated 'my airplane' and I released the controls. The resulting swerve was not controllable and the airplane ground looped approximately 150 degrees; coming to rest on the runway. We immediately shut down the aircraft and exited. No propeller or wingtip contact with the runway occurred. The aircraft remained on the runway. No injuries occurred.
column123: The mission was to fly with 2 friends and give them tail wheel instruction. There was a crosswind from the right and we discussed crosswind correction and the plan. [The pilot flying] conducted the wheel landing and shortly after putting the tail down; the airplane started shaking violently. I thought this may have initially been a tail wheel shimmy; which was odd since it didn't shimmy since the tail wheel springs were replaced in month; but the airplane started veering to the left so I announced 'my controls' and corrected it with right rudder. The shaking got worse and the plane then swung around to the right and ground looped. The wings did not contact the surface and there was no prop strike. The airplane was then towed to the hangar where I worked with a mechanic to change the wheel and tire so that the plane could be moved while inside the hangar. I did identify some contributing factors. There was a strong right crosswind; it was my student's first landing; and I had previous issues with my tires slipping on grooved runways. They are 8.50 oversize tires. I do believe the left main tire went flat on landing; and with the strong crosswind and delay of taking the controls from my student; it was unrecoverable. I'm filling out this report to detail human factors issues that contributed to damage to my precious airplane; and as the investigation unfolds it will determine if there is need for additional caution when using larger tires.

column121: Upon landing on the centerline of runway at ZZZ the airplane made an uncommanded turn to the left. Even with rudder input and braking I could not keep the airplane on the runway. The left wing clipped the 3;000 ft. remaining sign. Winds were 340 (degrees) at 12 (knots) gusting to 19; (pretty much straight down the runway). Inspection of the airplane showed that during the excursion the nose gear was completely sideways such that the 'Goodyear' was worn off of the sidewall.I do not have 'lazy feet.' I believe that my quick response to the airplanes movements prevented more damage as I was able to stop the airplane just off the runway. I have been flying PA46 airplanes for eleven years including 300+ hours in the Matrix and 1000+ hours in the Meridian/M600.There is no good explanation for the airplane's diversion. Subsequently I found out that there have been 5 other M600s that have experienced no less than 7 similar excursions. I and others believe there is a material problem with the PA46 M600.

column121: On arrival into ZZZ from the south; we were vectored off of the ZZZZZ procedure and held high due to traffic. We had been previously queried as to our RVR requirements for [Runway] XXR. We were set up and briefed for CATIII approach to XXR. Just prior to ZZZZZ waypoint; we heard traffic ahead cleared to descend via ZZZZZ arrival landing north. We were then given clearance to descend via the arrival landing north and were unable to meet the new crossing restrictions. Due to 71 knot crosswinds our heading was 320. The vector we were given for descent was 340. This was assumed to be a 360 for descent. As we turned through a heading of 260 ish we were told to make an immediate right turn for restricted airspace. We complied. We continued on the arrival and had an uneventful hand off to approach.I believe that the ATC is currently overworked due to COVID cutbacks. Many communications are being missed in the last months because ATC are covering too many sectors. Our lack of fore warnings regarding the changes to airport configuration left us with inadequate situational awareness. We followed the vectors given to us with incorrect assumptions. I'm grateful that this situation was resolved without incident. My recommendation is that we begin to re-staff ATC to normal pre-COVID operation.
column123: Flight was left high on the ZZZZZ Arrival.   ATIS had us landing on [Runway] XXR.   We were cleared for the ZZZZZX but never informed by ATC that ATIS X special was issued at XA22z we only learned that there was a runway change due from listing to arrival aircraft in front of us.   We were now high for landing [Runway] YYL by 6;000 feet at the same time we were instructed to slow to 190 kts.  We can slow down but not go down at the same time.   We were instructed to fly heading 340 as we descended the controller once again said turn left 340 which we were on and we assumed he wanted us to do a 360 which is common to loose altitude and rejoin the arrival.   He was busy with several other aircraft left high the weather was poor in mild turbulence.  The next call was for us to turn I believe 040 because he said we are going to hit a restricted area.I was heads down retrieving the new ATIS as well and programming the FMS for a different runway.    This was a combination of change of runways; poor weather; and aircraft being left high by two controllers and high winds with an approach to mins.   This was a series of events that lead to an aircraft being out of position.When the weather is poor we cannot skimp on air traffic controllers they need to be tentative to aircraft and where they are going.   If the aircraft is out of position.  It should be quickly realize especially when a runway change has been posted and an aircraft may not have received the special ATIS.
column125: Air carrier flight crew reported airspace; heading; and altitude deviations during approach.  The Captain stated a late runway change and Controller workload were contributing factors.

The first resulted in a uncommanded right roll of approximately 30 degrees followed by a left roll of 15 degrees. A go around was executed. The second was a uncommanded 20 degree left roll; with a prompt recovery and landing. No further wake was encountered. A go-around could also have been executed here; I planned to if the turbulence did not subside after the first correction. Fuel remaining was a factor in this decision; as it would put us below minimum (but above emergency) fuel for return. In both cases; a momentary (1 sec) stick shaker occurred as a result of being at Ref in a high bank angle.

column121: Aircraft X was vectored to their final approach course approximately 3 to 4 miles in trail of a preceding Aircraft Y. We were VFR conditions but had low ceilings hovering around 1;000 feet. Aircraft weren't coming out of the clouds until a 1 to 2 mile final. When Aircraft X joined the approach they has an absurdly high overtake speed with the Aircraft Y in front of them of 80 plus knots. There was no way separation was ever going to be maintained. When the realization set in of what was happening Local Control issued instructions to cancel the approach and to climb to 3000 feet and to turn 20 degrees to the left.  I began coordination with Departure Control for a handoff with vectors to bring the aircraft back around for another approach. In the meantime Aircraft X advised Local Control that they were minimum fuel and were continuing the approach. Local Control advised me of this; so I canceled the coordination; and redirected my attention to an aircraft awaiting clearance. Then Local Control advised me that Aircraft X was indeed going around with the 20 degree left turn; and 3000 foot altitude assignment. Traffic was light; so I instructed Local Control to climb Aircraft X to 6000 feet and issue a turn to get them into the dispersal area as per our joint agreement. I again initiated coordination with Departure Control for the handoff; with Aircraft X climbing to 6000 feet and on a 100 or 105 heading.Then I came back to the aircraft on Clearance Delivery and had them read back their clearance. During the read back; Departure was trying to call me and say that Aircraft X needed to climb immediately for an aircraft that was departing southeast bound off a satellite airport. I responded that the altitude had been issued and would find out why Aircraft X stopped their climb at 3000 feet. I relayed the information to Local Control as I was receiving it.  Local Control told me that Aircraft X would not accept the climb due to their minimum fuel situation; which I relayed back to Departure. Departure Control requested we ship Aircraft X to them; so that they could maintain separation with the traffic departing the satellite airport which I relayed to Local. Local was able to get Aircraft X to climb to 4;000 feet before shipping them. There was a loss of lateral separation on final approach; but there may have been altitude separation; although I am not sure about that. The problem is that we are struggling with spacing issues on final and even have a collaborative work group established with Approach to resolve these problems. Approach is continually handing Tower these overtakes;  and less than coordinated arrival spacing for departure gaps; but Tower is always held accountable for the consequences of each event. This is the standard here at  even though the separation problem originates with the Approach Controller. Even our local management tends to initially stand behind Approach and their actions while pointing their fingers at the Tower controllers trying to tell us that what we did was incorrect; even though we are only trying our best to prevent an separation error from occurring or getting worse.  Standardize spacing requirements here at 3 miles or required and coordinated arrival spacing distances and never transfer communications of aircraft on final until there is less than a 30 knot overtake speed. In other words; follow the guidance in the 7110.65 and ensure separation before transferring communications to another controller. And on top of this; performance management to ensure controllers are being held accountable to the basic standards.

column121: We were at 4;000 feet on a vector to final about 15 southeast of ZZZ when a TA came up on TCAS on the nose co-altitude. Right after it came up we were given one more turn to join final for [Runway] XXL.  In the turn the TA became a descending RA and we complied per the maneuver's section in the QRH.  We descended about 800 feet and finished the turn at which time we were Clear of Conflict.  As we leveled the wings we saw the traffic go by our right wing a little high as they were still at 4;000 feet; appearing to be no more than a half mile away. It appeared to be a smaller GA aircraft but went by quickly.  We told ATC we descended for an RA; at which time he said to just maintain 3;000 feet.  He seemed unconcerned and asked no questions nor gave us any information.  We never received a Traffic Advisory call prior to the initial TA. The Controller was busy; but did not seem to be swamped. Maybe staffing as a result of COVID-19 is a factor.

column121: On arrival into IAH we were assigned to descend via the LINKK1 RNAV arrival and subsequently cleared the ILS Runway 27 approach. We selected 1;500 ft. in the MCP to comply with the restriction at the FAF. Over GILLL intersection ATC asked me to maintain present speed of 240kts. I speed intervened to comply with the clearance as the aircraft would have started to slow to 210kts over RDFSH. The aircraft came out of VNAV Path into VNAV Speed and descended below the path. I went into vertical speed mode and selected a descent rate to approximate the path using the VDI (Vertical Descent Indicator) scale. My expectation was that I was going to be given a 'resume normal speed' clearance fairly quickly as there was not any traffic behind us as was apparent to me. Unfortunately; we were not given the clearance and I was slow to stop the descent using vertical speed mode over SYYKO intersection and elected to disengage the autopilot and hand flew the aircraft to stop the descent in a more expeditious manner. We climbed back to 3;000 ft. and resumed the profile uneventfully.

column113: Deviation - Altitude Excursion From Assigned Altitude; Deviation / Discrepancy - Procedural Published Material / Policy; Deviation / Discrepancy - Procedural Clearance; Inflight Event / Encounter CFTT / CFIT; Inflight Event / Encounter Weather / Turbulence
column118: Flight Crew Became Reoriented
column121: Flight was uneventful until cleared the ILSZ RWY 36 via direct QIT VOR at 18;000 feet assigned. A large build up was directly situated over QIT therefore ATC was asked for a left deviation due to the buildup. During the decent briefing the approach and contingencies were briefed; however; a storm disrupting the approach was not anticipated. A left deviation was started and I decided to proceed direct to point D111G as the best course of action. The FO had his terrain display in terrain mode allowing me to see any conflicts ahead. The FO correctly pointed out that going direct point D254I would be a better course of action and allow more time for the approach. I agreed and we asked for and was given direct to that point by ATC. At this point the situation was less than ideal. The weather was mostly cloudy with only brief glimpses of the terrain. I had the altitude of 10;500 feet in my mind thinking I was heading for my original point (D111G) when in reality we were required to be at 14;200 feet point D254I. I had started the descent when the FO pointed out the error and I immediately started to climb. At this point we were still not on the approach due to the weather. We were still proceeding direct to D254I. I was also slowing and configuring for the landing. I ended up disconnecting the autopilot in order to reestablish myself on to a stable approach. The terrain display never showed any conflicts and no verbal warnings of the system was encountered. I knew approximately where the aircraft was at all times. My error was in the decent altitude while forced off of the approach due to weather. We eventually did get on the approach; were stable when we broke out of the weather and had an uneventful landing. The large cell situated over the UIO VOR forced an unprepared diversion around it resulting in an off route decent in order to land. This in turn resulted in attaining a lower than desired altitude in heavily mountainous terrain. This was my first time into UIO. Although I reviewed the required airport familiar special qualification briefing; in this situation it was not adequate. I consider myself very experienced in mountainous terrain and I believe this particular field should require a Check Airman checkout. 'Picking the brain for gotchas' would have been invaluable.

column121: On [a] flight to XNA we were cleared for the visual traffic pattern to land Runway 16. The Captain as the pilot flying had properly briefed the visual approach; to include the obstacles in the vicinity and highest obstacle on the approach plate. On right base; the Captain turned autopilot off with flaps 3 and gear down. I announced visual contact/no factor on the highest obstacles out the right window - a set of towers. We continued to slow and configure for landing and maintain an altitude of more than 1;000 ft. AGL. We received a caution from the GPWS; followed by a PULL UP verbal command. The Captain took the appropriate actions and immediately increased the airplane's altitude. We were in daytime visual conditions; and in accordance with company policy; determined we were still in a safe position to stabilize and conduct a safe landing after clearing the obstacle. We finished the turn to final; configured the aircraft for flaps 5 landing; completed the before landing checklist; were fully stable by 1;000 ft. and in a safe position to land the aircraft. Upon landing; we debriefed the situation; reviewing the approach plate for any obstacles. At more than 1;000 ft. AGL and no visual contact with an obstacle; we were unsure why the GPWS gave us the PULL UP command; but agreed that it was the most appropriate and safest action to respond to the command as we didGPWS PULL UP verbal command resulted in appropriate action of increasing aircraft altitude.A visual traffic pattern is one of the most difficult approaches we fly. I would suggest additional training during initial and recurrent training on distances and altitudes for different types of visual traffic patterns. I will personally work to improve my awareness as the pilot flying to ensure 1;500 ft. AGL on downwind and the 3:1 descent path ratio throughout the visual traffic pattern. I will review my flying skills to ensure I am flying this way as well. While I believe we took the appropriate action both during the event and after in the debrief; I will use this as a learning experience to ensure I am always a safe and skilled pilot.

column121: After being cleared for the visual approach and landing into ZZZ on Runway XX; we continued to configure the aircraft for landing. We proceeded to descend below 3;000 just inside the ZZZZZ fix. As we turned final around 2;600 feet MSL around 1;300 AGL we received a 'Obstacle' GWPS warning which we were given an RA to pull up. I immediately complied with the command until it confirmed and we visually confirmed that we were clear and continued the approach to land without incident. During the incident we did not observe nor did Tower observe any obstacles that we were in close proximity to. We maintained proper clearance of at least 1;000 feet AGL from all of the published towers that were on the approach plate.Cause - There were no noted obstacles that were within 1;000 feet AGL clearance and can only come to the conclusion that it was an anomaly with the GWPS system.  At 2;800 feet we had turned inside the Tower that was 1;740 feet and turned well before the Tower at 1;809 maintain safe and legal separation from structures.Suggestions - Continue to follow protocol and respect a Resolution Advisory.

column121: I departed ZZZ on an IFR flight plan to ZZZ1. The aircraft had sat for roughly 3 days over the weekend before this flight and was difficult to start that morning (battery seemed weak). 40 minutes into the flight; the first symptoms of an electrical malfunction began to show up. I was roughly 15 nm from the IAF for the RNAV XX approach into ZZZ1 at 8;000 feet MSL; in actual IMC with light to moderate rime icing. I had just switched from ZZZ2 Center to the CTAF for the ZZZ1 airport when the auto-pilot for the aircraft unexpectedly disconnected. All of the lights on the autopilot control panel went dark; and the autopilot would not turn back on. Initially; I decided to continue the approach by hand and have maintenance check the autopilot malfunction on the ground. After briefly troubleshooting the autopilot; I returned to my instrument scan. At first glance; my HSI indicated that I was on Heading/Course. Needles were centered and heading bug was situated at the top of the instrument. But when I looked over at the Garmin 430 GPS; it indicated that I was off heading by 20-30 degrees. By the time I looked back towards my HSI; there was a 'HDG' flag starting to flicker in the top right hand corner. I looked across the panel at the co-pilot's instrument cluster and noted that the co-pilot's artificial horizon was flopped over; indicating a 45* right bank and 20* nose up attitude. The co-pilot's side DG was also off by 90* from my magnetic compass. As I looked back towards the pilot's side instrument cluster; my HSI began spinning around and around like a top.At this point; I had obviously determined that several of my gyroscopic instruments had failed; and I had difficulty determining which instruments were trustworthy and which were not. From this point on; I did my best to maintain straight and level flight with my remaining instruments and the Garmin 430 GPS ground track. I looked out the window at my wings (I was still in IMC and light to moderate icing [at] this point) and saw that during my troubleshooting I had enough ice accumulation that small 'horns' of rime ice were begging to grow on the leading edges and my de-ice boots were mostly white with a layer of ice. When I pressed the switch on the overhead panel to activate my de-ice boots; nothing happened. I immediately switched back to ZZZ2 Center and [received priority]; advising them that I had gyroscopic instrument failures and inoperative anti-icing equipment. I requested an immediate climb in an attempt to escape the icing conditions and find visual flight conditions before my remaining instrumentation failed; along with vectors back to ZZZ (which I knew had good VFR weather conditions...ZZZ1 conditions appeared to be worsening when I had last listened to the automated weather). ATC was extremely helpful and professional. Immediately offering any assistance they could; giving me no-gyro vectors towards ZZZ and approving a climb to 12;000 feet MSL. I had difficulty maintaining a heading and climb pitch-attitude initially; but got vaguely stabilized within a few minutes. Once stabilized; I noticed that the left alternator light was on; I attempted to re-cycle the alternator master switch twice without success. I then began to load-shed all electrical equipment which was not absolutely necessary for the continuation of the flight; and I advised ATC that I had experienced an alternator failure. 	I initially broke out of the top of the cloud layer and entered VMC around 10;000 feet roughly 15 minutes after the first indication of electrical failure. I had to request a higher altitude shortly thereafter as the tops of the clouds sloped higher towards ZZZ. ATC cleared me for a block altitude 8;000 feet to 12;000 feet; then eventually up to 14;000 feet; and authorized any course deviations necessary for me to remain in VFR conditions.Upon leveling off; I was initially concerned about what appeared to be a fairly significant performance loss. My first assumption was that it was due to ice build up on the wings/tail. In a level flight attitude; at 38 of manifold pressure and 2400 rpm (typical climb power) I was only getting 120 knots indicated airspeed. 160 knots to 170 knots indicated is more typical during cruise flight at a much lower power setting (30'/2300rpm). I alerted ATC that I was seeing abnormally low air speeds. But after a quick glance at my GPS (which was showing a 230 knots ground speed) I realized that the airspeed discrepancy was most likely due to pitot icing. ATC confirmed that they were showing me with a ground speed in the high 220's. By this point; the aircraft was out of icing conditions and flying just fine in VMC. So most of the potential danger had past. The last thing I had to do was search around for a hole in the clouds so that I could get back into ZZZ (there was an overcast layer between my aircraft and the ground). With the help of ZZZ3 Approach; ZZZ Tower; and several other aircraft in the area giving pilot reports; I found an opening near ZZZ4 and circled down for a visual approach into Runway XXL at ZZZ. The vast majority of the ice which had accumulated on the aircraft came off in the decent due to higher airspeeds and increasing temperatures. I landed without further incident; parked the aircraft; and immediately took down notes regarding the order of events while they were still fresh in my mind. I then called the Chief Pilot to relay what happened."

column121: While entering the downwind via the 45 for Runway XX; right traffic at ZZZ; we were following a Cessna. I was sitting in the passenger seat as the NFP (Non-Flying Pilot)/Safety Pilot observing my friends landing. We completed our landing checks and continued following the Cessna. As the Cessna reached our four o'clock position I told the PF (Pilot Flying) that he was clear to turn base leg behind the Cessna. We made our radio call; and turned to base leg. After we made another radio call to final; a young woman flying some sort of low wing canopy glass cockpit airplane turned directly in front of us and cut us off in the pattern. She did make a radio call announcing her turn to base. Approaching her turn to final she then queried 'how many airplanes are in the pattern and where are you'. I am sure she was completely unaware of our position. Out of the turn we were approximately less than a quarter of a mile at her seven o'clock position. Clearly she had no idea that she had just cut us off and was now on a simultaneous approach with another aircraft. The PF did not have the conflicting aircraft in sight until late after turning final. In the interest of safety; I took control of the airplane and elected to fly a parallel to her track. I passed by her left wing approximately 400 feet lateral distance and nearly level with her aircraft. Once I knew we were safely passed her aircraft I executed missed approach. We then came around for another successful landing to a full stop.
column123: As background; ZZZ was heavily congested with training and weekend aviators. The approach to the downwind leg was reported via UNICOM as 4NM southeast to enter a 45 - downwind entry. We coordinated spacing with another crosswind aircraft to follow us for spacing in the pattern. Our aircraft was operating with LED strobe lights and all landing lights for visibility to other aircraft.Reported downwind; uneventfully. And recognized the aircraft we were to follow and mimicked their pattern to minimize spacing conflicts. Reported our base turn to UNICOM; uneventfully. Our track on base leg was approximately 2 NM from the end of the runway. At no time was our location questioned by the conflicting near miss aircraft.Reported a base-to-final turn; uneventfully and unquestioned. Upon the completion of the turn to final; I focused on vertical alignment to the runway and did not see the conflicting near miss aircraft. The PNF (Pilot Not Flying); in a radio call questioned the conflicting near miss aircraft to determine her position upon hearing the UNICOM base report. After it was determined the conflicting aircraft turned inside of our location; resulting in cutting us off placing our aircraft in a hazardous flight profile; PNF took control of the aircraft in a professionally coordinated manner. At this time; the conflicting near miss aircraft was well within 1/4 NM and somewhat above our altitude; which was approximately 300 ft. AGL. Because of her proximity to us; and her apparently being unaware of our location; the resolution was complex. Keep in mind in her base to final turn (Right Hand pattern) with a low wing aircraft she would have then been unable to locate us visually. In a UNICOM radio call; the conflicting near miss aircraft questioned 'how many aircraft are in the pattern and where are you'; indicating her further lack of awareness to traffic as she was a close in final; an inappropriate time to establish traffic.Suspecting her unpredictable actions; we were prevented from a turn to side step since it would have blocked our ability to monitor her activities. Our inter-cockpit discussion created the point; given our closure rate; that our only recourse; in the interest of safety; was to parallel her path; fly past her which would have given her awareness of our location; then when ahead of the conflicting aircraft; we could safely initiate a missed approach; which we did.We remained in the pattern for another approach and full stop landing; uneventfully.

column121: After two right 360s we were told to extend downwind for runway X; and look for helicopter traffic (Aircraft Y) three miles west of the field at 700 feet. We called out traffic in sight; and continued. After Aircraft Y didn't respond to two radio calls; we were told to do another 360. Rolling out of the 360 we were told to look for Aircraft Y one mile ahead at 900 feet orbiting. We called traffic in sight; and began a climb to 1;300 feet. It appeared as though Aircraft Y was still climbing as they passed in front from our left to right. After a controller change; Tower called our position to Aircraft Y; they did not respond. Shortly after that call; Aircraft Y made a sharp climbing right turn directly into our flight path. To avoid a collision; I took the controls and made a climbing left turn as sharp as I could. Shortly after I started that turn; Aircraft Y banked sharply to their right to avoid us. I can't put an exact number on the distance between us; but it was close enough that I felt the need to bank sharply towards traffic on final; and Aircraft Y banked hard enough for us to see the bottom of their helicopter. Once a safe distance away; we turned to rejoin downwind; and began a descent back to pattern altitude. After that; Aircraft Y asked tower to keep aircraft away from that area while they followed a pursuit. Hearing other aircraft have difficulty with separation; we departed the pattern to ZZZ1 to avoid a similar situation.It is normal for us to fly over helicopter operations at 500-600 feet while in the pattern at 1;100 feet. It is not normal to fly the pattern with unpredictable traffic at the same altitude.

column121: Prior to top of descent we briefed the NNCEE 1 RNAV Arrival into IAH.  The arrival says to plan on flying the RWY 8R transition.  So we briefed the ILS RWY 8R approach.  Houston Approach Control then told us to fly the RNAV (RNP) Y RWY 9 approach.  The CA was the PM and told the FO he would change the approach in the FMC.  After selecting the new approach the CA told the FO that there was a discontinuity at HOWLN.  By selecting the HOWLN transition two separate HOWLN fixes were created separated by a discontinuity.  The discontinuity appeared at the top of page two in the legs page of the FMC.  So the CA brought up the second HOWLN fix and entered it into the discontinuity boxes and assumed that this action closed the discontinuity.  But he never went back to page one to verify that the discontinuity was closed and he did not ask the FO to verify it as the FO was busy managing the descent.  The MCP displayed LNAV and VNAV were engaged and so did the FMAs.  Approach Control then said at HOWLN you are cleared the RNAV (RNP) Y RWY 9 approach.  So the CA set FAF altitude of 2;000 in the MCP.  The FO went through LAVSFT and at HOWLN (IAF) had me set TDZE of 100.  Then we both said why isn't the airplane turning?  Tower immediately cancelled our approach clearance and vectored us for the ILS RWY 9 approach.  The CA was on a larger scale than the FO and noticed that the magenta line showed both the RF leg turn inbound but also extended beyond HOWLN as well and said I think I did not clean up the discontinuity correctly.  The contributing factors were both pilots did not verify the legs page after the Captain cleaned up the discontinuity and the high workload environment in IMC.  Additionally it was the FO's first trip in over a month.
column123: I briefed the ILS 8R per listed as the runway for flying the NNCEE1 Arrival.  During our descent when checking in with Houston approach we were given a new runaway RNAV Y runway 9.  The captain put the new runway into the FMS and Verbalized he was cleaning it for the RF.  I was reviewing the approach plate and didn't verify his FMS inputs.  The CA noticed right away that the plane should be starting to turn for the RF; which ATC cancelled our approach and told to fly a heading.

column121: On the arrival into ZZZ on the ZZZZZ1X arrival; we were told to maintain 250 knots to ZZZZZ; cleared the approach; ILS DME X rwy XXR. We had LNAV and VNAV engaged; I recommended the FAF altitude of 1300 feet be set in the MCP altitude window; the PF; F/O agreed and I set it. The controller told us to slow to 190 knots followed by a slow now call; the F/O (first flight from a long break; several months) was a little hesitant to get it done; so I told him he could hand fly bringing the nose up to slow aside from the speed brakes that he was using; or he could use vertical speed and decrease the descent rate VNAV was currently giving him. He opted to use the vertical speed. I got a call to switch to the Tower; as I did; I got distracted from resetting the MCP altitude of 2000 feet for the mandatory fix altitude at ZZZZZ1. We crossed ZZZZZ1 at approximately 1700 feet. Recognizing this; I had him climb back up to 2000 feet until we intercepted the glide path. We were visual with the runway the entire time and the aircraft in front of us; that is perhaps the reason I wasn't giving due diligence to my approach plate.

column121: We were descending via the ZZZZZX arrival with the Autopilot off - on profile and speed. FO decided to hand fly the remainder of the approach and landing and disconnected both the Autopilot and Autothrust near ZZZZZ1 intersection. A minute later I looked away and when I looked again; I noticed the FO was high on profile and I mentioned that we needed to cross ZZZZZ1 level at 6;000 feet. The FO increased the rate of descent and crossed ZZZZZ1 level at 6;000 but then continued to descend to approximately 5;900 feet and around 250 knots - with accelerating trend. I verbalized both altitude and speed; and the FO quickly recovered both. We were soon level at 6;000 feet - 240 knots...and soon to slow to 210 knots by ZZZZZ2. There was very light traffic in the terminal area and ATC made no mention of conflicts of any kind. We then proceeded to land uneventfully on Runway XX. The main contributing factor was the FO being new as well as not having flown much for the last several months. We fully debriefed everything; including what to improve on; once at the gate when duties permitted.

column121: We were descending via the DRLLR5 arrival for Runway 26R transition and we had everything briefed and ready to go. At a point ATC changed the runway on us from [Runway] 26R to 26L so we reloaded correct runway and its respective ILS approach procedure to back up the approach. We rebriefed the approach and reloaded all frequencies again so we were ready once again for the approach. At the end of the arrival there's a fix named ZOEEE; right upon crossing that fixed; my First Officer selected HDG sync; immediately follow by HDG mode. And just like that; we were auto sequenced for the approach. ATC then came to us with right turn for a right base followed by another right turn turn intercept the localizer for Runway 26L. At that point the FO (First Officer) armed APP and we confirmed APP1 was armed. So we continue with the approach but the airplane didn't intercept the LOC but instead it continued. Before we reached the final approach course I told my First Officer 'Be careful I don't think it's going to intercept' (he didn't acknowledge) right when we reached the course I told my First Officer 'turn now...turn now!' But he didn't; so at that point I said 'I have the flight controls' and I hand flown the airplane back the final approach course. At that point ATC came to us to let us know we were showing left of course to what my First Officer responded 'We are correcting. This happened like at about 3000 ft. AGL. After I restablished the airplane on it's final approach course I asked my First Officer if he wanted the controls back and he said YES. So I transferred the controls back to him. Then I quickly checked the information and I verified that the FMS was loaded with the ILS26L; we had a good preview with right identifier IJYV and we were showing an inbound course of 267 and APP 1 was armed. So I had to reach the FMS and I activated vectors then I forced V/L on the Pilot Flying side after asking the flying pilot if he agreed with every single step. Then the airplane's automation 'woke up' and the First Officer followed the FD all the way. We were stable at 1000 ft. and we continued to landing. I think the new FMS program has a glitch or something. Apparently it doesn't auto sequence the approach the same way. This happened in another aircraft which has the latest FMS update. From now on I'm not going to trust the auto sequence on the new loads and I am going to manually sequence it from a fix on the final approach fix. This should prevent the same issue from happening again."

column121: I was conducting my first training flight after completing training as of Date. The plan was to fly to the southeast/southwest training area of ZZZ1 bravo airspace and show effects of controls. The flight went extraordinarily well and got through the lesson. I received information D winds were 180 @ 8 altimeter was 30.08'' hg. Originally I was flying from the west and advised to set up for Left downwind for XXL. Once coming into 3-4 nm; I was then advised by tower to switch to right pattern for XXR. I set up and we looked and felt good; I was flying my usually pattern I would do in a rental I do out of ZZZ. I set up for my standard 45 degree angle; and believed I was setting up for XXR; but all I could see was XX over my shoulder. I then continued to reduce power and begin my descent. I started my turn and per training they require a 20 [degree] angle of bank and no more. I did go thirty to make sure I would not overshoot. Unfortunately to my knowledge I checked runway XX but not left or right. Upon touch down I was advised to pull off on taxiway A; which is when I noticed the runway XXR and was even advise to hold short of XXR. I then took blame and was expecting a phone number. When I landed; I shook it off and knew I had to continue my teaching with my students and advised them of my mistake.

column121: I was conducting a private flight in my aircraft.  I had utilized VFR flight following and was handed off to Tower.   I heard numerous bangs in quick succession. They were alarming and my wife whom was in the front passenger seat abruptly turned to me and exclaimed in a concerned tone; 'what was that'? I too was extremely concerned and began checking all aircraft systems. Fortunately; all systems were functioning and I continued the approach to an uneventful landing. Upon examination of the aircraft post-landing; I noted damage to the fairing protruding from the underwing of the right main landing gear. Although I did not see what caused the damage; I am certain the damage was not present before the return flight due to my preflight inspection. I surmise the aircraft was struck by an unmanned aerial system as there were no signs of blood or feathers consistent with a bird strike.

column121: I was the Controller in Charge position (CIC) listening to Local (LC); Ground Control (GC); and Clearance Delivery (CD). The Local Controller (LC) was located on the south side of the tower; I was on the opposite side of the tower watching LC's sequence via the STARs radar display.  The sun was directly in our face at the time which makes it difficult to visually acquire aircraft on final; especially small aircraft. Aircraft X was told to land on Runway 34R; Aircraft X acknowledged his clearance to land Runway 34R. Aircraft Y was cleared to land Runway 34L. Aircraft Y acknowledged his clearance to land Runway 34L. Aircraft X was ahead of Aircraft Y for the parallel runway. Aircraft Y initiated a go-around; as I went to do the paper work and find out why Aircraft Y went around; the LC and I noticed that Aircraft X had exited the runway and was in between Runway 34L and 34R indicating that he had landed on Runway 34L (the wrong runway). I called NORCAL to verify that was the reason Aircraft Y initiated a go-around; and it was. I suggest that all aircraft be required to turn on their landing lights during periods of difficult visual condition to include sun glare.

column121: I was Pilot Monitoring on flight [on] DRLLR 5 RNAV STAR to IAH. Aircraft X had one MEL for right FMS inoperative. Single FMS operation. We entered the STAR during preflight and 26R runway per the DRLLR 5 STAR. ATC instructed us to change to 26L just prior to MPORT. This changes the final point on the STAR from SKLER to ZOEEE. I entered the new runway and verified that no duplicate waypoints existed. I also verified and noted that the waypoint sequence on the MFD looked correct; noting the large gap between ZOEEE and BOZZZ on the 26L approach. I also noted the line on the MFD depicted from DOMNO to ZOEEE. For unknown reasons the airplane approached DOMNO; overflew DOMNO; and the autopilot went immediately into ROL mode and continued flying straight. It did not sequence to ZOEEE as expected. We noticed the change immediately; selected heading mode to start the turn to ZOEEE; and then re-engaged LNAV after verifying the FMS was sequencing properly to ZOEEE. As we started to sort out the flight path error; I was distracted when ATC issued heading calls and pilot deviation calls; which removed my attention from assisting the pilot flying. No other deviation occurred that we were aware of. Both pilots were fully engaged in managing the flight. We acted as fast as we could to regain flight path control. [I] suggest verifying the integrity of the FMS arrivals into IAH. Delay pilot deviation calls until on the ground and the airplane has been secured.
column123: We were on the DRLLR 5 arrival and planning for Runway 26R. We had checked the route for no double waypoints and all altitudes were correct. We were assigned Runway 26L and made the appropriate checks of waypoints and altitudes. At DOMNO the plane went into Roll mode and continued south. We saw it right away and began left turn as ATC told us to turn left and gave us a heading (070 I think). A few minutes later the controller gave us a phone number to call about a possible deviation. This plane had the #2 FMS MEL'd which may have contributed since we don't often operate with 1 FMS. Better planning for operations with one FMS. Closer attention while passing DOMNO; which has known issues. However we had no duplicate waypoints so this may be a new issue.
column125: Flight crew reported experiencing an unplanned autopilot/FMS mode shift causing a tract deviation during the DRLLR 5 STAR into IAH. Flight crew cited a runway assignment change; plus one FMS being inoperative as causal factors.

column121: Great weather; normal fuel burn. Entering terminal area we were vectored around then low and slow which created more fuel burn in terminal area. Did go-around for XXL due to an animal on runway. Aircraft in front of us did same. Declared minimum fuel on go-around in order to expedite landing since fuel now was an issue with extra fuel burn and not much to play. Controller was going to extend us downwind behind an arriving aircraft which would have added more time and fuel so I elected to [request] an immediate turn to the FAF for XXC. I didn't want XXL because of animal issue and another possible go-around. Controller vectored preceding aircraft off their approach and cleared us for visual XXC. Landed uneventfully with 3.9 fuel.....2.10 left tank and 1.8 right tank. Reserve Fuel on Flight Plan was 3.78.Cause: Dispatched with normal arrival fuel with very good weather. Controller vectored us low and slow which increased fuel burn. Suggestions: Next time I will not accept so many vectors and low and slow increasing fuel burn when I do not have extra fuel to play.

column121: Descending on the DSNEE 4 arrival from EMLLD; we became aware of congested airport environment with two different TCAS traffic alerts very close by. Approach had us at 190 knots departing DSNEE; and we were expecting the visual to runway 20R. We had gone to flaps 2 configuration. We were handed off late to the final controller; and when we switched over we heard a company call sign with nearly identical numeric as ours; cleared for the RNAV (RNP) Z runway 20R. We heard the other aircraft read back the clearance; we knew a VERY similar call sign was on frequency. We thought we might be cleared for the same approach. We checked in and asked if we were going to get the RNAV approach; and the controller said no; we were going to get a visual unless we asked for the approach. Shortly after; the controller cleared an aircraft to descend to 3000'; but neither of us were sure that it was our call sign. The First Officer (FO) attempted to clarify; but the controller was very busy and talking nonstop. We flew a few more miles; and before we could clarify he repeated the clearance to descend to 3000' and asked if we had the airport in sight. We called the airport; and said we were high. He cleared us to fly through final approach course and intercept from the right. FO read back the clearance; and we both agreed it was going to be tight. I immediately called for gear down and got the flaps to full; I also used the speed brakes as long as safely practical. We switched over to tower control; and I made the glideslope at approx. 1400' AGL; but we were still 40kts fast. I told the FO we were going to do a 'Soft Go'. At 1000' AGL I called for 'Go Around; Takeoff/around (TOGA)' and advanced the thrust levers to TOGA. I then called 'climb' as I brought the thrust levers back to what I thought was the climb position; but was actually the Maximum Continuous Thrust (MCT) detent. The tower initially held us to 2000'; so I reduced our climb rate - we were accelerating rapidly. As we cleaned up and were cleared to 3000'; the FO recognized that we were in MCT detent and brought it to my attention. By then we were clean and rapidly approaching 250 KIAS. I brought the thrust levers back to idle as we passed 260; and we saw a maximum speed of about 280 before we began to slow down again. I selected speed mode and set 220 KIAS; positioned the thrust levers in the climb detent and re-engaged auto thrust. We slowed and turned back into a right downwind for an uneventful visual approach and landing at SNA.Causes(1)	Late clearance to descend. SNA is challenging even when on a normal profile; and a few miles made all the difference.(2)	Task saturation. The L.A basin is one of the busiest airspaces in the world. Two Traffic Alerts and nonstop chatter from the approach controller indicated that he was task saturated; as well.(3)	Call sign confusion. The scheduling shop should never have had two call signs separated by one digit arriving at the same airport at the same time.(4)	Approach expectations. We were going to do a visual; and changed our setup and expectations when we heard preceding traffic approach clearance.(5)	Fatigue. Our FAR 117 duty day limit was 10 hours. We had been awake since 3.5 hours earlier to make sign-in; and were at 9:00 of our 10:00 limit when we arrived at SNA. When we knew we were too high to likely make a successful approach; we could have asked for a vector and more time/distance to descend. Flight scheduling needs to de-conflict call signs so very similar ones are not scheduled to arrive at the same airport at the same time. The ATC controllers seemed very busy; perhaps a few more controllers on-shift would help divide the workload better. Crew scheduling could alleviate fatigue factors by avoiding sequence builds that have crews flying into airports at the back end of their duty periods that are known to present unusual challenges (even if they aren't officially 'special qualification' airports).

column121: Arriving DEN on the CLASH2 arrival from the southeast; I planned and briefed an easterly flow arrival to land 17R. ATIS advertised 17R; 16L; 16R ILS; visual and RNAV Z approaches. I briefed for the RNAV RNP Z 17R. The final point for the arrival was QWIKE intersection and I left a discontinuity between that QWIKE and the QWIKE point starting the RNAV RNP Z 17R. Denver Center gave us a clearance to descend via the CLASH2 arrival landing south.The PM (Pilot Monitoring) and I confirmed that that was what we briefed and the FMC was properly programmed. The PM checked on with DEN Approach and we were told to expect RNAV Z to 16R. The PM installed that approach and I briefed the new arrival points and crossing restrictions on the CLASH2 that coincided with that runway; which were now a west downwind; the final fix being CLFFF. I briefed the approach as well. We did the same thing; leaving a discontinuity between CLFFF on the arrival and CLFFF as the IAF on the approach. The PM pulled the landing data for 16R and we briefed the changes on the engine-out missed approach which was slightly different then 17R. Approximately EVRLY intersection as we were descending through FL180; DEN Approach then said runway change; expect visual or ILS (I can't remember) to 16L. I remember it was at FL180 because it coincided with the altimeter change and the need to verify that VNAV is still able to maintain. The PM installed runway 16L and I did a briefing for the ILS 16L. This changed the FMC to an east downwind; the same fixes as for runway 17R but I did not note the changes in arrival fixes. As we were approaching JURKY intersection; I noticed on the FMC that the next waypoint was not what I was expecting. I had in my mind that we would be on a west downwind for the 16 runways. I said to the PM; it's not the correct fix next for runway 16R. The PM then asked DEN Approach to verify the runway assignment.Approach responded with a frequency change but did not respond to the request for runway clarification. This was at the point where we needed to go left or right depending on the runway. I thought it was 16R; so I went to the next fix for that runway; ROOTZ. As soon as the PM checked on with the next frequency; the Controller asked our runway assignment. The PM said we were trying to clarify. Approach then gave us 16R as a runway assignment. In fact; the previous clearance was for runway 16L. And that runway is an east downwind; not a west downwind even though the runway is on the west side of the airfield. This was absolutely my responsibility to ensure the correct runway and corresponding waypoints for the arrival. Going forward my personal correction will be to write down the runway assignment given by ATC each time it changes; whether on the yoke clip notepaper or on the iPad graphic. I also got complacent after doing 2 full briefings into DEN on a clear day. With the 3rd briefing; I did an abbreviated briefing. A full briefing; stepping through every arrival point would have caught it earlier. Additionally; as suggested by the Jump Seater; instead of clarifying the runway and having to verify the points quickly; I will ask ATC for the next waypoint they are expecting in order to give myself some time to figure out any ambiguities between the FMC and the chart. If we are adding to the data for DEN arrival deviations though...I didn't think this could happen to me. We briefed the threat of runway changes going into DEN. But DEN giving runway changes that impact arrival points without sufficient time for us to properly program and confirm was a contributing factor. Its 11.4 miles from EVRLY where we got the change to JURKY where the arrival splits. To reprogram; confirm; brief; get landing data and brief the engine-out profile changes while monitoring the descent is challenging.
column123: CLASH2 RNAV into DEN we initially planned for a Visual Approach to 17R backed up by the ILS. Initial Controller told us to expect 16R so we loaded RNAV/RNP 16R with the CLFFF transition to the approach; per ATIS. Cleared to descend via the CLASH2 arrival so we set 11;000 feet in the MCP (Mode Control Panel). ATC then advised us to expect Visual Approach to 16L. I then loaded the 16L ILS in the box and the Captain continued the descent on the arrival around the JURKEY fix. When the Captain started to brief the new arrival she noticed the arrival fixes did not match up with the new runway.I asked ATC to verify our runway assignment as all 3 of us in the cockpit were unclear at that point. After a pause; ATC gave us a new frequency instead of answering my query regarding which runway. At that point the Captain manually entered the ROOTZ fix. We changed our altitude in the MCP to correspond to the 13;000 feet minimum at ROOTZ while we checked in with the final Controller. Upon checking in the Controller asked us which runway we were planning on. I asked again which runway as we were not certain at that point. He then stated Visual Approach 16R. I updated the box AGAIN to the ILS 16R and; we were then given a heading and new altitude. The rest of the arrival and approach were uneventful.
