column121: I was approaching F70 from the southeast after a personal flight while making radio calls every few miles as I noticed on my ADS-B in several targets circling around the airport due to intense student activity.Someone made a radio call a few minutes before I was in the vicinity of another aircraft cutting them off on downwind too and I prepared myself for heavy traffic in the pattern and trying to find an opening so I could join the downwind.Upon entering the 45 for downwind I noticed visually an aircraft on the upwind taking off and repeatedly asked his intentions and if he could see me.There was no answer and seeing him proceed on runway heading I continued on the 45 for downwind being confident he wouldn't start a turn to crosswind until he reached close to pattern altitude as is recommended for the noise abatement procedure out of F70 for departures from Runway 18.Then I saw him maneuver for the crosswind maybe 500 feet below my altitude which was at pattern and make a radio call in that regards. At this point I had contacted him several times regarding his intentions and whether he could see me but without any answer.Seeing him turn and put me on the direct collision course with me although 300 ft. below me I decided to execute a 360 degree turn saw him coming extremely near me. Finally he acknowledged my presence in a subsequent radio call.I continued the full turn and reentered downwind.F70 was extremely busy that day with several schools on the field and at one point I saw no less than 5 aircraft in the traffic pattern. Needless to say a Tower is badly needed in that field for the safety of flight as the indiscriminate addition of several aircraft from several schools without any sort of coordination between those schools makes this urgently needed.A bit of courtesy goes a long way too and someone was nice enough to extend their downwind for me earlier in the day so I could depart as I had to hold short of the active runway at least 10 minutes as one aircraft after another was landing with no end in sight.On my side I should have waited outside the pattern on a hold in a safe area until there was no aircraft on upwind and the downwind leg would be fully opened; with adequate radio communications ensuring everyone in the pattern has knowledge of any other traffic with acknowledgement for all.This was my first experience with this field with that many aircraft in the pattern and the lesson learned here is to stay outside an uncontrolled field until there is fewer than a set limit of aircraft that one can set as personal limit.Such a personal limit is not taught at all as part of any training curriculum nor in the AIM in my knowledge.Most uncontrolled field are uncontrolled because of very low traffic but some reach levels just below the threshold needed for a tower and F70 seems to be in that category.Also the schools on the field could coordinate some kind of schedule among themselves so not everyone is up in the air at the same time. Poisson's law dictates things happen in bursts unless there is coordination to avert it.

We're reduced staffing due to COVID-19; and having to train forces us to combine positions to accomplish the numbers that management is pushing for.

column121: Aircraft X was on the RNAV Z approach for Runway 22L. The aircraft had been cleared to land. The approach naturally brings aircraft in on a slight angle to the runway. Aircraft X appeared to be coming in at an angle on short final; but aligned with the runway prior to crossing the threshold. However; an ASDE alert triggered with Taxiway Y; which is parallel to Runway 22L. I instructed the aircraft to go-around. The aircraft went around and later landed without incident.This approach is likely to be problematic in the future because the angle it brings in aircraft on lines up with Taxiway Y until the last minute. It might be best this approach not be used if it can be avoided.

column121: Initially coming in on the IAH LINKK1 Arrival expecting 27 but then just before descent; we were switched to NNCEE arrival expecting 8R (parking north side). Once with 2nd approach controller we were given change to RNAV 9. We asked if they could please work out 8R; a few minutes later cleared RNAV 9. Started setting it up; reprogrammed the box and reconfigured the aircraft. We then received 'maintain 200 until advised; level 6;000MSL at HOWLN'. Came out of box to open MCP speed window for 200 (FMC VNAV speed would be 210 per the arrival). Looked back down and saw us going through TEXXN where the ARC was supposed to commence.I immediately clicked off autopilot and started 30 degree turn to get back on ARC. Halfway through the turn (past HHOOG) received call from ATC stating he saw that we went past TEXXN and to turn left to 300 but we were already 45 off from runway (base). ATC asked if we had runway in sight; we confirmed and were then cleared visual Runway 9. Visual approach was executed and landing was normal. We programmed FMS to perform RNAV 9 from HOWLN but don't think it closed the loop automatically like it does on LINKK1 to 27 approach and that's why the aircraft continued past ARC point.

column121: Initial setup and brief was LINKK arrival; with visual ILS 27. Weather was clear; with excellent visibility. At TOD IAH changed to East flow; and we were given the NNCEE arrival. Approach control said expect RNP Y 9. We requested 8R since our parking gate was on the north side. Were told 'standby; we'll see'. Frequency change; and no reference to 8R request. Then cleared RNAVP Y 9 via HOWLN; maintain 200 knots. I observed Pilot Flying choose RNAV Y 9; with HOWLN transition. Other radio calls and congestion and nonstandard speed change prevented from looking into the LEGS page to see it tie into the HOWLN we were proceeding toward. Immediately after TEXXN we realized it did not turn and turned the aircraft with heading select.Approach queried about being outside the turn as our actual heading turned through approximately 330. Approach control said maintain current heading'. I immediately asked what heading do you want; we are currently in the turn. First response was 260 heading. We initiated a turn reversal; then approach said; I see you are in the turn; turn right 060; descend 3000; cleared the visual runway 9. I acknowledged. Soon thereafter were given 'maintain 6000; turn heading 100.' Responded with climbing back to 6000. Controller asked if we were still visual with 9. (It was perfectly clear; with unusual 30+ mile visibility). Responded yes; still visual. Re-cleared the visual runway 9. Flew the visual to an uneventful landing."

column121: Landed Runway 35R and was instructed by DFW tower to taxi Q - Z hold short of Runway 35C. Approaching Runway 35C DFW Tower instructed us to cross 35C at Z landing traffic will land and hold short. As we began to proceed across Runway 35C; after crossing the hold short line; the Runway Entry Lights (REL's) illuminated red. Landing traffic was still airborne on approach to Runway 35C. Since we were already moving; I determined it would be safer to continue across the runway and exit rather than stop in the runway environment. Notified Tower Controller once clear of the Runway that the REL's illuminated as we crossed Runway 35C. The Controller apologized and stated that was not supposed to happen since they were in Land and Hold short (LAHSO) operations.Possible ground equipment failure or programming error.Not certain. Perhaps a question for DFW Tower.
column123: Landed Runway 35R and was instructed by DFW Tower to 'taxi Q-Z; hold short of 35C.' Approaching 35C tower instructed us to cross 35C at Z; landing traffic will land and hold short. As we began to cross 35C; the runway entry lights illuminated red. Landing traffic was still airborne on approach and we were already across the hold short line; so we decided it was safest to continue and exit the runway. The Captain notified the Controller that the REL's had illuminated; and the controller apologized and said that shouldn't have happened.Possible equipment malfunction. I don't have any suggestions other than checking the operation of the equipment.

column121: We were dispatched [to] ZZZ without alternate fuel. Forecast for ZZZ was few clouds at 25;000 and more than 6 sm visibility. This forecast did not require an alternate.While on downwind leg; a fog bank rolled in over the airport and the weather dropped below CAT II mins. ZZZ1; normally a good diversion airport was also reporting fog. We estimated such a diversion would have put us with only 1;500 lbs of fuel remaining. Enroute we were given several delay vectors. Running low on fuel and out of options; we decided our safest options was to execute the CAT II even though visibility was below our required minimums of 1;200 RVR.Approach and subsequent landing were uneventful. We landed with approximately 2;900 lbs of fuel remaining.Unforecast weather; ZZZ is notorious for unforecasted fog in the late fall and winter. Anytime with clear skies and close temp dew point like today; I will ask for an alternate even if official forecast does not require.

column121: We were conducting Aircraft X at night into ABE; vectored from the north; and then given a clearance for a Visual Approach. As always; we had an approach set up; ILS 24; and while descending from the north side of the airport from 3;000 feet; the vectored altitude; to 2;400 feet; at an altitude of about 2;650 feet we received a one time (only 1) 'Terrain' warning from the TAWS (Terrain Alert Warning System); while descending very gently at about 300 feet per minute. We leveled off for a moment; there was no other terrain warning and never a 'Terrain; Pull Up' command; then as we intercepted the final; we continued to the 2;400 feet glideslope intercept altitude. There was a ridgeline north of the ILS course; which we had briefed on; but it did not show an altitude greater than 1;000 feet; so we did not think it would be a problem to start a shallow descent from 3;000 to 2;400 feet; especially at a shallow rate. This is a potential problem at night; as there are no obstacle lights on the ridge line; so I believe Approach should simply provide vectors to the ILS approach instead of just giving a 'visual.'  A little more work for ATC but a safer way of operating. Caused by ATC clearing crew to do a night visual instead of providing vectors; knowing that there are unmarked; unlit; terrain ridge lines to the north of the airport. Also caused by crew not requesting full vectors to the ILS at night. Most ATC facilities in small airports don't want to work that hard. Suggest that crews start asking ATC at small airports to provide vectors to the Approach being used to back up the Visual when in night VMC conditions; as a means to improve the level of safety; even if it means that ATC might have to work a bit more.

column121: We were descending on the JANNY5 Arrival.  At the KOPLE waypoint; we were given 'Direct JANNY at 8;000' by ATC.  We began our descent with Joshua Approach.  Around the JANNY waypoint; we were transferred to SoCal Approach for terminal instructions to Burbank airport. The controller initially gave us a 'descend 7;000' instruction which was followed shortly thereafter with a 'descend 5;000' this descent was well prior to the usual instructions given to Aircraft X from this location.  The crew complied with all instructions from ATC.  We were receiving vectors to intercept the ILS-Z Runway 8 Approach into Burbank.  We became aware that the controller was vectoring us to intercept the localizer between the SILEX and BUDDE intersection.  While approaching from the north at 5;000 ft MSL; we were heading over one of the small mountains northwest of Burbank Airport.  We were vectored near the highest point on that hill which is charted at 3;734 ft.  While going over the hill the aircraft gave us a 'TERRAIN; TERRAIN; PULL UP.' I looked at the RA as the first 'TERRAIN' was enunciated; and saw the RA indicator show around 1;500 feet; then jumped immediately to 2;500 feet.  The warning was only given once by the aircraft.  The PF (Pilot Flying); Captain; reported not seeing any altitude indication on the RA.  We continued the approach and the remainder of the flight was uneventful.  We aren't sure whether it was an erroneous aircraft indication or if ATC may have descended us a little too low for that sector. Clarify with ATC on altitude assigned and whether assigned altitude is appropriate for sector.

column121: The flight departed the from Gate X in ZZZ1 at XA:10z and proceed to flight level 200 to ZZZZZ intersection to join the arrival into ZZZ without incidence. At approximately XB:10Z while descending through 8;000 MSL for 7;000 MSL in IMC conditions with heavy rain and continuous light and occasionally moderate turbulence we received a single chime and an ELT ON Master Caution message was displayed on the EICAS primary page. Approximately one to two seconds later a very loud ELT signal began sounding through the flight deck intercom system. I notified ATC that we had an indication that our ELT had activated and that it was in error and could be disregarded. I further advised them that the ELT tone was sounding on the flight deck and it was making radio communication and flight deck communication very difficult. I turned the number two comm radio off of the guard frequency and reduced the volume; but it did not have any effect on the volume of the ELT. The First Officer (FO) began to attempt to find a resolution [in] the QRH and we attempted QRH procedure several times to try to silence the tone without success. The noise was deafening and flight deck communication and air to ground communication was very difficult.  At 4;000 MSL we were given a heading of 360 degrees then descended to 3;000 MSL and turned to a heading of 085 degrees and instructed to join the localizer for Runway X. At this time both aircraft localizers failed; and a red LOC indication was displayed on both PFD's.  We advised ATC and asked if there was a problem with the localizer.  ATC queried several other aircraft and all advised they were receiving the localizer.  We broke off of the approach per ATC instruction and attempted to set up for the ILS to Runway YL but could not receive any ILS signal.  I advised ATC that we would need a GPS approach and we were instructed to prepare for the GPS YL.  After having set up the GPS YL ATC advised that the wind had shifted and were now from 275 degrees gusting to 25 kts. We were further advised that the airport was now in a west flow.  We requested RNAV YY and began preparing that approach. We received radar vectors to RNAV GPS YY and completed the approach without incidence.Due to extreme flight deck noise from the ELT throughout the event communication on the flight deck and with ATC was very difficult. The noise and inability to communicate throughout added a high level of stress and complexity.  Continuous turbulence and instrument meteorological conditions were present throughout; with ceilings and visibility at or near minimums on arrival at the runway.  The double failure event coupled with the inability to sync the two MFD's due to a previously existing MEL added complexity and were additional threats. To mitigate these factors myself and my FO followed SOP to the extent that we were able. We resisted the urge to rush and after preparing the RNAV approach we briefed the approach; and the runway change items in an attempt to slow down the process and bring the flight back into the normal envelope as much as possible. In hindsight I wish that I would have tried the EMER function on the RCP (Remote Compensation Panel). I don't know if it would have helped but the noise made concentration and communication very difficult and it was very fatiguing. Overall I was satisfied with my actions and those of my FO given the circumstances and without the benefit of hindsight I thought that we met or exceeded expectations.

column121: On the Visual [Runway] XXR approach into ZZZ. Cleared for the approach about 5 miles out; and given a heading 090 to join the localizer. We joined the localizer and got a TA and noticed another RJ (Regional Jet) headed around 150 degrees to join the localizer for [Runway] XXL; which wasn't going to be a factor. We were never left of the [Runway] XXR localizer at any point. We caught the glide slope and headed down while configuring. At this point we received a RA that the [Runway] XXL RJ had blown through the localizer and was right over the top of us; about 300 feet of vertical separation. Captain was flying; he disconnected the AP (Autopilot) and flew the breakout procedure. I told ZZZ Tower we had a RA and he was swamped today so he initially wanted us to go around; to which I said we had flown the breakout and were established on the approach - going around and flying his instructions would've led to a second RA since we were instructed to climb right into that jet that just flew over the top of us. Tower changed his mind and gave us [Runway] XXR landing clearance. ZZZ ATC workload was extremely high and the [Runway] XXL RJ didn't intercept the localizer at all; nor were they aware of the traffic around them (us).High ATC workload and neighboring jet on the other approach had low SA (Situational Awareness).ZZZ workload review and talk to the crew who blew through the [Runway] XXL localizer and crossed into us.

column121: We started out vectored off our arrival by ZZZ Center to the southwest with several step down altitudes.  30 miles from the airport we found ourselves at 20;000 MSL which is about 10;000 feet higher than usual for a normal descent.  ATC; specifically ZZZ Center; was overloaded with aircraft going into south State X.  The Controller brought us around and eventually around 10;000 MSL he told us to go direct to the ZZZ airport.  At that time; we were about 15-18 miles away at just under 10;000 MSL.  A few minutes later the Controller asked if we had the field in sight to which we replied that we did; but we needed a vector because we were too high to safely attempt the visual approach.  We were given a left 360 degree turn and cleared down to 3;000 MSL also while given direct to the ZZZ airport.  Upon leveling off at 3;000 MSL we were in a cloud layer and couldn't see the field.  We requested lower and were given 1;500 MSL.  Once we reach that altitude; we could see the field and was cleared the visual approach to Runway X and to switch to Tower.On final; after fully configuring the aircraft; Tower told us to turn right to a heading of 180.  Very confused; we asked him to repeat the instructions at which point we executed a missed approach for a disabled aircraft on the runway.  Tower never actually told us to go-around.  We were given heading 180 and a climb to 4;000 MSL.  Following the level off we were instructed to hold present position however the aircraft will not hold present position on HDG mode.  It can only PPOS HOLD while on and active FMS leg with less than .25 NM cross track.  Knowing this; we told the Controller we needed a fix to hold and we were given the ZZZ2 NDB which doesn't exist in the database.  We instead requested the missed approach fix for the RNAV X approach.We weren't sure or told how long the runway was going to be shut down so the crew started to discuss diverting options which ZZZ1 was the only real viable option.  The FMS indicated it would take about 3;500 lbs of fuel and we had about 2;300lbs of reserve fuel on board.  From my quick calculation; I had to make a decision fairly soon considering the following:  we had about 5;700lbs on fuel on board; unknown runway closure; time; FMS calculation of 3;500 lbs of fuel required to ZZZ1; high volume of ZZZ Center traffic and high controller work load.  I sent a message to Dispatch informing them we would attempt one more approach and if unable to make it in we would divert to ZZZ1.We notified ZZZ Center Controller we needed to attempt another approach or we would soon have need to divert.  We were given vectors around and cleared for the visual to Runway X once again.  As the pilot flying; I opted to proceed direct to the FAF of the RNAV X and continue the visual approach.  At about the base leg portion I turned off the autopilot and turned towards the runway.  I believe that a slight turn inside of the FAF did not sequence the approach nor the glide path information which led to a position above the glide path.  I flew down to correct the glide path all while in and out of 1;000 FPM VVI.  I acknowledged the momentary deviations of descent and quickly corrected as I was now on the visual indications from the PAPI's.  On short final; a thermal briefly interrupted our descent and with a quick correction back to glide path I corrected the path for a safe landing.I believe the accumulation of all factors above could have led to a critically low fuel situation much quicker than expected.  I believe the saturation of airspace on a very busy travel day could have been underestimated in the current climate of understaffed controllers.  ZZZ Center controllers were so saturated with aircraft that any chance to hand an aircraft off to a visual approach whether it was really safe to do so or not was attempted.  Unsure about divert fuel and runway state created a high-importance; high stress moment of deciding when a divert should occur without having proper data supporting it.  Lastly; in a heightened sense of urgency with a tight turn inbound to the runway and sub-sequence 'Cutting the corner' led to the aircraft being in a position higher than the glide path.I would suggest the following for operations into ZZZ Captain only approach and landing.  I believe most captains take it upon themselves to make the approach and landing due to the short runway as it is.  There should be more 'hip pocket' information whether in the form of a bulletin or some other format that provides rough numbers of divert options; fuel required; routing; etc when a formal alternate is not filed.  I also suggest fuel planning take into account busy travel days and add as much fuel as practical for contingencies even on days with VMC conditions that does not preclude landing due to being over max landing weight.  I also believe during all forms of training (initial; recurrent; etc) special authorization airports and airports such as ZZZ are discussed. It is my suggestion that ZZZ be a mandatory training event.  I can tell you everything you need to know about flying to ZZZ2 yet we don't actually go there in the aircraft.  However; airports such as ZZZ are rarely mentioned when we fly there every week.  I suggest more 'hangar flying' where scenarios are discussed (such as this one) in a classroom/training environment to get captains to start thinking about scenarios such as these.  I'd also like to recommend that one or two approaches from ZZZ3 be included in our Jeppesen charts or company pages.  Should ZZZ be shut down for any reason and there is not enough divert fuel to safely make it back to the mainland there will be a 10;000 foot runway available; especially in IMC conditions.  Lastly; I suggest that pilots refrain from attempting to cut corners while approaching the runway from greater than 90 degrees.  Instead; they should fly the published approach in its entirety including glide slopes or glide paths so that they maintain a safe; stable approach to the runway.

The additional vectoring caused them to be concerned with their fuel state and made a request to be cleared as soon as possible.  The crew was cleared for another approach and landing and reported being unstable/high before safely landing the aircraft.

column121: Reflecting back on this flight today; I fear we may not have obtained verbal landing clearance from Tower before touchdown on Runway X at ZZZ. This was my first flight in over two months; and was assigned with another Captain as my First Officer; with no one else present on the flight deck. I had also been on leave earlier in the year. Before we flew; I briefed that two captains flying together as a two-pilot crew complement is a threat; as it does not happen often; and there is an operational bias for the acting First Officer (co-Captain). During the descent; Air Traffic Control vectored us to a close-in base leg to Runway X. Approach Control asked us if we had the field in sight; and I elected to continue with a visual approach; which I believe now was not the best choice for myself. My acting First Officer momentarily lost sight of the field during my turn from base to final; however I had it acquired visually the entire time. I pointed out the field to assist; and the pilot monitoring re-acquired it. This should have resulted in a go-around to rejoin the approach; as this is a team environment; and a two-pilot airplane. I know I did switch the nose-wheel landing light to 'on' during the final approach leg; as that is SOP after being cleared to land; according to FOM. I was focused on flying the plane; slowing down early; and ensuring a stabilized approach.  I was not completely in tune with what my co-captain was or was not doing. Stabilized approaches are a topic often discussed in training; so it weighed heavily in my priorities on this day. My co-Captain (pilot monitoring) may not have switched to Tower as it was somewhat a high workload environment and unusual operation; at least to me.I should have recognized the threat of two captains flying together; along with my low state of landing currency; would lead to a chain reaction easily. I should have requested the full ILS to Runway X; or at least a longer final approach leg. I would recommend after a Captain returns to the line after a lengthy vacation; and in my case leave earlier in the year as well; to not pair up with another Captain; but only a highly current First Officer. Although everything was completely my responsibility; this would have eliminated the threat. I recognize that sound aeronautical decision making as it relates to all phases of duty; is the number one factor influencing safety; and it must be practiced even in high-workload environments.

column121: My flight began at ZZZ1 with the intent to fly direct to ZZZ for touch and goes and return to ZZZ1. I have flown this particular airplane multiple times with no issues. The start; taxi; and run-up were normal and no different than any other time; except that the strobe lights were blinking slower than usual but working as expected.The airplane was flown earlier; and no issues were reported; hence there was no reason to suspect a problem. I did two take-offs and landings before departing to ZZZ; I had no issues. During a normal approach to landing at ZZZ; all-electric equipment failed at an altitude of 300 feet from touching down on Runway XX. I was forced to immediately clear the runway and exit onto the first taxiway I saw; Taxiway XX; I had a disabled radio; transponder; and ADS-B.Although clear from the tower for a touch and go; I could not continue the flight safely with disabled equipment; therefore a go-around was not an option. Luckily; I have a handheld radio and was able to contact the control tower to explain what happened; and request directions to the parking area. I had to deviate from the touch and go clearance since I dint know what else was wrong with the airplane and continuing the flight would have been unwise.After arriving at the parking area; I talked to the mechanic who was only available by phone and asked me to go through my checklist again to troubleshoot the issue. I made multiple attempts to restart the airplane; loud cabin speaker noises could be heard as the power switch was turned on as if there was a short circuit in the system; but all fuses were okay and there was no power to start the engine. The mechanic determined that I had a dead battery and it could only mean that the airplane suffered an alternator failure on landing; grounding the airplane immediately.The flight club from which I rented the airplane has multiple airplanes of the same make and model and provided me with one so I could fly back to ZZZ1. The return flight was uneventful.

column121: On RNAV to Runway XX due to GS being inop on the ILS; winds; and airport conditions.  Outside FAF; attempted to extend Landing Gear and got only the 3 red UNLOCKED lights.  No landing gear operation.  Our airspeed was around 165 KIAS.  We attempted to recycle the gear and got exact same thing.  We executed a missed approach; [advised ATC]; and attempted to contact Dispatch on ACARS.  We tried several frequencies but could not make voice contact.  ZZZ1 airspace was undergoing some sort of COVID cleaning so not sure if that had anything to do with ARINC in that vicinity.  We just used the ACARS to communicate as best we could.  Dispatch texted that the only thing he was showing was LANDING GEAR NOT DOWN LOCKED (same as us) but had no other system malfunctions that he could see.  We followed the ECAM which instructed us to use the gravity extend.  We did; it worked; and we landed safely.  We texted Dispatch once on the ground that we made it safely; Maintenance came with the tug and towed us to the gate.  Passengers and flight attendants were informed in the air and on the ground as to what was happening.
column123: Just after midnight; outside the FAF to Runway XX at ZZZ; I was the PM and was asked to extend the landing gear. The gear failed to extend. Landing gear not down-locked. Recycled landing gear. No joy. Told Tower we needed to go-around to troubleshoot. Requested priority handling and then transferred controls; I was now PF while the Captain ran the ECAM and coordinated with Dispatch/Maintenance Control/FAs. I requested vectors away from the weather and near the approach so we could quickly join final when complete; requested they roll the trucks; and advised ATC we would need a tow off the runway. The Captain had difficulties talking with Dispatch and Maintenance over ARINC due to our low altitude (3;000) but was able to exchange some messages over ACARS with both. He ran the landing gear gravity extension checklist as directed by the ECAM. Landing gear came down. Captain updated FAs and made a PA to the passengers. We transferred controls and I was then PM for the final approach; visibility was poor due to a weather system. Rough winds and conditions but great landing by the Captain. Full stop on the runway. Crash truck circled and checked the plane out. Tow arrived and we shutdown motors and were towed to the gate.

column121: Descending on LINKK 1 arrival ATC cleared us at some point (think above 10.000 feet) for the ILS 27 approach. Pilot Monitoring re-selected the ILS 27 connecting the transition from RDFSH (which was not done on initial FMC setup). Our altitude was set at 6000 passed RDFSH. We never set FAF altitude to keep the descent.Houston approach checked if we were cleared for the approach. After that; I realized we were supposed to descend via the transition. No further ATC communication was received however after the crew debrief and looking at the approach plate again; the altitudes were mandatory altitude restrictions and we don't think we made GEYGR and/ or SYYKO.
column123: I was the First Officer and pilot monitoring on this flight IAH. We were cleared to descend via the LINKK1 arrival except maintain 300 knots. Then; 320 knots. Then; as fast as practical until LINKK. Then we were cleared for the approach from RDFSH and maintain 210 knots until FESTA. We had 6000 feet in the MCP window as this was the bottom altitude for the STAR. After being cleared the approach; I failed to set a lower altitude. Shortly after RDFSH (and level at 6000') ATC asked if we were descending on the approach. We were immediately aware of our mistake and the captain began descending. GEYGR and SYYKO (the next two fixes on the approach after RDFSH) had mandatory altitudes of 4000' and 3000'; respectively. Because of our delayed descent after RDFSH; we were high at both of those fixes. The multiple airspeed changes on the arrival caused a distraction and I failed to notice that I hadn't set a lower altitude to continue the approach after RDFSH. That being the case; it was still my job as pilot monitoring to set the lower altitude and I failed to do it.

column121: On final; Runway XXL; ZZZ; Night; 1000 foot ceiling; Me (Captain) flying; gusty winds; very light load; FO low currency- only 150 hrs in type since hired 1 year ago; and has not been flying much. I was hand flying at 2;000 feet on normal ILS. Called for gear down; flaps 15 at approximately 2;000 feet and flaps 30 landing; check at about 1500 feet. FO ran check list; while I hand flew the approach. The winds were gusty; our target was +15 ref and it was night.  I saw flap refs change on airspeed tape; and glanced at gear lights when FO ran checklist; but I did not have full attention to FO duties since it was dark and gusty. We broke out at 1;000 feet; I called clear to land and set missed approach altitude. At 500 feet we simultaneously got the 500 auto call out and Too Low Flaps. I simultaneously; started a Go-Around (finger to toga button) and looked at flap gauge - which was at 25. But I hesitated on the go-around when I saw why we got the GPWS. I commanded flaps set to 30; which the FO immediately did. We were then about 350-400 feet and stable; and I continued to a normal landing. After landing I realized; even though there was adequate time to set the flaps to 30; I should have gone around. We debriefed after parking. The FO said he just missed the flaps in setting and checking on the checklist; most likely due to being low time in aircraft and rusty from time off. I missed his wrong setting; due to the dark and the gusty winds requiring my attention. I said I should have gone around; and that he as well should have told me to. I am confident if I had not immediately recognized why we got the too low flaps and immediately corrected it; I would not have continued.

column121: The landing was excellent; Rollout of the aircraft was straight and smooth. When reaching for the toe brakes; I had a left brake but no right break. The toe brakes are little round pads on the top outsides of each rudder and are not easily identified with your toes even with soft sole shoes which I wear to fly in. I did not realize my foot had slipped off or was not on it properly until it was too late. The aircraft took a left direction as I was wishing to slow down. I let go of the left brake and caught it/straightened it with the right rudder. Right after I had it straight; I removed my right foot to lift it higher up to engage the right brake. The aircraft took a sharp left direction went onto the grass which with then full right rudder and the right brake did not help. The slope away from the runway is very steep and the aircraft was not able to be brought back up onto the runway; so I kept it straight and aimed at the taxiway; With the airplane tailwheel not willing to provide steering since it was on grass and the slope was still steep; the aircraft slid over the other side of the taxiway. The aircraft refused to turn right with all my efforts; so my next choice was to hit trees and or a building; so I choose to ground loop the airplane to avoid the trees and building. NO damage was done to any property including taxi signs or even lights. The aircraft came to a stop 180 degrees to the landing direction. No one was hurt in any manner since the speed was not very fast. The right gear leg collapsed as it stopped and the prop hit the grass and broke one of the tips. The engine was shut down and everything was turned off. The Runway XX is a challenging runway since there is zero room for error.
column125: Private pilot did not have proper foot placement and lost control of the aircraft during the landing rollout. The aircraft  had a prop strike and right main gear failure.

column121: I was on a instrument approach to CID talking with Chicago Center and picking up ice. Chicago asked me to cancel IFR clearance as early as possible because a (Company) plane was landing behind me. I changed to the CTAF frequency and made a 10 miles out announcement. I broke out around 2;000 ft msl/1;200 ft. AGL and change back to Chicago Center and cancelled my IFR clearance with ATC. I switched the comm back to CTAF and continued the approach; however; the button push failed to change to CTAF and stayed on Chicago Center and I missed that. During the approach I was stunned to see a vehicle pull out onto the approach end of the runway and stop at the very end just beyond the runway end lighting. I was still approximately 1-1 1/2 miles out. I had to make a decision to land or go back up into the icing. As the runway was 8;000 ft. long I elected to extend and land in the 2nd half of the runway as opposed to risk picking up excessive ice and endangering my aircraft.

column121: As background; ZZZ was heavily congested with training and weekend aviators. The approach to the downwind leg was reported via UNICOM as 4nm SE to enter a 45 - downwind entry. We coordinated spacing with another crosswind aircraft to follow us for spacing in the pattern.Reported downwind uneventfully and recognized the aircraft we were to follow and minimized their pattern to minimize spacing conflicts. Reported [our] base turn to UNICOM; uneventfully. Our track on base leg was approximately 2 nm from the end of the runway. At no time was our location questioned by the conflicting near miss aircraft.Reported a base to final turn; uneventfully and unquestioned. Upon the completion of the turn to final; I focused on vertical alignment to the runway and did not see the conflicting near miss aircraft. The PNF (Pilot Not Flying); in a radio call questioned the conflicting near miss aircraft to determine the position upon hearing the UNICOM base report. For clarification; the safety pilot - PNF; is a seasoned 121 CA (Captain) and highly experienced in the PA24-250 with close to 2;000 hours in type. After it was determined the conflicting aircraft turned inside of our location; resulting in cutting us off placing our aircraft in a hazardous flight profile; PNF took control of the aircraft in a professionally coordinated manner.At this time; the conflicting near miss aircraft was well within 1/4 NM and somewhat above our altitude; which was approximately 300 ft AGL. Because of the proximity to us; and apparently being unaware of our location; the resolution was complex. Keep in mind in their base to final turn (RH pattern) with a low wing aircraft [they] would have then been unable to locate us.Additionally; as we both rolled out on final we were both nearly lined up on the centerline; the closure rate was approximately 42 FPS and without evasive action; contact would have occurred in about 30 seconds; short of the runway; all due to the difference in V-ref and approach speeds. The PNF recognized this before I had visual contact with the conflicting traffic and they took control of the aircraft and became the PF.The evasive maneuver was complex in that [the other aircraft's] intentions seemed unpredictable and we did not want to initiate a go-around with the associated high deck angle; which would interrupt visual conflict with the conflicting traffic. Communication from their aircraft was weak and seemed confused as they; on short final; radioed 'how many aircraft are in the pattern and where are you located'. We discussed the possibility that they may begin a go around and that would worsen the conflict since we may have lost visual contact and keep in mind... at this time we were behind their location and out of view.In the interest of safety; we chose to sidestep slightly left and to pass them on the level and then after we cleared the aircraft to begin a go around. The point we were trying to achieve was to maintain visual contact with the conflicting aircraft until we passed clear of their position.

column118: Air Traffic Control Issued Advisory / Alert
column121: Aircraft X was landing HHR on an IFR flight plan. There were multiple weather cells in the airspace and LAX Aircraft were deviating around the final to the north. I had DYNR/EAFR combined and was quite busy handling the coordination for the deviations with STAR/ZUMR and figuring out the sequence for dependent simultaneous ILS's. I advised Aircraft X of some weather; but did not issue a heavy precipitation on the route to HHR. I asked Aircraft X if they could advise HHR in sight because the HHR weather was reporting BKN045 and LAX Aircraft could not get the visual. We also did not have staffing available in the facility to open up parallel monitors for independent simultaneous approaches. Aircraft X responded HHR in sight and I cleared them for the visual approach. Traffic for LAX was increasing and frequency congestion was becoming a problem. Aircraft x did have delays with their read backs/understanding as well. Aircraft X went down to 1100 ft. in a 2000 ft. MVA and I issued a low altitude alert. Aircraft X responded with; 'correcting'. Then Aircraft X kept heading toward the LAX finals and I asked them once or twice if they still see HHR. Then the HHR weather deteriorated to IFR and I advised Aircraft X that they'd need the localizer approach. I told the CIC (Controller in Charge) to open up monitors; they were trying to find staffing at the time already. I then got Aircraft X above the MVA and vectored them for the approach while continuing to deal with LAX deviations. After clearing Aircraft X for the localizer runway 25; I switched them to HHR tower; monitors had opened at this time. Aircraft X then proceeded to do a left turn southbound on about a 7 mile final without notification to me from HHR tower. Monitor 1 then called HHR and asked what was going on. Aircraft X then checked back on frequency with me heading 160; no coordination. I climbed Aircraft X above the MVA and vectored them for another approach. I also asked the pilot if he could accept the approach and they agreed they could. I was then relieved.[We need] staffing. We have more than enough people on a shift to facilitate monitors being open. So many coworkers are worried about their long breaks and getting done early that we are leaving the operational floor short between shifts.

column121: We were descending on the DSNEE 1 arrival but had been assigned several speeds; the last was 200 KIAS. I had been using speed intervention (SPD INTV) to manage the speed but switched to Level Change after passing the last intermediary altitude restriction and being asked to expedite our descent through 5000' for traffic. I announced the new mode and verified 4000' in the altitude window (the final altitude on the STAR).After we were clear off traffic; SoCal cleared us for the ILS Runway 30. I closed the discontinuity in the FMS after MIDDS; checked with the Pilot Monitoring (PM) and executed it. My PM then prompted that we could set 1600' in the altitude window for the FAF at GUNEY. I did so but failed to verify that we were in VNAV. At 3800' the PM noticed we were below the 4000' restriction at GOOFY. I reversed the descent and started climbing back to 4000'. We ended up 300' low at GOOFY. At that point; SoCal gave us a heading to join final and a descent to 2000'. We complied with the new clearance; were re-cleared for the ILS; and continued without further event.The continued use of SPD INTV would have done the same job but protected us from descending below a coded altitude on the arrival. Next time I need to descend quickly; I will try to stay in VNAV if I am continuing on an arrival that connects with an approach with step-down altitudes. Also using VVMI to verify my level of automation after an altitude selection will trap this kind of error in the future.
column125: Captain reported descending below the altitude for crossing GOOFY intersection on the DSNEE four arrival to LGB citing management of the flight guidance system to comply with ATC speed requests put the system in a mode that did not protect for crossing altitude restriction compliance.

column121: We were cleared for the ILS for Runway XXR in ZZZ. ZZZ at the time was CAT II minimums showing 3/4 mile visibly and 300 feet ceilings. We started getting a LOC flag prior to final approach fix. We broke off the approach and executed a missed approach. Knowing that there are many variables as to why we would not be getting reliable LOC information we elected to try a CAT II again but to a different runway. We go set up again for the ILS for Runway XXR. While intercepting we received a LOC flag again about 18 DME from the LOC. Again knowing that there are many variables that go into receiving a good signal we decided to give it a few more miles. We caught the LOC and GS and executed a CAT II to a landing. The only thing I can think that was improper about this circumstance was the continuation of a CAT II after receiving a LOC flag. It's being 18 miles out I believe we were in some gray area. At any rate we wrote up the LOC on the aircraft so that it could be ruled out as the cause. Perhaps the LOC on Runway XXR was not strong enough or there was interference from the proceeding aircraft causing the localizer. Or it was the aircraft itself. I think we did most everything right. It just felt weird to write up the LOC after a successful CAT II approach. I didn't see any reason we shouldn't try the CAT II we had plenty of fuel and had an alternate already specified.

column121: We were approximately 20 miles SE of the airport in the mountains on a sight-seeing flight. I was PIC (Pilot In Command) and the only pilot on board with 2 passengers. At 4;500 MSL the AWOS was intermittent because of the mountains. The MEF (Maximum Elevation Figure) on the sectional chart was 4;400 MSL and I was working my around some shorter hills. While experiencing some lighter bumps over a ridge; the 'GEAR IN TRANSIT' annunciator light lit up. I immediately checked to make sure I hadn't selected the gear handle down because we were too far away from the airport to have the gear down. The selector handle was still in the 'GEAR UP' position so my next instinct was to grab the emergency gear override and hold it in the 'UP' position to keep the gear from extending so far out. I checked the circuit breaker and everything seemed normal. I cycled the gear down and got 3 green lights and when cycled back up the green lights went away but the transit light never turned off. The AWOS suddenly kicked in and led me to decide on Runway 8 which meant I would cross the airport midfield and enter a tear drop for a left downwind to Runway 8. On the sectional I saw the altitude as 1;907 and decided to over fly the field at 2;500. In my mind 1;907 was pattern altitude and not the airport altitude. My mind was still on making sure my gear didn't fall down and create more drag for me that far away from the airport. About 1.5 miles to the south of the airport I once again communicated on the CTAF of my intentions to over fly the field at 2;500. One aircraft said they were back taxiing and going to depart Runway 8. As we approached the airport I felt like I was too low even though I was at 2;500 and then realized my mistake by how well I could see details on the ground. I quickly announced my intentions to climb up to traffic pattern altitude of 2;900 feet just before reaching the south side of the airport pattern which meant I was then climbing into the pattern instead of already being at the correct altitude. Directly over the runway we were back to 2;900 feet and I continued out to do a right hand turn and enter a tear drop to the left downwind. The gear came down fine and we landed safely. The combination of trying to troubleshoot an annunciator light while listening to incoming traffic and the intermittent AWOS left me to be distracted and mixing up proper altitudes for the pattern.Better self-briefing prior to arrival to an airport would have greatly reduced the chance of this occurring. Along with remembering that I had plenty of fuel and could have circled at a safe altitude to troubleshoot and then with a clearer mind come up with a plan to land at the airport normally.

column121: I had taken position after coming down from the tower working Ground Control. A priority handling had been coordinated with Aircraft X with an engine out. This had been coordinated at least 45 minutes prior to the aircraft's arrival. It was briefed from the relieved controller that this aircraft had requested 'wide vectors for an ILS.' At the same time Aircraft Z was inbound on the RNAV arrival along with Aircraft Y call sign unknown. In my judgement as well as the relieved Controller both of these aircraft would have arrived easily before Aircraft X came in on the ILS. I cleared Aircraft Y for the Visual Approach to the right parallel runway and decided to change the Aircraft Y to the left after they requested the ILS. Aircraft X changed his mind for the Visual Approach which I approved. I asked Aircraft X if they could visually follow the Aircraft Y and they said they would not because of wake turbulence. Since I did not issue a 30 degree intercept we coordinated with the tower to issue a go-around to Aircraft Y. We decided not to send the Aircraft Y around because he was established on the ILS even though I had not issued a 30 degree intercept for Aircraft X. In my opinion wake turbulence to a priority handling aircraft was more of a factor than a parallel runway approach.Due to the busy nature of this session I think splitting the radar position may have been warranted. The relieved Controller had been on position for over 1 hour and 45 minutes. Aircraft X changing his mind mid sequence also caused a lot of problems. I was wary of issuing delay vectors to an aircraft that was a priority handling.

column121: We were cleared for the visual approach to Runway 35R at 9;000 feet. The DRUMM approach segment was to 9;000 feet; and we started down to 7;000 feet for FRONZ approach segment. ATC alerted us of a MVA altitude alert. We corrected up to the altitude of 7;500 feet as ATC said that altitude was needed for that segment. We were cleared for the visual and were following the step down altitudes.I contacted one of our managers here at the Company; and they followed up on it for me and there was no documentation noted by ATC of any issues or violations. Company Manager said that we might have touched the Class B airspace limit and that would trigger the same alert per DEN ATC. The rest of the flight was uneventful.
column123: We were at 9;000 feet MSL approximately three miles east of DRUMM on the visual to Runway 35R. We had Runway 35R in sight and were cleared the visual. Abeam DRUMM we started a descent to 7;000 feet MSL. We leveled at 7;000 feet in between DRUMM and the FAF FRONZ. ATC informed us of a low altitude alert and advised the MVA was 7;500 feet. We were approximately 1;200 feet above the terrain and out of caution climbed up to 7;500 feet and intercept glidepath shortly later. No GPWS alerts occurred.

column121: We were cleared for a visual approach following a Boeing 777 to Runway 25L at LAX. On short final we encountered what was most likely wake turbulence from the proceeding aircraft on short final ~300 feet AGL. This presented as a roll and shear to a headwind. This apparent shear to a headwind along with an addition of some thrust put me high on the profile and ~15 knots above target speed. The severity of the event was below what I would describe as an 'upset'. When the turbulence subsided we appeared to be in a good position to land; so I continued the approach and landed. Being high on profile and bleeding off the excess speed in the flare resulted in a touchdown that was most likely beyond the touchdown zone. Despite this we were in position; with normal braking; to exit at taxiway H8. Tower instructed us to exit at H9.
column123: Upon landing on Runway 25L at LAX we landed outside of the normal 3000 feet of the touchdown zone after being preceded by a 777 to landing during light tail wind conditions. The briefing and approach were completely routine. We had originally planned to land on 24R but we were prepared for a possible change to Runway 25L. We were told on downwind that we would be following a heavy 777 and could expect 25L. Conditions were clear and we had the aircraft in sight. As I reconfigured the aircraft for landing on the south runway; the Captain made the comment that he would be prepared to fly a dot high. The Captain flew as he briefed and we experienced mostly calm winds all the way down the glideslope. By 200 feet AGL; we were back on glide slope.  The aircraft sank somewhat and by 100 feet we were almost a half dot low when we started to feel some of the wake of the 777.  The winds were still light; about 300/7.  The plane was very controllable and the Captain pitched up and added power slightly to stay on top of the wake. In doing so; the speed increased almost 15 knots and I considered calling for a go around.  It still appeared that we were going to be touching down in the first 3000 feet so I allowed the approach to continue.  The Captain flared the aircraft so that it looked like we would touch down right at about 3000 feet.  Without trying to hold the aircraft off; the plane continued down the runway at what seemed like 1 foot. The aircraft finally settled a couple hundred feet later and we turned off the runway and proceeded to the gate without any further issues.This was a case where one limit almost being crossed led to another limit being crossed. It looked like it was all going to work out.  We have all encountered 'disturbed' air from a wake before on landing especially at busy airports like LAX.  The question is how much of an adjustment do you have to make and at what point do you go around.  This looked very doable.  We had plenty of runway and performance.  This looked familiar with a landing in the touchdown zone.  I only thought later and realized that the Captain had elected to do a flaps 40 landing in a 737-900ER.  I know I have not done many flaps 40 landings in a 900ER in the last couple years; so I think my sight picture was off a bit for the flare and the touchdown.  We may have also gotten a bit more of ground effect than what I would have imagined.  The speed did not bleed off as fast as I had anticipated.
column125: B737 flight crew reported encountering wake turbulence from preceding B777 on approach to LAX that resulted in a long landing.

column121: I was the pilot flying into ROA at night. We were at 6;000 abeam the airport on a left downwind for Runway 6. We were cleared for the visual approach and I asked for 3;000 feet to be set into the FCP (Flight Control Panel). I was aware there was a ridge we would need to cross on our left base turn; charted at 2;000 feet on the RNAV (GPS) Y approach plate. I planned to descend to 3;000 while turning base and turn onto final at HIVLU; charted at 2;700 on the vertical profile of the approach plate. I began a descent at 2;000 FPM just prior to turning base. On the base leg; I believe due to the rate of descent; a GPWS terrain warning occurred. I immediately increased the power; increased pitch; and arrested the descent. The warning stopped. The ridge was visible throughout the event and the aircraft did not descend below 3;000 until past the ridge and beginning a turn to intercept the final approach course. We continued and made a normal landing.Being unfamiliar with this approach I should have communicated my plan to the Captain and asked if he agreed. After landing we discussed the approach and he explained that we could have descended on downwind to 5;200; continued past the ridge; turned base at or just inside DIXXY; and then followed the descent profile on the approach plate. I agree with his assessment; that would have been a better course of action. In the future I will verbalize my intentions; particularly on approaches I am unfamiliar with; and have a discussion to collaboratively determine the best course of action.

column121: Cleared for visual approach to Runway XXL on modified downwind; at 3;000 feet. Maneuvered aircraft to arrive at ZZZZZ at 3;000 to intercept LOC/ Glideslope simultaneously. Thought I was going to be above glideslope at ZZZZZ; asked FO to set 2;000 feet acquired Localizer inside of ZZZZZ; still at flaps 3. Drove into glideslope; selected flaps full. Radio altimeter briefly showed 800 feet due to terrain variation. Broke SOP of being fully configured by 1;000 feet.I accepted a night visual approach after a 12 hour; three leg duty day. Fatigue was a contributor. Hurried visual; should have intercepted final at IAF vs. FAF. My currency was spotty due to reduced flying on reserve.Never accept short visual approaches at end of long duty day.
column123: I was acting as PM. Approaching ZZZ at night from the SW with a high overcast layer above. ATC had us on a downwind leg for [Runway] XXL at 4;000. The autopilot and auto thrust were engaged. Approximately abeam the end of the runway; ATC asked us if we had the field in sight. The CA/PF says he does; so I reported the field in sight. ATC then cleared us for the visual XXL. The PF then set 3;000 in the FCU and I confirmed and referenced the plate showing the FAF ZZZZZ at 3000. I believe the PF went open descent to 3;000 from 4;000. The PF then began his base leg turn. The intercept angle initially looked like it was going to join the final just outside of the FAF. As we approached 3;000 the PF continued the turn in. He asked me to set 2;000 into the FCU and set 1;000 v/s down. I complied as it appeared to me that the PF was trying to intercept the course and I assumed he was going to join inside of ZZZZZ/FAF. He readjusted his intercept to join the final at ZZZZZ or just outside it. By that time I began to realize we were getting low on the profile. I still had visual contact with the runway. At approximately that time; the airplane leveled at 2000 just inside of ZZZZZ and ZZZ tower advised us of a low altitude alert and to check our altitude with the current altimeter setting. I replied with our call sign. We continued at 2;000 until intercepting the glideslope and landed safely. We were approaching the end of a long duty day and it was a non eventful and quiet flight; so fatigue was a possible factor. I am also familiar with operating into ZZZ; so I was a bit complacent; as well as being in VFR conditions and being able to see the runway. Having both pilots confirm and verify any further altitude changes in the FCU after the setting of the lowest charted altitudes for the approach used.

column121: We were established on final a few miles outside of the FAF. All was as it should be. We were stable on the localizer at 170 knots with flaps 15 autopilot on. Winds were 210 at 50 knots; steady. In a split second we went from straight and level to a left hand bank I'm guessing 20 degrees maybe more and the hook came up and we got a momentary stick shaker. As it happened I added power as we rolled wings level. We were about 20 degrees off heading; but the autopilot recovered to intercept the localizer; and we never lost any altitude. Like I said; it happened so quickly and recovered that I saw no need to disconnect the autopilot. We re-intercepted the localizer followed by the glide slope and shot the approach with no other concerns. I'm not sure if the winds were the reason or we hit someone's wake turbulence. I'm guessing it was wake turbulence. [Cause:] not sure - perhaps wake turbulence. The winds were strong but steady. 210 at 50 knots at 3000 ft. Perhaps ATC had us too close; but as I recall my traffic was a 757; 7 miles ahead; maybe it was wake from a plane on the parallel runway.

column121: While on the visual approach into LEX Runway 4; inside the final approach fix and following the precision approach path indicator (PAPI) lights with two white and two red lights indicating on glide path; we started to feel we were low according to our actual sight picture. After referencing the electronic glide slope from the ILS we saw we were slightly more than half scale deflection low and corrected back onto the glide slope. We received a 'GLIDESLOPE' GPWS indication while correcting. When back on the glide slope we looked out at the PAPI again and saw it still showed two white and two red lights despite the change in glide path. The ILS chart for runway 4 does not mention that the glide slope and visual glide path are not coincident. I believe when we noticed we were low on the glide slope; the PAPI should have been indicating three reds and a white or maybe even four reds.There is something wrong with the alignment of the PAPI equipment for the runway. It should not have shown us to be two white and two red lights that low on the glide slope. The conditions were visual and easily fixed once we referenced the electronic glide slope fully.The Airport Operations Staff and the FAA need to conduct an alignment of the PAPI equipment. There could be a situation in low visibility or very dark conditions where an aircraft arrives at the minimum decision altitude; has the runway environment or approach lights in sight and relies on the PAPI to help keep the pilot's eyes outside to land; which could lead to an issue where they either see themselves as too high according to the PAPI (if it's aligned too low) or; if it only indicates two white two red at any altitude; may follow the PAPI to an unsafe landing either outside of the touchdown zone or short of the runway.
column125: Air carrier Captain reported a problem with the alignment of the PAPI lights at LEX airport.

column121: While on a visual approach to [Runway] XXR; I was the PF. I hadn't flown in a month and a half or so; and the weather was conducive to trying to practice skills; so I turned off the AP/AT/FD. While over the ZZZ1 area; we received an RA directing me to level off. After complying with the RA; I began to descend to catch up to the approach; but at the altitude I was at; and the descent rate required to get back on the approach; I would not have been able to do so within stabilized approach criteria; so I conducted a go-around.The go-around was not smooth; and left room for improvement; but all call outs were made; and the checklists were accomplished. After passing through about 1;300 feet or so; we were assigned an altitude of 2;000 feet. The FO selected 2;000 feet and made the comment that we were about 700 feet away. We were climbing at a high rate as we were in TOGA (Still no AP/AT); and I was trying to get the plane slowed down; and arresting the climb rate; the FD began to turn to follow the missed approach for the ILS; as we had loaded it as the backup. We had yet received instruction as to what direction to turn; since we were assigned the visual approach. Between the FD turning and leveling off; it was at this point that I was focused on wanting the aircraft to continue straight on runway heading; and I flew through the 2;000 feet restriction; to my recollection by about 300 feet. The PM attempted to get some sort of instruction from ATC as to what they would like us to do; but struggled to get a word in on the radio. Finally they called and assigned us a vector. They also said since we went past 2;000; to level off at 2;500 feet. At this point; we had a moment; and was able to match up the information on the PFD with what I was wanting; and was able to add back in the AP/AT.This event felt like a culmination of a few problems. The root cause is that I; as the Captain; wasn't as prepared as I should have been. Having flown so little the last few months after upgrade sign off in month; I have been limited to just XX hours of total flying since month; my skill upkeep has been mainly book studying. While this is important; it does not replace being behind the controls of an aircraft.

column121: This was a second flight of the night shift; and was unusually busy; because the hospital we are associated with is understaffed because of COVID. We got the call to go to ZZZ; and the weather was marginal; but good enough to get in. We accepted the trip; and flew the RNAV approach to Runway XX. Later; I looked at the approach chart; and it said that the approach was not authorized at night. I think that being busy after a long period of hardly flying contributed to the error; and also that it was XA:00 in the morning. Also; due to COVID-induced rules; we had to wear masks flying; and it fogs up my glasses; making it harder to see. I need to make sure that I check all the notes on an approach before accepting a trip.

column121: Aircraft X was an arrival into PVU airport. He came to my sector from the northwest.  I had to vector the aircraft to get him in trail of SLC arrivals. I turned the aircraft in trail of a SLC B752.  As I turned the aircraft further north so final turn wasn't so tight; the pilot called the airport in sight. PVU was advertising Visual Approach Runway 13. I cleared the aircraft for the Visual Approach. They turned towards the airport; I shipped them to the Tower approximately 10 miles out when I saw them turning towards the airport. Almost immediately the aircraft started turning further south towards a 8;900 foot Minimum Vectoring Altitude descending out of 8000 feet. I called PVU and they said they were talking to him. I asked what they were doing and was advised the pilot stated they were making S turns or something. The aircraft descended to 5;100 feet 7 miles from the airport; elevation 4;497 feet.  At that point PVU called stating the pilot had lost the airport and was climbing to 9;000 feet. The pilot checked in with me heading 180 and climbing to 9;000 feet. I said roger. They then asked for vectors to an ILS and I stated I would when they were at Minimum Vectoring Altitudes. I vectored for the ILS with no further complications. I don't really have recommendations on my end. PVU after this incident continued to advertise Visual Approaches. My Supervisor did not want to fill out any paperwork on the incident; so they said nobody did anything wrong and to leave it at that. I felt like this should be reported somehow. The pilot was extremely low a long distance from the airport trying to scud run the Visual Approach. I knew visuals were iffy because when I descended the aircraft to 9;000 feet they were IMC; I turned them and descended them to 8;000 feet (also telling them to expect the ILS) and they called the airport. It's not surprising they were able to call the airport in sight at that point; the mountain sits below and often they can descend slightly and get the airport in sight. What's surprising is the pilot accepted the approach if clouds were that bad; that we didn't 'declare an emergency' for the aircraft when they lost the airport at 5;100 feet in an 8;900 foot Minimum Vectoring Altitude MVA; and that the supervisor wouldn't at least want to follow up with the pilot. I truly don't know why we wouldn't want to look at this further.
column125: A TRACON Controller reported an aircraft cleared for a Visual Approach turned away from the airport due to losing sight of the airport and flew below the Minimum Vectoring Altitude.

column121: We were two hours late due to ATC closure (ZZZ Center). After discussing with Dispatch that we would be very close to Crew day; we launched from ZZZ to ZZZ1. Landed approximately two hours late and took off from ZZZ1 in time to land back at ZZZ within Crew day; however; upon landing (with 20 minutes to spare) there were approximately 10 aircraft waiting for a gate. I sent Dispatch an ACARS telling them that we were going to exceed our 13-hour Crew day. I was told by ZZZ Ops and Dispatch that gates were being issued 'first-come; first-serve.' We waited approximately 45 minutes before making it to a gate. Total day was approximately 30 minutes over allowed hours.

column121: It was an uncontrolled airfield; visual approach; left traffic to Runway 17. Daylight calm winds and ceiling and visibility unlimited. Terrain factors were discussed on our briefing. We built the escape maneuver on the secondary flight plan. On the UNICOM frequency we were aware of a (Air Carrier) jet that was north of the field landing south on 17. They decided to slow so we could go first. We made a shorter approach than usual and while turning towards the final approach fix and putting out flaps 3; we descended away from the terrain as it sloped down hill and we got a terrain warning and reacted appropriately. We were very aware of the terrain and were completely surprised that the GPWS sounded off. We continued the approach and landed normally.We should have just extended our downwind leg and went number 2 to the runway.Maybe a little better understanding of the normal traffic patterns on the company pages.

column121: We had no operational issues whatsoever. This debrief is simply an observation. The SNA Tower closed for COVID exposure and SNA has become an uncontrolled field. The flight experience of the GA traffic that saturates that airspace is typically very beginner level and a Control Tower is essential. Uncontrolled field operations were nothing short of chaotic at that airport; and I really do see a potential for an accident to occur. Simply put; the pilots that are operating at that airport don't have the experience they need to merge with airline operations. The radio was saturated; there was confusion of who's going to take the runway next; a twin engine airplane decided to do a 360 to provide spacing while a commercial aircraft was 4 miles behind them on final.  A small aircraft almost took the runway with an aircraft on short final. I saw another small aircraft make left traffic for [Runway] 20R when the traffic pattern is only right.  In my professional opinion; I suggest that SNA be only an IFR only operation only during non-towered operations.  The amount of threats at that field are countless. Every pilot has to start somewhere and I'm sensitive to that; but a new small aircraft pilot practicing landings; turning an early base while an air carrier is on final (hypothetical example; that didn't happen); has no business in such a busy environment without a Tower Controller there to mitigate risk.Again; there was no event. This is an observation. Lack of traffic management is the factor in a potential disaster.If there is to be no tower at SNA; it needs to be commercial or IFR only operations.
column125: Air carrier First Officer reported flying into an airport with the Tower closed due to COVID-19 and described the associated issues flying in the same environment with inexperienced general aviation pilots.
