column121: ATC said there was rain on the field. There was a B737 flight ahead of us on the approach by 5 to 10 minutes. We were listening to both Approach and Tower to monitor their approach and landing. We discussed holding; prior to the preceding aircraft landing. The preceding B737 reported safe on deck; medium braking; and no issues. They said they broke out 300 feet above minimums; 500 feet above ground. This gave us confidence to conduct the approach and landing. We complied with the wet runway requirements on the 10-7 page (max braking).We were fully configured with flaps 30 by the final approach fix with the landing checklist complete. We were stable at both the 1000 feet and 500 feet calls. Approach was mostly smooth in the clouds with no turbulence. Auto throttles moved very little. The rain was moderate; maybe moderate to high. Once we broke out; we could see the runway the entire time. Wiper were keeping up; but on high. We broke out about 300 feet. Saw some ground before that; so knew we were close to breaking out. Saw the Runway End Identifier Lights at 300 feet and the runway edge lights and center line lights shortly after. Captain called 'approaching minimums' just prior to breaking out and I said I had the field in sight. The auto pilot and auto throttles were disengaged around 200 feet.Landed a little left of center line; but the right main gear was to the right of the center line. Nose was pointed down the runway. Landing was firm to hard. Flare was normal. Raised nose to arrest descent at 30 feet and power was at idle at touchdown. We were both surprised by the firmness of the landing. Nose wheel was set down normally. I thought we might have skipped a little bit; but the Captain did not think so. Maybe we hydro-planed then the tires grabbed. We immediately started sliding left. I don't remember us turning to the left. It seemed we were pointed down the runway but going left. I stepped on the right rudder; but it didn't seem to have an effect. The left main mount went off the pavement on the left side of the runway. It seemed to take a while to get it back on the pavement with full right rudder and full manual braking. With both pilots stepping on full manual brakes; the aircraft did not seem to slowdown. Never felt the brakes grabbing; and with max brakes; we should have felt the brakes grab. I do not know if that Captain was using the nose wheel steering to assist getting it back to the right.Once back on the pavement; we started to veer to the right; but never left the pavement on the right side. Once on the runway; we added full reverse. We put in full left rudder to keep it on the runway. We got the aircraft direction under control; but it was still pulling to the right with full braking and full left rudder. I think the Captain was countering the pull with nose wheel steering. The aircraft was still difficult to slow down.We exited the runway on the high-speed taxiway on the right at a slow speed and came to a stop just off the runway. Tower asked about our position and if we needed assistance. The aircraft instruments; indications and controls were all normal. The only noticed adverse indication was an 'anti-skid inop ' light. The Captain released the brakes and rolled forward a little to check for a blown tire and brake function. All seemed fine so we said we can proceed to the gate. The taxi in was uneventful.On the taxi in we informed Tower that we may have departed the paved surface and there may be Foreign Object and Debris (FOD)on the runway. After shutdown; the Captain got up and checked on the Flight Attendants and passengers. All seemed fine. One Flight Attendant said she was scared while it was occurring; but otherwise no adverse comments. They were obviously a little shaken at the time; but not excessively so. We contacted Company maintenance and wrote up the maintenance items that we knew about. Several of the Flight Attendants were working our deadhead flight back the next morning. Theystill did not seem to have any after effects from the event and were friendly toward us.Maybe the initial left drift was due to the right gear hydro planning while the left gear had full auto-brakes using anti-skid. Once the left gear returned to the pavement; it's ant-skid may have been damaged; causing the veering to the right under full brakes.
column123: Leg 3 of a transitioning First Officer on a 4 day 15 hour IOE (Initial Operating Experience). The First Officer had just flown the previous leg and done well. He shot the ILS Approach and flew a nice approach to a very nice landing. During the 3 hour sit prior to our flight we discussed the upcoming flight including the special airport requirements and 10-7 pages. Reviewed the flight plan NOTAMs and maintenance status of the aircraft. Noted the sequenced flashing lights to our planned arrival runway were out of service.First Officer made a nice takeoff and climb out to cruise and we continued to update the weather forecasts on the flight. Reviewed 10-7 pages decided to use max braking for training and for possibility of a wet runway. Worst weather report we saw was 4000 feet broken; light rain; and winds 240 at 8. Briefed the arrival and RNAV Approaches due to winds. We also briefed the ILS. Approaching top of descent asked Center which runway was in use. The ILS was the approach in use. Pulled up the landing data. Approaching with radar on noticed a cell and heard [an] aircraft ask for deviations. We asked for and received direct ZZZZZ on the arrival. Descending to 12;000 feet were cleared for the arrival and preceding aircraft ask for direct to a fix. There appeared to be cells just south of the approach. Approach informed us there was rain on the field; we discussed possible holding when we heard [another aircraft] cleared for the approach.I tuned VHF 2 to Tower frequency to get a heads up on the preceding aircraft. They were cleared to land and reported to Tower breaking out a few hundred feet above minimums landed and reported braking action medium. Approach cleared us direct to a fix and to report on the localizer cleared for the approach. Slowed toward 180 kts.; flaps 5 auto pilot on LNAV/VNAV; armed the app mode; made 2;500 feet call; reported established and was told to switch to Tower. Called Tower and reported 9 mile final was cleared to land. I believe Tower also said preceding aircraft broke out at 500 feet and braking action medium. Localizer and glideslope engaged set Touchdown Zone Elevation. Approaching the final approach fix gear down flaps 15 rolled speed back then selected flaps 30 and landing checklist.IMC and moderate rain but little to no turbulence or wind shifts. Passing approximately 1;000 to 800 feet saw ground and lights looking down called 1;000 feet then set Missed Approach altitude. Approach was stable and verified at 500 feet. Called approaching minimums slightly early approximately 400 feet to prepare for possibility of a go around. Approximately 300 feet broke out with Runway End Lights; High Intensity Runway Lights and runway center line in sight. First Officer disengaged the auto pilot and auto throttle approximately 150 to 200 feet. Still raining with wind shield wipers on high but could clearly see the runway environment. Short final dipped 1/4 dot low to try and land in the first 1;000 feet. Heard 50 feet just prior to crossing the threshold then 40; 30. At 30 the First Officer began the flare slowing the rate of descent then began reducing power. Everything appeared normal till the last 5 to 10 feet when it felt like the bottom dropped out to a firm to hard landing. Written up as a hard landing.There was no bounce. Power levers at idle and speed brakes deployed. I have used max auto brakes often due to the demo requirement for Operational Experience. Ordinarily they grab and push you forward in the seat. There was no such sensation other than a feeling the anti-skid was cycling and the aircraft did not appear to be slowing. I believe the nose was pointed straight down the runway and the touchdown had the nose just left of the center line. On touchdown the aircraft began to immediately slide to the left. I believe the aircraft hydroplaned after landing. The First Officer depressed full right rudder with no effect. First Officer was slow to go into reverse so I came on the controlsset max reverse and followed full right rudder yet the aircraft continued to slide left and the left main mount left the prepared surface. I used nose wheel steering to bring the aircraft back toward center line. All this happened immediately after touchdown or so I believe. As we continued down the runway with both of us standing on the brakes the aircraft began to slide toward the right side of the runway. With full reverse full manual brakes and what I believed to be full left rudder it required nose wheel input to keep the aircraft from leaving the right side of the runway. At no point in time did I feel the aircraft nose was pointed at or mostly toward either side of the runway from initial touchdown to stop.As we continued down the right side of the runway the aircraft still did not appear to be slowing normally. Approaching the high speed taxiway on the right side of the runway I elected to allow the aircraft to veer onto the taxi way. We had finally slowed where we were able to bring the aircraft to a stop. We were not 100 percent sure the left main mount had left the prepared surface so we discussed the possibility and the possibility of blown tires. Tower asked if we needed assistance. We did not note any usual indications or issues all instruments appeared normal. With the exception of the anti-skid fault light. I allowed the aircraft to roll forward checking the brakes which appeared to operate normally at slow speed and we had no indication of a deflated tire. I decided the aircraft could be taxied to the gate to deplane the passengers safely at slow speed and was cleared by Tower to do so. We also informed Tower there may be FOD (Foreign Object and Debris) on the runway. Uneventful taxi to parking with good clear area all the way into the gate. Performed the parking checklist then left the flight deck to check on the passengers and flight attendants. No injuries were reported. One flight attendant said she was a little shaken. Conferred with base ops then did a walk around with the contract mechanic. Called Company maintenance and made maintenance write ups.

column121: There is a trend with 737 windshields in that they no longer repel water when operating in rain. In addition; multiple aircraft I've flown have window crazing exacerbating the visual distortion in rain. In the past; I have contacted Maintenance and completed write ups which all resulted in being told there is no mitigation for this. Upon doing some research I learned Boeing used to have; and may still have; a procedure for applying Rain X to the windows. In the interest of safety; I'd like to suggest crazing be polished out of windows in accordance with Boeing's procedures; and some sort of hydrophobic coating be applied. It can be argued that windshields should be written up prior to accepting an aircraft; but there are two reasons this argument doesn't stand. First; a crew may fly an aircraft from an area with no rain to an area with rain and not realize the windows don't allow for adequate visibility until in the landing phase; and second; because I've attempted to rectify this numerous times either with Maintenance or through the reporting process with no changes being made. As far as writing an aircraft up after an event; I know there won't be anything done about it from past experience.
column125: Air carrier First Officer reported the B737 windshields no longer repel water when operating in rain.

column121: There was no adverse event or situation on this flight. I am making this report out of concern for safety and to hopefully prevent a future accident.Flying into PDK; Peachtree Airport in Atlanta; last night was typical - IFR approach in IMC using the best low approach procedure at the airport; the ILS to 21L. On final; breaking out of the overcast; my copilot and I did not see the runway because it was not lit despite our having triggered pilot controlled lighting on 120.0. We repeated the procedure and the runway lit up. Landing was uneventful.Lighting at this airport is poor. PDK is located in an urban area and is surrounded by ground lights not associated with the airport. One pilot; reportedly told ATC that all he could see was a 'sea of lights' when asked to call the airport in sight at night. I have had the same experience on multiple occasions. The pilot controlled lighting is erratic; and it is on a different frequency than CTAF after the Tower closes. The typical visual approach we get is from the side where it is hard to see the runway lights even when they are all on; and the beacon is weak and usually obscured by surrounding obstacles at the approach altitudes we are typically given. Moreover; the PAPIs have been NOTAMed out of service for quite some time now; so there is no visual vertical guidance at night.In my opinion; this setup is an accident waiting to happen; hence this report.
column125: Pilot reported airfield lighting at PDK is poor and erratic. Surrounding ground lights not associated with the airport compound the issue further.

column121: We were on our final phase of flight into ZZZ. It was IMC conditions with light snow upon arrival. The approach was ILS [Runway] XX. Upon landing; I used normal procedures with minimal braking for possible wet/icy runway and thrust reversers. All flight deck indications were as normal as we exit the runway and taxied to the ramp area for the conclusion of the flight. The next afternoon; we prepared to fly back to ZZZ1. The conditions had improved with clear skies and no precipitation. We began to taxi out to the runway completing all of the pre-flight checks and procedures. After takeoff; I began to execute the normal 'after takeoff checklist' with bringing the gear up. The gear did not retract and remained in the down and locked position. I am unsure if the gear came partly up or did not retract a small percentage at all. We climbed to 13;000 ft. and then 15;000 ft. attempting to resolve the situation. Once all procedures and attempts were exhausted; the decision was made to fly with the gear remaining in the down and locked position and head to ZZZ2. The airport weather was clear with light winds; and 10;000 ft. of runway. ZZZ2 was east of the mountains and clear of any weather. We attempted to burn as much fuel as possible as to not land overweight; flying at 17;000 ft. to also clear the terrain between our current position near ZZZ and ZZZ2. As we initiated our descent checklist and before landing checklist; our nose wheel steering was not illuminated and no indications of thrust reversers were available. Approach control and tower were notified of our situation and the possible need for Crash; Fire; Rescue upon landing.We landed at ZZZ2 without incident; I was able to taxi the plane off the runway using a highspeed taxiway. At that point; I stopped the aircraft and requested a tug to FBO on the field. There were no injuries to anyone on board the aircraft. The conclusion of events from information gathered from professional personnel; we encountered FOD upon landing at ZZZ or came in contact with ice buildup at some point during landing or touchdown. It is also possible that we could have encountered FOD upon takeoff the following day with our planned route to ZZZ1. Either occurrence of these two may have rendered our normal systems to function improperly.
column125: LR60 Captain reported a failure of landing gear to retract and elected to fly gear down to a diversion field.

column121: On a normal approach to landing Runway XX at ZZZ airport; the aircraft experienced a temporary loss of engine power. We were on the middle glide path of the PAPI (2 red; 2 white) on runway centerline at approximately 200 ft. AGL. Approach speed was approximately 85 mph with 20 degrees of flaps deployed. The throttle was at a low power setting and the aircraft was sinking slightly below glideslope; so I pushed it in a bit to increase power. There was no increase in power so I pushed the throttle in further; eventually to full. With no change in engine sound or perceived power I continued the descent maintaining airspeed to the ground. As the aircraft was already low; we struck two approach lights (MALSR) and knocked them over. In inspection afterwards it appears that the lights struck the lower engine cowling and dented it. The plane touched down and at this point I contacted the tower letting them know we had a loss of power. During rollout engine power came back due to the pushed in throttle. I opted to shut the engine down at this point as the state of the airplane was not known. Once crews arrived I had already exited the plane and did not see much visible damage. With the fire crews there we opted to restart the engine to taxi off the runway with no further issue.I believe this incident could be caused by carburetor icing or perhaps a contaminant in the fuel system. The aircraft should have had approximately 20 gallons of fuel remaining at time of landing; and was in a stabilized decent so I would rule out temporary interruption of fuel supply. There was no indication of low fuel on either gauge and the fuel selector was set to both. A contaminant in the fuel system seems possible; though I am not sure what would make this kind of incident transitory. It's hard to say for sure; but I believe we were still hearing the engine running and not just the windmilling prop the entire time; so I don't believe the engine had shut off entirely at any point.Carburetor icing seems like the most likely explanation I can think of; but the symptoms are not consistent with how I was trained to detect it. At no time did we experience engine roughness during the flight; which I believed was a characteristic sign of carb icing. Prior to the descent to land; the aircraft was circling at 10;000 ft. for aerial observation. Power settings during the decent were reduced and I remember thinking that the throttle position used during the cruise descent was further in than I would expect for the observed manifold pressure. I had only flown this plane briefly before; so I didn't think much more of it; but if carb ice was the blame this was probably the main indication. At 2;000 ft. I was reducing to lower power settings and I activated carburetor heat. There was no roughness after this point that I would expect to associate with carb icing as it cleared.The fact that the landing after sunset and starting to get dark was not a major factor; aside from difficulty seeing what was below (besides the lights) as the plane came down short of the runway. Maintaining the approach speed close to best glide after power loss was important in avoiding any injury or more significant damage to the aircraft. Given the long length of the runway (9;800 ft.) I could have avoided hitting anything by planning to land above the PAPI glidepath.
column125: C182 Pilot reported loss of engine thrust caused aircraft to sink and strike two approach lights during landing.

column121: I was practicing landings in the pattern at Spruce Creek with the winds favoring Runway 24.I was on left base leg descending through 600 ft. when; in my peripheral vision; I thought I saw a large bird directly off my left side. I shifted my focus toward it and realized that it was a drone with 4 rotors just about at my altitude close off my left wing.I resumed my pattern and landed. I then cautioned anyone on the frequency about the drone.What really concerned me is what happened next. I tried to report this to the Orlando FSDO. I made three phone calls and they could have cared less. I got a call back the next week and they said an inspector would call and get a statement. Nobody ever bothered. It has been over a month and they never followed up. Is there some sort of registry for airplane and drone near misses? How can the FAA know if these near misses are happening if they won't record them? The FAA should be gathering data on this. Hopefully; NASA ASRS will.
column122: Reporter stated the main concern is that the Orlando FSDO never replied after 3 calls. Reporter also stated not being aware of any other UAV incidents at that airport.
column125: Pilot of single engine aircraft was on base leg of approach and had a near miss with a multi rotor drone. Reporter was upset they never heard back from the local FSDO.

column121: Flight originated in ZZZ as a Part 135 scheduled flight to ZZZ1. There were no passengers on board.  ZZZ Center advised us that ZZZ4 Center would be going to an 'ATC Zero' condition for 1 hour 30 minutes at 15 minutes prior to our scheduled arrival due to a COVID situation in the Center facility. I made the decision to divert to ZZZ3 and accomplished training enroute and landing late afternoon. Weather in ZZZ3 was IFR with a report that fog was believed to be moving into the ZZZ3 area. We refueled and departed for ZZZ1. Training was again accomplished enroute...landing was at night with 2;500 broken; 3;100 overcast and 3 mile in R and BR. This was the end of a long day that started early morning for a departure to ZZZ5 from ZZZ1...scheduled time was 1 hour 50 minutes with a 20 minute turn. Due to waiting for a dead heading crew member we left ZZZ5 one hour late; arriving ZZZ1 at noon. I picked up another FO and departed for ZZZ for a planned 1 hour 40 minute flight. Landing at ZZZ; we then departed for a planned 1 hour 40 minute flight to ZZZ1. As noted previously; we were forced to divert to ZZZ3; due to ATC Zero. I exceeded 8 hours but; felt that; per our FOM and the FARs; I was legal to start and finish because the divert to ZZZ3 was not planned and the scheduled times were within legal limits. Note that I had over 14 hours rest before the flying the following day and was off duty after the days flying was complete and no other flying scheduled for several days.The issue that bothers me is that I was accomplishing training at night and in solid IMC conditions...never again. There was some unspoken pressure to get the training accomplished due to approaching holiday and a lack of aircraft to accomplish the training during daylight hours. I also should not have pressed the flying...the start of the problem was the unplanned ZZZ5 turn due to the scheduled Captain timing out and for whom I was asked to cover. As soon as we knew we would have to divert to ZZZ3; I should have terminated any further training and proceeded to ZZZ1 as soon as ZZZ4 Center re-opened. Several 'takeaways'...I will not allow this to happen to me again; even though I believed (and believe) I was legal...fatigue was not an issue. Training should only be accomplished in solid VMC conditions and preferably in daylight.

column121: Aircraft X was on ILS approach to Runway 4 at GSP when a VFR aircraft inbound to GMU went across final under Aircraft X. Traffic was called. GMU [Tower] was called. Aircraft X was below the clouds and stated he would execute missed approach if there was a RA. The pilot seemed to stay above glide slope to avoid the RA. Both aircraft eventually got each other in sight and Aircraft X landed.This is a known safety problem. The closest proximity of 2 aircraft in this situation is zero lateral and less than 100 feet vertical if the GSP arrival is on the glideslope and the VFR is in the GMU Delta airspace. However; it should not be taken for granted that aircraft on instrument and visual approaches will be at or above glideslope altitude. Since the VFR is under the GSP arrival; which is usually a jet; there are also wake turbulence issues.The Charlie and Delta airspace design is flawed. The FAA is either unwilling or unable to redesign the airspace to provide for the safety of the flying public.From my experience; the VFR aircraft involved are always inbound to GMU from SC72 (Chandelle). It is probably less than half a dozen aircraft that occasionally do this. When they depart GMU; they are instructed to fly over VPLOW when GSP Runway 4 is in use. If they were instructed to fly over VPLOW when inbound to GMU; the problem would be resolved. GMU Tower can instruct aircraft to remain outside the Delta and instruct aircraft where to enter the Delta; so there is no reason this solution would not work. It could be agreed to; briefed and implemented in 24 hours.This situation develops quickly and there are no guarantees that the controllers will see it before the RA or collision alert. When it is seen; or when the alarms go off; what does FAA management want the controllers to do?  Should we be breaking off the approach as a preventive measure?

column121: While on visual approach to Runway 09R at 2;000 MSL on right downwind per clearance; I was instructed to extend my downwind by the Tower Controller and that he would call my base turn. He called traffic to me at 3 miles; 2 o'clock position at same altitude and converging from right to left. I did not immediately establish visual contact with the traffic. The Controller called the other aircraft 3 times to alert him as to my position. The other aircraft never gained visual contact with me. Almost immediately after another call to me to point out the traffic I gained visual contact and received the RA to 'monitor vertical speed.' I informed ATC that I had traffic and was immediately descending to avoid conflict. Controller called the other aircraft and instructed them to maintain 2;000. I passed approximately 300 feet below the other aircraft. I resumed my assigned heading and upon clearance to land resumed visual approach and landed without incident.

column121: Flight from ZZZ to SJC. Nighttime VMC conditions. ATIS reported GS for the ILS 30L was OTS. We requested the RNP 30L from HEPAP since it was the last fix on our route. We were not on a STAR. We were cleared to descend to 4;700 feet until established; then cleared for the approach. We were high on our VSD and configured early to get on profile and on PATH. We were on PATH and met our requirements to set field elevation in then MCP. Just after passing HEPAP; ATC told us cancel approach clearance; climb and maintain 6;000 feet. Told us they got a low altitude alert. I told them we had the terrain and airport in sight. We started the climb as instructed; but had to ask for a heading as we were still on LNAV; the lateral track of the approach. ATC vectored us out for a downwind. They then asked us if we wanted vectors to YADUT for another approach. I told them negative; we still have the airport in sight for the visual. We were told to look for traffic and follow them for the visual 30L. We did and approach and landing was without incident.  We were not told to cross HEPAP at 4;700 feet. We were told to descend to 4;700 feet until established. This caused the confusion.We were not on a STAR. As I look back at it now; HEPAP is not the IAF; YADUT is. YADUT to HEPAP is 4;000 feet. Then after HEPAP; 3;600 feet. We need to have a better chart from coming in from ZZZ.

column121: Made visual contact with a GA aircraft on final approach to SEA runway 16R ahead of us. Continuing down the glideslope; we came to within 300 feet of the aircraft and a TCAS RA was issued; we climbed clear of the conflict and were then able to return to our glide path and make a stabilized approach and landing.Given the location of the aircraft relative to the approach path it is likely the GA aircraft had violated Seattle's Class B airspace; which is obviously not something we can control. However the TCAS system worked as designed and conflict was avoided without any abrupt maneuvering. Beyond additional focus on airspace awareness within the GA community; I do not have any additional recommendations.

column121: I am aware that the pilot-controlled lighting frequency at PDK is different from the CTAF frequency of 120.9. I was on a VFR approach to PDK at night with a commercial instrument-rated pilot in the right seat when the Tower was closed. We both found it extremely difficult to find the PCL frequency buried in the Chart Supplement. We tried keying on multiple frequencies including CTAF 120.9 and UNICOM 122.95 but could not activate the runway lights. We heard another pilot attempting to turn on PCL on the CTAF frequency as well. I was able to continue to a safe landing by referencing taxiway lights and the runway environment illuminated by my own landing lights. After landing; we finally found the PCL frequency of 120.0 in the Chart Supplement and found that the lights were working. I understand that it is the PIC's responsibility to be aware of such exceptions to common procedures; but I think it would be highly beneficial; especially to those unfamiliar with the airport; to publish critical information such as PCL frequencies in a more accessible manner; e.g. on the sectional/TAC or on the airport diagram. An even better solution would be to consolidate the PCL frequency onto CTAF to reduce pilot workload in a critical phase of flight.

We were at 2;500 ft. abeam ZZZ on a right downwind leg the autopilot was engaged at the time of the first warning. The autopilot was disengaged and I began an immediate climb and the Captain notified ZZZ approach of the terrain warning. We climbed to 3;500 ft. to silence the warning. We asked the Controller if we maintained IFR separation and he said that we did maintain it. The approach and landing continued normally.

column121: On flight ABCD from ZZZ to ZZZ1 while boarding passenger seated in seat XX1 came on that aircraft with his mask below his nose and between his lips. I told that passenger his mask must come over his nose and mouth. He responded 'He will try.' I then offered him a company approved surgical mask that were provided by the company. He responded 'No thanks.' That was the first time of me asking him to put his mask on properly. During my compliant check before doing the safety demo; the mask was down again. I told him once again about the mask. That was the second time. During cabin service the mask was down again and I had to remind him once more. Once cabin service was over and passengers were given time to eat and drink; I then went around to collect trash and he was asleep with his mask down. I called out to him and he opened his eyes and I told him for the third time about his mask. Once we got to the final descent his mask was down and I reminded him once again for the fourth time. Once I told him this time he just looked at me and shook his head. This passenger (XX1) and his wife seated in XX2 were both sick. They were both coughing without covering their cough.The couple kept coughing during takeoff; throughout the flight and landing. The coughing drew the attention of other passengers to look. People are very scared and when they hear coughing and other passengers [are] not wearing mask properly they worry. Many verbal warnings were given.

column121: I was scheduled to fly out of ZZZZ to ZZZZ1. While in cruise; I noticed the rudder pedals on the pilot side didn't mobilize as they should when pressed on to engage the brakes. I asked my SIC to perform the same checks on his side and he noted the pedals felt operational. We decided to continue our flight to ZZZZ1 and land taking into consideration that we might not have brake pressure and would have to perform a go-around and diversion to a longer runway. Rain had just ended in our destination and the runway was slightly wet. After touchdown; I try to engage the brakes to verify their status. Brakes started engaging on and off while we rolled on the runway. We decided to go-around. Before diverting to ZZZZ2 we acknowledge the fact that the runway was slightly wet and decided to try landing again. This second landing was firmer than the first one; I; as pilot flying; applied the brakes once more and this time around; no braking action was noticed therefore we decided to perform a go-around and divert to ZZZZ2. After touching down in ZZZZ2; brakes didn't engage appropriately; they were intermittent. After shutting down at the main ramp in ZZZZ2; deplaning and sending the passengers to a lounge; I talked to our mechanic on the phone and we both noted the brake fluid was lower than usual. The company flew another aircraft with a mechanic which happens to be also a pilot. He inspected the brakes visually and noted that the system looks fine. With that assessment; we accepted a ferry permit to fly back to base ZZZZ without passengers; for proper maintenance action. After touchdown in ZZZZ RWY XX and noting the fact that my 'now' SIC is a mechanic; I pushed on the rudder pedals to engage the brakes and try to assess what the issue was. This prompted no braking action. I communicated this to my SIC/Mechanic; and I increased pressure on the rudder pedals trying to note a response from the system with no luck. Eventually; after I kept applying pressure to the rudder pedals; the left and right main gear tires deflated disabling our aircraft in the runway. After this happened; we notified ATC (ZZZZ Tower) and shut down on the runway.
column125: Pilot reported that the brakes did not show any braking action during several landing attempts. Pilot diverted and while attempting to brake upon landing; both main gear tires deflated.

column121: On [Date]; we were scheduled to fly [Aircraft X] out of ZZZZ to ZZZZ1 at XA:00 local time. While en cruise; my Captain noticed the rudder pedals on the pilot side didn't mobilize as they should when pressed on. My Captain requested for me to step on the rudder pedals to see if I could notice any anomalies with the brakes on the Pilot Monitoring side. I did not notice anything unusual about the rudder pedals. We then proceeded to set up for the approach at ZZZZ1. We did a approach and landed on the runway and on the rollout I noticed that we were not slowing down like we should; the plane seemed to stutter (as if the brakes were engaging and then disengaging) The Captain then performed a go around. After the go around we both agree that we are in a position to safely try again. This time we come in a little bit slower and once again on the roll out my Captain was pressing the brakes and we were not decelerating as normal. After the second go around we both agree our best option is to divert to ZZZZ2 where there is a significantly longer runway. We land at ZZZZ2 without incident and deplane the passengers.

Once in the air; I transited through the congested ZZZ1 airspace to return to my home airport. Because of this; I pay more attention to flying the airplane; while radio work is handled habitually; and I wasn't letting each radio transaction sink in; nor ensuring I make all the necessary calls.  This leads to diminished piloting skills; and more practice is necessary to operate in congested airspace.

column121: We came to land at ZZZ airport; executed ILS Runway XX to approach to it even the weather conditions where completely Visual. Everything was normal; we touched down and begun our landing roll; lowered the nose of the aircraft to the ground; deployed reverse thrust and applied brakes; suddenly master warning illuminated indicating a failure; I was not able to determine the source of the failure at that very moment; I have increased progressively the pressure on the brakes and reverse thrust; aircraft begin to decelerate rapidly and begin to veer slightly to the left side; condition I counteracted with differential braking and thrust reverse power reduction; reached for the steering wheel to continue counteracting the veering tendency and steering wheel was stiff and locked unable to move it; I mention to my First Officer we have lost steering control; continued counteracting with rudder pedal and differential braking; but rudder aerodynamic capability diminish with speed reduction; I mention again to my First Officer that we had lost steering control and felt diminish braking action; airplane slowly overrun into the grass to the left side and lost braking action; I went to and transfer to emergency braking and recover partial control of the aircraft finally stopping on the grass.Executed passenger evacuation checklist to secure aircraft; passengers and crew evacuated through main door; no injury occurred to any one onboard; airplane is in good condition; we called FSS to advise of our incident during landing.

column121: We were flying to ASE and I was the Pilot Monitoring. As we approached Aspen; we were given vectors to intercept LOC E into ASE. We accepted clearance to maintain 13;000 ft until established and clearance to execute LOC E approach. As we were intercepting LOC we picked up visual with the airport and commenced descent towards it while having visual reference with the airport and all the terrain around. In the midst of all things happening quickly and the excitement of having visual with the airport; I failed to communicate to ATC my intention of executing visual approach. At some point we lost the visual with the airport due to a cloud layer just ahead of us; but not the terrain. At the same time ATC issued an altitude alert to us due to us being lower than what LOC approach altitudes called for. I advised that we had terrain in sight but lost the visual with the airport and said that we would be going missed and starting a climb. We got another altitude alert from ATC. We advised we are in a climb and asked vectors; until we decide what to do. We proceeded to ZZZ airport afterwards.  At no time we were in IMC conditions and maintained visual clearance from all the terrain. My mistake in this case was deviating from ATC clearance and going visual without communicating that to ATC. And also; not staying on a LOC due to a cloud layer over the airport. At no time was the aircraft in IMC conditions and we were maintaining clearance with terrain at all times. By all means the aircraft was never put in any unsafe condition.  I did learn from this experience to clearly and concisely communicate any and all of my intentions with ATC; so there is no misunderstanding at any point of the flight.

column118: Air Traffic Control Issued New Clearance; Air Traffic Control Provided Assistance; Flight Crew Diverted; Flight Crew Took Evasive Action; Flight Crew Requested ATC Assistance / Clarification; Flight Crew Executed Go Around / Missed Approach
column121: I was the PF (Pilot Flying) on Aircraft X from ZZZ2-ZZZ1. I flew the RNAV RNP [Runway] XX approach with rain and wind in the vicinity. I attempted to configure early but a strong tailwind made it challenging. I encountered a wind gust which caused me to counter increasing airspeed by pitching up and slightly reduce power to maintain airspeed and continue configuring. This resulted in a position higher than anticipated. The PM (Pilot Monitoring) noticed this also and we articulated that. About that time; ATC directed a go-around. We executed the missed approach and climbed to 3;000 [feet] on vectors for another approach.Noting the conditions; I configured early for the second RNAV RNP XX approach. Rain and wind were still predominant throughout the approach but fewer gust excursions and we remained stable throughout the approach all the way to minimums. At minimums; we both had the approach lights and runway environment in sight and I continued the approach. As I turned final aligning with Runway XX; I lost sight of the runway environment due to the weather. The PM called go around and I initiated go around procedures. We climbed to 3;000 feet on vectors as directed by ATC. We communicated with one another and ensured we were both in the green; we had ample fuel; and agreed to attempt a third approach. On the third RNAV RNP XX approach; I configured early again and remained stable throughout the approach. The wind was still a factor though and required careful monitoring and aircraft correction to maintain a stable approach. Fully configured and in the vicinity of arriving at minimums; ATC advised a wind shift on the field of 330 degrees at 26 knots of wind. ATC asked for our intentions. Due to airline limitations on Maximum Tailwind Component for Landing; the PM told ATC we could not land and we would go-around. ZZZ1 Tower handed us off to ZZZ Departure and they advised us that they 'turned the airfield around' and they would give us vectors for the ILS RWY XY approach and we would be 'number 1' to the field. Again; the Captain and I communicated that we were both in the green; we noted that we had ample fuel; and we prepared for an ILS approach to Runway XY.After intercepting the localizer for the ILS to Runway XY; I configured fully and continued down the glideslope from the final approach fix. It was raining outside and was windy but we remained stable. Shortly prior to 1;000 feet AGL; it grew increasingly difficult to maintain a stable approach. I told the PM that I didn't feel comfortable; the weather was worsening; and it was likely that we would not be stable at 1;000 feet. We agreed and elected to go-around. Just as I began the go around procedure; we encountered a Wind Shear Warning and I proceeded with the Wind Shear escape maneuver. We exited the conditions quickly and transitioned to ATC's directives. We quickly agreed to divert to ZZZ3 and the PM informed ATC of our intention to divert. We climbed to 3;000 feet and flew vectors to ZZZ3.We flew the ILS RWY XYC approach and landed safely at ZZZ3 and taxied to the gate. We shut down the aircraft with approximately 7.4k pounds of fuel remaining. During the deplaning process; the Captain coordinated with the Maintenance and Dispatch and agreed to continue on to ZZZ1 as the weather cleared the area. We added fuel departing with 11.8k pounds of fuel. The Captain flew as the PF and I was the PM for the return flight to ZZZ1. The Captain maintained appropriate communication throughout the event. He communicated with the passengers explaining as best as possible what occurred and what we were doing to ensure their safety while doing our best to land at our intended destination. He kept the flight attendants apprised of the situation after each approach and we discussed each approach; verified we were both in the green; and maintained situational awareness at all times.
column123: Flew RNAV RNP [Runway] XX approach. FO (First Officer) was PF (Pilot Flying) and was configuring the aircraft early. He was at gear down; flaps 15; and we encountered a wind gust that drove the airspeed indication almost to redline. The FO raised nose slightly and reduced power to slow the airspeed in order to continue configuring the aircraft. As this occurred; aircraft got a little high. As PM; I noticed this and was in process of informing FO when ATC said that we looked a little high and to just go around. We executed the missed approach as directed by ATC and climbed on a heading to 3;000 ft. to vector for another approach.On the second RNAV RNP XX; FO configured the aircraft early and shot approach to minimums. At minimums; approach lights and runway lights were visible; but as we turned to final approach; visibility was lost.  I called go-around; and FO initiated go-around. We climbed on a heading to 3;000 feet. We communicated with each other that we were both in the green; had plenty of fuel; and prepared for another approach.On the third RNAV RNP XX; the FO had fully configured the aircraft; when ATC advised that the wind had shifted out of the north at 330 degrees and we had 26 knots of wind at our tail. ATC asked our intentions; I told them that we could not land with those winds and that we were going around. At that point; ATC handed us off to departure; and departure immediately advised us that they had turned the airport around and could give us an immediate left downwind to be number 1; shooting ILS Runway XY. FO and I communicated with each other that we were both in the green; had plenty of fuel; and prepared for an approach using the new runway.On attempt to Runway XY; we intercepted localizer and G/S. We had continual high winds indicating to me that the aircraft would not be able to remain stable throughout approach and landing. As PIC; I called for a go-around. FO communicated to me his agreement; and I immediately notified Tower of our intention to divert to ZZZ3. FO initiated the miss. As he did; we encountered a brief Wind Shear Warning. FO finished cleaning aircraft up and we diverted to ZZZ3 without incident.We were vectored to ZZZ3 and landed on Runway XYC and taxied to the gate with 7.4 pounds of fuel. I called Dispatch and she said we had plenty of fuel to get to ZZZ1 but as a precaution due to the frontal weather passage; she added more fuel and brought us up to 11.8 pounds. After the front passage; I flew the aircraft as PF uneventfully to ZZZ1. During all of the approaches and the divert I kept the flight attendants up to date and communicated with the passengers at all times. We exercised good situational awareness and followed SOPs during the entire flight.

AGL and about 0.4 SM from the runway threshold; ZZZ Tower cleared a Cirrus for takeoff. As the Cirrus began entering the runway; I rejected the landing and began to apply full power before the tower instructed me to go around and side-step to the right. I acknowledged the go-around; and requested a north departure; which was granted to follow the Cirrus that had just taken off. column121: On Date about XA:45; I; Name; was receiving instruction for checkout in a C-172 Skyhawk. We had been on downwind abeam the landing point when we were instructed to do a right 360-turn for traffic. Upon completion of the turn; we continued downwind and then were instructed to do another 360-degree turn. While exiting the second turn we were given several traffic alerts for aircraft landing Runway XX. We turned onto base and final when we received a traffic alert. My instructor announced she had the aircraft controls and began a turn away from the other aircraft. After clearing the conflict; she gave me back the controls and we landed on Runway XX without incident.Our aircraft was [a] Cessna 172 Skyhawk. I believe everyone involved in this incident was trying to operate in a safe and standard manner. My instructor did an excellent job of spotting; announcing; and reacting to the threat.
column125: Pilot reported an NMAC with another aircraft while receiving instruction for a checkout.

column121: Prior to entering Class D airspace at ZZZ; I found the AWOS system was down. Upon contact with ATC; they gave an altimeter only and asked my preferred runway. I chose [Runway] 'XX' based on my latest wind report. I landed on 'XX' and during roll-out I was hit by severe windshear. It lifted my right wing; I still had full flaps; and became airborne again. I was just off the landing strip pavement. I corrected the flight path; flew through a small bush; and finished the landing in the dirt alongside the runway. Tower advised me to taxi to transient parking and report to Tower. Upon exiting the airplane; I noticed damage to the prop. During the interview with the airport supervisor; we noticed the windsock changing directions frequently. It was a hot day in the mountains at 6000 ft. MSL; more turbulence was expected.
column125: Cessna 182 pilot reported wind shear during landing rollout which caused them to become airborne.  Pilot regained control but landed off of the runway surface with damage sustained to the propeller.

column118: Flight Crew Took Evasive Action
column121: While making right traffic at TVC for Runway 36 in Aircraft X; I had a near collision with Aircraft Y.Pattern was busy already. I was told to follow aircraft that had extended their upwind. We followed that traffic and made our right crosswind turn. While in the crosswind; the Tower told Aircraft Y to follow the traffic ahead of them in the right crosswind. Aircraft Y confirmed that they had the traffic in sight. Tower then confirmed with Aircraft Y that it was the traffic in the crosswind turn not the traffic in the downwind and Aircraft Y again confirmed that they had me in sight. We made our turn into the right downwind. After rolling out of our turn we got a traffic alert. I looked at the MFD (Multi-function Flight Display) to confirm where this traffic was. At that time I saw that Aircraft Y had turned right crosswind into us and that they were 100 feet above us. I then took control from the student and immediately descended to 500 feet AGL in the right downwind of Runway 36. Then I told the Tower that I was descending. The Tower Controller confirmed that they saw Aircraft Y cut us off. Aircraft Y then continued to make right traffic above us. Aircraft Y asked if they should make a left 360 turn to follow us. Tower told them to continue in the downwind. After this continued for a few seconds I asked tower if I should make a left 360 turn to follow Aircraft Y and Tower told me to continue in the downwind. After that I accelerated to gain distance between myself and Aircraft Y and eventually was able to gain enough distance to comfortably climb back up to a reasonable altitude for where I was at the time. Had I not taken action I believe that this near miss could have very easily been an in air collision.
column125: General aviation instructor pilot reported a near miss with another aircraft in the traffic pattern. The instructor pilot took the controls and maneuvered away from the other aircraft when they did not follow the traffic sequence instructions from ATC.
